facets.emotional: Emotional life without sacred element.
facets.cognitive: Mind doesn’t recognize either Brahman form.
facets.psychic: The soul knows neither form, with no relationship to the Divine in any mode.

deficient.text:
Materialist denial of both Brahman forms—seeing only physical phenomena.

Somatic: Body interacts with phenomena without sacred dimension.

Emotional: Emotional life without sacred element.

Cognitive: Mind doesn’t recognize either Brahman form.

Psychic: The soul knows neither form, with no relationship to the Divine in any mode.
deficient.vignette: For Alan there is only the measurable: atoms, incentives, neurons firing. Talk of the sacred—whether as a beloved deity or a formless ground of being—strikes him as a category error, a pleasant fiction at best. It is not that he has weighed the transcendent and rejected it; the dimension simply never appears to him at all. He lives in a well-lit room with the windows painted over, and does not know the windows are there.

attributes.attribute: Rajas Projects, Tamas Veils, Sattva Reveals
attributes.title: Rajas Projects, Tamas Veils, Sattva Reveals
attributes.aspect: The three gunas each have specific functions in obstructing or revealing truth: Tamas veils (hides truth); Rajas projects (creates illusory appearance); Sattva reveals (clears the veiling). Liberation requires reducing tamas, channeling rajas, cultivating sattva, then transcending even sattva.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental (discernment of guṇa-function) · Ontic mode: Functional schema (the operations of the three guṇas) · Valence: Neutral — a discriminative tool · Guṇa: all three (each named by its function) · Scale: Individual, with cosmic ground · Causal status: Mechanism (the operational role of each guṇa) · Key relation: Summary schema of the guṇa triad; pairs with the veiling teaching.
coordinate.ontic_mode: Functional schema (the operations of the three guṇas)
coordinate.guna: all three (each named by its function)
coordinate.causal_status: Mechanism (the operational role of each guṇa)
coordinate.key_relation: Summary schema of the guṇa triad; pairs with the veiling teaching.

balanced.label: Balanced expression

facets.overview: Sattva predominantly cultivated; rajas channeled toward goal; tamas reduced. Eventually transcending even sattva.
facets.somatic: Body’s biology shifted toward sattvic balance.
facets.emotional: Emotional clarity from sattva-predominance.
facets.cognitive: Mind discriminating, calm, revealing—sattvic-quality dominant.
facets.psychic: The soul grasps the gunas’ functions — tamas veils, rajas projects, sattva reveals — and cultivates the revealing power.

balanced.text:
Sattva predominantly cultivated; rajas channeled toward goal; tamas reduced. Eventually transcending even sattva.

Somatic: Body’s biology shifted toward sattvic balance.

Emotional: Emotional clarity from sattva-predominance.

Cognitive: Mind discriminating, calm, revealing—sattvic-quality dominant.

Psychic: The soul grasps the gunas’ functions — tamas veils, rajas projects, sattva reveals — and cultivates the revealing power.
balanced.vignette: Slowly, over years of practice, Ondine’s life has tilted toward the light. Her drive now serves a genuine aim rather than mere craving; the old dullness has thinned; and she sees people and situations more truly than she used to. Even her hard-won clarity she has begun to hold loosely—grateful for it, but sensing that it too is a place to pass through, not to camp. The veils are lifting, one at a time.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Sattva-attachment becoming subtle bondage. Sattvic pride. Sticking at sattva instead of transcending.
facets.somatic: Body forced into ‘pure’ patterns rigidly.
facets.emotional: Spiritual pride in pure feeling.
facets.cognitive: Attachment to clarity as identity.
facets.psychic: The soul over-systematizes the scheme, analyzing the gunas rather than working with them.

excess.text:
Sattva-attachment becoming subtle bondage. Sattvic pride. Sticking at sattva instead of transcending.

Somatic: Body forced into ‘pure’ patterns rigidly.

Emotional: Spiritual pride in pure feeling.

Cognitive: Attachment to clarity as identity.

Psychic: The soul over-systematizes the scheme, analyzing the gunas rather than working with them.
excess.vignette: Julian is, by every measure, a ‘good’ meditator—calm, disciplined, wise-sounding—and he has become quietly attached to being so. His serenity is now an identity he defends; his clarity, a possession he is proud of; his equanimity, subtly performed. He has climbed to sattva and pitched a tent, mistaking the clean, high resting-place for the summit. The very luminosity meant to be passed through has become his most refined attachment.

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.overview: Tamas-rajas dominance. Veiling and projection ruling. Truth-revealing function dormant.
facets.somatic: Body in tamas-rajas patterns of lethargy or restlessness.
facets.emotional: Emotional darkness or volatility.
facets.cognitive: Mind veiled or projecting, no clear seeing.
facets.psychic: The soul is blind to the mechanism, projection and veiling running unchecked.

deficient.text:
Tamas-rajas dominance. Veiling and projection ruling. Truth-revealing function dormant.

Somatic: Body in tamas-rajas patterns of lethargy or restlessness.

Emotional: Emotional darkness or volatility.

Cognitive: Mind veiled or projecting, no clear seeing.

Psychic: The soul is blind to the mechanism, projection and veiling running unchecked.
deficient.vignette: In Carl’s world, projection and concealment run the show. He sees his own fears in everyone’s faces—rajas projecting—while a dull haze hides from him what is actually there—tamas veiling—so that he is forever reacting to a world mostly of his own making. The quiet faculty that would simply reveal things as they are lies dormant. He is certain he sees clearly, and that certainty is the veil.

attributes.title: Brahma Sattva — The Contemplative Universal
attributes.aspect: Named after Brahmā, the Creator: pure consciousness reflecting on its own nature

coordinate.raw: Seat: Psychic–Mental · Ontic mode: Type — a mānasika-prakṛti archetype (personality type) · Valence: Neutral temperament (a given disposition to work with) · Guṇa: Sattva-dominant · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: Constitutional disposition · Key relation: A sattvic type (1 of 7) — named for Brahmā; pure contemplative consciousness.
coordinate.valence: Neutral temperament (a given disposition to work with)
coordinate.key_relation: A sattvic type (1 of 7) — named for Brahmā; pure contemplative consciousness.

additional_info.text: The Brahma-sattvic individual lives from the deepest possible centre. Their inner experience is one of quiet luminosity—a mind that naturally rests in clarity the way water rests in stillness. They possess profound spiritual knowledge (Jnāna and Vijnāna), an excellent memory, and an extraordinary power of discrimination. They are capable of scientific, philosophical, and religious discourse with equal facility, because for them these are not separate domains but facets of a single truth. Emotionally, they are free from desire, anger, greed, conceit, infatuation, envy, dejection, and intolerance. This is not the coldness of repression but the warmth of a love so vast that it extends impartially to all beings—seeing divinity in every form of life. They observe fasts and rituals not from obligation but from a natural attunement to the sacred rhythms of existence. In lived experience, the Brahma type is the person in any community who seems to carry an inner stillness that others instinctively trust. They are the natural counsellor, the person whose presence calms a room. Their suffering, when it arises, tends to come not from personal desire but from witnessing the suffering of others—a compassionate ache that motivates them toward service rather than withdrawal.

attributes.title: Rishi Sattva (Arsha) — The Devoted Scholar
attributes.aspect: Named after the Rishis, the ancient seers: knowledge pursued as sacred duty

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental–Psychic · Ontic mode: Type — a mānasika-prakṛti archetype (personality type) · Valence: Neutral temperament (a given disposition to work with) · Guṇa: Sattva-dominant · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: Constitutional disposition · Key relation: A sattvic type — named for the Ṛṣis; knowledge pursued as sacred duty.
coordinate.valence: Neutral temperament (a given disposition to work with)
coordinate.key_relation: A sattvic type — named for the Ṛṣis; knowledge pursued as sacred duty.

additional_info.text: The Rishi-sattvic individual experiences life as a vocation of study and devotion. Their inner world is one of disciplined focus—the mind naturally inclines toward learning, ritual, and the pursuit of truth through sustained intellectual effort. They are endowed with keen perception and an understanding of scientific truth; their memory is sharp, and they are eloquent speakers who can articulate complex ideas with precision and beauty. They are devoted to sacrifice, study, vows, and spiritual practices; celibacy often comes naturally to them—not as suppression but as a channelling of vital energy toward higher pursuits. They are hospitable, devoid of pride, conceit, attachment, hate, infatuation, greed, and anger. Their humility is not performed but genuine: they are so absorbed in their pursuit of knowledge that self-promotion feels irrelevant. In lived experience, the Rishi type is the scholar-practitioner whose depth of learning inspires awe, yet whose personal manner is warm and unpretentious. They are often found in academic, monastic, or research settings—any environment where sustained inquiry is valued. Their challenge is that their devotion to study can sometimes create a distance from the messy, passionate dimensions of ordinary life.

attributes.title: Aindra (Mahendra) Sattva — The Noble Administrator
attributes.aspect: Named after Indra, King of the Devas: leadership rooted in ethical authority

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental–Vital · Ontic mode: Type — a mānasika-prakṛti archetype (personality type) · Valence: Neutral temperament (a given disposition to work with) · Guṇa: Sattva-dominant · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: Constitutional disposition · Key relation: A sattvic type — named for Indra; leadership rooted in ethical authority.
coordinate.valence: Neutral temperament (a given disposition to work with)
coordinate.key_relation: A sattvic type — named for Indra; leadership rooted in ethical authority.

additional_info.text: The Aindra-sattvic individual experiences life through the lens of stewardship and responsibility. Their inner world is one of strategic clarity—they naturally perceive the dynamics of groups, institutions, and systems, and feel called to organize and lead them toward their highest potential. They are intelligent, strategic, and capable of long-term planning. They are efficient decision-makers and command respect naturally, not through coercion but through the evident quality of their judgment. They possess a strong physical and psychological presence and are brave—willing to face difficult situations and make hard decisions. They manage resources and people effectively, using their power for both personal fulfilment and the welfare of their followers and community. They are authoritative in speech and can inspire large groups toward a common purpose. In lived experience, the Aindra type is the natural leader who is found heading organizations, communities, or movements. Their challenge is the temptation of power—when the sattvic quality dims, the authority that once served others can become self-serving. At their best, they embody the Vedic ideal of the just ruler: firm, wise, and dedicated to the welfare of all.

attributes.title: Yama Sattva — The Disciplined Solitary
attributes.aspect: Named after Yama, Lord of Dharma: unwavering adherence to principle

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental–Vital · Ontic mode: Type — a mānasika-prakṛti archetype (personality type) · Valence: Neutral temperament (a given disposition to work with) · Guṇa: Sattva-dominant · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: Constitutional disposition · Key relation: A sattvic type — named for Yama; unwavering adherence to principle.
coordinate.valence: Neutral temperament (a given disposition to work with)
coordinate.key_relation: A sattvic type — named for Yama; unwavering adherence to principle.

additional_info.text: The Yama-sattvic individual experiences life as a matter of principle. Their inner world is ordered, structured, and governed by a powerful moral compass. They are strong-willed, intelligent, and focused on single-minded goals. They follow their aims with a determination that others may find intimidating, yet they are not aggressive—merely unshakeable. They exhibit patience and are free from excessive hatred or envy. They are highly moral and abide strictly by laws and principles—not out of fear of punishment but from a deep conviction that right action is its own reward. They are independent, non-quarrelsome, and often solitary by preference. They do not seek social validation and are comfortable making decisions that others may not understand or support. In lived experience, the Yama type is the principled individual who stands firm when others waver—the judge, the ethicist, the monk who maintains a vow for decades. Their challenge is the risk of rigidity: when the sattvic quality becomes too concentrated in structure, it can lose the warmth of compassion and become a cold, unyielding legalism.

attributes.title: Varuna Sattva — The Eloquent Guardian
attributes.aspect: Named after Varuna, Lord of Cosmic Order: truth spoken with grace

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental–Vital · Ontic mode: Type — a mānasika-prakṛti archetype (personality type) · Valence: Neutral temperament (a given disposition to work with) · Guṇa: Sattva-dominant · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: Constitutional disposition · Key relation: A sattvic type — named for Varuṇa; truth spoken with grace.
coordinate.valence: Neutral temperament (a given disposition to work with)
coordinate.key_relation: A sattvic type — named for Varuṇa; truth spoken with grace.

additional_info.text: The Varuna-sattvic individual experiences life as an arena for truth and justice, expressed with eloquence and composure. Their inner world is characterized by emotional balance—they are gifted speakers who can maintain composure in the most difficult situations, yet they are not cold. They exhibit controlled emotions, displaying appropriate anger or pleasure when the situation demands it, and they fight passionately for the right causes. They are inherently pure and clean, disliking messiness, disorder, or chaotic environments—not from fastidiousness but from a deep alignment with cosmic order (Rita). They are deeply spiritual and honest, and their speech carries a natural authority because it is rooted in truthfulness rather than manipulation. In lived experience, the Varuna type is the advocate, the diplomat, the spiritual teacher whose words carry weight because they emerge from integrity. They are found in settings where truth must be articulated under pressure—courtrooms, negotiation tables, podiums. Their challenge is that their commitment to order can sometimes make them intolerant of the creative chaos that is also a part of life.

attributes.title: Kubera Sattva — The Generous Prosperer
attributes.aspect: Named after Kubera, Lord of Wealth: abundance pursued and shared

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital–Physical · Ontic mode: Type — a mānasika-prakṛti archetype (personality type) · Valence: Neutral temperament (a given disposition to work with) · Guṇa: Sattva-dominant · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: Constitutional disposition · Key relation: A sattvic type — named for Kubera; abundance pursued and shared.
coordinate.valence: Neutral temperament (a given disposition to work with)
coordinate.key_relation: A sattvic type — named for Kubera; abundance pursued and shared.

additional_info.text: The Kubera-sattvic individual experiences life through the lens of abundance—not merely material wealth, but the richness of social connection, intellectual engagement, and generous exchange. They possess shrewd intelligence directed toward acquiring and managing resources, and they enjoy debate and intellectual discussion. They pursue wealth acquisition with passion, yet they do so with a consciousness of self-dignity and ethical boundary. They are courageous and patient, enjoying a rich social life surrounded by family and friends. They are socially extroverted and generous with their wealth, using it to support others and to create environments of beauty and comfort. They maintain their self-dignity without arrogance, understanding that true wealth includes the quality of one’s relationships. In lived experience, the Kubera type is the philanthropist, the successful entrepreneur who builds communities, the host whose home is always open. Their challenge is the boundary between sattvic generosity and rajasic acquisitiveness—when the drive to accumulate overshadows the impulse to share, the Kubera type slides toward the rajasic spectrum.

attributes.title: Gandharva Sattva — The Refined Artist
attributes.aspect: Named after the Gandharvas, celestial musicians: beauty as a path to the divine

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital–Psychic · Ontic mode: Type — a mānasika-prakṛti archetype (personality type) · Valence: Neutral temperament (a given disposition to work with) · Guṇa: Sattva-dominant · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: Constitutional disposition · Key relation: A sattvic type — named for the Gandharvas; beauty as a path to the divine.
coordinate.valence: Neutral temperament (a given disposition to work with)
coordinate.key_relation: A sattvic type — named for the Gandharvas; beauty as a path to the divine.

additional_info.text: The Gandharva-sattvic individual experiences life as an aesthetic and sensory celebration. Their inner world is one of refined sensitivity—they perceive beauty in form, sound, colour, and movement that others might overlook. They are talented in the creative arts: music, dance, poetry, and visual expression come naturally to them. They have a sophisticated and elegant sense of style. They are prone to enjoying life’s pleasures in a refined manner—they can be sensual, but they often channel their vitality into creative expression and charming social interactions rather than mere indulgence. Their physical beauty and grace are often remarked upon, but it is their aesthetic intelligence that truly distinguishes them. In lived experience, the Gandharva type is the artist, the dancer, the musician, the designer whose work elevates the experience of those around them. Their challenge is the thin line between sattvic aesthetic refinement and rajasic sensual indulgence—when pleasure becomes the primary motivation rather than the natural by-product of creative expression, the Gandharva type risks losing their luminous quality.

attributes.title: Asura Rajasika — The Ambitious Dominator
attributes.aspect: Named after the Asuras, the power-seeking anti-gods: strength without surrender

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital–Mental · Ontic mode: Type — a mānasika-prakṛti archetype (personality type) · Valence: Neutral temperament (a given disposition to work with) · Guṇa: Rajas-dominant · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: Constitutional disposition · Key relation: A rajasic type (1 of 6) — named for the Asuras; strength without surrender.
coordinate.valence: Neutral temperament (a given disposition to work with)
coordinate.key_relation: A rajasic type (1 of 6) — named for the Asuras; strength without surrender.

additional_info.text: The Asura-rajasic individual experiences life as a contest of wills. Their inner world is one of relentless ambition—they possess strong will and determination, and they are often shrewd and intelligent, capable of sophisticated strategy. But their intelligence is harnessed to the project of dominance rather than harmony. They are assertive, dominant, and proud; they pursue their goals aggressively and seek power and recognition above all else. Emotionally, they are prone to envy and self-praise. They are tough taskmasters who push themselves and others hard, often achieving impressive external results at the cost of inner peace and genuine human connection. They can be deceptive when it serves their purposes, and their charm often masks a calculating quality. In lived experience, the Asura type is the ruthless competitor, the domineering executive, the person whose presence generates both admiration and fear. Their suffering is the suffering of perpetual dissatisfaction: no achievement is ever enough, no victory is ever final, and beneath the confident exterior there is often a deep insecurity that fuels the drive for more. The path toward balance involves redirecting their formidable energy from conquest to contribution—learning that true power lies in service rather than domination.

attributes.title: Rakshasa Rajasika — The Wrathful Intensity
attributes.aspect: Named after the Rakshasas, fierce beings of myth: raw power unleashed without restraint

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital · Ontic mode: Type — a mānasika-prakṛti archetype (personality type) · Valence: Neutral temperament (a given disposition to work with) · Guṇa: Rajas-dominant · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: Constitutional disposition · Key relation: A rajasic type — named for the Rākṣasas; raw power without restraint.
coordinate.valence: Neutral temperament (a given disposition to work with)
coordinate.key_relation: A rajasic type — named for the Rākṣasas; raw power without restraint.

additional_info.text: The Rakshasa-rajasic individual experiences life with an intensity that can be both terrifying and compelling. Their inner world is one of surging emotional force—anger, desire, and appetite are felt with overwhelming power. They have high levels of energy that can quickly turn aggressive or cruel. They are intolerant, deceitful, and fond of strong sensory experiences including non-vegetarian food and addictive substances. They are highly irritable, prone to violence, and their actions are driven by intense desires and a fundamental lack of self-control. Their relationships are often stormy and marked by cycles of passion and conflict. They may be physically imposing and present a formidable, assertive presence. In lived experience, the Rakshasa type is the person whose rages terrify others, whose appetites seem bottomless, and whose life is a series of dramatic escalations and crises. Their suffering is the suffering of a fire that consumes its own fuel: the intensity that makes them powerful also makes them destructive, both to others and to themselves. The path toward balance involves learning to channel their extraordinary energy through discipline—martial arts, structured physical practice, or service in environments where intensity is an asset rather than a liability.

attributes.title: Paishacha Rajasika — The Isolated Indulger
attributes.aspect: Named after the Pishāchas, beings of the shadow: appetite unchecked by awareness

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital–Physical · Ontic mode: Type — a mānasika-prakṛti archetype (personality type) · Valence: Neutral temperament (a given disposition to work with) · Guṇa: Rajas–Tamas · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: Constitutional disposition · Key relation: A rajasic type — named for the Piśācas; appetite unchecked by awareness.
coordinate.valence: Neutral temperament (a given disposition to work with)
coordinate.key_relation: A rajasic type — named for the Piśācas; appetite unchecked by awareness.

additional_info.text: The Paishacha-rajasic individual experiences life through the lens of appetite and compulsion. Their inner world is dominated by hunger—not just for food, but for sensory experience in all its forms. They are gluttonous, with a ravenous quality to their desires. They may live in luxury yet feel perpetually unsatisfied. They love company but often keep it in secret, and may struggle with secretive indulgences. They may show a lack of engagement in mental or intellectual activities and a tendency toward disregard for cleanliness and social norms. They may prefer isolation and struggle with self-regulation—the boundary between enough and too much is perpetually blurred. In lived experience, the Paishacha type is the person caught in cycles of bingeing and withdrawal—whether with food, substances, relationships, or experiences. Their suffering is the suffering of a void that cannot be filled from the outside. The path toward balance involves learning to turn awareness inward—to recognize that the hunger is not for more stimulation but for a quality of presence that can only be found through stillness and self-knowledge.

attributes.title: Sarpa Rajasika — The Cunning Survivor
attributes.aspect: Named after the Sarpa (serpent): watchful, coiled, and unpredictable

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital–Mental · Ontic mode: Type — a mānasika-prakṛti archetype (personality type) · Valence: Neutral temperament (a given disposition to work with) · Guṇa: Rajas-dominant · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: Constitutional disposition · Key relation: A rajasic type — named for the serpent; watchful, coiled, unpredictable.
coordinate.valence: Neutral temperament (a given disposition to work with)
coordinate.key_relation: A rajasic type — named for the serpent; watchful, coiled, unpredictable.

additional_info.text: The Sarpa-rajasic individual experiences life as a landscape of threat and opportunity. Their inner world is one of watchful tension—quick-witted but prone to anxiety and apprehension. They are usually indolent, yet become heroic in anger; timid in daily life but capable of sudden, intense aggression when they feel cornered. They are expert at counselling others but often use indirect means, trickery, or deceit to achieve their own aims. They have strong habits and are given to the pleasures of the senses, yet there is always a cautious, calculating quality to their indulgence. Their actions are often unpredictable, blending fear with cunning in a way that keeps others off balance. In lived experience, the Sarpa type is the person who is difficult to read—charming and helpful one moment, sharp and vengeful the next. Their suffering is the suffering of chronic vigilance: because they see the world as threatening, they can never fully relax, and their relationships are often undermined by the very suspicion and manipulation that they believe protects them. The path toward balance involves cultivating trust—learning, slowly, that not every situation demands a defensive posture, and that vulnerability is not the same as weakness.

attributes.title: Preta Rajasika — The Hungry Ghost
attributes.aspect: Named after the Pretas, spirits of insatiable craving: desire without fulfilment

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital · Ontic mode: Type — a mānasika-prakṛti archetype (personality type) · Valence: Neutral temperament (a given disposition to work with) · Guṇa: Rajas–Tamas · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: Constitutional disposition · Key relation: A rajasic type — named for the Pretas; desire without fulfilment.
coordinate.valence: Neutral temperament (a given disposition to work with)
coordinate.key_relation: A rajasic type — named for the Pretas; desire without fulfilment.

additional_info.text: The Preta-rajasic individual experiences life through a lens of chronic dissatisfaction and craving. Their inner world is marked by greed, irritability, and a pervasive sense of dejection. They love food and are deeply attached to material comforts, yet they are disinclined to work—the gap between desire and effort creates a perpetual frustration. They have no power of discrimination and are extremely jealous. Their conduct, character, and pastimes are such as to involve others in suffering. They may harbour personal vendettas or spread hatred, driven by a sense of having been denied what they deserve. There is a fundamentally acquisitive quality to their engagement with the world, yet no acquisition brings lasting satisfaction. In lived experience, the Preta type is the chronically dissatisfied person—the one who complains that others have what they deserve, who hoards yet feels impoverished, who desires yet resents the effort required to obtain. Their suffering is the suffering of the hungry ghost: surrounded by abundance yet unable to receive nourishment. The path toward balance involves the practice of gratitude and generosity—the deliberate cultivation of satisfaction with what one has, and the discovery that giving is the most effective cure for the feeling of lack.

attributes.title: Shakuni Rajasika — The Restless Seeker
attributes.aspect: Named after the Shakuni (bird): always in flight, never settled

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital–Mental · Ontic mode: Type — a mānasika-prakṛti archetype (personality type) · Valence: Neutral temperament (a given disposition to work with) · Guṇa: Rajas-dominant · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: Constitutional disposition · Key relation: A rajasic type — named for the bird; always in flight, never settled.
coordinate.valence: Neutral temperament (a given disposition to work with)
coordinate.key_relation: A rajasic type — named for the bird; always in flight, never settled.

additional_info.text: The Shakuni-rajasic individual experiences life as a constant search for the next stimulation. Their inner world is one of fickleness and restless movement—they are always seeking food, recreation, and new experiences, but their attention never rests on anything for long. They are passionate but intolerant, incapable of sustained remunerative work, suspicious of others, and prone to strong attachments that form quickly and dissolve just as fast. They have strong sexual desires and a tendency toward excessive indulgence, yet their relationship with pleasure is characterized by variability—they are easily distracted by the next possibility before the current one has been fully experienced. They are unacquisitive in the long term because they lack the sustained attention needed for accumulation. In lived experience, the Shakuni type is the person who flits from interest to interest, relationship to relationship, plan to plan—leaving a trail of unfinished projects and unfulfilled promises. Their suffering is the suffering of scattering: because attention is always divided, no experience is ever fully received, and the deep satisfaction that comes from sustained engagement remains elusive. The path toward balance involves the practice of concentration—learning to rest attention on one thing, one person, one purpose long enough for depth to emerge.

attributes.title: Pashu (Pashava) Tamasa — The Creature of Habit
attributes.aspect: Named after Pashu (animal): instinctive existence, unreflective and passive

coordinate.raw: Seat: Physical–Vital · Ontic mode: Type — a mānasika-prakṛti archetype (personality type) · Valence: Neutral temperament (a given disposition to work with) · Guṇa: Tamas-dominant · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: Constitutional disposition · Key relation: A tamasic type (1 of 3) — named for the animal; instinctive, unreflective existence.
coordinate.valence: Neutral temperament (a given disposition to work with)
coordinate.key_relation: A tamasic type (1 of 3) — named for the animal; instinctive, unreflective existence.

additional_info.text: The Pashu-tamasic individual experiences life at the level of basic instinct and routine. Their inner world is one of dullness, absence of sustained reflection, and passivity. They are mentally deficient in terms of intellectual engagement—not necessarily incapable, but deeply resistant to the effort that thinking requires. They are absent-minded, slow-moving, timid, foolish, and lazy. They are dirty in dress and ignorant in their dealings. They are given to eating, drinking, and sensuality—not with the passionate intensity of the rajasic types, but with the mechanical quality of an animal satisfying a drive. They are sleepy and prone to rejecting everything—new ideas, new people, new experiences. Their default response to the world is withdrawal and resistance. In lived experience, the Pashu type is the person who seems to exist rather than live—going through motions without engagement, responding to the world with the minimum possible effort. Their suffering is the suffering of unconsciousness: they are unable to articulate what is wrong because self-awareness itself is the faculty that tamas most effectively suppresses. The path toward balance begins with simple physical activity—movement, routine, the creation of basic structure—which introduces just enough rajas to begin loosening the grip of inertia.

attributes.title: Matsya Tamasa — The Driven Drifter
attributes.aspect: Named after Matsya (fish): restless motion without direction or understanding

coordinate.raw: Seat: Physical–Vital · Ontic mode: Type — a mānasika-prakṛti archetype (personality type) · Valence: Neutral temperament (a given disposition to work with) · Guṇa: Tamas-dominant · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: Constitutional disposition · Key relation: A tamasic type — named for the fish; restless motion without direction.
coordinate.valence: Neutral temperament (a given disposition to work with)
coordinate.key_relation: A tamasic type — named for the fish; restless motion without direction.

additional_info.text: The Matsya-tamasic individual experiences life as a kind of frightened restlessness—they are driven by instinctual urges but lack the cognitive resources to make sense of or direct their movement. They are mentally deficient, cowardly, prone to stupidity, gluttonous, fickle, and susceptible to anger and sensuality in rapid alternation. They love to travel and are drawn to water—a restless quality that is often mistaken for rajasic activity but is actually a tamasic flailing without purpose. Their inner world is marked by apprehension and a reactive quality: they respond to stimuli not with understanding but with reflex. Their engagement with the world is primarily driven by the most basic needs—hunger, fear, and the pursuit of immediate sensory comfort. In lived experience, the Matsya type is the person who is always moving but never arriving—changing jobs, changing cities, changing partners, but always carrying the same confusion and restlessness with them. Their suffering is the suffering of disorientation: they lack the inner compass that would allow them to distinguish meaningful action from mere agitation. The path toward balance involves grounding—establishing stability in one place, one relationship, one practice—long enough for the turbid waters of the mind to begin to settle.

attributes.title: Vanaspati Tamasa — The Still Sleeper
attributes.aspect: Named after Vanaspati (plant): existence without volition or awareness

coordinate.raw: Seat: Physical · Ontic mode: Type — a mānasika-prakṛti archetype (personality type) · Valence: Neutral temperament (a given disposition to work with) · Guṇa: Tamas-dominant · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: Constitutional disposition · Key relation: A tamasic type — named for the plant; the deepest tamasic inertia.
coordinate.valence: Neutral temperament (a given disposition to work with)
coordinate.key_relation: A tamasic type — named for the plant; the deepest tamasic inertia.

additional_info.text: The Vanaspati-tamasic individual represents the deepest level of tamasic experience. Their inner world is one of near-total absence of mental activity—extreme laziness, complete indifference to surroundings, and minimal engagement with the world. They spend most of their time eating and sleeping. They are completely devoid of all mental faculties as traditionally defined—lacking initiative, curiosity, ambition, and even the restless agitation that still animates the Pashu and Matsya types. They show the lowest level of mental activity and significant indifference to everything around them. Psychologically, they are characterized by extreme passivity, a complete lack of initiative, and a mode of existence that resembles a static form of life. In lived experience, the Vanaspati type is the person who has essentially withdrawn from participation in life—not through the active choice of a contemplative, but through the weight of inertia that has compressed awareness to its minimal expression. Their suffering is invisible even to themselves because the capacity for self-reflection that would allow them to recognize suffering has been almost entirely suppressed. The path toward balance is the longest and most difficult of all the types, requiring external intervention: compassionate others who introduce stimulation, structure, and care—the first rays of light entering a room that has been dark for a very long time.

attributes.title: Ahamkara (Ego/I-am-ness)
attributes.aspect: Ahamkara is the ego-sense, the feeling of ‘I’ and ‘mine.’ It is the false identification with the body-mind and individual personality. Ahamkara creates separation and attachment.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental–Psychic (the ‘I’-sense / ego-function) · Ontic mode: Faculty (a function of the antaḥkaraṇa — the ‘I’-maker) · Valence: Faculty tending to affliction (needed for agency; the knot of bondage when over-identified) · Guṇa: Variable (rajasic in assertion, tamasic in rigidity) · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: Root mechanism (the ‘I’-maker; source of doership and mine-ness) · Key relation: Part — one of the four-fold antaḥkaraṇa; kin to Asmitā (the klesha of Dimension 19).
coordinate.ontic_mode: Faculty (a function of the antaḥkaraṇa — the ‘I’-maker)
coordinate.valence: Faculty tending to affliction (needed for agency; the knot of bondage when over-identified)
coordinate.guna: Variable (rajasic in assertion, tamasic in rigidity)
coordinate.causal_status: Root mechanism (the ‘I’-maker; source of doership and mine-ness)
coordinate.key_relation: Part — one of the four-fold antaḥkaraṇa; kin to Asmitā (the klesha of Dimension 19).

balanced.label: Balanced expression

facets.overview: When Ahamkara is healthy, a person has appropriate self-respect, sense of identity, and healthy self-esteem. The ego functions to protect and organize experience without being destructive.
facets.somatic: Confident and upright posture, healthy body image, self-care and respect for body, good physical boundaries, strong physical presence.
facets.emotional: Healthy self-esteem and self-respect, emotional confidence, ability to receive and give love appropriately, healthy emotional boundaries, emotional stability and security in identity.
facets.cognitive: Clear sense of self and identity, appropriate self-confidence, ability to stand by convictions, mental organization and clarity, healthy differentiation from others.
facets.psychic: The soul holds the ego as a useful instrument — the ‘I’ that acts and relates — without mistaking it for its true self.

balanced.text:
When Ahamkara is healthy, a person has appropriate self-respect, sense of identity, and healthy self-esteem. The ego functions to protect and organize experience without being destructive.

Somatic: Confident and upright posture, healthy body image, self-care and respect for body, good physical boundaries, strong physical presence.

Emotional: Healthy self-esteem and self-respect, emotional confidence, ability to receive and give love appropriately, healthy emotional boundaries, emotional stability and security in identity.

Cognitive: Clear sense of self and identity, appropriate self-confidence, ability to stand by convictions, mental organization and clarity, healthy differentiation from others.

Psychic: The soul holds the ego as a useful instrument — the ‘I’ that acts and relates — without mistaking it for its true self.
balanced.vignette: Deepa can take a hard critique of her design without flinching and defend a good idea without apology, because her sense of who she is does not rise and fall with either. She receives love easily and sets limits cleanly. The ‘I’ in her is a working instrument—it acts, relates, and holds its ground—without swallowing everything it touches.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Excessive Ahamkara creates arrogance, superiority complex, defensiveness, inability to accept criticism, and lack of empathy. It blocks spiritual growth.
facets.somatic: Rigid and defensive posture, physical aggression or domineering presence, poor treatment of body as extension of ego, inability to relax.
facets.emotional: Emotional defensiveness and rigidity, arrogance and superiority, inability to receive feedback or criticism, lack of empathy and compassion, emotional reactions to perceived slights, constant need for validation.
facets.cognitive: Mental arrogance and pride, inability to admit mistakes, defensive thinking patterns, constant judging of others, spiritual arrogance, inability to learn from others.
facets.psychic: The soul is wholly identified with the ego, every experience referred back to ‘me and mine.’

excess.text:
Excessive Ahamkara creates arrogance, superiority complex, defensiveness, inability to accept criticism, and lack of empathy. It blocks spiritual growth.

Somatic: Rigid and defensive posture, physical aggression or domineering presence, poor treatment of body as extension of ego, inability to relax.

Emotional: Emotional defensiveness and rigidity, arrogance and superiority, inability to receive feedback or criticism, lack of empathy and compassion, emotional reactions to perceived slights, constant need for validation.

Cognitive: Mental arrogance and pride, inability to admit mistakes, defensive thinking patterns, constant judging of others, spiritual arrogance, inability to learn from others.

Psychic: The soul is wholly identified with the ego, every experience referred back to ‘me and mine.’
excess.vignette: Every conversation with Rohan somehow becomes about Rohan. Criticism, however gentle, is met as an attack; praise is expected as a right; other people’s successes are quietly resented. He cannot say the words ‘I was wrong,’ and he has begun to lose the friends who once tried to help him see it. The self has swollen until it fills the whole room, and there is no longer space in it for anyone else.

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.overview: Deficient Ahamkara leads to poor self-esteem, identity confusion, inability to establish healthy boundaries, self-harm, susceptibility to manipulation.
facets.somatic: Poor posture and weak physical presence, self-neglect and poor self-care, poor physical boundaries, vulnerability to physical harm, physical hesitancy and lack of confidence.
facets.emotional: Poor self-esteem and self-worth, emotional insecurity and anxiety, difficulty asserting needs, susceptibility to emotional manipulation, emotional dependence on others’ approval.
facets.cognitive: Identity confusion, lack of sense of self, inability to make independent decisions, susceptibility to suggestions and manipulation, poor self-trust, mental fragility.
facets.psychic: The ‘I’-sense is so weakened the soul cannot function as an effective agent in the world.

deficient.text:
Deficient Ahamkara leads to poor self-esteem, identity confusion, inability to establish healthy boundaries, self-harm, susceptibility to manipulation.

Somatic: Poor posture and weak physical presence, self-neglect and poor self-care, poor physical boundaries, vulnerability to physical harm, physical hesitancy and lack of confidence.

Emotional: Poor self-esteem and self-worth, emotional insecurity and anxiety, difficulty asserting needs, susceptibility to emotional manipulation, emotional dependence on others’ approval.

Cognitive: Identity confusion, lack of sense of self, inability to make independent decisions, susceptibility to suggestions and manipulation, poor self-trust, mental fragility.

Psychic: The ‘I’-sense is so weakened the soul cannot function as an effective agent in the world.
deficient.vignette: Anya cannot tell you what she wants for dinner, let alone what she wants from her life. She defers to whoever spoke last, apologizes for existing, and lets a domineering colleague take the credit for her work without a murmur. It is not humility; it is that the ‘I’ who might have objected never quite formed. Without a self to stand on, she is furniture in her own story.

attributes.title: Sankalpa-Vikalpa (Mental Oscillation)
attributes.aspect: Sankalpa-Vikalpa describes the chaotic, dualistic nature of the lower mind (Manas) as it constantly vacillates between acceptance (attraction/Sankalpa) and rejection (repulsion/Vikalpa). This oscillation is the fundamental mechanism of mental suffering.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental (the deliberating / oscillating mind) · Ontic mode: Process (the mind’s to-and-fro of ‘yes / no’) — a function of manas · Valence: Neutral faculty (deliberation is useful; endless churn is the fault) · Guṇa: Rajas-dominant · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: Mechanism (manas weighing alternatives) · Key relation: Function of manas; steadied by Sankalpa (directive will).
coordinate.ontic_mode: Process (the mind’s to-and-fro of ‘yes / no’) — a function of manas
coordinate.valence: Neutral faculty (deliberation is useful; endless churn is the fault)
coordinate.key_relation: Function of manas; steadied by Sankalpa (directive will).

balanced.label: Balanced expression

facets.overview: When mental oscillation is transcended, there is steadiness and equanimity. The mind maintains equilibrium without being tossed between acceptance and rejection.
facets.somatic: Steady and calm physical presence, relaxed and balanced body, peaceful breathing, good nervous system regulation, physical ease despite circumstances.
facets.emotional: Emotional stability and equanimity, acceptance of circumstances without emotional turmoil, emotional peace despite contrasting experiences, emotional resilience.
facets.cognitive: Steady and focused mind, freedom from mental oscillation, mental clarity and peace, ability to observe thoughts without being swept away, mental stillness.
facets.psychic: The soul watches the mind’s oscillation between yes and no without being dragged by every swing.

balanced.text:
When mental oscillation is transcended, there is steadiness and equanimity. The mind maintains equilibrium without being tossed between acceptance and rejection.

Somatic: Steady and calm physical presence, relaxed and balanced body, peaceful breathing, good nervous system regulation, physical ease despite circumstances.

Emotional: Emotional stability and equanimity, acceptance of circumstances without emotional turmoil, emotional peace despite contrasting experiences, emotional resilience.

Cognitive: Steady and focused mind, freedom from mental oscillation, mental clarity and peace, ability to observe thoughts without being swept away, mental stillness.

Psychic: The soul watches the mind’s oscillation between yes and no without being dragged by every swing.
balanced.vignette: The offer needs a yes or no by Friday, and Marcus notices the familiar pull—should I, shouldn’t I, but what if—and simply watches it swing for a while without grabbing either end. He gathers what he needs, sleeps on it, and by Thursday the answer has quietly settled. The mind still oscillates, as minds do; he has just stopped being dragged along by every swing of it.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: When oscillation is excessive and chaotic, the mind is tormented by constant back-and-forth movement between desires and aversions. There is no peace or stability.
facets.somatic: Physical agitation and restlessness, tremors and nervous movements, poor sleep and constant movement, tension throughout the body, nervous system dysregulation.
facets.emotional: Emotional turbulence and constant mood swings, emotional suffering from constant back-and-forth, inability to feel stable, emotional reactivity, emotional exhaustion.
facets.cognitive: Racing and scattered thoughts, constant mental conflict, inability to settle on anything, obsessive mental oscillation, mental torment and suffering, inability to concentrate.
facets.psychic: The soul is caught in ceaseless oscillation, doubt and counter-doubt churning without rest.

excess.text:
When oscillation is excessive and chaotic, the mind is tormented by constant back-and-forth movement between desires and aversions. There is no peace or stability.

Somatic: Physical agitation and restlessness, tremors and nervous movements, poor sleep and constant movement, tension throughout the body, nervous system dysregulation.

Emotional: Emotional turbulence and constant mood swings, emotional suffering from constant back-and-forth, inability to feel stable, emotional reactivity, emotional exhaustion.

Cognitive: Racing and scattered thoughts, constant mental conflict, inability to settle on anything, obsessive mental oscillation, mental torment and suffering, inability to concentrate.

Psychic: The soul is caught in ceaseless oscillation, doubt and counter-doubt churning without rest.
excess.vignette: Should she text him? She types, deletes, retypes, deletes. Should she take the job? Yes—no—yes—the reasons for and against multiplying until they cancel out and leave only the churn. Lena’s mind is a pendulum that never comes to rest; every decision spawns its own counter-decision, and she is exhausted not by choosing but by the endless not-choosing. There is no peace in the back-and-forth, only motion.

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.overview: Deficiency manifests as mental dullness where the mind cannot even properly oscillate. There is stagnation, lack of discernment about options, and inability to decide.
facets.somatic: Sluggish and heavy body, poor physical responsiveness, lack of physical motivation or engagement, stagnant physical presence.
facets.emotional: Emotional dullness and apathy, inability to feel engaged, emotional heaviness, lack of emotional response or motivation, emotional flatness.
facets.cognitive: Mental lethargy and dullness, inability to think clearly about choices, confusion, inability to discriminate between options, mental heaviness.
facets.psychic: The soul’s mind is so dulled it cannot even weigh alternatives, decision collapsing into apathy.

deficient.text:
Deficiency manifests as mental dullness where the mind cannot even properly oscillate. There is stagnation, lack of discernment about options, and inability to decide.

Somatic: Sluggish and heavy body, poor physical responsiveness, lack of physical motivation or engagement, stagnant physical presence.

Emotional: Emotional dullness and apathy, inability to feel engaged, emotional heaviness, lack of emotional response or motivation, emotional flatness.

Cognitive: Mental lethargy and dullness, inability to think clearly about choices, confusion, inability to discriminate between options, mental heaviness.

Psychic: The soul’s mind is so dulled it cannot even weigh alternatives, decision collapsing into apathy.
deficient.vignette: Presented with three options, Sam feels not conflict but a grey blankness—he cannot muster the interest to weigh them, or even to care which wins. The mind that should turn a choice over has gone slack and heavy; deliberation collapses before it begins. He takes whatever requires the least motion, or takes nothing, and calls it going with the flow.

attributes.title: Sankalpa (Will as Higher Than Mind)
attributes.aspect: Sankalpa is the directive power of consciousness—the will that organizes mind, determines action. It is higher than mere mind because mind follows what sankalpa wills. The ascending teaching: meditate on Will as Brahman, recognizing that what you will is what you become.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental (directive will / resolve) · Ontic mode: Faculty (the organizing will — higher than the deliberating mind) · Valence: Faculty to be cultivated (steady will organizes the mind) · Guṇa: Sattva-dominant (clarified will), rajasic when willful · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: Mechanism (organizes and directs the lower mind) · Key relation: Antagonist-complement to the oscillating mind — the resolve that ends deliberation.
coordinate.ontic_mode: Faculty (the organizing will — higher than the deliberating mind)
coordinate.valence: Faculty to be cultivated (steady will organizes the mind)
coordinate.causal_status: Mechanism (organizes and directs the lower mind)
coordinate.key_relation: Antagonist-complement to the oscillating mind — the resolve that ends deliberation.

balanced.label: Balanced expression

facets.overview: Conscious cultivation of right Sankalpa. Will directed toward liberation, not bondage. Powerful intentional living.
facets.somatic: Body follows directed will, no scattered action.
facets.emotional: Emotional life organized by clear intent.
facets.cognitive: Mind serves wisely-directed will.
facets.psychic: The soul exercises directive will, the higher faculty that organizes the mind and steadies intention toward a goal.

balanced.text:
Conscious cultivation of right Sankalpa. Will directed toward liberation, not bondage. Powerful intentional living.

Somatic: Body follows directed will, no scattered action.

Emotional: Emotional life organized by clear intent.

Cognitive: Mind serves wisely-directed will.

Psychic: The soul exercises directive will, the higher faculty that organizes the mind and steadies intention toward a goal.
balanced.vignette: Priya decided, three years ago, to become a doctor, and every day since has quietly organized itself around that resolve—the early study, the declined distractions, the steady forward pull. It is not grim; it is clear. Her will is the keel of her life, giving the scattered days a single direction, and she can feel the difference between this and mere wishing.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Will-power as ego-tool—forcing reality through sheer willpower, missing surrender.
facets.somatic: Body strained from willful forcing.
facets.emotional: Emotional rigidity from over-willed life.
facets.cognitive: Mind in constant control-mode.
facets.psychic: The soul’s will hardens into willfulness, forcing outcomes and overriding wisdom.

excess.text:
Will-power as ego-tool—forcing reality through sheer willpower, missing surrender.

Somatic: Body strained from willful forcing.

Emotional: Emotional rigidity from over-willed life.

Cognitive: Mind in constant control-mode.

Psychic: The soul’s will hardens into willfulness, forcing outcomes and overriding wisdom.
excess.vignette: Tom will make it happen through sheer force if it kills him—the deal, the renovation, the marathon, the marriage—jaw set, muscling every outcome into place, allergic to the words ‘let it go.’ What cannot be forced he forces anyway, and calls the strain discipline. The will that should serve his life has hardened into willfulness, overriding the very wisdom that would tell him when to yield.

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.overview: Will weak and scattered. Life as drift without intentional direction. Random desires drive action.
facets.somatic: Body scattered in undirected action.
facets.emotional: Emotional life as reactive drift.
facets.cognitive: Mind without organizing will, scattered.
facets.psychic: The soul’s will is feeble, intentions dissolving before they can organize anything.

deficient.text:
Will weak and scattered. Life as drift without intentional direction. Random desires drive action.

Somatic: Body scattered in undirected action.

Emotional: Emotional life as reactive drift.

Cognitive: Mind without organizing will, scattered.

Psychic: The soul’s will is feeble, intentions dissolving before they can organize anything.
deficient.vignette: Ravi’s days happen to him. Whatever desire is loudest at the moment—the snack, the notification, the sudden urge to quit—steers the ship, and the ship drifts. He has intentions, vaguely, but no resolve to organize them, so a decade has passed in a series of unfinished starts. Without a directive will, his life is a boat with no one at the tiller.

attributes.title: Chariot Allegory (Rathopama)
attributes.aspect: Know the Self as the rider; body as the chariot; intellect as charioteer; mind as reins; senses as horses; sense-objects as paths. He whose mind is uncontrolled never reaches the destination.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental–Psychic (a map of the inner apparatus and the Self) · Ontic mode: Framework (an allegorical model of the inner instrument) · Valence: Neutral — an orienting model · Guṇa: Sattva (a clarifying map) · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: Framework (orders the relation of Self, intellect, mind, and senses) · Key relation: Whole — integrates buddhi (charioteer), manas (reins), senses (horses), and the Self (rider).
coordinate.seat: Mental–Psychic (a map of the inner apparatus and the Self)
coordinate.ontic_mode: Framework (an allegorical model of the inner instrument)
coordinate.causal_status: Framework (orders the relation of Self, intellect, mind, and senses)
coordinate.key_relation: Whole — integrates buddhi (charioteer), manas (reins), senses (horses), and the Self (rider).

balanced.label: Balanced expression

facets.overview: Intellect (charioteer) holds the mind (reins) firmly, controls the senses (horses), so the Self (rider) reaches its destination.
facets.somatic: Body (chariot) maintained well, serves the journey.
facets.emotional: Senses (horses) controlled by reins (mind), emotions disciplined.
facets.cognitive: Intellect (charioteer) discerning, mind (reins) firm, controlling sensory horses toward the Self’s destination.
facets.psychic: The soul keeps the chariot in order — intellect as charioteer, mind as reins, senses as horses — and rides toward the Self.

balanced.text:
Intellect (charioteer) holds the mind (reins) firmly, controls the senses (horses), so the Self (rider) reaches its destination.

Somatic: Body (chariot) maintained well, serves the journey.

Emotional: Senses (horses) controlled by reins (mind), emotions disciplined.

Cognitive: Intellect (charioteer) discerning, mind (reins) firm, controlling sensory horses toward the Self’s destination.

Psychic: The soul keeps the chariot in order — intellect as charioteer, mind as reins, senses as horses — and rides toward the Self.
balanced.vignette: On the trading floor, under real pressure, Nadia keeps her hands on the reins. Her judgment—the charioteer—holds her restless mind steady and her sharp appetites in check, and so the decisions she makes actually carry her toward where she means to go. The senses still pull—the fear, the greed, the noise—but they pull in harness, and the chariot runs true.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Forced harsh control of horses—damaging senses through suppression, brutal charioteering.
facets.somatic: Body abused as if separate enemy.
facets.emotional: Suppressed senses creating distortion.
facets.cognitive: Harsh intellectual control without wisdom.
facets.psychic: The soul over-controls, gripping the reins so tightly the chariot cannot move naturally.

excess.text:
Forced harsh control of horses—damaging senses through suppression, brutal charioteering.

Somatic: Body abused as if separate enemy.

Emotional: Suppressed senses creating distortion.

Cognitive: Harsh intellectual control without wisdom.

Psychic: The soul over-controls, gripping the reins so tightly the chariot cannot move naturally.
excess.vignette: David’s answer to temptation is a whip. He starves the senses, suppresses every appetite, white-knuckles his way through life in the name of self-mastery—no sweets, no rest, no softness, a brutal charioteer flaying the very horses meant to carry him. The control is real, and so is the damage; a chariot driven by cruelty may stay on the road, but it arrives broken.

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.overview: Wild horses pulling chariot anywhere—senses ruling, intellect weak. The Self never reaches its destination.
facets.somatic: Body driven everywhere by senses.
facets.emotional: Emotional horses out of control.
facets.cognitive: Weak intellect, no real charioteering, life as drift.
facets.psychic: The reins are dropped; the runaway senses drag the chariot wherever they will, the Self forgotten.

deficient.text:
Wild horses pulling chariot anywhere—senses ruling, intellect weak. The Self never reaches its destination.

Somatic: Body driven everywhere by senses.

Emotional: Emotional horses out of control.

Cognitive: Weak intellect, no real charioteering, life as drift.

Psychic: The reins are dropped; the runaway senses drag the chariot wherever they will, the Self forgotten.
deficient.vignette: Jake’s senses run the show, and the intellect that should steer has gone weak and quiet. One craving hands off to the next—scroll, snack, drink, scroll—and the chariot lurches wherever the horses bolt. He means, always, to get somewhere; but with no charioteer holding the reins, the Self he might have become is dragged in circles and never arrives.

attributes.title: Antahkarana (Four-Fold Inner Instrument)
attributes.aspect: Sankara’s psychological model: antahkarana (inner instrument) operates through four functions. Manas = emotional appraisal (modern: affect). Buddhi = executive cognition (modern: discrimination). Ahamkara = self-schema (‘I-am-doer’). Chitta = unconscious memory/tendencies. All four are one organ in different modes. Personality is their interaction.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental–Psychic (the whole inner instrument) · Ontic mode: Faculty — the composite inner organ (manas, buddhi, ahaṁkāra, citta) · Valence: Neutral faculty (the instrument of all inner processing) · Guṇa: Spans all three (each faculty colored differently) · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: Instrument (the apparatus between awareness and world) · Key relation: Whole — the aggregate of which Manas, Buddhi, Ahaṁkāra, and Citta are the parts.
coordinate.ontic_mode: Faculty — the composite inner organ (manas, buddhi, ahaṁkāra, citta)
coordinate.valence: Neutral faculty (the instrument of all inner processing)
coordinate.guna: Spans all three (each faculty colored differently)
coordinate.causal_status: Instrument (the apparatus between awareness and world)
coordinate.key_relation: Whole — the aggregate of which Manas, Buddhi, Ahaṁkāra, and Citta are the parts.

balanced.label: Balanced expression

facets.overview: All four functions harmonized. Buddhi commands; manas serves; ahamkara functions without inflating; chitta releases patterns gradually.
facets.somatic: Body’s responses coordinated through harmonized antahkarana.
facets.emotional: Manas regulated; emotional appraisals proportionate.
facets.cognitive: Buddhi clear, dominant; ahamkara functional not inflated; chitta gradually purified.
facets.psychic: The soul works its inner instrument well — mind, intellect, ego, memory each in its place — a coordinated whole.

balanced.text:
All four functions harmonized. Buddhi commands; manas serves; ahamkara functions without inflating; chitta releases patterns gradually.

Somatic: Body’s responses coordinated through harmonized antahkarana.

Emotional: Manas regulated; emotional appraisals proportionate.

Cognitive: Buddhi clear, dominant; ahamkara functional not inflated; chitta gradually purified.

Psychic: The soul works its inner instrument well — mind, intellect, ego, memory each in its place — a coordinated whole.
balanced.vignette: In Meera, the inner faculties work like a good crew: her clear judgment gives the orders, her busy mind carries them out without arguing, her ego does its job without puffing up, and the old grooves of habit are slowly, patiently worn smooth. Nothing dominates; everything serves. From the outside it simply looks like a person who is, unusually, all of one piece.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: One function dominates pathologically. Ahamkara inflates; manas overwhelms buddhi; chitta-patterns drive behavior.
facets.somatic: Body driven by imbalanced antahkarana.
facets.emotional: Manas overrides buddhi.
facets.cognitive: Ahamkara expanded; chitta unconscious patterns dominate.
facets.psychic: The soul over-identifies with the inner instrument, mistaking its functions for the awareness that uses them.

excess.text:
One function dominates pathologically. Ahamkara inflates; manas overwhelms buddhi; chitta-patterns drive behavior.

Somatic: Body driven by imbalanced antahkarana.

Emotional: Manas overrides buddhi.

Cognitive: Ahamkara expanded; chitta unconscious patterns dominate.

Psychic: The soul over-identifies with the inner instrument, mistaking its functions for the awareness that uses them.
excess.vignette: One faculty has seized the wheel in Karan, and it shows. His ego has inflated until it drowns out his better judgment; his restless mind overwhelms the quiet voice of discernment; and the old conditioned patterns, unchecked, simply run him. The instrument that should play in harmony has one string screaming over all the others, and the music has become noise.

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.overview: Buddhi too weak; manas reactive; ahamkara confused; chitta patterns run unchecked.
facets.somatic: Body responses driven by unintegrated functions.
facets.emotional: Emotional appraisals confused.
facets.cognitive: Mind without governance, fragmented functioning.
facets.psychic: The inner instrument is disordered, its functions at cross-purposes, the soul with no clear seat within.

deficient.text:
Buddhi too weak; manas reactive; ahamkara confused; chitta patterns run unchecked.

Somatic: Body responses driven by unintegrated functions.

Emotional: Emotional appraisals confused.

Cognitive: Mind without governance, fragmented functioning.

Psychic: The inner instrument is disordered, its functions at cross-purposes, the soul with no clear seat within.
deficient.vignette: Nothing in Sam’s inner apparatus is quite strong enough to lead. His judgment is too faint to command, his mind flinches and reacts, his sense of self is muddled, and the old tapes play on with no one to stop them. It is not that a tyrant has taken over; it is that the throne sits empty, and in the vacancy the conditioning quietly runs the whole show.

attributes.title: Phase 3: Manas Re-orientation (Affective Integration)
attributes.aspect: Third phase: emotional re-orientation through cultivated dispassion. Pleasures and pains are witnessed without being driven by them. This dissolves affect-driven compulsion. The emotional life is not eliminated but recalibrated—feelings still arise but don’t dictate.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital–Mental (the feeling-mind re-anchored) · Ontic mode: Stage (a phase in the developmental re-education of the mind) · Valence: Cultivated practice (a phase of transformation) · Guṇa: Rajas-to-sattva movement · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: Process (re-anchoring the emotions from old cravings to a new center) · Key relation: Sequence — a numbered phase in the developmental arc; re-educates manas.
coordinate.ontic_mode: Stage (a phase in the developmental re-education of the mind)
coordinate.causal_status: Process (re-anchoring the emotions from old cravings to a new center)
coordinate.key_relation: Sequence — a numbered phase in the developmental arc; re-educates manas.

balanced.label: Balanced expression

facets.overview: Manas re-oriented. Emotions arise and pass, witnessed without commanding action. Affective freedom.
facets.somatic: Body free from emotional reactivity.
facets.emotional: Feelings continue but lose compulsive force.
facets.cognitive: Mind witnesses emotional movements clearly.
facets.psychic: The soul re-educates its feeling-mind, the emotions gradually re-anchored from old cravings toward the new center.

balanced.text:
Manas re-oriented. Emotions arise and pass, witnessed without commanding action. Affective freedom.

Somatic: Body free from emotional reactivity.

Emotional: Feelings continue but lose compulsive force.

Cognitive: Mind witnesses emotional movements clearly.

Psychic: The soul re-educates its feeling-mind, the emotions gradually re-anchored from old cravings toward the new center.
balanced.vignette: The anger still comes when Leena is cut off in traffic—she feels the heat rise, clean and real—but it no longer seizes the wheel. She notices it, lets it crest, and chooses her response from a step behind the feeling. Emotions arise in her and pass through, fully felt and no longer in command; she has become, at last, their witness rather than their servant.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Forced emotional detachment becoming dissociation. Loss of healthy emotional engagement.
facets.somatic: Body cold and disconnected.
facets.emotional: Emotional flatness mistaken for dispassion.
facets.cognitive: Mental rejection of all feeling.
facets.psychic: The soul forces the re-orientation, suppressing feeling rather than integrating it.

excess.text:
Forced emotional detachment becoming dissociation. Loss of healthy emotional engagement.

Somatic: Body cold and disconnected.

Emotional: Emotional flatness mistaken for dispassion.

Cognitive: Mental rejection of all feeling.

Psychic: The soul forces the re-orientation, suppressing feeling rather than integrating it.
excess.vignette: Daniel has learned to ‘not be controlled by his emotions’ so thoroughly that he now feels almost nothing at all. At the funeral, the breakup, the reunion, he observes his own life from behind glass, mistaking numbness for equanimity and distance for peace. The detachment that was meant to free his feelings has quietly killed them; in fleeing their tyranny he has exiled himself from his own heart.

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.overview: Continuing affect-driven compulsion. Emotions still dictate behavior.
facets.somatic: Body driven by emotional reactivity.
facets.emotional: Emotional storms control daily life.
facets.cognitive: Mind cannot witness, only react.
facets.psychic: The soul leaves its emotions un-reoriented, the feeling-mind still ruled by old attractions and aversions.

deficient.text:
Continuing affect-driven compulsion. Emotions still dictate behavior.

Somatic: Body driven by emotional reactivity.

Emotional: Emotional storms control daily life.

Cognitive: Mind cannot witness, only react.

Psychic: The soul leaves its emotions un-reoriented, the feeling-mind still ruled by old attractions and aversions.
deficient.vignette: Whatever Priya feels, she does—the flash of jealousy becomes an accusation before she can think, the wave of despair becomes a cancelled week, the surge of desire becomes a text she will regret. There is no gap between the emotion and the act; the feeling arrives already in charge. She is not living her life so much as being driven through it by each affect as it comes.

attributes.title: The Waking State (Jāgrat): The World of Sensory Perception

coordinate.raw: Seat: Physical–Mental · Ontic mode: Plane — a material plane of consciousness (waking) · Valence: Neutral (a plane of experience) · Guṇa: Rajas–Sattva · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Ground (the sensory world) · Key relation: First of the three material planes; corresponds to Vaiśvānara (the four states).
coordinate.ontic_mode: Plane — a material plane of consciousness (waking)
coordinate.key_relation: First of the three material planes; corresponds to Vaiśvānara (the four states).

attributes.title: The Dreaming State (Svapna): The World of Samskāras

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental–Vital · Ontic mode: Plane — a material plane (dream) · Valence: Neutral · Guṇa: Rajas–Tamas · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Ground (the world of saṁskāras) · Key relation: Second material plane; corresponds to Taijasa; the subtle-impression world.
coordinate.key_relation: Second material plane; corresponds to Taijasa; the subtle-impression world.

attributes.title: The Sleeping State (Suṣupti): The World of the Psychic

coordinate.raw: Seat: Psychic–Physical · Ontic mode: Plane — a material plane (deep sleep) · Valence: Neutral · Guṇa: Tamas · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Ground (the causal seed-state) · Key relation: Third material plane; corresponds to Prājña; the causal ground.
coordinate.key_relation: Third material plane; corresponds to Prājña; the causal ground.

attributes.title: The Transcendental State (Turīya): The Bridge Between Matter and Spirit

coordinate.raw: Seat: Psychic · Ontic mode: Plane — the transcendental pivot (Turīya) · Valence: Neutral / the goal · Guṇa: Sattva, beyond the guṇas · Scale: Individual, transcendent · Causal status: Bridge / substratum · Key relation: The pivot between matter and spirit; the fourth; the witness of the three states.
coordinate.key_relation: The pivot between matter and spirit; the fourth; the witness of the three states.

attributes.attribute: God Consciousness: The Experience of Supreme Love
attributes.title: God Consciousness: The Experience of Supreme Love

coordinate.raw: Seat: Psychic–Vital · Ontic mode: Plane — a spiritual plane (supreme love) · Valence: Neutral (a plane of realization) · Guṇa: Sattva-dominant · Scale: Cosmic (Ādhidaivika), lived individually · Causal status: Ground (the plane of divine love) · Key relation: First spiritual plane; the experience of supreme love.
coordinate.key_relation: First spiritual plane; the experience of supreme love.

attributes.attribute: Unity Consciousness: The Transcendence of Prakriti
attributes.title: Unity Consciousness: The Transcendence of Prakriti

coordinate.raw: Seat: Psychic · Ontic mode: Plane — a spiritual plane (transcendence of prakṛti) · Valence: Neutral / the goal · Guṇa: Beyond the guṇas · Scale: Cosmic / transcendent · Causal status: Ground · Key relation: Second spiritual plane; the transcendence of prakṛti.
coordinate.ontic_mode: Plane — a spiritual plane (transcendence of prakṛti)
coordinate.key_relation: Second spiritual plane; the transcendence of prakṛti.

attributes.attribute: Cosmic Consciousness: The Totality of Sat and Chit
attributes.title: Cosmic Consciousness: The Totality of Sat and Chit

coordinate.raw: Seat: Psychic · Ontic mode: Plane — a spiritual plane (Sat-Chit totality) · Valence: Neutral / the goal · Guṇa: Beyond the guṇas · Scale: Cosmic / transcendent · Causal status: Ground · Key relation: Third spiritual plane; the totality of Sat and Chit.
coordinate.key_relation: Third spiritual plane; the totality of Sat and Chit.

attributes.title: Three States of Consciousness (Jagrat-Svapna-Sushupti)
attributes.aspect: Indian psychology classifies consciousness into three states, each with a corresponding body. The three are levels of identification. Liberation is becoming aware in each state—knowing oneself as the witness beyond all three.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Psychic (states of the whole field of awareness) · Ontic mode: State — a triad of recurring conditions of consciousness · Valence: Neutral — natural states to be witnessed · Guṇa: Spans all three (waking rajasic, dream mixed, sleep tamasic) · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: Manifestation (the three manifest conditions; Turīya is their ground) · Key relation: Triad — the three states whose continuous witness is Turīya.
coordinate.seat: Psychic (states of the whole field of awareness)
coordinate.ontic_mode: State — a triad of recurring conditions of consciousness
coordinate.valence: Neutral — natural states to be witnessed
coordinate.guna: Spans all three (waking rajasic, dream mixed, sleep tamasic)
coordinate.causal_status: Manifestation (the three manifest conditions; Turīya is their ground)
coordinate.key_relation: Triad — the three states whose continuous witness is Turīya.

balanced.label: Balanced expression

facets.overview: When one becomes aware in all three states—knowing that ‘body walks in me, mind dreams in me, sleep happens in me’—the witness is established. Turiya is realized.
facets.somatic: Healthy in waking, recuperative in sleep, body functioning naturally.
facets.emotional: Emotionally balanced in waking, learning from dreams, peace in deep sleep.
facets.cognitive: Witness-awareness maintained across states, recognition of one’s true nature beyond states.
facets.psychic: The soul observes itself passing through waking, dream, and deep sleep, sensing the awareness continuous through all three.

balanced.text:
When one becomes aware in all three states—knowing that ‘body walks in me, mind dreams in me, sleep happens in me’—the witness is established. Turiya is realized.

Somatic: Healthy in waking, recuperative in sleep, body functioning naturally.

Emotional: Emotionally balanced in waking, learning from dreams, peace in deep sleep.

Cognitive: Witness-awareness maintained across states, recognition of one’s true nature beyond states.

Psychic: The soul observes itself passing through waking, dream, and deep sleep, sensing the awareness continuous through all three.
balanced.vignette: After years of practice, Anand has begun to notice a strange, steadying fact: the body walks in him, the mind dreams in him, sleep happens in him—and something quiet stays awake behind all three. He is no longer only the waker, the dreamer, or the sleeper. From that witnessing seat, the ordinary day loses its power to fully capture him.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Pathological dominance of one state—obsessive waking activity, dream-addiction, oversleeping. Or premature claims of Turiya without genuine awareness in three states.
facets.somatic: Imbalanced sleep, exhaustion, or oversleeping.
facets.emotional: Stuck in waking concerns, dream-disturbance, depressive sleep.
facets.cognitive: Mental imbalance from poor state regulation.
facets.psychic: The soul fixates on analyzing the states, mapping rather than recognizing the witness behind them.

excess.text:
Pathological dominance of one state—obsessive waking activity, dream-addiction, oversleeping. Or premature claims of Turiya without genuine awareness in three states.

Somatic: Imbalanced sleep, exhaustion, or oversleeping.

Emotional: Stuck in waking concerns, dream-disturbance, depressive sleep.

Cognitive: Mental imbalance from poor state regulation.

Psychic: The soul fixates on analyzing the states, mapping rather than recognizing the witness behind them.
excess.vignette: Ravi announces, three months into meditation, that he has ‘realized the witness’—yet he is still glued to his phone every waking hour, chases lucid dreams like a hobby, and sleeps until noon on weekends. He has adopted the vocabulary of awareness without the awareness. The claim has raced far ahead of the fact, and the gap between them is invisible only to him.

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.overview: Unconscious movement through three states—no awareness of dreams, no recovery from sleep, no witness in waking. Identification with all three.
facets.somatic: Mechanical functioning, no awareness of body across states.
facets.emotional: Caught in each state’s content without awareness.
facets.cognitive: No witness consciousness, complete identification with state-contents.
facets.psychic: The soul takes each state as a separate, disconnected reality, never sensing the continuity beneath.

deficient.text:
Unconscious movement through three states—no awareness of dreams, no recovery from sleep, no witness in waking. Identification with all three.
