deficient.vignette: She sits at her desk staring at the screen. The task isn’t hard, but starting feels impossible. Hours pass without momentum. Even pleasant invitations feel like burdens. At night, she goes to bed early—not from satisfaction, but from depletion.

attributes.title: Phenomenological Dimension: Clarity
attributes.aspect: *Can I see what is actually happening—internally and externally—without distortion?*

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental (the clarity of perception and cognition) · Ontic mode: Experiential axis (a dimension of first-person experience) · Valence: Neutral faculty (balance is clear seeing; over-fixation is also a fault) · Guṇa: Sattva-linked (fog tamasic, over-illumination rajasic) · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: Manifestation (the transparency of the mind / buddhi) · Key relation: Bipolar axis (over-fixation–diffusion) — corresponds to sattvic buddhi.
coordinate.seat: Mental (the clarity of perception and cognition)
coordinate.ontic_mode: Experiential axis (a dimension of first-person experience)
coordinate.valence: Neutral faculty (balance is clear seeing; over-fixation is also a fault)
coordinate.causal_status: Manifestation (the transparency of the mind / buddhi)
coordinate.key_relation: Bipolar axis (over-fixation–diffusion) — corresponds to sattvic buddhi.

balanced.label: Equilibrium — Clear Seeing (Sattvic Buddhi)

facets.overview: Experience appears defined, navigable, and trustworthy.
facets.somatic: The body feels awake and oriented. Sensations have edges—pressure, warmth, movement are distinguishable. There is no buzzing or dullness. Breath supports alertness without strain.
facets.emotional: Emotions are recognizable as specific tones rather than vague moods. Sadness is sadness, not confusion. Interest is interest, not agitation. Feelings inform rather than flood.
facets.cognitive: Thoughts arise in coherent sequences. Attention can rest on one object without drifting or fragmenting. Decisions feel grounded because the relevant variables are visible.
facets.psychic: At the soul level, there is **viveka**—discernment. Awareness trusts itself. The self feels *located*, not scattered. Even uncertainty is clear as uncertainty.
facets.external: Work is structured and adaptive. Tasks are prioritized without obsession. Communication is precise, not over-explained. Habits are intentional because perception is reliable.

balanced.text:
Experience appears defined, navigable, and trustworthy.

Somatic: The body feels awake and oriented. Sensations have edges—pressure, warmth, movement are distinguishable. There is no buzzing or dullness. Breath supports alertness without strain.

Emotional: Emotions are recognizable as specific tones rather than vague moods. Sadness is sadness, not confusion. Interest is interest, not agitation. Feelings inform rather than flood.

Cognitive: Thoughts arise in coherent sequences. Attention can rest on one object without drifting or fragmenting. Decisions feel grounded because the relevant variables are visible.

Psychic: At the soul level, there is **viveka**—discernment. Awareness trusts itself. The self feels *located*, not scattered. Even uncertainty is clear as uncertainty.

External: Work is structured and adaptive. Tasks are prioritized without obsession. Communication is precise, not over-explained. Habits are intentional because perception is reliable.
balanced.vignette: She reviews her schedule and notices a tight overlap between two commitments. The recognition is immediate and calm. She adjusts one meeting, informs the other party, and proceeds. Throughout the day, she knows when she is tired, when she is focused, and when she needs a pause. Nothing feels mysterious or overwhelming—just present.

excess.label: Excess — Hyper-Clarity (Over-Fixation / Over-Illumination)

facets.overview: Experience is sharp but rigid; detail overwhelms meaning.
facets.somatic: The body feels tense and over-held. Eyes strain. Breath is shallow, as if maintaining constant alertness. There is little softness.
facets.emotional: Emotion is tightly monitored. Small fluctuations feel significant. There is anxiety about missing something, misunderstanding something, or making the wrong move.
facets.cognitive: Thought becomes hyper-analytical. Details multiply. The mind dissects endlessly, losing the gestalt. Certainty hardens into control.
facets.psychic: The witnessing self narrows into the analyzer. Buddhi becomes a blade without a sheath. Insight loses humility.
facets.external: Work becomes perfectionistic. Decisions are delayed by over-analysis. Relationships suffer from excessive explanation or correction. Habits skew toward micromanagement and mental rigidity.

excess.text:
Experience is sharp but rigid; detail overwhelms meaning.

Somatic: The body feels tense and over-held. Eyes strain. Breath is shallow, as if maintaining constant alertness. There is little softness.

Emotional: Emotion is tightly monitored. Small fluctuations feel significant. There is anxiety about missing something, misunderstanding something, or making the wrong move.

Cognitive: Thought becomes hyper-analytical. Details multiply. The mind dissects endlessly, losing the gestalt. Certainty hardens into control.

Psychic: The witnessing self narrows into the analyzer. Buddhi becomes a blade without a sheath. Insight loses humility.

External: Work becomes perfectionistic. Decisions are delayed by over-analysis. Relationships suffer from excessive explanation or correction. Habits skew toward micromanagement and mental rigidity.
excess.vignette: He rereads an email for the fifth time, adjusting phrasing minutely. A colleague’s casual comment echoes for hours. At home, he replays conversations, searching for hidden meanings. Everything is clear—but nothing is at rest.

deficient.label: Deficiency — Low Clarity (Fog / Diffusion)

facets.overview: Experience lacks definition; orientation is lost. A haze descends—confusion mixed with unease. The mind jumps to conclusions: *“Something is wrong. I’m failing.”* The lack of clarity is mistaken for danger. The system learns: *uncertainty is unsafe*. Cognitive fog thickens under stress. Trust in perception erodes further. The same fog appears. This time it is named accurately: *“Clarity is low right now.”* No immediate meaning is assigned. Clarity gradually returns. The nervous system learns: *fog can be navigated*. Confidence in perception strengthens. The buddhi becomes resilient rather than brittle. Clarity is not brilliance. When clarity is misunderstood: - Fog becomes fear - Sharpness becomes rigidity When clarity is honored: - Uncertainty becomes workable - Insight remains supple In Vedic terms, liberation at this level is not constant knowing, but **trustworthy seeing**— the capacity to meet experience at whatever resolution it arrives, without panic, distortion, or collapse. To live with phenomenological integrity in clarity is to know this:
facets.somatic: The body feels dull or disconnected. Sensations blur together—fatigue, hunger, tension are hard to distinguish. There is a sense of floating or heaviness.
facets.emotional: Emotion is vague. One feels “off” without knowing why. Irritability or sadness emerges without a clear object.
facets.cognitive: Thoughts are scattered or slow. Decisions feel confusing. Attention slips easily. Reality can feel unreal or distant.
facets.psychic: The soul’s signal is obscured. Not absent—just veiled. There is diminished trust in one’s own perception.
facets.external: Work feels disorganized or overwhelming. Communication becomes imprecise. Habits drift—sleep, eating, and focus lose rhythm. Life feels slightly unreal. She withdraws, avoids clarification, replays the feedback internally without structure. Or she reacts defensively, filling gaps with assumptions. She asks a clarifying question (**Karma Yoga**), pauses to reflect (**Jñāna**), and grounds attention in breath and posture (**Yoga**).

deficient.text:
Experience lacks definition; orientation is lost. A haze descends—confusion mixed with unease. The mind jumps to conclusions: *“Something is wrong. I’m failing.”* The lack of clarity is mistaken for danger. The system learns: *uncertainty is unsafe*. Cognitive fog thickens under stress. Trust in perception erodes further. The same fog appears. This time it is named accurately: *“Clarity is low right now.”* No immediate meaning is assigned. Clarity gradually returns. The nervous system learns: *fog can be navigated*. Confidence in perception strengthens. The buddhi becomes resilient rather than brittle. Clarity is not brilliance. When clarity is misunderstood: - Fog becomes fear - Sharpness becomes rigidity When clarity is honored: - Uncertainty becomes workable - Insight remains supple In Vedic terms, liberation at this level is not constant knowing, but **trustworthy seeing**— the capacity to meet experience at whatever resolution it arrives, without panic, distortion, or collapse. To live with phenomenological integrity in clarity is to know this:

Somatic: The body feels dull or disconnected. Sensations blur together—fatigue, hunger, tension are hard to distinguish. There is a sense of floating or heaviness.

Emotional: Emotion is vague. One feels “off” without knowing why. Irritability or sadness emerges without a clear object.

Cognitive: Thoughts are scattered or slow. Decisions feel confusing. Attention slips easily. Reality can feel unreal or distant.

Psychic: The soul’s signal is obscured. Not absent—just veiled. There is diminished trust in one’s own perception.

External: Work feels disorganized or overwhelming. Communication becomes imprecise. Habits drift—sleep, eating, and focus lose rhythm. Life feels slightly unreal. She withdraws, avoids clarification, replays the feedback internally without structure. Or she reacts defensively, filling gaps with assumptions. She asks a clarifying question (**Karma Yoga**), pauses to reflect (**Jñāna**), and grounds attention in breath and posture (**Yoga**).
deficient.vignette: She opens her laptop but cannot decide where to begin. Tasks blur together. A mild sadness lingers without cause. By evening, she feels exhausted without having done much. The day passes like a dream she cannot fully recall.

attributes.title: Phenomenological Dimension: Self-Location (Spatiality)
attributes.aspect: Self-location determines whether life feels *inhabited* or *watched*, *immediate* or *remote*.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Psychic–Mental (where the sense of ‘I’ is located) · Ontic mode: Experiential axis (a dimension of first-person experience) · Valence: Neutral faculty (balance is centered, embodied presence) · Guṇa: Variable · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: Manifestation (the felt locus of the self) · Key relation: Bipolar axis (dissociation–over-identification) — relates to the witness and the ego-sense.
coordinate.seat: Psychic–Mental (where the sense of ‘I’ is located)
coordinate.ontic_mode: Experiential axis (a dimension of first-person experience)
coordinate.valence: Neutral faculty (balance is centered, embodied presence)
coordinate.causal_status: Manifestation (the felt locus of the self)
coordinate.key_relation: Bipolar axis (dissociation–over-identification) — relates to the witness and the ego-sense.

balanced.label: Equilibrium — Centered Self-Location (Embodied Presence)

facets.overview: Awareness is stably seated within the body and situation.
facets.somatic: The body feels occupied from within. Sensations arise where they occur—feet feel the ground, hands feel contact. There is weight and balance. Posture supports presence.
facets.emotional: Emotions are contained rather than spilling outward or collapsing inward. Feeling arises *here*, in the body, not at a distance. There is an implicit sense of safety in occupying one’s own space.
facets.cognitive: Attention is oriented outward from a stable center. Perception flows from “here” to “there” without confusion. Thoughts reference the body as the anchor point.
facets.psychic: At the soul level, there is **sthiti**—abidance. Awareness knows itself as present *in* the body but not limited *to* it. The witness is near, not aloof.
facets.external: Movement is coordinated and intentional. Work feels participatory rather than observational. Relationships feel intimate without being intrusive. Habits support grounding—regular meals, movement, rest.

balanced.text:
Awareness is stably seated within the body and situation.

Somatic: The body feels occupied from within. Sensations arise where they occur—feet feel the ground, hands feel contact. There is weight and balance. Posture supports presence.

Emotional: Emotions are contained rather than spilling outward or collapsing inward. Feeling arises *here*, in the body, not at a distance. There is an implicit sense of safety in occupying one’s own space.

Cognitive: Attention is oriented outward from a stable center. Perception flows from “here” to “there” without confusion. Thoughts reference the body as the anchor point.

Psychic: At the soul level, there is **sthiti**—abidance. Awareness knows itself as present *in* the body but not limited *to* it. The witness is near, not aloof.

External: Movement is coordinated and intentional. Work feels participatory rather than observational. Relationships feel intimate without being intrusive. Habits support grounding—regular meals, movement, rest.
balanced.vignette: She walks into a crowded room and feels her feet first. The noise registers, but it does not displace her. During conversations, she feels herself speaking from her chest, listening from her body. When she leaves, she feels complete—nothing left behind.

excess.label: Excess — Displaced Self-Location (Hyper-Observational / Dissociated-Above)

facets.overview: The self retreats from the body into distance or surveillance.
facets.somatic: The body feels faint, distant, or unreal. Sensations are muted or delayed. There is a sense of hovering above or behind the body.
facets.emotional: Emotion is flattened or abstracted. Feelings are analyzed rather than felt. Safety is sought through distance.
facets.cognitive: The mind narrates experience from the outside. Thoughts take the form: *“I notice that I am…”* rather than *“I feel…”* There is excessive self-monitoring.
facets.psychic: The witness separates too far. Sākṣī becomes exile rather than refuge. Awareness floats without embodiment.
facets.external: Work becomes observational rather than engaged. Social interactions feel performative. Habits skew toward over-thinking, avoidance of sensation, or excessive screen immersion.

excess.text:
The self retreats from the body into distance or surveillance.

Somatic: The body feels faint, distant, or unreal. Sensations are muted or delayed. There is a sense of hovering above or behind the body.

Emotional: Emotion is flattened or abstracted. Feelings are analyzed rather than felt. Safety is sought through distance.

Cognitive: The mind narrates experience from the outside. Thoughts take the form: *“I notice that I am…”* rather than *“I feel…”* There is excessive self-monitoring.

Psychic: The witness separates too far. Sākṣī becomes exile rather than refuge. Awareness floats without embodiment.

External: Work becomes observational rather than engaged. Social interactions feel performative. Habits skew toward over-thinking, avoidance of sensation, or excessive screen immersion.
excess.vignette: He attends a meeting but feels as though he is watching himself speak. His voice sounds distant, as if coming from someone else. Afterward, he cannot recall how he felt—only what he said. The day passes without landing.

deficient.label: Deficiency — Collapsed Self-Location (Over-Identified / Pulled-Outward)

facets.overview: The self is pulled into objects, others, or sensations. The comment lands and the self instantly leaves the body—either shrinking away or scattering into the room. The sensation is misread as *“I am unsafe here.” The system learns: *presence is dangerous*. Displacement becomes habitual. The self increasingly lives outside the body. The same comment lands. He feels his feet, his breath. He notices the heat in the chest **from here**, without leaving. The old pattern weakens. The nervous system learns: *I can stay here*. Self-location stabilizes. Presence becomes trustworthy. Self-location is not mystical abstraction. When spatiality is misunderstood: - Distance masquerades as safety - Reactivity masquerades as engagement When spatiality is restored: - The body becomes an ally - The world becomes reachable without consuming the self In Vedic terms, liberation here is not leaving the body, but **dwelling rightly within it**. To live with phenomenological integrity at the level of self-location is to know this:
facets.somatic: The body feels invaded or porous. Sensations overwhelm boundaries. There is little sense of containment.
facets.emotional: Emotion spills outward. Others’ moods feel intrusive. Reactivity replaces presence.
facets.cognitive: Attention is pulled toward stimuli. Thoughts orbit external events. The sense of “where I am” depends on what is happening around.
facets.psychic: The soul is diffused. Awareness is scattered across objects of experience rather than seated in itself.
facets.external: Work becomes reactive. Boundaries in relationships blur. Habits are shaped by external pressures rather than internal rhythm. He dissociates—goes quiet, becomes overly agreeable, or mentally checks out. Or he reacts defensively, as if attacked from everywhere. He responds simply (**Karma Yoga**), maintains inner anchoring (**Yoga**), and discerns what is useful from what is not (**Jñāna**).

deficient.vignette: She enters the day already absorbed by messages, news, demands. Every interruption hijacks her. By evening, she feels exhausted, unsure where the day went—or where she went.

attributes.title: Phenomenological Dimension: Temporal Orientation (Subjective Time)
attributes.aspect: Temporal orientation determines whether life feels *burdened*, *rushed*, or *alive*.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental (the mind’s orientation in time) · Ontic mode: Experiential axis (a dimension of first-person experience) · Valence: Neutral faculty (balance is integrated time-sense, Sthira Kāla-Bodha) · Guṇa: Variable (rajasic in Kāla-Grāha, tamasic in stagnation) · Scale: Individual, against the ground of cosmic time (Kāla) · Causal status: Manifestation (the felt relation to past, present, and future) · Key relation: Bipolar axis (time-grasping–temporal flatness) — the personal register of cosmic time (Dim 14).
coordinate.ontic_mode: Experiential axis (a dimension of first-person experience)
coordinate.valence: Neutral faculty (balance is integrated time-sense, Sthira Kāla-Bodha)
coordinate.guna: Variable (rajasic in Kāla-Grāha, tamasic in stagnation)
coordinate.scale: Individual, against the ground of cosmic time (Kāla)
coordinate.causal_status: Manifestation (the felt relation to past, present, and future)
coordinate.key_relation: Bipolar axis (time-grasping–temporal flatness) — the personal register of cosmic time (Dim 14).

balanced.label: Equilibrium — Integrated Time (Sthira Kāla-Bodha)

facets.overview: Past, present, and future are available without domination.
facets.somatic: The body feels paced. Movements have rhythm rather than haste. Breath is steady, neither held nor hurried. There is a sense of arriving fully in each moment.
facets.emotional: Emotion flows without dragging residue from the past or projecting anxiety into the future. Feelings arise and complete themselves. The dominant tone is *timeless sufficiency*.
facets.cognitive: Memory informs without imprisoning. Planning occurs without obsession. Attention returns easily to what is happening now. Time feels like a river one is in, not a force pushing from behind or pulling ahead.
facets.psychic: At the soul level, there is **sthiti**—abiding in the present while holding continuity. Awareness knows itself as untouched by time even as it moves within it.
facets.external: Work unfolds sequentially. Tasks are finished before new ones are started. Relationships feel present rather than nostalgic or anticipatory. Habits align with natural cycles—work, rest, renewal.

balanced.text:
Past, present, and future are available without domination.

Somatic: The body feels paced. Movements have rhythm rather than haste. Breath is steady, neither held nor hurried. There is a sense of arriving fully in each moment.

Emotional: Emotion flows without dragging residue from the past or projecting anxiety into the future. Feelings arise and complete themselves. The dominant tone is *timeless sufficiency*.

Cognitive: Memory informs without imprisoning. Planning occurs without obsession. Attention returns easily to what is happening now. Time feels like a river one is in, not a force pushing from behind or pulling ahead.

Psychic: At the soul level, there is **sthiti**—abiding in the present while holding continuity. Awareness knows itself as untouched by time even as it moves within it.

External: Work unfolds sequentially. Tasks are finished before new ones are started. Relationships feel present rather than nostalgic or anticipatory. Habits align with natural cycles—work, rest, renewal.
balanced.vignette: She plans the week in the morning, then closes the calendar. During meetings, she listens fully rather than rehearsing the next task. When a memory arises in the afternoon, she notes it without being pulled back. The day ends with a sense of completion, not rush.

excess.label: Excess — Future- or Past-Dominated Time (Kāla-Grāha)

facets.overview: Awareness is captured by memory or anticipation.
facets.somatic: The body feels tense or restless. There is forward-leaning posture in future-fixation, or heaviness and collapse in past-fixation. Breath mirrors this—either rushed or sighing.
facets.emotional: In future-dominance: anxiety, urgency, control-seeking. In past-dominance: regret, sadness, resentment. Emotion feels repetitive, as if replaying on a loop.
facets.cognitive: Thoughts orbit around *what should have been* or *what might happen*. The present moment feels thin, merely a bridge between mental time zones.
facets.psychic: The soul is displaced from the now. Awareness identifies with narrative rather than presence. Kāla is experienced as enemy or judge.
facets.external: Work becomes rushed or avoidant. Relationships are filtered through memory or expectation rather than direct encounter. Habits become compulsive—checking, rehearsing, revisiting.

excess.text:
Awareness is captured by memory or anticipation.

Somatic: The body feels tense or restless. There is forward-leaning posture in future-fixation, or heaviness and collapse in past-fixation. Breath mirrors this—either rushed or sighing.

Emotional: In future-dominance: anxiety, urgency, control-seeking. In past-dominance: regret, sadness, resentment. Emotion feels repetitive, as if replaying on a loop.

Cognitive: Thoughts orbit around *what should have been* or *what might happen*. The present moment feels thin, merely a bridge between mental time zones.

Psychic: The soul is displaced from the now. Awareness identifies with narrative rather than presence. Kāla is experienced as enemy or judge.

External: Work becomes rushed or avoidant. Relationships are filtered through memory or expectation rather than direct encounter. Habits become compulsive—checking, rehearsing, revisiting.
excess.vignette: He wakes already thinking about a conversation from last night—or one yet to come. At work, he is physically present but mentally elsewhere. Even rest is haunted by anticipation. Time feels like it is always running out or already gone.

deficient.label: Deficiency — Collapsed Time (Temporal Flatness / Stagnation)

facets.overview: The sense of time loses depth or direction. Awareness collapses into the future. Every sensation is interpreted as a sign. The mind says, *“I can’t live until this resolves.” The system learns: *the present is unlivable*. Anxiety deepens. Future-fixation strengthens. The same uncertainty exists. This time it is seen clearly: *“The outcome is future; this breath is now.”* Awareness returns to the current moment without denial. The pull of the future weakens. Time opens. Presence becomes stable. Kāla transforms from threat into flow. Time itself is not the problem. When temporal orientation is distorted: - Memory becomes a prison - Anticipation becomes a tyrant When temporal orientation is integrated: - The past informs - The future guides - The present lives In Vedic understanding, liberation here is not escaping time, but **standing rightly within it**— so that each moment becomes inhabitable. To live with phenomenological integrity in time is to know this: Agency answers a quiet but decisive question: Is this happening through me, or to me?
facets.somatic: The body feels heavy or inert. Movement lacks momentum. Breath is shallow or monotonous. Days blur together.
facets.emotional: Emotion feels muted. There is little anticipation or recollection. Motivation wanes. Life feels stalled.
facets.cognitive: Planning feels pointless. Memory lacks emotional charge. The future feels vague or unreachable. Time becomes static.
facets.psychic: The soul’s dynamism is veiled. Awareness remains, but without temporal texture. The lesson here is re-engagement, not urgency.
facets.external: Work is done mechanically. Relationships drift without initiative. Habits lose rhythm—sleep, eating, activity blur. He refreshes email obsessively, replays scenarios, neglects the present task. Action is driven by anticipation, not reality. He completes the next meaningful task (**Karma Yoga**), reflects on what is within control (**Jñāna**), and grounds attention in rhythm (**Yoga**).

Cognitive: Planning feels pointless. Memory lacks emotional charge. The future feels vague or unreachable. Time becomes static.

He completes the next meaningful task (**Karma Yoga**), reflects on what is within control (**Jñāna**), and grounds attention in rhythm (**Yoga**).
deficient.vignette: She looks at the clock, surprised it is already afternoon. The morning left no impression. The evening arrives without anticipation. Life feels like it is happening, but not progressing.

attributes.title: Phenomenological Dimension: Agency (Ownership of Action)
attributes.aspect: Agency determines whether life feels participatory or imposed.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Psychic–Mental (the felt sense of doership) · Ontic mode: Experiential axis (a dimension of first-person experience) · Valence: Neutral faculty (balance is aligned doership) · Guṇa: Variable (rajasic in control-fixation, tamasic in passivity) · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: Manifestation (the felt ownership of action) · Key relation: Bipolar axis (hyper-doership–helplessness) — relates to Ahaṁkāra and the teaching of non-doership.
coordinate.ontic_mode: Experiential axis (a dimension of first-person experience)
coordinate.guna: Variable (rajasic in control-fixation, tamasic in passivity)
coordinate.key_relation: Bipolar axis (hyper-doership–helplessness) — relates to Ahaṁkāra and the teaching of non-doership.

balanced.label: Equilibrium — Integrated Agency (Aligned Doership)

facets.overview: Action flows with conscious participation but without egoic inflation.
facets.somatic: The body feels responsive rather than reactive. Movements begin from intention, not tension. Breath and posture support deliberate action.
facets.emotional: There is a sense of capability without urgency. Motivation arises steadily. Emotions accompany action but do not overpower it.
facets.cognitive: Thoughts form around “I can choose” rather than “I must” or “I can’t.” Decisions are weighed, then enacted. Responsibility feels dignified, not burdensome.
facets.psychic: At the soul level, there is recognition: *I participate in action, but I am not the total cause of all outcomes.* This is karma yoga—engagement without egoic entanglement.
facets.external: Work is proactive but not controlling. Relationships are accountable without domination. Habits are shaped intentionally rather than compulsively.

balanced.text:
Action flows with conscious participation but without egoic inflation.

Somatic: The body feels responsive rather than reactive. Movements begin from intention, not tension. Breath and posture support deliberate action.

Emotional: There is a sense of capability without urgency. Motivation arises steadily. Emotions accompany action but do not overpower it.

Cognitive: Thoughts form around “I can choose” rather than “I must” or “I can’t.” Decisions are weighed, then enacted. Responsibility feels dignified, not burdensome.

Psychic: At the soul level, there is recognition: *I participate in action, but I am not the total cause of all outcomes.* This is karma yoga—engagement without egoic entanglement.

External: Work is proactive but not controlling. Relationships are accountable without domination. Habits are shaped intentionally rather than compulsively.
balanced.vignette: She receives critical feedback. She feels the sting but says, calmly, “Let me reflect and adjust.” She makes changes where needed, leaves what is irrelevant. She neither collapses nor defends reflexively. The action is hers, but not her identity.

excess.label: Excess — Inflated Agency (Hyper-Doership / Control Fixation)

facets.overview: The self assumes excessive responsibility for outcomes.
facets.somatic: The body is tense, forward-leaning. Jaw tight, breath shallow. There is a subtle gripping, as if holding the world together.
facets.emotional: Anxiety hides beneath competence. There is fear of failure and intolerance for uncertainty. Rest feels irresponsible.
facets.cognitive: Thoughts revolve around control: *“If I don’t handle this, everything will collapse.”* Responsibility becomes identity.
facets.psychic: Ahaṅkāra expands. The self mistakes participation for omnipotence. Outcomes are personalized excessively.
facets.external: Work becomes perfectionistic. Delegation is difficult. Relationships feel micromanaged. Burnout looms because one feels solely accountable for everything.

excess.text:
The self assumes excessive responsibility for outcomes.

Somatic: The body is tense, forward-leaning. Jaw tight, breath shallow. There is a subtle gripping, as if holding the world together.

Emotional: Anxiety hides beneath competence. There is fear of failure and intolerance for uncertainty. Rest feels irresponsible.

Cognitive: Thoughts revolve around control: *“If I don’t handle this, everything will collapse.”* Responsibility becomes identity.

Psychic: Ahaṅkāra expands. The self mistakes participation for omnipotence. Outcomes are personalized excessively.

External: Work becomes perfectionistic. Delegation is difficult. Relationships feel micromanaged. Burnout looms because one feels solely accountable for everything.
excess.vignette: He stays late correcting minor details no one asked him to fix. At home, he mentally replays tasks to ensure nothing was missed. Even praise feels incomplete—there is always more to secure.

deficient.label: Deficiency — Fragmented Agency (Helplessness / Passivity)

facets.overview: The self feels acted upon rather than acting. Confusion arises. Instead of examining it, the mind concludes: *“I have no real choice.”* Fear is interpreted as destiny. The same fear and uncertainty appear. This time, they are acknowledged: *“There is fear. But there is also choice.”* The self distinguishes between emotion and authorship. Agency is not about dominating life. Nor is it about surrendering to it. Distorted agency says: - “I must control everything.” or - “I control nothing.” Integrated agency says: “I am responsible for my participation, not the universe.” In Vedic understanding, liberation at this dimension is recognizing: - Action belongs to prakṛti. - Witnessing belongs to consciousness. - Participation belongs to the embodied human. To live with phenomenological integrity in agency is to know this: Below is the same **phenomenological narrative translation**, now applied precisely to the dimension of **Relational Quality (Intersubjectivity / Context)**, articulated as **lived experience** and grounded in Vedic insight (ṛta, saṅga–asaṅga, mitra–dveṣa, lokasaṃgraha), behavioral psychology, and contemporary social life. Relational quality answers a quiet but decisive question: How am I situated with others and the world right now—met, missed, or merged?
facets.somatic: The body feels heavy or slack. Movements are delayed. There is little initiative in posture or gesture.
facets.emotional: A tone of resignation or shame lingers. Motivation is low. Effort feels futile.
facets.cognitive: Thoughts repeat: *“It doesn’t matter what I do.”* Decisions are avoided. Responsibility feels overwhelming rather than empowering.
facets.psychic: The witness is overshadowed by perceived powerlessness. The self confuses surrender with collapse.
facets.external: Work is reactive. Opportunities are missed due to inaction. Relationships may lean toward dependency or avoidance. Habits drift without intention. He avoids deciding. Or he acts impulsively to escape discomfort. Responsibility is outsourced—to circumstances, to others. Outcome (Saṃskāra Reinforced) The nervous system learns: *I am not the author of my path.* Helplessness deepens. Agency contracts further. He lists options clearly (**Jñāna**), commits to a considered step (**Karma Yoga**), and accepts uncertainty without collapse (**Yoga of steadiness**). Outcome (Saṃskāra Exhausted) The act of choosing strengthens inner authorship. The system learns: *I can act without controlling outcomes.* Agency becomes resilient rather than inflated.

deficient.vignette: She delays replying to an important email, convinced her response won’t matter. Tasks accumulate. When problems escalate, they confirm her belief of incapacity.

attributes.title: Phenomenological Dimension: Relational Quality (Intersubjectivity / Context)
attributes.aspect: Relational quality determines whether life feels **inhabited together** or endured alone.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital (the emotional field of connection) · Ontic mode: Experiential axis (a dimension of first-person experience) · Valence: Neutral faculty (balance is attuned presence, Saṁgata) · Guṇa: Variable (fusion vs. alienation) · Scale: Interpersonal (Ādhibhautika) · Causal status: Manifestation (the felt quality of connection) · Key relation: Bipolar axis (over-relationality–relational collapse).
coordinate.ontic_mode: Experiential axis (a dimension of first-person experience)
coordinate.valence: Neutral faculty (balance is attuned presence, Saṁgata)
coordinate.key_relation: Bipolar axis (over-relationality–relational collapse).

balanced.label: Equilibrium — Attuned Relational Field (Saṃgata Presence)

facets.overview: Connection without fusion; autonomy without isolation.
facets.somatic: The body feels open yet bounded. Breath flows easily during interaction. There is no bracing or collapsing when others enter the field.
facets.emotional: Emotion includes warmth and responsiveness. Empathy arises without overwhelm. One feels neither invisible nor overexposed.
facets.cognitive: Others are perceived as subjects, not threats or utilities. Interpretation is flexible. Misunderstandings are corrected rather than assumed.
facets.psychic: At the soul level, there is **saha-bhāva**—a sense of shared being. Awareness recognizes itself in relation without losing itself.
facets.external: Work involves collaboration without competition or withdrawal. Relationships include listening and speaking in balance. Habits support connection—regular contact, clear boundaries, restorative solitude.

balanced.text:
Connection without fusion; autonomy without isolation.

Somatic: The body feels open yet bounded. Breath flows easily during interaction. There is no bracing or collapsing when others enter the field.

Emotional: Emotion includes warmth and responsiveness. Empathy arises without overwhelm. One feels neither invisible nor overexposed.

Cognitive: Others are perceived as subjects, not threats or utilities. Interpretation is flexible. Misunderstandings are corrected rather than assumed.

Psychic: At the soul level, there is **saha-bhāva**—a sense of shared being. Awareness recognizes itself in relation without losing itself.

External: Work involves collaboration without competition or withdrawal. Relationships include listening and speaking in balance. Habits support connection—regular contact, clear boundaries, restorative solitude.
balanced.vignette: She moves through the day meeting people’s eyes, responding appropriately, letting conversations end when complete. In the evening, she enjoys quiet without loneliness. Connection feels available, not compulsory.

excess.label: Excess — Over-Relationality (Fusion / Hyper-Attunement)

facets.overview: The self dissolves into others’ expectations and moods.
facets.somatic: The body feels porous. Tension arises quickly in social settings. There is little sense of boundary—others’ presence is felt as invasion.
facets.emotional: Emotion mirrors others excessively. Approval-seeking and fear of disconnection dominate. One’s own needs feel secondary or unclear.
facets.cognitive: Thoughts revolve around others’ reactions: *“Did I upset them?”* *“Am I enough?”* Meaning is outsourced to social feedback.
facets.psychic: Awareness forgets its own center. Connection becomes survival strategy. The soul trades presence for acceptance.
facets.external: Work becomes people-pleasing. Boundaries blur in relationships. Habits skew toward constant availability, over-communication, or emotional caretaking.

excess.text:
The self dissolves into others’ expectations and moods.

Somatic: The body feels porous. Tension arises quickly in social settings. There is little sense of boundary—others’ presence is felt as invasion.

Emotional: Emotion mirrors others excessively. Approval-seeking and fear of disconnection dominate. One’s own needs feel secondary or unclear.

Cognitive: Thoughts revolve around others’ reactions: *“Did I upset them?”* *“Am I enough?”* Meaning is outsourced to social feedback.

Psychic: Awareness forgets its own center. Connection becomes survival strategy. The soul trades presence for acceptance.

External: Work becomes people-pleasing. Boundaries blur in relationships. Habits skew toward constant availability, over-communication, or emotional caretaking.
excess.vignette: He says yes automatically—to meetings, favors, emotional labor. By evening, he feels drained and resentful, unsure where his energy went. Connection has become depletion.

deficient.label: Deficiency — Relational Collapse (Isolation / Alienation)

facets.overview: The relational field thins or disappears. A sense of disconnection arises. It is misread as *“I don’t matter to anyone.”* Fear of rejection colors perception. The same vulnerability arises. This time it is recognized: *“There is a wish to be met.”* The feeling is owned, not projected. Relational quality is not about being liked. Nor is it about standing alone. Distorted relationality says: - “I must merge to belong.” or - “I must withdraw to be safe.” Attuned relationality says: “I can meet you without losing myself.” In Vedic understanding, liberation at this dimension is remembering that **being is inherently relational**, but **wholeness does not require erasure**. To live with phenomenological integrity in relationship is to know this: Somatic presence answers a quiet but decisive question: Am I living from within my body—or merely operating it from a distance?
facets.somatic: The body feels closed or withdrawn. Shoulders curl inward. Eye contact feels effortful or avoided.
facets.emotional: Emotion is muted or guarded. There is a low-grade loneliness or distrust. Connection feels risky or pointless.
facets.cognitive: Thoughts interpret others as distant, judgmental, or irrelevant. Meaning is constructed privately, without relational feedback.
facets.psychic: The soul contracts into self-sufficiency. Awareness forgets its relational nature. Isolation masquerades as independence.
facets.external: Work is done alone when possible. Relationships fade or remain superficial. Habits lean toward withdrawal—screens replace presence, routines replace dialogue. She withdraws or overshares impulsively, seeking validation. Either way, connection is distorted by neediness or avoidance. Outcome (Saṃskāra Reinforced) The nervous system learns: *relationship is unsafe or unreliable*. Alienation deepens. Trust erodes. She chooses one trusted person and speaks honestly (**Bhakti**), listens without defense (**Jñāna**), and remains embodied (**Yoga**). Outcome (Saṃskāra Exhausted) The old fear loosens. The system learns: *connection can be selective and safe*. Relational quality stabilizes.

The system learns: *connection can be selective and safe*. Relational quality stabilizes.
deficient.vignette: She passes through the day without real exchange. Messages go unanswered. Even when among people, she feels unseen. The world feels flat, like a backdrop rather than a shared space.

attributes.title: Phenomenological Dimension: Somatic Presence (Embodiment)
attributes.aspect: Somatic presence determines whether life is **felt, regulated, and metabolized**—or merely conceptualized.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Physical (the felt sense of inhabiting the body) · Ontic mode: Experiential axis (a dimension of first-person experience) · Valence: Neutral faculty (balance is grounded embodiment) · Guṇa: Variable (somatic flooding vs. numbing) · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: Manifestation (the felt degree of embodiment) · Key relation: Bipolar axis (over-sensitization–somatic absence) — the somatic register (Annamaya).
coordinate.seat: Physical (the felt sense of inhabiting the body)
coordinate.ontic_mode: Experiential axis (a dimension of first-person experience)
coordinate.key_relation: Bipolar axis (over-sensitization–somatic absence) — the somatic register (Annamaya).

balanced.label: Equilibrium — Embodied Presence (Grounded Inhabitation)

facets.overview: Experience is fully lived through the body without overwhelm.
facets.somatic: The body feels warm, responsive, and inhabited. Breath moves naturally. Sensations—pressure, movement, temperature—are clear but not intrusive. There is a sense of *being here*.
facets.emotional: Emotion is felt in the body—tightness in the chest, warmth in the belly—without needing immediate discharge. Feelings rise, move, and settle. There is affect tolerance.
facets.cognitive: Thought references bodily signals. Decisions include gut sense alongside reasoning. The mind listens to the body rather than overriding it.
facets.psychic: At the soul level, awareness recognizes the body as an ally and instrument, not an obstacle. Presence descends fully into form without losing spaciousness.
facets.external: Work is paced with bodily feedback. Relationships include physical cues—posture, tone, proximity. Habits support embodiment: movement, rest, nourishment, breath.

balanced.text:
Experience is fully lived through the body without overwhelm.

Somatic: The body feels warm, responsive, and inhabited. Breath moves naturally. Sensations—pressure, movement, temperature—are clear but not intrusive. There is a sense of *being here*.

Emotional: Emotion is felt in the body—tightness in the chest, warmth in the belly—without needing immediate discharge. Feelings rise, move, and settle. There is affect tolerance.

Cognitive: Thought references bodily signals. Decisions include gut sense alongside reasoning. The mind listens to the body rather than overriding it.

Psychic: At the soul level, awareness recognizes the body as an ally and instrument, not an obstacle. Presence descends fully into form without losing spaciousness.

External: Work is paced with bodily feedback. Relationships include physical cues—posture, tone, proximity. Habits support embodiment: movement, rest, nourishment, breath.
balanced.vignette: She notices hunger before irritability sets in. She stretches when stiffness appears. During conversation, she senses when she needs to pause or speak. By evening, her body feels used, not depleted. Sleep arrives naturally.

excess.label: Excess — Hyper-Embodiment (Over-Sensitization / Somatic Flooding)

facets.overview: The body dominates awareness.
facets.somatic: Sensations are loud and intrusive. Pain, tension, or pleasure hijack attention. The body feels overwhelming, demanding constant management.
facets.emotional: Emotion spills rapidly into sensation. Anxiety surges as tightness, anger as heat, sadness as heaviness. Regulation feels difficult.
facets.cognitive: Thoughts orbit bodily states: *“What’s wrong with me?”* *“I can’t handle this feeling.”* Sensation is misread as danger.
facets.psychic: Awareness collapses into the body. The witness is lost in sensation. The soul confuses intensity with identity.
facets.external: Work is disrupted by bodily preoccupation. Relationships strain under emotional reactivity. Habits skew toward constant self-monitoring, symptom checking, or avoidance.

excess.text:
The body dominates awareness.

Somatic: Sensations are loud and intrusive. Pain, tension, or pleasure hijack attention. The body feels overwhelming, demanding constant management.

Emotional: Emotion spills rapidly into sensation. Anxiety surges as tightness, anger as heat, sadness as heaviness. Regulation feels difficult.

Cognitive: Thoughts orbit bodily states: *“What’s wrong with me?”* *“I can’t handle this feeling.”* Sensation is misread as danger.

Psychic: Awareness collapses into the body. The witness is lost in sensation. The soul confuses intensity with identity.

External: Work is disrupted by bodily preoccupation. Relationships strain under emotional reactivity. Habits skew toward constant self-monitoring, symptom checking, or avoidance.
excess.vignette: He wakes already scanning his body. A tight chest becomes panic. A headache derails the morning. Even minor discomfort feels catastrophic. The day is organized around managing sensation.

deficient.label: Deficiency — Disembodiment (Somatic Absence / Numbing)

facets.overview: The body drops out of experience. A tightness arises in the chest. It is misinterpreted as *“Something is wrong with me.”* Sensation is feared or ignored. The same tightness arises. This time it is named accurately: *“There is tension here.”* No story is added. The body is not a problem to be solved. Nor is it an object to be escaped. Distorted embodiment says: - “I am my sensations.” or - “I am not my body.” Integrated embodiment says: “This body is where life happens.” In Vedic understanding, liberation at this dimension is not transcending the body, but **fully arriving within it**— so that sensation becomes signal, not threat. To live with phenomenological integrity in embodiment is to know this:
facets.somatic: The body feels distant, dull, or unreal. Sensations are muted or ignored. One may forget to eat, move, or rest.
facets.emotional: Emotion feels abstract or blunted. Feelings are described rather than felt. There is emotional flatness or delayed reaction.
facets.cognitive: Thought dominates. Decisions are made cognitively, often ignoring bodily signals. Fatigue or stress appears suddenly, without warning.
facets.psychic: Awareness retreats upward, leaving the body under-inhabited. The soul experiences life at a remove.
facets.external: Work is mentally driven but physically draining. Relationships feel distant. Habits neglect the body—poor sleep, irregular eating, lack of movement. He dissociates—scrolls endlessly, overeats, numbs with stimulation—or reacts impulsively to discharge sensation. Outcome (Saṃskāra Reinforced) The nervous system learns: *the body is unsafe or irrelevant*. Disembodiment or reactivity deepens. He grounds attention in breath (**Yoga**), allows sensation to move (**Prāṇa awareness**), and responds with simple care (**Karma Yoga**). Outcome (Saṃskāra Exhausted) The sensation completes its cycle. The body is re-trusted. Embodiment strengthens.

Outcome (Saṃskāra Exhausted) The sensation completes its cycle. The body is re-trusted. Embodiment strengthens.
deficient.vignette: She works through meals without noticing hunger. By evening, exhaustion hits like a wall. She realizes she has barely felt her body all day—only thoughts.

attributes.title: Rati (रति) — Love, Attachment, Delight
attributes.aspect: Rati is the centripetal force of the human heart—the gravitational pull toward beauty, connection, and union. It is not limited to romantic desire, though it certainly includes it. Rati is the soul’s fundamental “yes” to life: the warmth that draws a parent to a child’s sleeping face, the quiet pleasure of a garden coming into bloom, the devotional surrender of the bhakta before the divine. It is the emotional current that makes us reach toward what we find beautiful, and in that reaching, discover something essential about ourselves.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital (the heart’s pull toward beauty and union) · Ontic mode: State (durable disposition / Sthāyibhāva) · Valence: Faculty — wholesome as generous love, binding as attachment · Guṇa: Sattva in equilibrium, rajasic in excess · Scale: Interpersonal (Ādhibhautika) · Causal status: Dispositional ground (a durable emotional foundation) · Key relation: Ripens into Śṛṅgāra Rasa; fed by vyabhicāribhāvas such as longing and jealousy.
coordinate.seat: Vital (the heart’s pull toward beauty and union)
coordinate.valence: Faculty — wholesome as generous love, binding as attachment
coordinate.guna: Sattva in equilibrium, rajasic in excess
coordinate.key_relation: Ripens into Śṛṅgāra Rasa; fed by vyabhicāribhāvas such as longing and jealousy.

balanced.label: Equilibrium: Love as Generous Presence

facets.overview: When Rati operates in equilibrium, there is a quality of warmth in the chest that is not grasping. The person experiences affection as something that flows outward without demanding return. Internally, thoughts about loved ones carry a tone of appreciation rather than anxiety. The body feels open—shoulders relaxed, breathing easy, a softness around the eyes. There is a natural attentiveness to beauty in small things: the way light falls across a table, the particular cadence of a friend’s laughter. In relationships, this manifests as genuine care without possessiveness. The person can hold space for another’s independence without experiencing it as rejection. At work, equilibrium Rati appears as aesthetic sensitivity and a capacity for collaborative warmth—the colleague who remembers birthdays not performatively but because they actually noticed. In daily habit, there is an orientation toward nourishment: the meal prepared with attention, the evening walk chosen for its beauty rather than its efficiency.

balanced.text: When Rati operates in equilibrium, there is a quality of warmth in the chest that is not grasping. The person experiences affection as something that flows outward without demanding return. Internally, thoughts about loved ones carry a tone of appreciation rather than anxiety. The body feels open—shoulders relaxed, breathing easy, a softness around the eyes. There is a natural attentiveness to beauty in small things: the way light falls across a table, the particular cadence of a friend’s laughter. In relationships, this manifests as genuine care without possessiveness. The person can hold space for another’s independence without experiencing it as rejection. At work, equilibrium Rati appears as aesthetic sensitivity and a capacity for collaborative warmth—the colleague who remembers birthdays not performatively but because they actually noticed. In daily habit, there is an orientation toward nourishment: the meal prepared with attention, the evening walk chosen for its beauty rather than its efficiency.
balanced.vignette:
She waters the herbs on the windowsill before checking her phone. The basil has put out new leaves overnight, and she runs a thumb along one, releasing its scent into the kitchen air. Her partner is still asleep. She makes tea for two—not because it was asked for, but because the act of preparing it is itself a small pleasure. The morning feels unhurried. There is nothing to prove.

Inner Presence (Psychic).** At the soul level, balanced Rati is the heart’s pure ‘yes’ to existence — love as participation in beauty that asks nothing back. The spiritual lesson is that affection freely given is itself a form of worship: one loves the particular and, through it, touches the universal.

excess.label: Excess: Love as Obsessive Clinging

facets.overview: When Rati becomes excessive—rājasic in the Guṇa framework—love curdles into possession. The internal landscape shifts from warmth to a kind of anxious heat. Thoughts about the beloved become repetitive, circling: Where are they? What are they feeling? Do they still feel the same? The body tightens—a constriction in the solar plexus, a restless energy in the limbs that refuses to settle. The “you complete me” mentality takes hold, and with it, a fragile dependence that swings easily from adoration to jealousy. Externally, excess Rati produces controlling behaviors dressed up as care. The partner who texts twelve times in an afternoon. The parent who cannot tolerate the child’s emerging autonomy. At work, it may appear as an unhealthy attachment to a mentor, a project, or an institution—an inability to separate self-worth from the object of devotion. Daily habits become organized around the beloved object: meals skipped, friendships neglected, the entire rhythmic structure of life deformed by the gravitational pull of a single attachment.

excess.text: When Rati becomes excessive—rājasic in the Guṇa framework—love curdles into possession. The internal landscape shifts from warmth to a kind of anxious heat. Thoughts about the beloved become repetitive, circling: Where are they? What are they feeling? Do they still feel the same? The body tightens—a constriction in the solar plexus, a restless energy in the limbs that refuses to settle. The “you complete me” mentality takes hold, and with it, a fragile dependence that swings easily from adoration to jealousy. Externally, excess Rati produces controlling behaviors dressed up as care. The partner who texts twelve times in an afternoon. The parent who cannot tolerate the child’s emerging autonomy. At work, it may appear as an unhealthy attachment to a mentor, a project, or an institution—an inability to separate self-worth from the object of devotion. Daily habits become organized around the beloved object: meals skipped, friendships neglected, the entire rhythmic structure of life deformed by the gravitational pull of a single attachment.
excess.vignette:
He has refreshed her social media profile four times in the last hour. She posted a photo with someone he does not recognize, and the pit in his stomach has not closed since. He drafts a casual text, deletes it, drafts another. His work sits untouched on the screen. The apartment is immaculate—he cleaned it this morning as a kind of offering, imagining her arriving, imagining her noticing. She has not called. The silence is not neutral; it is a verdict.

Inner Presence (Psychic).** The soul has mistaken a person or object for its own ground, pouring into the finite the longing that belongs to the infinite. The lesson asking to be learned is that the beloved is a doorway, not a destination — and that grasping at the form strangles the very love it seeks to hold.

deficient.label: Deficiency: The Heart Gone Numb

facets.overview: When Rati is suppressed or depleted—the tāmasic state—the world loses its luster. Internally, there is a flatness: not active suffering, but an absence of warmth. The person may go through the motions of connection without actually feeling connected. Thoughts about others carry no emotional charge. The body feels dull, heavy, untouched—as though wrapped in a thin layer of insulation that prevents the world from reaching the skin. Beauty passes by unnoticed. In relationships, deficient Rati manifests as emotional withdrawal that the person themselves may not recognize. They are present physically but absent in quality. They stop initiating contact, not out of anger but out of a vague sense that it does not matter. At work, there is competence without passion—tasks completed, deadlines met, but nothing sparks genuine interest. Daily habits become mechanical: eating for fuel, sleeping from exhaustion, the rituals of living stripped of their savor.

The person may go through the motions of connection without actually feeling connected. Thoughts about others carry no emotional charge. In relationships, deficient Rati manifests as emotional withdrawal that the person themselves may not recognize. They are present physically but absent in quality. They stop initiating contact, not out of anger but out of a vague sense that it does not matter. At work, there is competence without passion—tasks completed, deadlines met, but nothing sparks genuine interest. Daily habits become mechanical: eating for fuel, sleeping from exhaustion, the rituals of living stripped of their savor.
deficient.vignette:
The sunset is reportedly beautiful tonight. His neighbor mentioned it. He glances out the window and registers the colors—orange, pink, a bruised purple along the horizon—the way one might register the time on a clock. Information received; nothing stirred. He turns back to the television. He cannot remember the last time something struck him as beautiful. He cannot remember the last time the absence troubled him.

───────────────────────────────────

Inner Presence (Psychic).** The soul has withdrawn its consent to life, and the warmth that connects it to the world has gone dormant. The lesson is reanimation: that beauty and connection are not luxuries but the soul’s native food, and that the heart, however numbed, can be gently re-opened.

attributes.title: Hāsa (हास) — Mirth, Laughter, Humor
attributes.aspect: Hāsa is the lightening energy of consciousness—the force that lifts, releases, and reminds us that the structures we build around our lives are not as solid as they seem. It is not merely the response to a joke. Hāsa is the cognitive capacity to perceive incongruity, the emotional willingness to delight in absurdity, and the social grace of shared vulnerability that laughter creates. In the classical view, it arises when expectations are subverted in a non-threatening way, and its function is to dissolve rigidity—in the body, in thought patterns, and in the fortress of the ego.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital (the lightness of mirth) · Ontic mode: State (durable disposition / Sthāyibhāva) · Valence: Faculty — wholesome levity, deflective when armored · Guṇa: Sattva in equilibrium · Scale: Individual, with interpersonal expression · Causal status: Dispositional ground · Key relation: Ripens into Hāsya Rasa; fed by transient delight and surprise.
coordinate.valence: Faculty — wholesome levity, deflective when armored
coordinate.key_relation: Ripens into Hāsya Rasa; fed by transient delight and surprise.

balanced.label: Equilibrium: Joy as Healing Intelligence

facets.overview: In equilibrium, Hāsa manifests as a lightness of being that does not deny difficulty but refuses to be crushed by it. Internally, the person carries a gentle background hum of amusement at the human condition—including their own participation in it. There is cognitive flexibility: the ability to hold two perspectives simultaneously and find the gap between them illuminating rather than threatening. The body reflects this—relaxed jaw, easy breathing, a readiness to smile that is not forced. In relationships, equilibrium Hāsa is the capacity to laugh together without anyone being diminished. It is the self-deprecating humor that builds trust, the playful banter that keeps long partnerships alive, the ability to defuse tension with a well-timed observation rather than a confrontation. At work, it appears as creative intelligence—the capacity to see problems from unexpected angles. In daily life, it is the spirit of līlā—play—that makes ordinary tasks tolerable and occasionally delightful.

balanced.text: In equilibrium, Hāsa manifests as a lightness of being that does not deny difficulty but refuses to be crushed by it. Internally, the person carries a gentle background hum of amusement at the human condition—including their own participation in it. There is cognitive flexibility: the ability to hold two perspectives simultaneously and find the gap between them illuminating rather than threatening. The body reflects this—relaxed jaw, easy breathing, a readiness to smile that is not forced. In relationships, equilibrium Hāsa is the capacity to laugh together without anyone being diminished. It is the self-deprecating humor that builds trust, the playful banter that keeps long partnerships alive, the ability to defuse tension with a well-timed observation rather than a confrontation. At work, it appears as creative intelligence—the capacity to see problems from unexpected angles. In daily life, it is the spirit of līlā—play—that makes ordinary tasks tolerable and occasionally delightful.
balanced.vignette:
The meeting has gone badly. The client rejected the proposal, the timeline is ruined, and the team is sitting in shell-shocked silence. She surveys the wreckage of their carefully prepared slides and says, quietly, “Well, at least the font choice was impeccable.” A beat. Then the project lead snorts, and the tension in the room cracks open like an egg. They begin to problem-solve. The laughter did not fix anything. It made fixing things possible.

Inner Presence (Psychic).** At the soul level, balanced Hāsa is the lightness of a being that does not take itself too seriously — the cosmic humor that sees the play (līlā) in things. The lesson is that joy and perspective are companions: to laugh kindly is to hold life’s contradictions without being crushed by them.

excess.label: Excess: Humor as Armor and Weapon

facets.overview: Excessive Hāsa—rājasic mirth—turns laughter into a defense mechanism or a tool of dominance. Internally, there is a compulsive need to find the joke in everything, a restless discomfort with sincerity or stillness. Beneath the humor runs a current of anxiety: if I stop being funny, will anyone stay? The body is performative—loud gestures, exaggerated expressions, a voice pitched for audience rather than intimacy. In relationships, excess Hāsa manifests as mockery disguised as wit, sarcasm that wounds while maintaining deniability (“I was just joking”), or an inability to be present during serious conversations. The person deflects vulnerability—their own and others’—with a punchline. At work, they may be the “class clown” who undermines authority or uses humor to avoid accountability. In daily habit, nothing is taken seriously enough to demand genuine engagement, and a creeping exhaustion develops beneath the performance.

excess.text: Excessive Hāsa—rājasic mirth—turns laughter into a defense mechanism or a tool of dominance. Internally, there is a compulsive need to find the joke in everything, a restless discomfort with sincerity or stillness. Beneath the humor runs a current of anxiety: if I stop being funny, will anyone stay? The body is performative—loud gestures, exaggerated expressions, a voice pitched for audience rather than intimacy. In relationships, excess Hāsa manifests as mockery disguised as wit, sarcasm that wounds while maintaining deniability (“I was just joking”), or an inability to be present during serious conversations. The person deflects vulnerability—their own and others’—with a punchline. At work, they may be the “class clown” who undermines authority or uses humor to avoid accountability. In daily habit, nothing is taken seriously enough to demand genuine engagement, and a creeping exhaustion develops beneath the performance.
excess.vignette:
His friends have stopped telling him important things. He learned about the diagnosis secondhand, a week after the fact. “We didn’t know how to bring it up,” they said. “You always turn everything into a bit.” He laughed when they told him this, because what else could he do? Later, alone, the laughter did not come. He sat with the silence and did not know what to put in it.

Inner Presence (Psychic).** The soul uses laughter as armor, deflecting every depth with a joke; mirth becomes a way of never having to land. The lesson is that not everything is to be laughed away — that some things ask to be felt, and that humor used to flee feeling becomes a subtle cruelty.

deficient.label: Deficiency: The Loss of Lightness

facets.overview: When Hāsa is deficient—the tāmasic state—life becomes unbearably heavy. Internally, everything is literal, weighty, consequential. The person cannot find the gap between expectation and reality where laughter lives; instead, every deviation from the expected is experienced as threat or failure. The body holds tension without release—clenched jaw, rigid posture, a face that has forgotten how to soften. In relationships, deficient Hāsa produces a grim seriousness that makes others uncomfortable. The person is “no fun”—not because they are unkind, but because they have lost access to the playful register of human connection. At work, they become the colleague who takes every email as a crisis, every piece of feedback as an attack. Daily life is a succession of obligations approached with dutiful grimness. The capacity for delight has atrophied, and with it, a certain resilience—because without humor, there is no shock absorber between the self and the world’s relentless impacts.

The person is “no fun”—not because they are unkind, but because they have lost access to the playful register of human connection. At work, they become the colleague who takes every email as a crisis, every piece of feedback as an attack. Daily life is a succession of obligations approached with dutiful grimness. The capacity for delight has atrophied, and with it, a certain resilience—because without humor, there is no shock absorber between the self and the world’s relentless impacts.
deficient.vignette:
Her daughter is doing a cartwheel in the living room and lands sideways on the couch cushions, legs flailing. The child looks up, grinning, expecting shared delight. She sees her mother’s face—attentive but unsmiling—and the grin fades. “What’s wrong, Mama?” “Nothing, sweetheart. That was good.” But the words are correct without being true, and the child, who is five and therefore the world’s most accurate emotional instrument, knows the difference.

───────────────────────────────────

Inner Presence (Psychic).** The soul has lost its lightness; the capacity to be amused, delighted, or surprised by life’s absurdity has dried up. The lesson is that gravity without levity becomes its own prison, and that a single genuine laugh can crack open a heart sealed by seriousness.

attributes.title: Śoka (शोक) — Sorrow, Grief, Loss
attributes.aspect: Śoka is the breaking open of the heart in the face of impermanence. It is the emotional acknowledgment that what we love can be taken, that what we build can crumble, that we ourselves are transient. Legend holds that the first poem—the Rāmāyaṇa—was born when the sage Vālmīki witnessed the killing of a bird and his raw grief spontaneously crystallized into rhythmic verse. The very word śloka (verse) derives from śoka (sorrow). In the classical view, grief is not merely something we suffer; it is something that creates—it gives birth to empathy, poetry, and ultimately to wisdom.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital (the weight of loss in the heart) · Ontic mode: State (durable disposition / Sthāyibhāva) · Valence: Faculty — wholesome as honest grief, afflictive when it becomes a home · Guṇa: Sattva–Tamas · Scale: Interpersonal (Ādhibhautika) · Causal status: Dispositional ground · Key relation: Ripens into Karuṇā Rasa; fed by dejection and anxiety.
coordinate.seat: Vital (the weight of loss in the heart)
coordinate.valence: Faculty — wholesome as honest grief, afflictive when it becomes a home
coordinate.key_relation: Ripens into Karuṇā Rasa; fed by dejection and anxiety.

balanced.label: Equilibrium: Grief as Sacred Opening

facets.overview: In equilibrium, Śoka softens rather than destroys. Internally, there is a deep ache—located often in the chest, sometimes behind the eyes—that is painful but not unbearable. Thoughts move slowly, with a quality of tenderness. The person is acutely aware of what matters: the preciousness of time, the fragility of connection, the weight of things left unsaid. The body wants stillness and warmth—a blanket, a window to sit beside, the presence of another person who does not need to speak. In relationships, equilibrium Śoka deepens capacity for empathy. The person who has grieved well—who has allowed sorrow its full passage—becomes someone others trust with their own pain. At work, it produces a quality of seriousness that is not grim but profound: the person who knows what matters and does not waste energy on what does not. In daily habit, there is a quality of ritual—the candle lit for the departed, the walk taken in the place that once was shared—that honors loss without being consumed by it.

balanced.text: In equilibrium, Śoka softens rather than destroys. Internally, there is a deep ache—located often in the chest, sometimes behind the eyes—that is painful but not unbearable. Thoughts move slowly, with a quality of tenderness. The person is acutely aware of what matters: the preciousness of time, the fragility of connection, the weight of things left unsaid. The body wants stillness and warmth—a blanket, a window to sit beside, the presence of another person who does not need to speak. In relationships, equilibrium Śoka deepens capacity for empathy. The person who has grieved well—who has allowed sorrow its full passage—becomes someone others trust with their own pain. At work, it produces a quality of seriousness that is not grim but profound: the person who knows what matters and does not waste energy on what does not. In daily habit, there is a quality of ritual—the candle lit for the departed, the walk taken in the place that once was shared—that honors loss without being consumed by it.
balanced.vignette:
It has been eight months since his father died. The acute pain has receded into something more like weather—a persistent atmospheric condition that colors everything without preventing function. This morning, folding laundry, he found one of his father’s handkerchiefs mixed in with his own shirts. He held it for a long time. He did not cry. He simply stood there, in the kitchen, holding the cloth, and let the absence be fully present. Then he folded it carefully and placed it in a drawer.

Inner Presence (Psychic).** At the soul level, balanced Śoka is the heart’s honest acknowledgment of loss — grief as the price and the proof of love. The lesson is that sorrow, fully felt and allowed to move, deepens rather than diminishes; it is the soul’s way of honoring what mattered.

excess.label: Excess: Grief as Identity

facets.overview: Excessive Śoka—rājasic sorrow—transforms grief from an experience into an identity. Internally, the person replays the loss compulsively, mining it for meaning or for evidence of injustice. There is a quality of self-pity that feeds on its own narrative: “Why me? Why this? No one understands.” The body is heavy but restless—unable to settle, unable to act. Tears come easily but bring no relief, because they are performed rather than released. In relationships, excess Śoka becomes manipulative, even if unconsciously so. Sorrow is used to claim attention, to avoid responsibility, to maintain a special status as the one who has suffered. Others begin to feel burdened rather than compassionate. At work, productivity collapses not because of incapacity but because grief has become a reason not to engage with anything that might demand energy. Daily life constricts around the loss until nothing else is visible.

excess.text: Excessive Śoka—rājasic sorrow—transforms grief from an experience into an identity. Internally, the person replays the loss compulsively, mining it for meaning or for evidence of injustice. There is a quality of self-pity that feeds on its own narrative: “Why me? Why this? No one understands.” The body is heavy but restless—unable to settle, unable to act. Tears come easily but bring no relief, because they are performed rather than released. In relationships, excess Śoka becomes manipulative, even if unconsciously so. Sorrow is used to claim attention, to avoid responsibility, to maintain a special status as the one who has suffered. Others begin to feel burdened rather than compassionate. At work, productivity collapses not because of incapacity but because grief has become a reason not to engage with anything that might demand energy. Daily life constricts around the loss until nothing else is visible.
excess.vignette:
It has been three years, and she still introduces the topic within the first ten minutes of any conversation. Friends have started to edit themselves around her—not mentioning their own joys, their vacations, their children’s milestones—because her grief fills the room so completely that anything else feels like trespass. She notices the distance growing and interprets it as further proof of her abandonment. The sorrow has become a fortress. She does not recognize that she is also its prisoner.

Inner Presence (Psychic).** The soul has taken up residence in its grief, identifying so wholly with the loss that mourning becomes a permanent home. The lesson is that even sorrow must be allowed to pass — that clinging to grief refuses to let love transform into memory and gratitude.

deficient.label: Deficiency: The Refusal to Grieve

facets.overview: Deficient Śoka—the tāmasic suppression of grief—produces a peculiar hardness. Internally, the person has sealed over the wound without cleaning it. There is no active pain, but there is also no depth. Thoughts about the loss are avoided, redirected, or intellectualized: “Everyone dies. It’s natural. I’ve moved on.” The body carries the unprocessed sorrow as tension—in the throat (the unshed tears), in the chest (the unlocked breath), in the shoulders (the weight that was never set down). In relationships, deficient Śoka manifests as an inability to be present with others’ pain. The person becomes the one who changes the subject, offers solutions instead of empathy, or simply disappears when things get heavy. At work, they may be hyperproductive—grief converted into relentless doing—but the work has a brittle, driven quality that lacks inspiration. Daily life is efficient but hollow. The person functions, but does not feel. And because they do not feel, they do not grow.

deficient.text: Deficient Śoka—the tāmasic suppression of grief—produces a peculiar hardness. Internally, the person has sealed over the wound without cleaning it. There is no active pain, but there is also no depth. Thoughts about the loss are avoided, redirected, or intellectualized: “Everyone dies. It’s natural.
