There is no fire in the belly. Thoughts are vague, circular, without traction. The body feels heavy, sluggish, resistant to movement. The distance between intention and action has become an uncrossable gulf. In relationships, deficient Utsāha manifests as passivity—the person who goes along with whatever is suggested, who has no opinions about where to eat or what to do, who has surrendered the initiative of their own life. At work, they do the minimum, not out of laziness exactly, but out of a dispiriting sense that nothing they do will matter. Daily life is not so much lived as endured.
deficient.vignette:
The application has been sitting on his desktop for six weeks. It is half-completed. Every morning he opens it, reads the next question, and closes it. The deadline is tomorrow. He stares at the screen and feels nothing—not anxiety, not determination, not even disappointment. Just a vast, gray indifference that he has begun to mistake for acceptance.

───────────────────────────────────

Inner Presence (Psychic).** The soul’s heroic spark has gone out, leaving an inertia that cannot begin or sustain anything. The lesson is that the will to engage is the soul’s signature in the world, and that the rediscovery of even a small purpose can rekindle the whole.

attributes.title: Bhaya (भय) — Fear, Anxiety, Vulnerability
attributes.aspect: Bhaya is the contraction of consciousness in the face of perceived threat. It is the organism’s oldest intelligence—the capacity to recognize danger and mobilize accordingly. But Bhaya is not only primal. It extends from the visceral panic of the body to the cognitive anxiety of the planner and the existential dread of the philosopher. In the classical framework, it reveals the fragility of egoic identity: we fear because we identify with something that can be lost.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital (the tremor of fear) · Ontic mode: State (durable disposition / Sthāyibhāva) · Valence: Faculty — wholesome caution, afflictive as pervasive dread · Guṇa: Rajas–Tamas · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: Dispositional ground · Key relation: Ripens into Bhayānaka Rasa; fed by apprehension and panic.
coordinate.valence: Faculty — wholesome caution, afflictive as pervasive dread
coordinate.key_relation: Ripens into Bhayānaka Rasa; fed by apprehension and panic.

balanced.label: Equilibrium: Fear as Prudent Awareness

facets.overview: In equilibrium, Bhaya is healthy caution married to clarity. Internally, there is an alertness that sharpens perception without distorting it. The person recognizes real risks and responds proportionately. Thoughts are evaluative but not catastrophic. The body is watchful—a heightened readiness that enhances rather than overwhelms. There is even, in the classical view, a moral dimension: the person who fears violating dharma acts with conscience and care. In relationships, equilibrium Bhaya appears as appropriate vulnerability—the willingness to acknowledge what is at stake without being paralyzed by it. At work, it is the quality that produces thorough risk assessment, careful planning, and the prudence to build margins of safety. In daily life, it ensures that the person locks the door, looks both ways, saves for the uncertain future—without being controlled by the possibility of catastrophe.

balanced.text: In equilibrium, Bhaya is healthy caution married to clarity. Internally, there is an alertness that sharpens perception without distorting it. The person recognizes real risks and responds proportionately. Thoughts are evaluative but not catastrophic. The body is watchful—a heightened readiness that enhances rather than overwhelms. There is even, in the classical view, a moral dimension: the person who fears violating dharma acts with conscience and care. In relationships, equilibrium Bhaya appears as appropriate vulnerability—the willingness to acknowledge what is at stake without being paralyzed by it. At work, it is the quality that produces thorough risk assessment, careful planning, and the prudence to build margins of safety. In daily life, it ensures that the person locks the door, looks both ways, saves for the uncertain future—without being controlled by the possibility of catastrophe.
balanced.vignette:
The biopsy results come back tomorrow. She is afraid. She notices the fear with a certain clarity: the tightness in her stomach, the way her mind keeps rehearsing worst-case scenarios. She lets the thoughts run without chasing them. She calls her sister—not for reassurance, but for company. They talk about the garden, about the neighbor’s new dog, about nothing in particular. The fear is still there when she hangs up. But she is not alone with it, and that makes it bearable.

Inner Presence (Psychic).** At the soul level, balanced Bhaya is the healthy reverence and caution that keeps the soul awake to real danger and humble before the vast. The lesson is that some fear is wisdom — the appropriate trembling before what is genuinely powerful or sacred, which protects and orients.

excess.label: Excess: Fear as Prison

facets.overview: Excessive Bhaya—rājasic fear—is anxiety unmoored from its object. Internally, the mind generates threats faster than it can evaluate them. Every email might contain bad news. Every unfamiliar sensation in the body might be the beginning of something terrible. Thoughts spiral into worst-case architectures of extraordinary complexity. The body is locked in sympathetic overdrive: elevated heart rate, shallow breathing, a persistent knot in the stomach that never fully unties. In relationships, excess Bhaya produces controlling behavior born from the terror of loss. The person micro-manages their partner’s safety, their children’s activities, their own exposure to any situation that cannot be fully predicted. At work, they become the bottleneck—unable to delegate because trust requires a tolerance for uncertainty that they do not possess. Daily life shrinks to the size of what can be controlled, and even that small domain is surveilled with exhausting vigilance.

excess.text: Excessive Bhaya—rājasic fear—is anxiety unmoored from its object. Internally, the mind generates threats faster than it can evaluate them. Every email might contain bad news. Every unfamiliar sensation in the body might be the beginning of something terrible. Thoughts spiral into worst-case architectures of extraordinary complexity. The body is locked in sympathetic overdrive: elevated heart rate, shallow breathing, a persistent knot in the stomach that never fully unties. In relationships, excess Bhaya produces controlling behavior born from the terror of loss. The person micro-manages their partner’s safety, their children’s activities, their own exposure to any situation that cannot be fully predicted. At work, they become the bottleneck—unable to delegate because trust requires a tolerance for uncertainty that they do not possess. Daily life shrinks to the size of what can be controlled, and even that small domain is surveilled with exhausting vigilance.
excess.vignette:
He checks the locks three times before bed. He has checked the stove twice. He sets two alarms because one might fail. Lying in the dark, he reviews tomorrow’s schedule for potential hazards: the highway merge, the meeting with the new client, the weather forecast. His wife’s breathing beside him is steady and slow. She is asleep. He envies this with an intensity that is itself a kind of fear—the fear that he will never be someone who simply rests.

Inner Presence (Psychic).** The soul has been colonized by fear, a free-floating dread that colors everything and shrinks life to the size of its anxieties. The lesson is that most of what is feared never comes, and that courage is not the absence of fear but the refusal to be ruled by it.

deficient.label: Deficiency: The Dangerous Absence of Caution

facets.overview: Deficient Bhaya—the tāmasic or excessively rājasic suppression of fear—produces recklessness that masquerades as courage. Internally, the person has severed connection with the body’s warning signals. There is a numbness to risk that is not true bravery but a kind of dissociation. Thoughts do not engage with consequences because consequences feel abstract, remote, irrelevant. In relationships, deficient Bhaya appears as emotional carelessness—the person who makes promises without considering whether they can keep them, who takes risks with other people’s wellbeing as readily as with their own. At work, it produces the high-risk decision-maker who is celebrated until the inevitable crash. Daily life has a quality of invulnerability that is, in fact, a refusal to acknowledge the truth that all living things are vulnerable.

deficient.text: Deficient Bhaya—the tāmasic or excessively rājasic suppression of fear—produces recklessness that masquerades as courage. Internally, the person has severed connection with the body’s warning signals. There is a numbness to risk that is not true bravery but a kind of dissociation. Thoughts do not engage with consequences because consequences feel abstract, remote, irrelevant. In relationships, deficient Bhaya appears as emotional carelessness—the person who makes promises without considering whether they can keep them, who takes risks with other people’s wellbeing as readily as with their own. At work, it produces the high-risk decision-maker who is celebrated until the inevitable crash. Daily life has a quality of invulnerability that is, in fact, a refusal to acknowledge the truth that all living things are vulnerable.
deficient.vignette:
He has not seen a doctor in seven years. His friends have mentioned it. His sister mentioned it pointedly after their father’s heart attack. He shrugs it off. He feels fine. He has always felt fine. The fact that “fine” includes the persistent chest tightness he has been ignoring for six months does not enter his calculations, because he has arranged his consciousness so that certain information simply does not arrive.

───────────────────────────────────

Inner Presence (Psychic).** The soul has lost the healthy fear that protects, growing reckless before genuine danger or blind to consequence. The lesson is that a measure of fear is the soul’s friend, and that fearlessness without wisdom is not courage but folly.

attributes.title: Jugupsā (जुगुप्सा) — Disgust, Moral Aversion, Repulsion
attributes.aspect: Jugupsā is the emotional recoil from what threatens bodily or moral integrity. It is the force that makes us turn away from decay, cruelty, and corruption—both physical and ethical. In its biological form, it protects against contamination. In its moral form, it serves as the foundation of ethical discernment, the visceral refusal to participate in what degrades. The classical tradition recognizes it as the root of both purity and prejudice, depending on whether it is guided by wisdom or by ignorance.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Physical–Vital (visceral revulsion) · Ontic mode: State (durable disposition / Sthāyibhāva) · Valence: Faculty — wholesome discernment, contempt when chronic · Guṇa: Sattva (as discernment)–Tamas (as contempt) · Scale: Individual, with a moral (Ādhibhautika) dimension · Causal status: Dispositional ground · Key relation: Ripens into Bībhatsa Rasa; fed by aversion.
coordinate.valence: Faculty — wholesome discernment, contempt when chronic
coordinate.key_relation: Ripens into Bībhatsa Rasa; fed by aversion.

balanced.label: Equilibrium: Discernment as Moral Clarity

facets.overview: In equilibrium, Jugupsā is viveka—discriminative wisdom. Internally, there is a clear sense of what aligns with one’s values and what does not. The feeling is not rage but recoil: a quiet, firm turning away from what is harmful or degrading. The body registers it as a subtle contraction—a pulling back of the hand, a narrowing of the eyes—that signals “not this.” In relationships, equilibrium Jugupsā appears as healthy boundary-setting—the ability to say “This is not acceptable to me” without hatred, and to withdraw from toxic situations without needing to destroy them. At work, it is the ethical backbone: the person who will not falsify data, who walks away from the deal that requires compromised values. In daily life, it manifests as integrity—the alignment of conduct with conviction.

balanced.text: In equilibrium, Jugupsā is viveka—discriminative wisdom. Internally, there is a clear sense of what aligns with one’s values and what does not. The feeling is not rage but recoil: a quiet, firm turning away from what is harmful or degrading. The body registers it as a subtle contraction—a pulling back of the hand, a narrowing of the eyes—that signals “not this.” In relationships, equilibrium Jugupsā appears as healthy boundary-setting—the ability to say “This is not acceptable to me” without hatred, and to withdraw from toxic situations without needing to destroy them. At work, it is the ethical backbone: the person who will not falsify data, who walks away from the deal that requires compromised values. In daily life, it manifests as integrity—the alignment of conduct with conviction.
balanced.vignette:
The contract is lucrative. The client is eager. But something in the arrangement does not sit right—a vagueness about the sourcing, an eagerness to skip the compliance review. She cannot point to a specific violation, but her stomach knows. She declines. The client is surprised. Her colleagues are puzzled. She does not have a compelling argument; she has something older and more reliable—a visceral refusal to participate in something that feels wrong.

Inner Presence (Psychic).** At the soul level, balanced Jugupsā is the soul’s healthy discernment of what truly harms or degrades — the refined revulsion that turns one from corruption toward purity. The lesson is that aversion, rightly tuned, is a moral compass that protects the soul’s integrity.

excess.label: Excess: Disgust as Contempt and Rigidity

facets.overview: Excessive Jugupsā—rājasic aversion—becomes judgmentalism and contempt. Internally, the person carries a running commentary of moral superiority: other people’s choices are disgusting, their habits are offensive, their values are corrupt. The emotional register narrows to a persistent state of disapproval. The body is held tight, as though perpetually bracing against contamination. In relationships, excess Jugupsā creates an atmosphere of conditional acceptance. Others feel perpetually evaluated, perpetually at risk of failing to meet the standard. At work, the person becomes the moral inquisitor—useful in small doses, unbearable in sustained proximity. Daily life is organized around avoidance: of certain foods, certain people, certain neighborhoods, certain ideas—a world that shrinks as the list of the intolerable grows.

excess.text: Excessive Jugupsā—rājasic aversion—becomes judgmentalism and contempt. Internally, the person carries a running commentary of moral superiority: other people’s choices are disgusting, their habits are offensive, their values are corrupt. The emotional register narrows to a persistent state of disapproval. The body is held tight, as though perpetually bracing against contamination. In relationships, excess Jugupsā creates an atmosphere of conditional acceptance. Others feel perpetually evaluated, perpetually at risk of failing to meet the standard. At work, the person becomes the moral inquisitor—useful in small doses, unbearable in sustained proximity. Daily life is organized around avoidance: of certain foods, certain people, certain neighborhoods, certain ideas—a world that shrinks as the list of the intolerable grows.
excess.vignette:
He has opinions about the way his neighbor parks, the way his colleague chews, the way the barista’s tattoo is slightly crooked. He has opinions about the music at the grocery store and the grammar in the company newsletter. Each opinion arrives with a small jolt of revulsion, a tightening of the mouth, a mental note filed under “things that are wrong with the world.” He is not unhappy, exactly. He is fastidious. And he cannot understand why people find him exhausting.

Inner Presence (Psychic).** The soul’s discernment has curdled into contempt, finding fault and impurity everywhere and recoiling from life itself. The lesson is that disgust turned chronic becomes its own poison, walling the soul off in a fastidious isolation that mistakes revulsion for refinement.

deficient.label: Deficiency: The Loss of Moral Compass

facets.overview: Deficient Jugupsā—the tāmasic state—produces a troubling moral flatness. Internally, the person has lost the capacity to feel revulsion at what ought to revolt. Ethical boundaries become negotiable, then invisible. The body does not register the warning signals that something is off. There is a numbness to degradation—one’s own and others’—that looks, from the outside, like extreme tolerance but is actually a failure of discrimination. In relationships, deficient Jugupsā manifests as the inability to leave situations that are harmful—staying in abusive dynamics, tolerating exploitation, confusing acceptance with passivity. At work, it is the person who goes along with unethical practices because “everyone does it” and has lost the capacity to feel that this is not sufficient justification. Daily life drifts toward entropy—standards of self-care, integrity, and aspiration gradually eroding without the person noticing.

deficient.text: Deficient Jugupsā—the tāmasic state—produces a troubling moral flatness. Internally, the person has lost the capacity to feel revulsion at what ought to revolt. Ethical boundaries become negotiable, then invisible. The body does not register the warning signals that something is off. There is a numbness to degradation—one’s own and others’—that looks, from the outside, like extreme tolerance but is actually a failure of discrimination. In relationships, deficient Jugupsā manifests as the inability to leave situations that are harmful—staying in abusive dynamics, tolerating exploitation, confusing acceptance with passivity. At work, it is the person who goes along with unethical practices because “everyone does it” and has lost the capacity to feel that this is not sufficient justification. Daily life drifts toward entropy—standards of self-care, integrity, and aspiration gradually eroding without the person noticing.
deficient.vignette:
The joke at the dinner party is cruel—aimed at someone not present, someone vulnerable. She laughs with the others. Driving home, she replays the moment and searches for the discomfort she thinks she should have felt. It is not there. A year ago, it would have been. She wonders, briefly, where it went. Then the thought passes, and she turns on the radio.

───────────────────────────────────

Inner Presence (Psychic).** The soul has lost its healthy aversion, no longer recoiling from what genuinely degrades, and so drifts toward the coarse without resistance. The lesson is that a clean revulsion at real wrong is part of the soul’s protection, and its absence is not tolerance but numbness.

attributes.title: Vismaya (विस्मय) — Wonder, Astonishment, Awe
attributes.aspect: Vismaya is the opening of consciousness when reality exceeds its current frame. It is the emotion of the threshold—the moment when the known dissolves and something unexpected, vast, or incomprehensible appears. In children, it is the default setting. In adults, it is the door that keeps closing and must be deliberately reopened. The classical tradition regards Vismaya as the seed of both scientific inquiry and spiritual vision—the state in which the ego’s certainties crack open to admit the possibility of the truly Real.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental–Psychic (the opening to mystery) · Ontic mode: State (durable disposition / Sthāyibhāva) · Valence: Faculty — wholesome awe, sensation-seeking in excess · Guṇa: Sattva-dominant · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: Dispositional ground · Key relation: Ripens into Adbhuta Rasa; fed by astonishment.
coordinate.valence: Faculty — wholesome awe, sensation-seeking in excess
coordinate.key_relation: Ripens into Adbhuta Rasa; fed by astonishment.

balanced.label: Equilibrium: Wonder as Receptive Intelligence

facets.overview: In equilibrium, Vismaya is a quality of openness that informs perception without overwhelming it. Internally, there is a spaciousness—a willingness to be surprised, to not know, to encounter the world as though it had something new to show you. Thoughts move with curiosity rather than conclusion. The body opens: eyes widen, breathing deepens, the chest expands. There is a quality of presence—the person is fully here, because wonder only exists in the present tense. In relationships, equilibrium Vismaya appears as genuine interest in the other—the capacity to be surprised by someone you have known for decades. At work, it fuels innovation and creative problem-solving: the mind that can wonder “what if” without immediately dismissing the unfamiliar. In daily life, it is the practice of attention that transforms the ordinary—the walk to the train station, the pattern of rain on glass—into occasions for quiet astonishment.

balanced.text: In equilibrium, Vismaya is a quality of openness that informs perception without overwhelming it. Internally, there is a spaciousness—a willingness to be surprised, to not know, to encounter the world as though it had something new to show you. Thoughts move with curiosity rather than conclusion. The body opens: eyes widen, breathing deepens, the chest expands. There is a quality of presence—the person is fully here, because wonder only exists in the present tense. In relationships, equilibrium Vismaya appears as genuine interest in the other—the capacity to be surprised by someone you have known for decades. At work, it fuels innovation and creative problem-solving: the mind that can wonder “what if” without immediately dismissing the unfamiliar. In daily life, it is the practice of attention that transforms the ordinary—the walk to the train station, the pattern of rain on glass—into occasions for quiet astonishment.
balanced.vignette:
He is fifty-three and has walked this trail a hundred times. Today the fog sits in the valley like a lake of white, and the hills rise above it like islands. He stops. He has seen fog before. He has seen valleys. But this—this particular arrangement of moisture and light and silence—is unrepeatable, and he knows it. He stands there for several minutes, doing nothing productive, accomplishing nothing measurable, and returns home slightly different than when he left.

Inner Presence (Psychic).** At the soul level, balanced Vismaya is the soul’s openness to be astonished — the wonder that keeps it humble, curious, and alive to the mystery in the ordinary. The lesson is that wonder is the beginning of wisdom; the soul that can still be amazed has not closed itself to the sacred.

excess.label: Excess: Wonder as Ungrounded Intoxication

facets.overview: Excessive Vismaya—rājasic wonder—becomes a kind of spiritual or intellectual intoxication that avoids the demands of ordinary life. Internally, the person is perpetually dazzled—chasing peak experiences, consuming novelty, seeking the extraordinary while neglecting the essential. Thoughts are scattered and grandiose: everything is connected, everything is meaningful, everything is a sign. The body may feel floaty, ungrounded—disconnected from the practical reality of gravity and bills and other people’s needs. In relationships, excess Vismaya produces the partner who is always elsewhere—entranced by a new idea, a new teacher, a new spiritual framework—while the dishes pile up. At work, it is the visionary who cannot execute, the dreamer who starts projects with evangelical fervor and abandons them when the wonder fades into the hard labor of follow-through. Daily life becomes a series of enthusiasms with no connective tissue.

excess.text: Excessive Vismaya—rājasic wonder—becomes a kind of spiritual or intellectual intoxication that avoids the demands of ordinary life. Internally, the person is perpetually dazzled—chasing peak experiences, consuming novelty, seeking the extraordinary while neglecting the essential. Thoughts are scattered and grandiose: everything is connected, everything is meaningful, everything is a sign. The body may feel floaty, ungrounded—disconnected from the practical reality of gravity and bills and other people’s needs. In relationships, excess Vismaya produces the partner who is always elsewhere—entranced by a new idea, a new teacher, a new spiritual framework—while the dishes pile up. At work, it is the visionary who cannot execute, the dreamer who starts projects with evangelical fervor and abandons them when the wonder fades into the hard labor of follow-through. Daily life becomes a series of enthusiasms with no connective tissue.
excess.vignette:
She has started her fourth meditation practice this year. Each one was, at the beginning, a revelation—this is the one, this is the path, this teacher truly understands. Then the novelty wore off, and the practice became practice—repetitive, demanding, ordinary—and she moved on. Her altar has accumulated the artifacts of abandoned devotions: a mala, a singing bowl, a book of kōans she has not opened since March. The wonder was real. The commitment was not.

Inner Presence (Psychic).** The soul is swept away by every marvel, chasing astonishment for its own sake and losing its ground in a constant search for the extraordinary. The lesson is that the deepest astonishment is found not in the spectacular but in the depth of the simple.

deficient.label: Deficiency: The Closed Mind

facets.overview: Deficient Vismaya—the tāmasic state—produces a cynicism that masquerades as maturity. Internally, the person has decided that they have seen it all, that nothing new is possible, that the world is exactly as dull as it appears. Thoughts are reductive: explanations that flatten mystery into mechanism, interpretations that refuse enchantment. The body mirrors this closure—narrow gaze, shallow breathing, a postural rigidity that says “I am not available to be moved.” In relationships, deficient Vismaya manifests as a lack of curiosity about the inner life of others. The person has categorized everyone and everything, and new information is bent to fit existing categories rather than allowed to challenge them. At work, it produces the person who has done things this way for twenty years and cannot conceive of an alternative. Daily life is predictable, controlled, and—in the deepest sense—uninhabited. The lights are on, but no one is looking out the windows.

deficient.text: Deficient Vismaya—the tāmasic state—produces a cynicism that masquerades as maturity. Internally, the person has decided that they have seen it all, that nothing new is possible, that the world is exactly as dull as it appears. Thoughts are reductive: explanations that flatten mystery into mechanism, interpretations that refuse enchantment. The body mirrors this closure—narrow gaze, shallow breathing, a postural rigidity that says “I am not available to be moved.” In relationships, deficient Vismaya manifests as a lack of curiosity about the inner life of others. The person has categorized everyone and everything, and new information is bent to fit existing categories rather than allowed to challenge them. At work, it produces the person who has done things this way for twenty years and cannot conceive of an alternative. Daily life is predictable, controlled, and—in the deepest sense—uninhabited. The lights are on, but no one is looking out the windows.
deficient.vignette:
His grandson is showing him something on the phone—a video of the James Webb Space Telescope images, the deep field, galaxies layered like sediment in an ocean of time. “Look, Grandpa. That light has been traveling for thirteen billion years.” He glances at the screen. “Very nice,” he says, and returns to the crossword. The child’s wonder meets no surface in him from which to reflect. It enters and falls, like a stone into sand.

───────────────────────────────────

Inner Presence (Psychic).** The soul has gone jaded; nothing surprises or moves it, and the world has flattened into the merely familiar. The lesson is that the loss of wonder is a kind of spiritual death, and that to recover the capacity for awe is to recover the soul’s aliveness.

attributes.title: Śama (शम) — Tranquility, Equanimity, Peace
attributes.aspect: Śama is the ground of being—the abiding stillness beneath all emotional weather. Championed by Abhinavagupta as the ninth and foundational Sthāyībhāva, it is not the absence of feeling but the presence of a witnessing awareness that allows all other feelings to arise and dissolve without disturbance. It is the rasa of the sage, the Sthita-prajña of the Gītā, the condition in which consciousness rests in its own nature rather than being swept along by its contents.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Psychic (the still peace beneath all feeling) · Ontic mode: State — the foundational Sthāyibhāva (Abhinavagupta’s ninth) · Valence: Virtue — the equanimity to be uncovered (not the withdrawal that counterfeits it) · Guṇa: Sattva, tending beyond the guṇas · Scale: Individual, opening to the transcendent · Causal status: Ground (the substratum to which the other states return) · Key relation: Ripens into Śānta Rasa; the still ground of the other eight sthāyibhāvas.
coordinate.seat: Psychic (the still peace beneath all feeling)
coordinate.valence: Virtue — the equanimity to be uncovered (not the withdrawal that counterfeits it)
coordinate.causal_status: Ground (the substratum to which the other states return)
coordinate.key_relation: Ripens into Śānta Rasa; the still ground of the other eight sthāyibhāvas.

balanced.label: Equilibrium: Peace as Integration

facets.overview: In equilibrium, Śama is not manufactured calm but natural spaciousness. Internally, there is a quality of “enoughness”—a sense that nothing is missing, that the present moment is complete as it is. Thoughts arise and pass without demanding pursuit. Emotions move through the field of awareness like weather across a sky that is itself unmoved. The body is relaxed without being limp, alert without being tense—a state of restful readiness. In relationships, equilibrium Śama appears as the capacity for non-reactive presence—the person who can sit with another’s pain without trying to fix it, who can receive criticism without collapsing, who can hold space for difficulty without becoming difficulty. At work, it is the quality of leadership that remains steady when everything else is in flux. In daily life, there is an unhurried quality—a willingness to be with what is, rather than compulsively arranging what should be.

balanced.text: In equilibrium, Śama is not manufactured calm but natural spaciousness. Internally, there is a quality of “enoughness”—a sense that nothing is missing, that the present moment is complete as it is. Thoughts arise and pass without demanding pursuit. Emotions move through the field of awareness like weather across a sky that is itself unmoved. The body is relaxed without being limp, alert without being tense—a state of restful readiness. In relationships, equilibrium Śama appears as the capacity for non-reactive presence—the person who can sit with another’s pain without trying to fix it, who can receive criticism without collapsing, who can hold space for difficulty without becoming difficulty. At work, it is the quality of leadership that remains steady when everything else is in flux. In daily life, there is an unhurried quality—a willingness to be with what is, rather than compulsively arranging what should be.
balanced.vignette:
The news is bad. The restructuring will eliminate her position. She hears this, and there is a moment—a familiar tightening in the chest, a flash of fear, the beginning of a narrative about unfairness. She notices these arrivals the way one notices birds landing on a wire: they are there, and then they will not be there. She takes a breath. She asks the necessary questions. She does not perform calmness; she inhabits it. Later, walking to her car, she will feel the grief arrive, and she will let it come. But it will come to someone who is not made of grief, and that makes all the difference.

Inner Presence (Psychic).** At the soul level, balanced Śama is the deep tranquility that is the ground of all the other states — the still peace that is not produced but uncovered. The lesson is that serenity is the soul’s native condition beneath its weather, and that from this stillness all right feeling and action arise.

excess.label: Excess: Peace as Spiritual Bypassing

facets.overview: Excessive Śama—what might paradoxically be called rājasic tranquility—is the weaponization of calm. Internally, the person has repressed rather than integrated the other eight bhāvas. They appear serene, but the serenity is achieved through avoidance rather than through the honest encounter with anger, grief, fear, and desire that genuine Śama requires. There is a subtle smugness to it: “I am beyond all that.” The body may look relaxed, but there is a rigidity to the relaxation—a held quality that reveals the effort beneath the surface. In relationships, excess Śama produces the infuriatingly unflappable partner who meets every conflict with detached equanimity and thereby denies the other person’s emotional reality. At work, it is the leader whose apparent calm is actually disengagement—who does not fight for what matters because fighting would disturb the performance of peace. Daily life becomes a spiritual performance: the meditation cushion, the measured tone, the carefully curated absence of urgency that mistakes itself for enlightenment.

excess.text: Excessive Śama—what might paradoxically be called rājasic tranquility—is the weaponization of calm. Internally, the person has repressed rather than integrated the other eight bhāvas. They appear serene, but the serenity is achieved through avoidance rather than through the honest encounter with anger, grief, fear, and desire that genuine Śama requires. There is a subtle smugness to it: “I am beyond all that.” The body may look relaxed, but there is a rigidity to the relaxation—a held quality that reveals the effort beneath the surface. In relationships, excess Śama produces the infuriatingly unflappable partner who meets every conflict with detached equanimity and thereby denies the other person’s emotional reality. At work, it is the leader whose apparent calm is actually disengagement—who does not fight for what matters because fighting would disturb the performance of peace. Daily life becomes a spiritual performance: the meditation cushion, the measured tone, the carefully curated absence of urgency that mistakes itself for enlightenment.
excess.vignette:
“**You never get upset about anything,” his wife says, and it is not a compliment. She has been trying to have this conversation for weeks. She is crying. He is sitting very still, nodding, breathing evenly, his face a mask of compassionate attention. He has read the books. He knows the framework. He is meeting her distress with “spacious presence.” What he is not doing is feeling anything. And she can tell. The empathy is structural, not emotional. The peace is a wall.

Inner Presence (Psychic).** The soul’s peace has slipped into withdrawal — a detachment that uses ‘serenity’ to avoid the legitimate claims and passions of life. The lesson is that true peace is not numbness or escape, and that a calm bought by disengagement is a counterfeit of the real stillness.

deficient.label: Deficiency: The Tyranny of Restlessness

facets.overview: Deficient Śama—the tāmasic or chronically rājasic absence of inner peace—is perhaps the defining condition of contemporary life. Internally, the person has no access to stillness. The mind runs constantly—commentary, planning, rumination, evaluation—and there is no off switch. Silence is intolerable; it must be filled with noise, with screens, with stimulation. The body mirrors this perpetual motion: fidgeting, scrolling, eating without hunger, consuming without satisfaction. In relationships, deficient Śama produces the person who cannot sit still long enough to listen, who is always reaching for the phone during conversation, who fills every pause with speech because silence makes them anxious. At work, they are perpetually busy but rarely productive in the deep sense—confusing activity with accomplishment. Daily life is a blur of stimulation: the podcast during the commute, the news during breakfast, the scroll before sleep. The person has not been genuinely quiet—internally, truly quiet—in so long that they have forgotten it is possible.

deficient.text: Deficient Śama—the tāmasic or chronically rājasic absence of inner peace—is perhaps the defining condition of contemporary life. Internally, the person has no access to stillness. The mind runs constantly—commentary, planning, rumination, evaluation—and there is no off switch. Silence is intolerable; it must be filled with noise, with screens, with stimulation. The body mirrors this perpetual motion: fidgeting, scrolling, eating without hunger, consuming without satisfaction. In relationships, deficient Śama produces the person who cannot sit still long enough to listen, who is always reaching for the phone during conversation, who fills every pause with speech because silence makes them anxious. At work, they are perpetually busy but rarely productive in the deep sense—confusing activity with accomplishment. Daily life is a blur of stimulation: the podcast during the commute, the news during breakfast, the scroll before sleep. The person has not been genuinely quiet—internally, truly quiet—in so long that they have forgotten it is possible.
deficient.vignette:
He reaches for the phone before his eyes are fully open. There are no urgent messages, but the act of checking has become the first movement of consciousness each morning—before thought, before intention, before the body has decided to inhabit the day. He scrolls through headlines while brushing his teeth. He listens to a podcast in the shower. He queues music for the drive. Somewhere beneath the layers of input, there is a person. He does not know what that person would think or feel in silence. He has not given them the chance to find out.

Inner Presence (Psychic).** The soul has no access to inner peace, driven ceaselessly by agitation with no still center to return to. The lesson is that beneath all turbulence lies an unshakable quiet, and that the whole of practice is, in the end, the recovery of this native serenity.

attributes.title: Nirveda — Dejection / World-Weariness
attributes.aspect: A quiet emptying of investment in the surface of life. In equilibrium it is clean sadness that opens into genuine inquiry; in excess it hardens into nihilism and contempt; in deficiency it is papered over by a brittle, reflexive positivity.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital–Mental · Ontic mode: Wave (transient episode / Vyabhicāribhāva) · Valence: Faculty — a signal of readiness for inquiry; affliction when it hardens into nihilism · Guṇa: Sattva (clean letting-go) / Tamas (despair) · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: Contributory feeder · Key relation: Ripens toward Śānta (dispassion) or, unrefined, into Karuṇā.
coordinate.valence: Faculty — a signal of readiness for inquiry; affliction when it hardens into nihilism
coordinate.key_relation: Ripens toward Śānta (dispassion) or, unrefined, into Karuṇā.

attributes.title: Glāni — Fatigue / Depletion
attributes.aspect: The felt exhaustion of body and spirit after exertion or grief. In measure it is an honest call to rest; in excess it becomes chronic collapse and lifelessness; its absence shows as a driven refusal to acknowledge one’s own limits.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Physical–Vital · Ontic mode: Wave · Valence: Faculty — a depletion signal · Guṇa: Tamas-dominant · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Contributory feeder · Key relation: Dampens the durable emotions; typically follows Śrama.
coordinate.key_relation: Dampens the durable emotions; typically follows Śrama.

attributes.title: Śaṅkā — Apprehension / Suspicion
attributes.aspect: A wary contraction before a suspected threat. In equilibrium it is protective vigilance that scans and then relaxes; in excess it curdles into corrosive, generalized suspicion; in deficiency it is a naive blindness to real danger.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital–Mental · Ontic mode: Wave · Valence: Faculty — protective wariness · Guṇa: Rajas-dominant · Scale: Individual, with interpersonal trigger · Causal status: Contributory feeder · Key relation: Feeds Bhaya, ripening toward Bhayānaka Rasa; peaks as Trāsa.
coordinate.key_relation: Feeds Bhaya, ripening toward Bhayānaka Rasa; peaks as Trāsa.

attributes.title: Asūyā — Envy / Intolerance of Others’ Merit
attributes.aspect: The wound of comparison — resentment at another’s good fortune or worth. In measure it can spur healthy aspiration; in excess it sours affection and hardens into begrudging spite; in deficiency it can mask as a flattering refusal to discern real merit.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital · Ontic mode: Wave · Valence: Faculty tending to affliction (envious comparison) · Guṇa: Rajas–Tamas · Scale: Interpersonal (Ādhibhautika) · Causal status: Contributory feeder · Key relation: Sours Rati and feeds Krodha.
coordinate.key_relation: Sours Rati and feeds Krodha.

attributes.title: Mada — Intoxication / Pride of Indulgence
attributes.aspect: The heady inflation of intoxication, wealth, or success. In small measure it is exuberant vitality; in excess it swells into arrogance that clouds judgment and courts a fall; its lack appears as an inability to enjoy one’s own good fortune.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital–Mental · Ontic mode: Wave · Valence: Faculty tending to affliction (inflation) · Guṇa: Rajas–Tamas · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Contributory feeder · Key relation: Feeds Garva; clouds the durable emotions.
coordinate.key_relation: Feeds Garva; clouds the durable emotions.

attributes.title: Śrama — Weariness of Exertion
attributes.aspect: The honest tiredness that follows real effort. In equilibrium it is a satisfying fatigue that calls for restoration; in excess it is pushed past into exhaustion and burnout; in deficiency it is the restlessness of one who cannot let effort come to rest.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Physical–Vital · Ontic mode: Wave · Valence: Faculty — an exertion signal · Guṇa: Rajas, tipping to Tamas · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Contributory feeder · Key relation: Precedes Glāni and Ālasya.
coordinate.key_relation: Precedes Glāni and Ālasya.

attributes.title: Ālasya — Lethargy / Indolence
attributes.aspect: A heavy disinclination to act. In measure it is restful ease; in excess it sinks into sloth and stagnation that becomes a way of life; in deficiency it is a compulsive inability to be still.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Physical–Vital · Ontic mode: Wave · Valence: Faculty — a rest signal; sloth in excess · Guṇa: Tamas-dominant · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Contributory feeder · Key relation: Dampens Utsāha; feeds the tamasic states.
coordinate.valence: Faculty — a rest signal; sloth in excess
coordinate.key_relation: Dampens Utsāha; feeds the tamasic states.

attributes.title: Dainya — Wretchedness / Self-Pity
attributes.aspect: A collapse of self-worth into helplessness and lowliness. In its cleanest form it can soften into true humility; in excess it becomes corrosive self-abasement and victimhood; in deficiency it stiffens into a pride that cannot bow.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital · Ontic mode: Wave · Valence: Faculty tending to affliction (self-diminishment) · Guṇa: Tamas-dominant · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Contributory feeder · Key relation: Feeds Śoka; opposes Utsāha.
coordinate.key_relation: Feeds Śoka; opposes Utsāha.

attributes.title: Cintā — Anxious Worry / Brooding
attributes.aspect: Future-projected worry that loops over imagined outcomes. In measure it is useful foresight; in excess it becomes corrosive rumination that generates catastrophe; in deficiency it is a heedless refusal to anticipate at all.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental · Ontic mode: Wave · Valence: Faculty — a foresight signal · Guṇa: Rajas-dominant · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Contributory feeder · Key relation: Future-projected; feeds Bhaya and Śoka (paired in the text with Viṣāda).
coordinate.key_relation: Future-projected; feeds Bhaya and Śoka (paired in the text with Viṣāda).

attributes.title: Moha — Delusion / Infatuation
attributes.aspect: A fog in which the map replaces the territory. In its mildest form it is ordinary uncertainty; in excess it is a clouded judgment that distorts every perception; its opposite is a brittle over-certainty that mistakes itself for clarity.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental · Ontic mode: Wave · Valence: Faculty tending to affliction (clouded judgment) · Guṇa: Tamas-dominant · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Contributory feeder / mechanism of concealment · Key relation: Distorts all the durable emotions; dispelled by Vibodha.
coordinate.key_relation: Distorts all the durable emotions; dispelled by Vibodha.

attributes.title: Smṛti — Recollection / Memory
attributes.aspect: The arrival of the relevant past into the present. In equilibrium it is a servant that supplies experience when needed; in excess it becomes rumination that will not release the past; in deficiency it is a forgetful unmooring from one’s own history.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental · Ontic mode: Wave · Valence: Faculty — wholesome recall · Guṇa: Sattva · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Contributory feeder · Key relation: Supplies the durable emotions with their remembered material; its failure is Apasmāra.
coordinate.key_relation: Supplies the durable emotions with their remembered material; its failure is Apasmāra.

attributes.title: Dhṛti — Fortitude / Steadfastness
attributes.aspect: A steady holding-firm through difficulty and a settled contentment with what is. In equilibrium it is the ballast that keeps the emotional weather from capsizing; in excess it stiffens into stubbornness; in deficiency it is a resolve that crumbles at the first obstacle.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental–Vital · Ontic mode: Wave · Valence: Faculty — wholesome steadiness · Guṇa: Sattva-dominant · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Contributory feeder (stabilizing) · Key relation: Steadies the durable emotions; allied to Buddhi and Mati.
coordinate.key_relation: Steadies the durable emotions; allied to Buddhi and Mati.

attributes.title: Vrīḍā — Shame / Bashfulness / Modesty
attributes.aspect: The contraction of being seen. In measure it is healthy conscience and modesty; in excess it becomes toxic self-erasure and hiding; in deficiency it is a shamelessness that has lost the inner check.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital · Ontic mode: Wave · Valence: Faculty — a conscience signal; toxic as self-erasure · Guṇa: Tamas-tinged (contraction) · Scale: Interpersonal (Ādhibhautika) · Causal status: Contributory feeder · Key relation: Modulates Rati, Bhaya, and Śoka.
coordinate.valence: Faculty — a conscience signal; toxic as self-erasure
coordinate.key_relation: Modulates Rati, Bhaya, and Śoka.

attributes.title: Capalatā — Fickleness / Impulsiveness
attributes.aspect: A restless inability to hold still. In measure it is quick responsiveness and spontaneity; in excess it is scattered volatility that starts everything and finishes nothing; its lack is a rigid immovability.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital · Ontic mode: Wave · Valence: Faculty — responsiveness; volatility in excess · Guṇa: Rajas-dominant · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Contributory feeder · Key relation: Destabilizes the durable emotions (paired in the text with Āvega).
coordinate.key_relation: Destabilizes the durable emotions (paired in the text with Āvega).

attributes.title: Harṣa — Joy / Gladness
attributes.aspect: The sunbreak of joy — an unbidden uprising of gladness. In equilibrium it is received and shared freely; in excess it inflates into a manic elation that cannot land; in deficiency it is a joyless flatness that cannot be gladdened.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital · Ontic mode: Wave · Valence: Faculty — a wholesome uprising of gladness · Guṇa: Sattva–Rajas · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Contributory feeder · Key relation: Nourishes Rati and Hāsa (toward Śṛṅgāra and Hāsya).
coordinate.valence: Faculty — a wholesome uprising of gladness
coordinate.key_relation: Nourishes Rati and Hāsa (toward Śṛṅgāra and Hāsya).

attributes.title: Āvega — Agitation / Shock / Sudden Surge
attributes.aspect: A sudden surge or shock that seizes the system. In emergency it is mobilizing; in excess it disorganizes into overwhelm and reactivity; its absence is a sluggish failure to respond when response is called for.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital · Ontic mode: Wave · Valence: Faculty — a mobilizing surge · Guṇa: Rajas-dominant · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Contributory feeder · Key relation: A sudden wave that colors many durable emotions.
coordinate.key_relation: A sudden wave that colors many durable emotions.

attributes.title: Jaḍatā — Stupor / Stunned Inertia
attributes.aspect: The paralysis of overwhelm — a protective freeze that suspends response. In measure it buys a moment before an unbearable stimulus; in excess it locks into numb immobility; in deficiency it is an inability to pause even when flooded.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Physical–Mental · Ontic mode: Wave · Valence: Faculty — a protective freeze · Guṇa: Tamas-dominant · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Contributory feeder · Key relation: Suspends the durable emotions.
coordinate.key_relation: Suspends the durable emotions.

attributes.title: Garva — Arrogance / Conceit
attributes.aspect: The fortress of self-regard. In its faint form it is healthy self-respect; in excess it inflates into pride that distorts perception and repels connection; in deficiency it collapses into self-effacement.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital–Mental · Ontic mode: Wave · Valence: Faculty tending to affliction (inflated self-regard) · Guṇa: Rajas–Tamas · Scale: Individual, with interpersonal display · Causal status: Contributory feeder · Key relation: Distorts the durable emotions; kin to Mada.
coordinate.key_relation: Distorts the durable emotions; kin to Mada.

attributes.title: Viṣāda — Despair / Grief-Collapse
attributes.aspect: Present-tense despair — a sinking heaviness when effort fails. Deeper and more static than anxious Cintā; in excess it becomes an immobilizing hopelessness; in deficiency it is a manic denial that cannot admit defeat.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital–Mental · Ontic mode: Wave · Valence: Faculty tending to affliction (despair) · Guṇa: Tamas-dominant · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Contributory feeder · Key relation: Feeds Śoka (toward Karuṇā); present-tense counterpart to Cintā.
coordinate.key_relation: Feeds Śoka (toward Karuṇā); present-tense counterpart to Cintā.

attributes.title: Autsukya — Longing / Eager Yearning
attributes.aspect: The sweet ache of longing — the soul’s memory of a wholeness it aches toward. In measure it is aspiration and anticipation; in excess it becomes restless craving that cannot rest in the present; in deficiency it is a flat absence of desire.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital · Ontic mode: Wave · Valence: Faculty — aspiration; craving in excess · Guṇa: Rajas–Sattva · Scale: Individual, with interpersonal object · Causal status: Contributory feeder · Key relation: Feeds Rati (toward Śṛṅgāra Rasa).
coordinate.key_relation: Feeds Rati (toward Śṛṅgāra Rasa).

attributes.title: Nidrā — Drowsiness / Sleepiness
attributes.aspect: The descent toward sleep. In measure it is natural restoration answering the body’s need; in excess it dulls waking life into perpetual somnolence and avoidance; in deficiency it is a wired inability to let go into rest.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Physical–Vital · Ontic mode: Wave · Valence: Faculty — a restoration signal · Guṇa: Tamas-dominant · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Contributory feeder · Key relation: Dims all the durable emotions; the threshold to Supta.
coordinate.key_relation: Dims all the durable emotions; the threshold to Supta.

attributes.title: Apasmāra — Forgetfulness / Loss of Self-Possession
attributes.aspect: A slipping-away of recollection and presence — a momentary blanking that unmoors the thread of experience. In its ordinary form it is simple absent-mindedness; in excess it is a disorienting loss of continuity; its opposite is an anxious over-holding of every detail.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental · Ontic mode: Wave · Valence: Faculty tending to affliction (the lost thread) · Guṇa: Tamas · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Contributory feeder · Key relation: Interrupts Smṛti; unsettles the durable emotions.
coordinate.valence: Faculty tending to affliction (the lost thread)
coordinate.key_relation: Interrupts Smṛti; unsettles the durable emotions.

attributes.title: Supta — Sleep / Dreaming Slumber
attributes.aspect: The state of being fully asleep — the deep withdrawal the inner weather subsides into. In balance it is the restorative night from which one returns renewed; in excess it becomes escape into oblivion; its lack is a depleting sleeplessness.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Physical–Psychic · Ontic mode: Wave (a transient state of sleep) · Valence: Faculty — natural rest · Guṇa: Tamas · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Contributory feeder · Key relation: The suspension into which the durable emotions rest; ended by Vibodha.
coordinate.key_relation: The suspension into which the durable emotions rest; ended by Vibodha.

attributes.title: Vibodha — Awakening / Return to Alertness
attributes.aspect: The moment the fog lifts — a return to alertness from sleep or confusion, a small awakening. In equilibrium it is fresh clarity; in excess it can become a hypervigilant over-alertness; in deficiency it is a lingering grogginess that never fully arrives.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental–Psychic · Ontic mode: Wave · Valence: Faculty — a wholesome flash of clarity · Guṇa: Sattva-dominant · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Contributory feeder · Key relation: Dispels Moha and Nidrā; lifts the durable emotions toward Śānta.
coordinate.valence: Faculty — a wholesome flash of clarity
coordinate.key_relation: Dispels Moha and Nidrā; lifts the durable emotions toward Śānta.

attributes.title: Amarṣa — Indignation / Restrained Anger
attributes.aspect: The slow burn of indignation at a violated value — the transient wave that rises to fuel durable anger. In measure it is a clean signal that something is wrong; in excess it smoulders into resentment; in deficiency it is a failure to register genuine violation.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital · Ontic mode: Wave (transient episode / Vyabhicāribhāva) · Valence: Faculty — a passing signal of violated value, neither virtue nor vice in itself · Guṇa: Rajas-dominant · Scale: Individual, with interpersonal trigger · Causal status: Contributory cause (a feeder that nourishes a durable state) · Key relation: Part that feeds the whole — rises to fuel Krodha (Sthāyibhāva), ripening toward Raudra Rasa.
coordinate.valence: Faculty — a passing signal of violated value, neither virtue nor vice in itself
coordinate.causal_status: Contributory cause (a feeder that nourishes a durable state)
coordinate.key_relation: Part that feeds the whole — rises to fuel Krodha (Sthāyibhāva), ripening toward Raudra Rasa.

attributes.title: Avahitthā — Dissimulation / Concealment of Feeling
attributes.aspect: The masking of true feeling behind composure. In measure it is social tact and self-possession; in excess it becomes a self-alienating suppression that hides the heart even from oneself; its absence is an unfiltered over-exposure.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital–Mental · Ontic mode: Wave · Valence: Faculty — tact; suppression in excess · Guṇa: Rajas–Tamas · Scale: Interpersonal (Ādhibhautika) · Causal status: Contributory feeder · Key relation: Conceals the durable emotions from view.
coordinate.key_relation: Conceals the durable emotions from view.

attributes.title: Ugratā — Ferocity / Harshness
attributes.aspect: A fierce, harsh intensity. In measure it is the righteous force that meets genuine wrong; in excess it loses restraint and becomes cruelty; in deficiency it is a limpness that cannot summon necessary force.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital · Ontic mode: Wave · Valence: Faculty tending to affliction (harshness) · Guṇa: Rajas–Tamas · Scale: Interpersonal (Ādhibhautika) · Causal status: Contributory feeder · Key relation: Intensifies Krodha toward Raudra Rasa.
coordinate.key_relation: Intensifies Krodha toward Raudra Rasa.

attributes.title: Mati — Resolve / Discernment
attributes.aspect: A settled clarity of judgment — the mind arriving at a firm, reasoned conclusion. In equilibrium it is wise decisiveness; in excess it stiffens into dogmatic certainty; in deficiency it is a chronic inability to reach any conclusion.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental · Ontic mode: Wave · Valence: Faculty — wholesome discernment · Guṇa: Sattva-dominant · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Contributory feeder (stabilizing) · Key relation: Steadies the durable emotions; allied to Buddhi and Dhṛti; resolves Vitarka.
coordinate.key_relation: Steadies the durable emotions; allied to Buddhi and Dhṛti; resolves Vitarka.

attributes.title: Vyādhi — Sickness / Physical Distress
attributes.aspect: The felt distress of illness — bodily suffering that colors mood and thought. In measure it humbles and clarifies what matters; in excess it defeats and consumes the whole field of experience; its denial is a reckless ignoring of the body’s signals.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Physical · Ontic mode: Wave · Valence: Faculty — a distress signal · Guṇa: Tamas-tinged · Scale: Individual, with environmental cause (Ādhibhautika) · Causal status: Contributory feeder · Key relation: Colors Śoka, Bhaya, and Glāni.
coordinate.key_relation: Colors Śoka, Bhaya, and Glāni.

attributes.title: Unmāda — Madness / Frenzy
attributes.aspect: The dissolving of the map — a disordering surge where ordinary coherence gives way. It is afflictive by nature; in its milder register it is a temporary loss of grip under extremity; recovered from, it can even leave a strange clarity in its wake.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental–Psychic · Ontic mode: Wave · Valence: Faculty — an extreme, disordering surge · Guṇa: Rajas–Tamas · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Contributory feeder · Key relation: Overwhelms the durable emotions.
coordinate.key_relation: Overwhelms the durable emotions.

attributes.title: Maraṇa — Death / Dying
attributes.aspect: The felt approach of death — the ultimate transition. As a lived state it reframes and gathers all the durable emotions; met consciously it can open into peace; fled from, it becomes terror; denied, it leaves life strangely unlived.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Physical–Psychic · Ontic mode: Wave (the terminal transition) · Valence: Faculty — the ultimate signal · Guṇa: Tamas, opening beyond the guṇas · Scale: Individual, with cosmic dimension · Causal status: Contributory feeder / terminus · Key relation: Gathers and reframes all the durable emotions (toward Karuṇā and Śānta).
coordinate.key_relation: Gathers and reframes all the durable emotions (toward Karuṇā and Śānta).

attributes.title: Trāsa — Fright / Panic / Terror
attributes.aspect: Sudden overwhelming fright — the acute spike of panic that seizes the whole system. In its function it is the alarm that saves in true danger; in excess it becomes disabling terror; its deficiency is a dangerous fearlessness before real threat.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital · Ontic mode: Wave · Valence: Faculty — acute alarm · Guṇa: Rajas-dominant · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Contributory feeder · Key relation: The acute peak of Śaṅkā; feeds Bhaya toward Bhayānaka Rasa.
coordinate.key_relation: The acute peak of Śaṅkā; feeds Bhaya toward Bhayānaka Rasa.

attributes.title: Vitarka — Deliberation / Reasoning-and-Doubt
attributes.aspect: The back-and-forth of reasoning that weighs alternatives. In measure it is careful deliberation that arrives at judgment; in excess it becomes an endless argumentative churn that cannot rest in any choice; in deficiency it is a heedless failure to weigh at all.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental · Ontic mode: Wave · Valence: Faculty — deliberation; paralysis in excess · Guṇa: Rajas–Sattva · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Contributory feeder · Key relation: Precedes and is resolved by Mati; unchecked, it feeds Cintā.
coordinate.key_relation: Precedes and is resolved by Mati; unchecked, it feeds Cintā.

attributes.title: Chapter 2: Karma Yoga — The Path of the Doer
attributes.aspect: The Doer’s engagement with reality is fundamentally kinesthetic. It is the path of those who think with their hands, who process experience through movement and productivity. The question that drives the Karma Yogi is not “What is true?” or “What do I feel?” but **“What must be done?”** When this faculty is balanced, it produces the experience the Gītā calls *niṣkāma karma*—selfless action that flows naturally, without the friction of personal agenda.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Physical–Vital (action, the will and body) · Ontic mode: Practice — a mārga (mode of engagement through selfless action) · Valence: Cultivated path · Guṇa: Rajas, refined toward Sattva · Scale: Individual, engaging the world (Ādhibhautika) · Causal status: Means (a path to freedom through action) · Key relation: One of the four mārgas (with Jñāna, Rāja, Bhakti) — the path of the Doer.
coordinate.ontic_mode: Practice — a mārga (mode of engagement through selfless action)
coordinate.causal_status: Means (a path to freedom through action)
coordinate.key_relation: One of the four mārgas (with Jñāna, Rāja, Bhakti) — the path of the Doer.

balanced.label: Equilibrium: Skill in Action

facets.somatic: In equilibrium, the body of the Karma Yogi feels like a well-tuned instrument. There is a warmth in the limbs, a readiness in the musculature, a sense that the body is *available* for whatever the day requires. Energy does not spike and crash; it flows in a steady current from morning through evening. The hands feel purposeful. The breath is even and unnoticed. There is a particular quality of physical satisfaction that comes after meaningful labor—not the heaviness of exhaustion but a pleasant tiredness, the feeling of a body that has been *used well*.
facets.emotional: The emotional tone is one of quiet dignity. There is no desperate hunger for recognition, no anxious scanning of the environment for approval. The underlying drive is toward contribution rather than accumulation. When a task is completed, there is a brief moment of satisfaction—then the attention moves naturally to the next thing that needs doing. The emotional current feels like a river: directional, purposeful, but without turbulence.
facets.cognitive: The thoughts of the balanced Doer are characterized by a notable absence of self-referential noise. The inner monologue is task-oriented rather than ego-oriented. Instead of “How will this make me look?” or “What will I get from this?” the dominant thought pattern is simply “This is what needs to be done, and I am here to do it.” The Gītā’s instruction is precise: do your duty, but do not worry yourself with the results; even while working, give up the pride of doership; do your work in a regulated and disciplined manner.
facets.psychic: At the soul level, balanced Karma Yoga is the experience of *yajña*—sacred offering. Every action becomes a form of worship, not in a grandiose sense but in the simple recognition that one’s labor is a participation in something larger than personal gain. The spiritual lesson is that freedom from the karmic cycle (*karma chakra*) comes not from inaction but from action performed without attachment—a paradox that resolves itself in the lived experience of flow.

balanced.text:
Somatic: In equilibrium, the body of the Karma Yogi feels like a well-tuned instrument. There is a warmth in the limbs, a readiness in the musculature, a sense that the body is *available* for whatever the day requires. Energy does not spike and crash; it flows in a steady current from morning through evening. The hands feel purposeful. The breath is even and unnoticed. There is a particular quality of physical satisfaction that comes after meaningful labor—not the heaviness of exhaustion but a pleasant tiredness, the feeling of a body that has been *used well*.

Emotional: The emotional tone is one of quiet dignity. There is no desperate hunger for recognition, no anxious scanning of the environment for approval. The underlying drive is toward contribution rather than accumulation. When a task is completed, there is a brief moment of satisfaction—then the attention moves naturally to the next thing that needs doing. The emotional current feels like a river: directional, purposeful, but without turbulence.

Cognitive: The thoughts of the balanced Doer are characterized by a notable absence of self-referential noise. The inner monologue is task-oriented rather than ego-oriented. Instead of “How will this make me look?” or “What will I get from this?” the dominant thought pattern is simply “This is what needs to be done, and I am here to do it.” The Gītā’s instruction is precise: do your duty, but do not worry yourself with the results; even while working, give up the pride of doership; do your work in a regulated and disciplined manner.

Psychic: At the soul level, balanced Karma Yoga is the experience of *yajña*—sacred offering. Every action becomes a form of worship, not in a grandiose sense but in the simple recognition that one’s labor is a participation in something larger than personal gain. The spiritual lesson is that freedom from the karmic cycle (*karma chakra*) comes not from inaction but from action performed without attachment—a paradox that resolves itself in the lived experience of flow.
balanced.vignette: She rises at 5:45, not because an alarm forces her awake but because there is a rhythm to her mornings that her body has internalized. She makes coffee, packs her daughter’s lunch, and reviews her calendar—all with a kind of choreographic efficiency that has no audience. At work, she leads a contentious project meeting. Someone takes credit for her idea. She notices the flicker of irritation in her chest, acknowledges it, and lets it pass. The idea is in the world now; that is what matters. By evening, she is tired but not depleted. She folds laundry while listening to a podcast, and there is a curious contentment in the folding itself—the warmth of the fabric, the small geometry of making order from chaos. She does not need the day to have been ‘successful’ in any dramatic way. She did the work. That is enough.

excess.label: Excess: The Compulsive Doer

facets.overview: When Karma dominates—when the will to act overrides all other faculties—the Doer tips into what contemporary psychology calls workaholism and what the Gītā recognizes as action driven by *Rajas*, full of passion, born of intense desire and attachment. The “I” becomes magnified: my benefit, my earnings, my reputation. This I-centric attitude, the Gītā warns, gives rise to a society of corruption, mistrust, and mental ill-health.
facets.somatic: The body of the compulsive Doer is a machine running hot. There is a persistent tension in the jaw, the shoulders, the lower back—the musculature of “holding everything together.” Sleep is shallow and dream-dense, often interrupted by middle-of-the-night surges of cortisol disguised as “ideas.” The hands are never still: checking the phone, tapping on the desk, reaching for the next thing to organize. There is a paradoxical fatigue—the body is exhausted, yet it cannot rest because stillness feels like failure.
facets.emotional: The underlying emotion is anxiety masquerading as productivity. If the Doer stops, a wave of guilt or existential dread rushes in. The emotional hunger is for validation through output. People are unconsciously categorized as “helpers” or “obstacles.” Relationships become transactional. There is a growing inability to receive—compliments feel hollow, rest feels selfish, leisure feels like stolen time.
facets.cognitive: The inner monologue is a relentless to-do list. The dominant thought pattern is: “If I don’t do it, nobody will.” or “The world will stop if I stop.” Every interaction is filtered through the lens of efficiency: “Is this conversation productive? Is this meeting necessary?” The concept of *niṣkāma karma* has been inverted; every karma is now loaded with personal stakes.
facets.psychic: At the soul level, excess Karma is the experience of *ahaṃkāra*—the inflation of ego through identification with action. The spiritual lesson being presented (but not yet learned) is that one’s worth is not equivalent to one’s output. The *ātman* is obscured by the smoke of ceaseless doing, and the soul’s call for stillness is mistaken for laziness.

excess.text:

The “I” becomes magnified: my benefit, my earnings, my reputation. This I-centric attitude, the Gītā warns, gives rise to a society of corruption, mistrust, and mental ill-health.

Somatic: The body of the compulsive Doer is a machine running hot. There is a persistent tension in the jaw, the shoulders, the lower back—the musculature of “holding everything together.” Sleep is shallow and dream-dense, often interrupted by middle-of-the-night surges of cortisol disguised as “ideas.” The hands are never still: checking the phone, tapping on the desk, reaching for the next thing to organize. There is a paradoxical fatigue—the body is exhausted, yet it cannot rest because stillness feels like failure.

Emotional: The underlying emotion is anxiety masquerading as productivity. If the Doer stops, a wave of guilt or existential dread rushes in. The emotional hunger is for validation through output. People are unconsciously categorized as “helpers” or “obstacles.” Relationships become transactional. There is a growing inability to receive—compliments feel hollow, rest feels selfish, leisure feels like stolen time.

Cognitive: The inner monologue is a relentless to-do list. The dominant thought pattern is: “If I don’t do it, nobody will.” or “The world will stop if I stop.” Every interaction is filtered through the lens of efficiency: “Is this conversation productive? Is this meeting necessary?” The concept of *niṣkāma karma* has been inverted; every karma is now loaded with personal stakes.

excess.vignette: His phone buzzes at 4:47 AM with a notification he set for himself. Before his feet touch the floor, he is already composing emails in his head. Breakfast is eaten standing up, one eye on the news feed. He arrives at work twenty minutes early, feeling a small, tight pride in beating the traffic. By 10 AM, he has reorganized a colleague’s spreadsheet without being asked, responded to forty-two emails, and volunteered for a committee he has no time for. When his wife texts asking if he’ll make it to their son’s recital, he feels a sharp pang—not of guilt, exactly, but of resentment that someone is asking him to stop. At 11 PM, he is in bed with his laptop, checking metrics. He tells himself he’ll ‘just finish this one thing.’ The one thing takes ninety minutes. He sleeps fitfully, dreaming of spreadsheets.

deficient.label: Deficiency: The Paralyzed Actor

facets.overview: When Karma is suppressed, the individual enters a state the tradition associates with *Tamas*—the darkness and crudeness born of ignorance, the force that binds through recklessness, laziness, and sleep. This is not simple tiredness. It is a fundamental collapse of the will to engage with the world, a condition in which even simple tasks feel like climbing a mountain.
facets.somatic: The body feels leaden, as though gravity has doubled. The limbs are heavy; the act of getting out of bed requires a kind of negotiation with oneself. There is a characteristic slumping in the posture—the shoulders roll forward, the chest caves slightly, the head drops. The musculature of the core feels weak, as though the body’s structural integrity has been compromised from within. Digestion slows. Breath becomes shallow and confined to the upper chest.
facets.emotional: The dominant feeling is not sadness but a kind of existential flatness—a world drained of urgency. There is no driving hunger, no fire of motivation. The emotional landscape resembles a desert: vast, quiet, and parched. Underneath this surface calm, there may be a buried shame—a sense that one should be doing more, that life is passing by—but even the shame lacks the energy to become a motivating force. The person feels like a victim of their schedule rather than the master of it.
facets.cognitive: The thoughts are characterized by a passive, defeatist narrative: “What’s the point?” or “Nothing I do makes a difference.” There is a collapse of agency—the belief that one is acted upon by the world rather than acting within it. Decision-making becomes agonizing because there is no internal compass of purpose to orient the choice. The inner monologue loops without resolution, like a record skipping.
facets.psychic: At the soul level, deficient Karma is the experience of disconnection from one’s *svadharma*—one’s own rightful duty. The spiritual lesson is that the *ātman* expresses itself through engagement with the world, and when that engagement ceases, the soul’s voice grows faint. The call is to remember, as Kṛṣṇa told Arjuna, that even imperfect action taken from one’s own nature is preferable to perfect inaction.

deficient.vignette: The alarm goes off at 7:00. She lies there. At 7:15, she picks up her phone and scrolls—not out of interest, but because the screen requires less effort than standing up. The emails from yesterday sit unanswered; the very act of reading them feels like pushing through wet sand. She eventually makes it to the kitchen, where the dishes from two days ago are still stacked. She knows she should wash them. She stares at them for a full thirty seconds, then pours a bowl of cereal and eats it on the couch. At her desk, she opens a document and reads the first paragraph three times without comprehension. The afternoon passes in a blur of half-started tasks and abandoned browser tabs. By evening, she feels a dull ache of shame—not for any specific failure, but for the general sense that she has been a spectator in her own day.

attributes.title: Chapter 3: Jñāna Yoga — The Path of the Observer
attributes.aspect: The tradition identifies four pillars of Jñāna Yoga: *Viveka* (conscious, deliberate discrimination of real from unreal), *Vairāgya* (non-attachment to worldly possessions and ego), *Ṣaṭ-Sampatti* (six methods of mental and emotional training), and *Mumukṣutva* (the burning desire for liberation). Its methodology proceeds through three stages: *Śravaṇa* (hearing or receiving knowledge), *Manana* (deep reflection and logical analysis), and *Nididhyāsana* (direct experiential realization).

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental (intellect and discernment) · Ontic mode: Practice — a mārga (mode of engagement through knowledge) · Valence: Cultivated path · Guṇa: Sattva-dominant · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: Means (a path to freedom through discriminative knowledge) · Key relation: One of the four mārgas — the path of the Observer; faculty of intellect and mind.
coordinate.ontic_mode: Practice — a mārga (mode of engagement through knowledge)
coordinate.causal_status: Means (a path to freedom through discriminative knowledge)
coordinate.key_relation: One of the four mārgas — the path of the Observer; faculty of intellect and mind.

balanced.label: Equilibrium: The Clear Mirror

facets.somatic: In equilibrium, the body of the Jñāna Yogi is characterized by a quality of stillness and composure. The posture is naturally upright—not rigid but alert, like a person who is genuinely listening. The nervous system operates with a refined sensitivity: the Observer notices subtle shifts in the body—a tightening in the stomach when something is not right, a lightening in the chest when truth is encountered—and uses these signals as data rather than being driven by them. The face tends toward a quality of calm attentiveness, the eyes focused but soft.
facets.emotional: The emotional tone is equanimity—not the suppression of feeling but the capacity to witness emotions without being captured by them. When someone insults the balanced Observer, they can intellectually deconstruct why that person is upset rather than taking it personally. There is a deep calm derived from understanding the “why” behind life events. The underlying drive is toward clarity rather than comfort; the Jñāna Yogi would rather know an uncomfortable truth than rest in a pleasant illusion.
facets.cognitive: The thoughts of the balanced Observer are characterized by precision and spaciousness. There is an inner “mental buffer”—a gap between stimulus and response in which discernment operates. The dominant cognitive activity is *viveka*: the continuous, deliberate sorting of the real from the projected, the essential from the superficial. Like the swan of classical metaphor that separates water from milk, the balanced intellect can perceive the *ātman* through the layers of conditioned experience. Knowledge serves liberation, not accumulation.
facets.psychic: At the soul level, balanced Jñāna is the experience of progressive unveiling. Each insight into the nature of reality thins the veil of *māyā* (illusion) slightly, and the practitioner begins to sense the unity of *Ātman* and *Brahman*—not as an abstract proposition but as a quiet, growing certainty that permeates daily life. The spiritual lesson is that true knowledge is not information but transformation: it changes the knower.
