facets.overview: Absence of Satya creates web of lies, self-deception, manipulation, fragmented consciousness, and powerlessness in speech.
facets.somatic: Body language contradicting words, stress symptoms from constant pretense, exhausted nervous system, somatic manifestations of deception.
facets.emotional: Emotional dishonesty, inability to feel authentic, emotional manipulation, disconnection from real feelings.
facets.cognitive: Mental fragmentation from lies, rationalization, self-deception, weak and ineffective speech (words have no power), mental confusion.
facets.psychic: The soul lives in falsehood, its words unmoored from any reality.

deficient.text:
Absence of Satya creates web of lies, self-deception, manipulation, fragmented consciousness, and powerlessness in speech.

Somatic: Body language contradicting words, stress symptoms from constant pretense, exhausted nervous system, somatic manifestations of deception.

Emotional: Emotional dishonesty, inability to feel authentic, emotional manipulation, disconnection from real feelings.

Cognitive: Mental fragmentation from lies, rationalization, self-deception, weak and ineffective speech (words have no power), mental confusion.

Psychic: The soul lives in falsehood, its words unmoored from any reality.
deficient.vignette: For Karan the truth is negotiable—small lies to smooth things, larger ones to protect himself, a running fiction he half-believes. His word has quietly stopped meaning anything, even to him.

attributes.title: Asteya (Non-Stealing)
attributes.aspect: Asteya is non-stealing in thought, word, and deed—not taking what is not freely given. It extends to time, energy, credit, and ideas. Patanjali states that when established in Asteya, all jewels come to the yogi (Sutra 2.37).

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital–Mental · Ontic mode: Virtue — a yama (non-stealing) · Valence: Virtue to cultivate · Guṇa: Sattva · Scale: Interpersonal (Ādhibhautika) · Causal status: Foundation (a root restraint) · Key relation: A yama (part of the Yama limb).
coordinate.key_relation: A yama (part of the Yama limb).

balanced.label: Balanced expression

facets.overview: When Asteya is established, abundance flows to the yogi naturally. There is contentment with what one has and integrity in interactions with what belongs to others.
facets.somatic: Physical respect for others’ belongings and space, healthy boundaries, abundance manifesting in physical reality, body free from anxiety of acquisition.
facets.emotional: Emotional contentment and integrity, freedom from envy and grasping, peace with what is one’s own, emotional abundance.
facets.cognitive: Honest thinking about what belongs to whom, freedom from coveting, mental abundance, wisdom about true ownership, integrity in ideas and attribution.
facets.psychic: The soul takes only what is freely given, and in non-stealing finds a strange abundance flowing to it.

balanced.text:
When Asteya is established, abundance flows to the yogi naturally. There is contentment with what one has and integrity in interactions with what belongs to others.

Somatic: Physical respect for others’ belongings and space, healthy boundaries, abundance manifesting in physical reality, body free from anxiety of acquisition.

Emotional: Emotional contentment and integrity, freedom from envy and grasping, peace with what is one’s own, emotional abundance.

Cognitive: Honest thinking about what belongs to whom, freedom from coveting, mental abundance, wisdom about true ownership, integrity in ideas and attribution.

Psychic: The soul takes only what is freely given, and in non-stealing finds a strange abundance flowing to it.
balanced.vignette: Sana takes only what is genuinely hers—no padded expense, no borrowed credit, no coveting of a colleague’s luck. There is a cleanness to her that comes from wanting nothing that isn’t hers to have.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Excessive Asteya can become excessive scrupulosity, paranoid concern about taking anything, inability to receive gifts or help, false purity.
facets.somatic: Refusing legitimate help or gifts, physical hoarding from fear of taking, anxiety about all possessions.
facets.emotional: Emotional rigidity about receiving, inability to accept emotional support, false saintliness about possessions, emotional anxiety.
facets.cognitive: Obsessive thinking about ownership, scrupulosity that paralyzes, false purity about taking, inability to engage normally.
facets.psychic: Non-stealing becomes anxious scrupulosity over every borrowed thing.

excess.text:
Excessive Asteya can become excessive scrupulosity, paranoid concern about taking anything, inability to receive gifts or help, false purity.

Somatic: Refusing legitimate help or gifts, physical hoarding from fear of taking, anxiety about all possessions.

Emotional: Emotional rigidity about receiving, inability to accept emotional support, false saintliness about possessions, emotional anxiety.

Cognitive: Obsessive thinking about ownership, scrupulosity that paralyzes, false purity about taking, inability to engage normally.

Psychic: Non-stealing becomes anxious scrupulosity over every borrowed thing.
excess.vignette: So scrupulous about taking anything, Priya cannot accept a gift, a compliment, or an offered hand without acute discomfort, insisting on paying back every kindness immediately. Her non-stealing has become an inability to receive.

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.overview: Absence of Asteya creates stealing in many forms—material, intellectual (plagiarism), emotional (stealing credit), time (lateness), energy (vampirism). Inner poverty results.
facets.somatic: Taking what isn’t given, stealing physical objects, body that takes more than its share, depletion from unfair acquisition.
facets.emotional: Emotional stealing (claiming others’ achievements), envy and coveting, taking emotional energy from others, emotional poverty despite acquisition.
facets.cognitive: Plagiarism of ideas, mental coveting, dishonest thinking about belongings, mental poverty (always needing more), inner emptiness driving acquisition.
facets.psychic: The soul takes what is not given — goods, credit, attention — grasping at what is not its own.

deficient.text:
Absence of Asteya creates stealing in many forms—material, intellectual (plagiarism), emotional (stealing credit), time (lateness), energy (vampirism). Inner poverty results.

Somatic: Taking what isn’t given, stealing physical objects, body that takes more than its share, depletion from unfair acquisition.

Emotional: Emotional stealing (claiming others’ achievements), envy and coveting, taking emotional energy from others, emotional poverty despite acquisition.

Cognitive: Plagiarism of ideas, mental coveting, dishonest thinking about belongings, mental poverty (always needing more), inner emptiness driving acquisition.

Psychic: The soul takes what is not given — goods, credit, attention — grasping at what is not its own.
deficient.vignette: Dan helps himself—to other people’s time, ideas, and credit, to the shared supplies, to a little more than his share—rarely with malice, simply as a habit of taking. The line between mine and yours has gone blurry in him.

attributes.title: Brahmacharya (Energy Conservation/Continence)
attributes.aspect: Brahmacharya is the conservation and proper direction of life energy (especially sexual energy) toward higher purposes. Patanjali states that when established in Brahmacharya, great vigor is gained (Sutra 2.38).

coordinate.raw: Seat: Physical–Vital · Ontic mode: Virtue — a yama (continence / energy-stewardship) · Valence: Virtue to cultivate · Guṇa: Sattva · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Mechanism (conserves and directs vital energy) · Key relation: A yama (part of the Yama limb); also treated in Dim 6 / 26.
coordinate.key_relation: A yama (part of the Yama limb); also treated in Dim 6 / 26.

balanced.label: Balanced expression

facets.overview: When Brahmacharya is established, vital energy is conserved and directed appropriately. The yogi has tremendous vigor, vitality, and creative power available for spiritual practice.
facets.somatic: Strong vital energy and vigor, conserved life force available for higher purposes, healthy sexual function used wisely, powerful body and presence, radiant vitality.
facets.emotional: Emotional strength from conserved energy, freedom from compulsive sexual emotions, emotional balance, healthy intimate relationships when chosen.
facets.cognitive: Mental clarity and power from conserved energy, focused mind, creative power, freedom from obsessive sexual thinking, mental vigor for practice.
facets.psychic: The soul conserves and rightly directs its vital energy, the gathered force fueling its practice.

balanced.text:
When Brahmacharya is established, vital energy is conserved and directed appropriately. The yogi has tremendous vigor, vitality, and creative power available for spiritual practice.

Somatic: Strong vital energy and vigor, conserved life force available for higher purposes, healthy sexual function used wisely, powerful body and presence, radiant vitality.

Emotional: Emotional strength from conserved energy, freedom from compulsive sexual emotions, emotional balance, healthy intimate relationships when chosen.

Cognitive: Mental clarity and power from conserved energy, focused mind, creative power, freedom from obsessive sexual thinking, mental vigor for practice.

Psychic: The soul conserves and rightly directs its vital energy, the gathered force fueling its practice.
balanced.vignette: Kavi manages his vital energy wisely—neither repressing his desires nor squandering himself on every impulse—so that his force is available for what he cares about. Moderation, in him, is a kind of power.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Excessive Brahmacharya as harsh suppression leads to repression, distortion of natural drives, eventual explosive release, and inability to engage healthily with sexuality.
facets.somatic: Physical tension from suppressed energy, sexual dysfunction, eventual physical explosion, harm from extreme suppression, distorted energy.
facets.emotional: Suppressed emotions seeking expression, emotional distortion, eventual emotional eruption, repression that creates pathology, sexual shame.
facets.cognitive: Mental obsession with what’s being suppressed, eventual breakdown of control, mental distortion from forced suppression, rigid thinking.
facets.psychic: Continence hardens into grim repression, the soul at war with its own vitality.

excess.text:
Excessive Brahmacharya as harsh suppression leads to repression, distortion of natural drives, eventual explosive release, and inability to engage healthily with sexuality.

Somatic: Physical tension from suppressed energy, sexual dysfunction, eventual physical explosion, harm from extreme suppression, distorted energy.

Emotional: Suppressed emotions seeking expression, emotional distortion, eventual emotional eruption, repression that creates pathology, sexual shame.

Cognitive: Mental obsession with what’s being suppressed, eventual breakdown of control, mental distortion from forced suppression, rigid thinking.

Psychic: Continence hardens into grim repression, the soul at war with its own vitality.
excess.vignette: Ben’s continence has hardened into a grim war on the body—every desire a threat, every pleasure a fall—until his life has gone rigid and joyless. The discipline meant to conserve energy is starving it.

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.overview: Absence of Brahmacharya creates depletion of vital energy through unconscious or compulsive expenditure—through sexuality, but also through wasted speech, scattered attention, and energy leaks.
facets.somatic: Depleted vital energy, exhaustion, weakened body, compulsive sexual expenditure, energy leaks through senses, premature aging.
facets.emotional: Emotional volatility from depleted energy, compulsive emotional and sexual seeking, emotional exhaustion, inability to sustain emotional commitment.
facets.cognitive: Scattered mind from energy leaks, inability to concentrate, mental fatigue, weak willpower, obsessive thinking driven by depleted state.
facets.psychic: The soul squanders its vital energy, leaving no reserve for the inner work.

deficient.text:
Absence of Brahmacharya creates depletion of vital energy through unconscious or compulsive expenditure—through sexuality, but also through wasted speech, scattered attention, and energy leaks.

Somatic: Depleted vital energy, exhaustion, weakened body, compulsive sexual expenditure, energy leaks through senses, premature aging.

Emotional: Emotional volatility from depleted energy, compulsive emotional and sexual seeking, emotional exhaustion, inability to sustain emotional commitment.

Cognitive: Scattered mind from energy leaks, inability to concentrate, mental fatigue, weak willpower, obsessive thinking driven by depleted state.

Psychic: The soul squanders its vital energy, leaving no reserve for the inner work.
deficient.vignette: Dan spends himself on every appetite as it arises—the binge, the fling, the compulsive scroll—with no governor at all, and wonders why he is always depleted. Nothing is conserved, so nothing accumulates.

attributes.title: Aparigraha (Non-Possessiveness) [Yoga Sutra Context]
attributes.aspect: Aparigraha is non-possessiveness, non-hoarding, freedom from greed. Patanjali states that when established in Aparigraha, the knowledge of the why and wherefore of births is gained (Sutra 2.39)—deep insight into karmic patterns.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital–Mental · Ontic mode: Virtue — a yama (non-grasping) · Valence: Virtue to cultivate · Guṇa: Sattva · Scale: Individual, with material dimension · Causal status: Mechanism (frees from possession) · Key relation: A yama (part of the Yama limb); also treated as a concept in Dim 10.
coordinate.key_relation: A yama (part of the Yama limb); also treated as a concept in Dim 10.

balanced.label: Balanced expression

facets.overview: When Aparigraha is established, the yogi gains insight into the nature of birth and karma. There is freedom from grasping that allows clear seeing of life patterns.
facets.somatic: Lightness from few possessions, physical freedom from material burden, body unencumbered, healthy minimalism, ease in physical existence.
facets.emotional: Freedom from anxiety of possession, emotional lightness, generosity, freedom from loss-fear, peace from non-grasping.
facets.cognitive: Mental clarity from unburdened mind, wisdom about karmic patterns, freedom from grasping thoughts, insight into nature of existence.
facets.psychic: The soul holds nothing as its own, and in non-grasping gains insight into the why of its births.

balanced.text:
When Aparigraha is established, the yogi gains insight into the nature of birth and karma. There is freedom from grasping that allows clear seeing of life patterns.

Somatic: Lightness from few possessions, physical freedom from material burden, body unencumbered, healthy minimalism, ease in physical existence.

Emotional: Freedom from anxiety of possession, emotional lightness, generosity, freedom from loss-fear, peace from non-grasping.

Cognitive: Mental clarity from unburdened mind, wisdom about karmic patterns, freedom from grasping thoughts, insight into nature of existence.

Psychic: The soul holds nothing as its own, and in non-grasping gains insight into the why of its births.
balanced.vignette: Kira holds her possessions and her plans lightly, gives easily, and feels no clutch of dread around losing what she has. There is a freedom in her that comes from not gripping.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Excessive Aparigraha as extreme asceticism creates deprivation, rejection of legitimate needs, ascetic pride, and impractical living.
facets.somatic: Physical deprivation, neglect of necessities, harm from extreme renunciation, weakness from inadequate resources.
facets.emotional: Emotional rejection of legitimate desires, harsh asceticism, pride in renunciation, denial of human needs.
facets.cognitive: Mental rigidity about possession, dogmatic anti-materialism, inability to function practically, escapism through renunciation.
facets.psychic: The soul makes non-possession a performance, clinging to its very renunciation.

excess.text:
Excessive Aparigraha as extreme asceticism creates deprivation, rejection of legitimate needs, ascetic pride, and impractical living.

Somatic: Physical deprivation, neglect of necessities, harm from extreme renunciation, weakness from inadequate resources.

Emotional: Emotional rejection of legitimate desires, harsh asceticism, pride in renunciation, denial of human needs.

Cognitive: Mental rigidity about possession, dogmatic anti-materialism, inability to function practically, escapism through renunciation.

Psychic: The soul makes non-possession a performance, clinging to its very renunciation.
excess.vignette: Determined to own nothing, Jon has renounced past the point of sense—refusing the help, the comfort, the ordinary security he actually needs—and calls the resulting deprivation ‘non-attachment.’

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.overview: Absence of Aparigraha creates hoarding, greed, anxiety about possessions, materialism, and karmic burden.
facets.somatic: Hoarding behaviors, cluttered environment, body weighed down by stress of possessions, illness from accumulation anxiety.
facets.emotional: Anxiety about losing possessions, emotional attachment to things, greed, emotional emptiness covered by acquisition, possession-fear.
facets.cognitive: Obsessive thinking about acquisition, mental burden of managing possessions, no insight into karma, mental contamination from greed.
facets.psychic: The soul grasps and hoards, bound by the possessions it mistakes for security.

deficient.text:
Absence of Aparigraha creates hoarding, greed, anxiety about possessions, materialism, and karmic burden.

Somatic: Hoarding behaviors, cluttered environment, body weighed down by stress of possessions, illness from accumulation anxiety.

Emotional: Anxiety about losing possessions, emotional attachment to things, greed, emotional emptiness covered by acquisition, possession-fear.

Cognitive: Obsessive thinking about acquisition, mental burden of managing possessions, no insight into karma, mental contamination from greed.

Psychic: The soul grasps and hoards, bound by the possessions it mistakes for security.
deficient.vignette: Dana accumulates and cannot stop—more things, more savings, more control—each acquisition trailing a fresh anxiety about keeping it. She is owned by what she owns.

attributes.title: Shaucha (Purity) [Yoga Sutra Context]
attributes.aspect: Shaucha is purification—both external (body, environment) and internal (mind, motives). Patanjali states that from Shaucha comes detachment from one’s body and disgust at contact with others’ bodies; further, purity of mind leads to cheerfulness, one-pointedness, control of senses, and capacity for Self-realization.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Physical–Mental · Ontic mode: Virtue — a niyama (purity, inner and outer) · Valence: Virtue to cultivate (fastidiousness in excess) · Guṇa: Sattva · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Condition (clarity from cleanliness) · Key relation: A niyama (part of the Niyama limb).
coordinate.ontic_mode: Virtue — a niyama (purity, inner and outer)
coordinate.key_relation: A niyama (part of the Niyama limb).

balanced.label: Balanced expression

facets.overview: When Shaucha is established, both external cleanliness and internal purity manifest. The yogi develops cheerfulness, mental focus, sense control, and prepares for higher states.
facets.somatic: Clean, well-cared-for body, healthy hygiene, clean environment, healthy physical detachment from body identification, radiant health.
facets.emotional: Cheerful and pure emotional states, freedom from corrupting emotions, emotional clarity, joy that comes from purity.
facets.cognitive: Pure thoughts, mental clarity, one-pointedness, control of senses, preparation for Self-realization, clean mental patterns.
facets.psychic: The soul keeps purity inner and outer, and from cleanliness arises clarity and a wholesome detachment from the body.

balanced.text:
When Shaucha is established, both external cleanliness and internal purity manifest. The yogi develops cheerfulness, mental focus, sense control, and prepares for higher states.

Somatic: Clean, well-cared-for body, healthy hygiene, clean environment, healthy physical detachment from body identification, radiant health.

Emotional: Cheerful and pure emotional states, freedom from corrupting emotions, emotional clarity, joy that comes from purity.

Cognitive: Pure thoughts, mental clarity, one-pointedness, control of senses, preparation for Self-realization, clean mental patterns.

Psychic: The soul keeps purity inner and outer, and from cleanliness arises clarity and a wholesome detachment from the body.
balanced.vignette: Leela keeps her body, her space, and her mind reasonably clean, and finds that the outer order supports an inner clarity. Purity, for her, is not fussiness but a clear channel.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Excessive Shaucha becomes obsessive cleanliness, germophobia, judgment of others’ impurity, and inability to engage with natural world.
facets.somatic: OCD-like cleaning behaviors, skin damage from over-washing, anxiety about contamination, inability to engage with natural mess.
facets.emotional: Disgust at any imperfection, emotional rigidity, judgment of others, anxiety about contamination.
facets.cognitive: Obsessive thinking about purity, perfectionism, mental rigidity, inability to engage with messy reality.
facets.psychic: Purity tips into obsessive fastidiousness, the soul disgusted by ordinary embodied life.

excess.text:
Excessive Shaucha becomes obsessive cleanliness, germophobia, judgment of others’ impurity, and inability to engage with natural world.

Somatic: OCD-like cleaning behaviors, skin damage from over-washing, anxiety about contamination, inability to engage with natural mess.

Emotional: Disgust at any imperfection, emotional rigidity, judgment of others, anxiety about contamination.

Cognitive: Obsessive thinking about purity, perfectionism, mental rigidity, inability to engage with messy reality.

Psychic: Purity tips into obsessive fastidiousness, the soul disgusted by ordinary embodied life.
excess.vignette: Sam’s need for cleanliness has become a tyranny—the endless washing, the disgust at the body, the horror of a speck of dirt. What began as purity has become an anxious, exhausting war on ordinary life.

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.overview: Absence of Shaucha creates physical uncleanliness, mental impurity, corrupt motives, and inability to develop higher states.
facets.somatic: Poor hygiene, unclean environment, disease from filth, physical degradation.
facets.emotional: Corrupt motives, toxic emotions, allowing pollution in feeling, emotional impurity.
facets.cognitive: Impure thoughts, corrupted thinking, inability to develop higher states, mental pollution, no foundation for practice.
facets.psychic: The soul neglects both inner and outer purity, its body and motives left clouded.

deficient.text:
Absence of Shaucha creates physical uncleanliness, mental impurity, corrupt motives, and inability to develop higher states.

Somatic: Poor hygiene, unclean environment, disease from filth, physical degradation.

Emotional: Corrupt motives, toxic emotions, allowing pollution in feeling, emotional impurity.

Cognitive: Impure thoughts, corrupted thinking, inability to develop higher states, mental pollution, no foundation for practice.

Psychic: The soul neglects both inner and outer purity, its body and motives left clouded.
deficient.vignette: Dan lives amid a slow accumulating clutter—of dishes, of clothes, of unprocessed feelings—and the outer disorder mirrors an inner fog. Nothing is quite clean, and nothing is quite clear.

attributes.title: Santosha (Contentment) [Yoga Sutra Context]
attributes.aspect: Santosha is contentment—satisfaction with what comes naturally. Patanjali states that from Santosha is gained unsurpassed happiness (Sutra 2.42). It is independence of happiness from external circumstances.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital–Mental · Ontic mode: Virtue — a niyama (contentment) · Valence: Virtue to cultivate (complacency in excess) · Guṇa: Sattva · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Condition (an unsurpassed happiness) · Key relation: A niyama (part of the Niyama limb) — the same virtue as the earlier Santoṣa entry, here placed among the niyamas.
coordinate.key_relation: A niyama (part of the Niyama limb) — the same virtue as the earlier Santoṣa entry, here placed among the niyamas.

balanced.label: Balanced expression

facets.overview: When Santosha is established, supreme happiness arises that does not depend on external circumstances. The yogi is content with what comes and uses it for practice.
facets.somatic: Physical ease and contentment, relaxed body from lack of striving, good health from peace, vitality from inner satisfaction.
facets.emotional: Profound emotional contentment, joy independent of circumstances, freedom from chronic dissatisfaction, deep happiness.
facets.cognitive: Mental peace and contentment, freedom from constant wanting, wisdom about happiness, mental focus from satisfied state.
facets.psychic: The soul rests in contentment, and from satisfaction with what is comes an unsurpassed happiness.

balanced.text:
When Santosha is established, supreme happiness arises that does not depend on external circumstances. The yogi is content with what comes and uses it for practice.

Somatic: Physical ease and contentment, relaxed body from lack of striving, good health from peace, vitality from inner satisfaction.

Emotional: Profound emotional contentment, joy independent of circumstances, freedom from chronic dissatisfaction, deep happiness.

Cognitive: Mental peace and contentment, freedom from constant wanting, wisdom about happiness, mental focus from satisfied state.

Psychic: The soul rests in contentment, and from satisfaction with what is comes an unsurpassed happiness.
balanced.vignette: On an ordinary Tuesday, stuck in traffic with a modest life around her, Meera notices she is simply, quietly okay. Her contentment does not depend on the next thing arriving; it rests on what is already here.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Excessive contentment becomes complacency, lack of motivation to improve, passive acceptance of unacceptable conditions.
facets.somatic: Physical complacency, lack of motivation to maintain health, sluggishness, accepting physical deterioration.
facets.emotional: Emotional flatness, lack of legitimate ambition, passive acceptance of harmful conditions, complacency.
facets.cognitive: Mental stagnation, lack of growth motivation, inability to pursue legitimate goals, complacency.
facets.psychic: Contentment curdles into complacency, excusing the discipline the soul avoids.

excess.text:
Excessive contentment becomes complacency, lack of motivation to improve, passive acceptance of unacceptable conditions.

Somatic: Physical complacency, lack of motivation to maintain health, sluggishness, accepting physical deterioration.

Emotional: Emotional flatness, lack of legitimate ambition, passive acceptance of harmful conditions, complacency.

Cognitive: Mental stagnation, lack of growth motivation, inability to pursue legitimate goals, complacency.

Psychic: Contentment curdles into complacency, excusing the discipline the soul avoids.
excess.vignette: Tom’s contentment is really a refusal to want anything better—the stalled career, the tepid marriage, the untended health all accepted with a shrug dressed as serenity. He mistakes giving up for peace.

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.overview: Absence of Santosha creates chronic dissatisfaction, endless wanting, inability to enjoy what is, and constant suffering.
facets.somatic: Stress from chronic dissatisfaction, exhaustion from endless pursuit, stress-related illness, body in chronic want.
facets.emotional: Chronic dissatisfaction, inability to enjoy present, anxious wanting, emotional exhaustion, no peace.
facets.cognitive: Constant mental wanting, restlessness, inability to be satisfied, mental exhaustion from seeking, no peace.
facets.psychic: The soul is chronically dissatisfied, its peace forever postponed to the next gain.

deficient.text:
Absence of Santosha creates chronic dissatisfaction, endless wanting, inability to enjoy what is, and constant suffering.

Somatic: Stress from chronic dissatisfaction, exhaustion from endless pursuit, stress-related illness, body in chronic want.

Emotional: Chronic dissatisfaction, inability to enjoy present, anxious wanting, emotional exhaustion, no peace.

Cognitive: Constant mental wanting, restlessness, inability to be satisfied, mental exhaustion from seeking, no peace.

Psychic: The soul is chronically dissatisfied, its peace forever postponed to the next gain.
deficient.vignette: Sara reaches every milestone and feels nothing but the pull toward the next one. Satisfaction evaporates on contact; there is always a further rung, and she is exhausted from climbing toward a summit that keeps rising.

attributes.title: Tapas (Austerity) [Yoga Sutra Context]
attributes.aspect: Tapas is the fire of disciplined practice—austerity that burns impurities. Patanjali states that from Tapas the body and senses gain perfection through removal of impurities (Sutra 2.43). It is heat that transforms.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital–Physical · Ontic mode: Virtue — a niyama (disciplined austerity) · Valence: Virtue to cultivate (self-punishment in excess) · Guṇa: Rajas refined to Sattva · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Mechanism (burns away impurity) · Key relation: A niyama and a limb of Kriya-Yoga.
coordinate.key_relation: A niyama and a limb of Kriya-Yoga.

balanced.label: Balanced expression

facets.overview: When Tapas is balanced, the fire of practice burns impurities without burning the practitioner. Body and senses are refined and gain extraordinary capacities.
facets.somatic: Body refined through practice, enhanced sensory capacities, perfected physical instrument, strength and resilience from discipline.
facets.emotional: Emotional purification through discipline, emotional strength from practice, transformed emotional patterns, healing through fire.
facets.cognitive: Mental purification, increased capacity for concentration, refined sensory perception, mental strength.
facets.psychic: The soul takes up disciplined austerity, the inner fire burning away impurity and perfecting body and senses.

balanced.text:
When Tapas is balanced, the fire of practice burns impurities without burning the practitioner. Body and senses are refined and gain extraordinary capacities.

Somatic: Body refined through practice, enhanced sensory capacities, perfected physical instrument, strength and resilience from discipline.

Emotional: Emotional purification through discipline, emotional strength from practice, transformed emotional patterns, healing through fire.

Cognitive: Mental purification, increased capacity for concentration, refined sensory perception, mental strength.

Psychic: The soul takes up disciplined austerity, the inner fire burning away impurity and perfecting body and senses.
balanced.vignette: Ravi keeps a discipline that costs him—the cold morning practice, the declined indulgence—not to punish himself but to temper himself, and he can feel it forging something. The heat of the effort purifies rather than harms.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Excessive Tapas becomes self-torture, harm from extreme austerity, pride in suffering, damage to body and mind.
facets.somatic: Physical damage from extreme practices, harm to body, depletion, illness from excess austerity, broken health.
facets.emotional: Harsh self-judgment, emotional masochism, pride in suffering, ego-driven austerity, emotional damage.
facets.cognitive: Mental rigidity, ego pride in austerity, distorted purpose, mental damage from extremism.
facets.psychic: Austerity hardens into self-punishment, mortification mistaken for purification.

excess.text:
Excessive Tapas becomes self-torture, harm from extreme austerity, pride in suffering, damage to body and mind.

Somatic: Physical damage from extreme practices, harm to body, depletion, illness from excess austerity, broken health.

Emotional: Harsh self-judgment, emotional masochism, pride in suffering, ego-driven austerity, emotional damage.

Cognitive: Mental rigidity, ego pride in austerity, distorted purpose, mental damage from extremism.

Psychic: Austerity hardens into self-punishment, mortification mistaken for purification.
excess.vignette: Ben’s austerities have tipped into self-punishment—too little sleep, too little food, too much grim endurance, all worn as a badge. The purifying fire has become a slow burning of the very self it was meant to refine.

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.overview: Absence of Tapas means no transformative practice, no burning of impurities, no progress on the path, indulgence without growth.
facets.somatic: Undisciplined body, no physical transformation, indulgence damaging health, stagnation.
facets.emotional: Emotional indulgence, no transformation of patterns, slavery to feelings, no growth.
facets.cognitive: Undisciplined mind, no transformation, scattered without practice, no progress.
facets.psychic: The soul shuns all discipline, its impurities never burned away.

deficient.text:
Absence of Tapas means no transformative practice, no burning of impurities, no progress on the path, indulgence without growth.

Somatic: Undisciplined body, no physical transformation, indulgence damaging health, stagnation.

Emotional: Emotional indulgence, no transformation of patterns, slavery to feelings, no growth.

Cognitive: Undisciplined mind, no transformation, scattered without practice, no progress.

Psychic: The soul shuns all discipline, its impurities never burned away.
deficient.vignette: Dan avoids every discomfort as a matter of course—no cold, no hunger, no effort he can dodge—and so nothing in him is ever tested or tempered. Without any friction, his character stays soft and unformed.

attributes.title: Svadhyaya (Self-Study/Scriptural Study)
attributes.aspect: Svadhyaya is study of self and scriptures, including chanting of mantras. Patanjali states that through Svadhyaya, union with the chosen deity is established (Sutra 2.44)—deep contact with the Divine.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental · Ontic mode: Virtue / practice — a niyama (self-study) · Valence: Cultivated practice · Guṇa: Sattva · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Mechanism (draws near the Divine through study) · Key relation: A niyama and a limb of Kriya-Yoga.
coordinate.causal_status: Mechanism (draws near the Divine through study)
coordinate.key_relation: A niyama and a limb of Kriya-Yoga.

balanced.label: Balanced expression

facets.overview: When Svadhyaya is established, deep contact with the chosen deity or higher reality is established. There is profound self-knowledge and spiritual connection.
facets.somatic: Body that resonates with chanted sounds, healthy effects of mantra, embodied wisdom, nervous system attuned to higher frequencies.
facets.emotional: Emotional connection to divine, peace from spiritual contact, emotional wisdom from self-knowledge, devotional warmth.
facets.cognitive: Self-knowledge and understanding, wisdom from scripture, mind aligned with higher reality, contact with chosen deity.
facets.psychic: The soul studies sacred text and itself, and through study draws near to the Divine it contemplates.

balanced.text:
When Svadhyaya is established, deep contact with the chosen deity or higher reality is established. There is profound self-knowledge and spiritual connection.

Somatic: Body that resonates with chanted sounds, healthy effects of mantra, embodied wisdom, nervous system attuned to higher frequencies.

Emotional: Emotional connection to divine, peace from spiritual contact, emotional wisdom from self-knowledge, devotional warmth.

Cognitive: Self-knowledge and understanding, wisdom from scripture, mind aligned with higher reality, contact with chosen deity.

Psychic: The soul studies sacred text and itself, and through study draws near to the Divine it contemplates.
balanced.vignette: Each evening Kira reads a little and reflects on how it lands in her own life—the text a mirror as much as a source. Her study is a genuine self-inquiry, and it slowly changes how she lives.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Excessive Svadhyaya becomes mere intellectualism, scriptural pride, study without realization, escape into theory.
facets.somatic: Physical neglect for study, body weakened by excessive sitting/reading, lack of practical embodiment.
facets.emotional: Emotional dryness from intellectualism, pride in knowledge, lack of warmth, scriptural arrogance.
facets.cognitive: Intellectual pride, scriptural debate without realization, mere theory without experience, escape through study.
facets.psychic: The soul accumulates study as an end, erudition standing in for realization.

excess.text:
Excessive Svadhyaya becomes mere intellectualism, scriptural pride, study without realization, escape into theory.

Somatic: Physical neglect for study, body weakened by excessive sitting/reading, lack of practical embodiment.

Emotional: Emotional dryness from intellectualism, pride in knowledge, lack of warmth, scriptural arrogance.

Cognitive: Intellectual pride, scriptural debate without realization, mere theory without experience, escape through study.

Psychic: The soul accumulates study as an end, erudition standing in for realization.
excess.vignette: Julian has read everything and applied none of it—a vast library in his head and no discernible change in his life, quoting scriptures to win arguments rather than to see himself. The study feeds his pride, not his growth.

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.overview: Absence of Svadhyaya creates ignorance of self, no contact with wisdom traditions, no scriptural guidance, blind practice.
facets.somatic: Lack of practice-supporting wisdom, physical practice without understanding, no embodied wisdom.
facets.emotional: Emotional ignorance about self, no spiritual nourishment, emotional blindness, lack of devotional connection.
facets.cognitive: Self-ignorance, no scriptural guidance, mental darkness, no contact with wisdom traditions, blind seeking.
facets.psychic: The soul neither studies scripture nor examines itself, its inner life unexamined.

deficient.text:
Absence of Svadhyaya creates ignorance of self, no contact with wisdom traditions, no scriptural guidance, blind practice.

Somatic: Lack of practice-supporting wisdom, physical practice without understanding, no embodied wisdom.

Emotional: Emotional ignorance about self, no spiritual nourishment, emotional blindness, lack of devotional connection.

Cognitive: Self-ignorance, no scriptural guidance, mental darkness, no contact with wisdom traditions, blind seeking.

Psychic: The soul neither studies scripture nor examines itself, its inner life unexamined.
deficient.vignette: Dan never turns a reflective eye on himself; the unexamined patterns repeat, the same mistakes recur, and it never occurs to him to ask why. His is a life lived entirely on the surface, unread.

attributes.title: Ishvara Pranidhana (Surrender to the Divine)
attributes.aspect: Ishvara Pranidhana is surrender of self and actions to the Divine. Patanjali states that from this surrender, Samadhi is attained (Sutra 2.45). It is the complete giving over of ego to higher consciousness.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital–Psychic · Ontic mode: Virtue / practice — a niyama (surrender) · Valence: Virtue to cultivate · Guṇa: Sattva · Scale: Individual, toward the Divine · Causal status: Mechanism (surrender that brings absorption near) · Key relation: A niyama, a limb of Kriya-Yoga, and the direct path to Samādhi.
coordinate.key_relation: A niyama, a limb of Kriya-Yoga, and the direct path to Samādhi.

balanced.label: Balanced expression

facets.overview: When Ishvara Pranidhana is established, the highest state of Samadhi (absorption) is attained. There is profound peace, surrender, and union with the Divine.
facets.somatic: Body surrendered as instrument of divine, healthy release of ego-tension, body that flows with divine will, samadhic states.
facets.emotional: Emotional surrender and peace, freedom from ego-burden, emotional union with divine, profound bliss.
facets.cognitive: Mind surrendered, ego dissolved, Samadhi attained, complete trust, mental union with divine.
facets.psychic: The soul surrenders itself and its actions to the Divine, and through surrender absorption comes near.

balanced.text:
When Ishvara Pranidhana is established, the highest state of Samadhi (absorption) is attained. There is profound peace, surrender, and union with the Divine.

Somatic: Body surrendered as instrument of divine, healthy release of ego-tension, body that flows with divine will, samadhic states.

Emotional: Emotional surrender and peace, freedom from ego-burden, emotional union with divine, profound bliss.

Cognitive: Mind surrendered, ego dissolved, Samadhi attained, complete trust, mental union with divine.

Psychic: The soul surrenders itself and its actions to the Divine, and through surrender absorption comes near.
balanced.vignette: Kira does her honest best and then, genuinely, lets go—entrusting the outcome to something larger and freeing herself from the exhausting grip of control. Her surrender is not passivity but a deep, working trust.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Excessive Ishvara Pranidhana without discrimination becomes passive fatalism, giving up personal responsibility, blind surrender to wrong figures.
facets.somatic: Physical inaction disguised as surrender, neglect of body care, false withdrawal from responsibility.
facets.emotional: Emotional dependency disguised as devotion, blind surrender that loses self, codependent spirituality.
facets.cognitive: Mental abdication of responsibility, fatalism, surrender without wisdom, gullibility to false teachers.
facets.psychic: The soul performs surrender while secretly withholding, the offering staged for itself.

excess.text:
Excessive Ishvara Pranidhana without discrimination becomes passive fatalism, giving up personal responsibility, blind surrender to wrong figures.

Somatic: Physical inaction disguised as surrender, neglect of body care, false withdrawal from responsibility.

Emotional: Emotional dependency disguised as devotion, blind surrender that loses self, codependent spirituality.

Cognitive: Mental abdication of responsibility, fatalism, surrender without wisdom, gullibility to false teachers.

Psychic: The soul performs surrender while secretly withholding, the offering staged for itself.
excess.vignette: Sam ‘surrenders everything to God’ as a way of doing nothing—no effort, no responsibility, the failing situation left to ‘divine will’ while he waits. His surrender has become fatalism, an excuse dressed as faith.

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.overview: Absence of Ishvara Pranidhana creates ego-based practice, no transcendence, attachment to results, no entry into Samadhi.
facets.somatic: Body that holds tension from ego-effort, physical exhaustion from non-surrendered work.
facets.emotional: Ego-driven emotions, no peace from surrender, emotional struggle, no entry into bliss.
facets.cognitive: Ego-bound mind, no transcendence, attachment to results, no Samadhi possible, mental burden.
facets.psychic: The soul surrenders nothing, facing existence as if it must manage everything alone.

deficient.text:
Absence of Ishvara Pranidhana creates ego-based practice, no transcendence, attachment to results, no entry into Samadhi.

Somatic: Body that holds tension from ego-effort, physical exhaustion from non-surrendered work.

Emotional: Ego-driven emotions, no peace from surrender, emotional struggle, no entry into bliss.

Cognitive: Ego-bound mind, no transcendence, attachment to results, no Samadhi possible, mental burden.

Psychic: The soul surrenders nothing, facing existence as if it must manage everything alone.
deficient.vignette: Dana grips every outcome with both hands, unable to release control for a single hour, convinced that if she stops managing, everything falls apart. The relief of trust is a country she has never allowed herself to visit.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Psychic (the causal storehouse of past action) · Ontic mode: Process / accumulation (the total store of accumulated karma) · Valence: Neutral — a given inheritance to work with · Guṇa: Mixed (bears every coloring) · Scale: Individual, across lifetimes · Causal status: Cause (the reservoir of latent consequence) · Key relation: Whole — the total store from which Prārabdha is drawn; one of the three karmas.
coordinate.seat: Psychic (the causal storehouse of past action)
coordinate.ontic_mode: Process / accumulation (the total store of accumulated karma)
coordinate.valence: Neutral — a given inheritance to work with
coordinate.key_relation: Whole — the total store from which Prārabdha is drawn; one of the three karmas.

balanced.label: Equilibrium

facets.somatic: The body feels like an instrument that has been well-played but carries no obvious tension from the playing. There is a neutral readiness in the musculature, a sense of being “settled into” the body without excess weight.
facets.emotional: A stable hum of quiet capability. You sense reserves of skill and intuition that seem to arrive without being summoned—what others call “natural talent.” There is no anxiety about these reserves; they simply exist, like groundwater.
facets.cognitive: Thoughts carry a quality of coherence. When faced with a new situation, the mind draws on a vast and varied library of past experience without becoming overwhelmed by it. Decisions feel informed rather than burdened.
facets.psychic: The witness recognizes that all current capacities are borrowed from a deep well—none are “personal achievements” in the egoic sense. There is gratitude without pride, capacity without inflation.

balanced.text:

Psychic: The witness recognizes that all current capacities are borrowed from a deep well—none are “personal achievements” in the egoic sense. There is gratitude without pride, capacity without inflation.

excess.label: The Vignette: Equilibrium

facets.overview: Maya knows she carries patterns older than she can trace—the quick temper, the pull toward certain people—and instead of fighting or excusing them, she works with them patiently, one groove at a time. The reservoir is vast, but she is not its prisoner; she tends what she can reach.

deficient.label: Excess: The Weight of Accumulated History

facets.somatic: A heaviness that cannot be attributed to any single cause—a sense of being “haunted” at the cellular level. The shoulders carry a load that no massage can release because the tension is not muscular but karmic.
facets.emotional: A pervasive undertow of “something unfinished.” The emotional landscape is tinged with a restless incompleteness, as though a debt exists that you cannot name. This may surface as chronic, low-grade anxiety or a sense of destiny that feels more like a cage than a calling.
facets.cognitive: Thought patterns spiral into fatalism. The mind tells stories of inevitability: “This is just who I am.” “I’ve always been this way.” The sheer volume of accumulated impression crowds out the possibility of genuine novelty.
facets.psychic: The spiritual lesson is one of purification through discernment. The witness sees the weight and recognises that not all of it must be carried forward. This is the territory where Jñāna Yoga—the path of discriminative knowledge—burns through layers of stored karmic residue.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Physical–Psychic (the given conditions of this life) · Ontic mode: Process / given portion (the karma now bearing fruit) · Valence: Neutral — the fixed conditions to accept · Guṇa: Mixed · Scale: Individual, this lifetime · Causal status: Effect-in-force (the portion now ripening as circumstance) · Key relation: Part — the portion of Sanchita allotted to this life; one of the three karmas.
coordinate.seat: Physical–Psychic (the given conditions of this life)
coordinate.ontic_mode: Process / given portion (the karma now bearing fruit)
coordinate.valence: Neutral — the fixed conditions to accept
coordinate.causal_status: Effect-in-force (the portion now ripening as circumstance)

balanced.label: Equilibrium

facets.somatic: The body is accepted as it is—its strengths, its limitations, its particular shape and rhythm. You wake with a chronic condition and manage it without resentment, treating the body as a “sacred constraint” rather than a punishment.
facets.emotional: A quality of philosophical acceptance permeates the emotional field. Difficult circumstances are met with a kind of dignified composure—not denial, but the recognition that resistance to the unchangeable consumes energy without producing change.
facets.cognitive: The mind focuses its analytical power not on “Why did this happen to me?” but on “Given that this is so, what is the wisest response?” This is the cognitive signature of high epistemic integrity in the face of Prārabdha.
facets.psychic: The witness understands that Prārabdha is not arbitrary but the precise curriculum needed for this particular soul’s growth. There is a deep, quiet trust in the intelligence of the process.

balanced.text:

Psychic: The witness understands that Prārabdha is not arbitrary but the precise curriculum needed for this particular soul’s growth. There is a deep, quiet trust in the intelligence of the process.

excess.label: The Vignette: Equilibrium

facets.overview: The diagnosis, the family he was born into, the leg that never healed right—Sam has stopped raging at the cards he was dealt and started, quietly, to play them well. Acceptance, for him, is not defeat but the ground he builds on. He tends the field he actually has.

excess.text: The diagnosis, the family he was born into, the leg that never healed right—Sam has stopped raging at the cards he was dealt and started, quietly, to play them well. Acceptance, for him, is not defeat but the ground he builds on. He tends the field he actually has.

deficient.label: Excess: The Overwhelming Givens

facets.somatic: The body feels besieged by conditions it did not choose. Chronic pain, disability, or persistent illness dominate the somatic field, making every physical act a negotiation.
facets.emotional: A sense of cosmic injustice simmers beneath the surface. The emotional signature is a mix of helplessness and resentment—the feeling that the dice were loaded before you were ever born.
facets.cognitive: Victimhood narratives crystallize: “Life is fundamentally unfair.” “I never had a chance.” These stories are not entirely wrong—the facticity is genuinely heavy—but they foreclose the possibility of agency within constraint.
facets.psychic: The spiritual challenge here is immense: to find meaning within severe limitation. This is the territory of Bhakti—the path of devotion—where surrender to the larger intelligence becomes not resignation but a radical form of trust.

attributes.attribute: Āgami Karma: The Karma of the Present Moment
attributes.title: Āgami Karma: The Karma of the Present Moment

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital–Mental (present action and its new seeds) · Ontic mode: Process (the karma now being generated by present action) · Valence: Neutral — but the very point of freedom · Guṇa: Colored by present action · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: Cause-in-the-making (new consequence being sown) · Key relation: Part — the newly-forming karma that feeds the reservoir; the site of free will; one of the three karmas.
coordinate.seat: Vital–Mental (present action and its new seeds)
coordinate.ontic_mode: Process (the karma now being generated by present action)
coordinate.valence: Neutral — but the very point of freedom
coordinate.key_relation: Part — the newly-forming karma that feeds the reservoir; the site of free will; one of the three karmas.

balanced.label: Equilibrium

facets.somatic: The body acts with what the Yoga tradition calls “effortless effort.” Movements are purposeful without being tense. The hands do their work without gripping.
facets.emotional: There is engagement without obsession. You care about the outcome of your action but you are not chained to it. The Bhagavad Gītā’s instruction to “act without attachment to the fruit” is not philosophical abstraction here—it is the felt quality of a particular emotional stance.
facets.cognitive: The mind is focused on process rather than result. Thoughts are instruments of service rather than vehicles of self-promotion. There is clarity about why you are acting and what the action is meant to accomplish beyond personal gain.
facets.psychic: The witness performs action as an offering. This is Karma Yoga in its purest form: the action is fully engaged, but the actor has stepped aside. The residue left by such action is minimal because the seeds of attachment were not planted.

balanced.text:
Somatic: The body acts with what the Yoga tradition calls “effortless effort.” Movements are purposeful without being tense. The hands do their work without gripping.

Emotional: There is engagement without obsession. You care about the outcome of your action but you are not chained to it. The Bhagavad Gītā’s instruction to “act without attachment to the fruit” is not philosophical abstraction here—it is the felt quality of a particular emotional stance.

Cognitive: The mind is focused on process rather than result. Thoughts are instruments of service rather than vehicles of self-promotion. There is clarity about why you are acting and what the action is meant to accomplish beyond personal gain.

Psychic: The witness performs action as an offering. This is Karma Yoga in its purest form: the action is fully engaged, but the actor has stepped aside. The residue left by such action is minimal because the seeds of attachment were not planted.

excess.label: The Vignette: Equilibrium

facets.overview: Kavya throws herself fully into the work and, at the same time, holds the outcome loosely—caring about the result without being chained to it. Her hands move without gripping. She is planting good seeds precisely because she has stepped, a little, out of the way of her own doing.

excess.text: Kavya throws herself fully into the work and, at the same time, holds the outcome loosely—caring about the result without being chained to it. Her hands move without gripping. She is planting good seeds precisely because she has stepped, a little, out of the way of her own doing.

deficient.label: Excess: The Rajasic Overdrive

facets.somatic: The body is wired, over-caffeinated, perpetually “on.” Muscles remain partially engaged even during rest. The jaw clenches. Sleep is shallow and dream-filled with action sequences.
facets.emotional: A compulsive, driven quality infects every task. The emotional signature is obsession—not passion but its distortion. You cannot stop working, not because the work is meaningful, but because stopping would require you to feel what lies beneath the busyness.
facets.cognitive: Thought is dominated by planning, strategising, and outcome-tracking. The mind generates to-do lists inside to-do lists. Every conversation is evaluated for its utility toward the goal.
facets.psychic: The soul is being taught that there is a difference between right action and compulsive action. The lesson is that doing more does not always mean doing better, and that the quality of intention matters more than the quantity of output.

attributes.title: Dimension II: Samskāras — The Grooves of Memory

coordinate.raw: Seat: Psychic–Mental (latent mental impressions) · Ontic mode: Process / latent residue (impressions laid down by action, deepened by repetition) · Valence: Neutral faculty tending to bondage (grooves that bind, or can be softened) · Guṇa: Takes the coloring of the action · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Mechanism / residue (the deposit that conditions future response) · Key relation: The feedback loop — laid down by Karma, driving Vāsanā; grooves that harden into habit.
coordinate.ontic_mode: Process / latent residue (impressions laid down by action, deepened by repetition)
coordinate.valence: Neutral faculty tending to bondage (grooves that bind, or can be softened)
coordinate.guna: Takes the coloring of the action
coordinate.causal_status: Mechanism / residue (the deposit that conditions future response)
coordinate.key_relation: The feedback loop — laid down by Karma, driving Vāsanā; grooves that harden into habit.

balanced.label: Equilibrium: Impressions as Quiet Competence

facets.somatic: The body moves through familiar tasks with an ease that bypasses deliberation. Your fingers find the piano keys without visual guidance; your legs navigate the staircase of your home in complete darkness. These are the physical signatures of well-formed, adaptive Samskāras—the kind that free attention for higher work.
facets.emotional: There is a warm familiarity in encountering situations that align with deep-set positive impressions. Meeting an old friend, entering a kitchen that smells like your grandmother’s, hearing a piece of music that was playing during a formative experience—these moments carry an emotional resonance that is richer and more complex than the immediate stimulus can account for.
facets.cognitive: The mind draws on stored patterns efficiently. Problem-solving feels less like invention and more like recognition: “I have seen something like this before, and I know a way through.” Expertise, in this sense, is the cognitive expression of well-organised Samskāras.
facets.psychic: The witness recognises that these capabilities are not possessions but deposits—the residue of past effort, neither permanent nor personal in the ultimate sense. There is gratitude for the grooves that serve, and vigilance about the grooves that bind.

excess.label: The Vignette: Equilibrium

facets.overview: Priya’s fingers find the keys without looking and her body navigates the dark house by feel—the deep grooves of practice doing their quiet work so her attention is free for what matters. She is grateful for the habits that serve, and watchful of the ones that bind.

excess.text: Priya’s fingers find the keys without looking and her body navigates the dark house by feel—the deep grooves of practice doing their quiet work so her attention is free for what matters. She is grateful for the habits that serve, and watchful of the ones that bind.

deficient.label: Excess: The Tyranny of Habit

facets.somatic: The body is locked into patterns it did not consciously choose. You reach for the phone before your eyes are fully open. You find yourself in the kitchen eating without having decided to eat. The hands, the mouth, the posture—all are on autopilot, executing programmes written years or decades ago.
facets.emotional: There is a claustrophobic quality to emotional life when Samskāras are excessive. The same emotional responses fire for the same triggers with mechanical predictability. You always get defensive when your partner uses that tone. You always feel anxious in elevators. You always comfort yourself with sugar after a stressful meeting. The emotional range has narrowed to a well-worn rut.
facets.cognitive: Thought becomes confirmation bias incarnate. The mind sees only what it has been trained to see. New information is filtered through old categories. The person who was once burned by a business partner now interprets every collaborative gesture as a potential betrayal. The groove has become a trench.
facets.psychic: The spiritual lesson of excessive Samskāra is that repetition without awareness is bondage. The soul is being called to wake up inside the pattern—to catch the program running and, in that catching, to introduce the possibility of a different choice.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital–Mental (the pull toward social approval) · Ontic mode: Faculty / latent tendency (a vāsanā — the social drive) · Valence: Faculty tending to affliction (binds when it rules) · Guṇa: Rajas-dominant · Scale: Interpersonal (Ādhibhautika) · Causal status: Latent cause (a driving tendency) · Key relation: One of the vāsanās; feeds Lokeśana (the craving for status).
coordinate.ontic_mode: Faculty / latent tendency (a vāsanā — the social drive)
coordinate.valence: Faculty tending to affliction (binds when it rules)
coordinate.key_relation: One of the vāsanās; feeds Lokeśana (the craving for status).

balanced.label: Equilibrium

facets.somatic: The body is at ease in social settings. Posture is open but not performative. Eye contact is natural rather than strategic. There is a physical relaxation that comes from not needing the room to approve of you.
facets.emotional: Social engagement carries a quality of genuine interest. You participate in community not because you need validation but because connection is inherently nourishing. The emotional tone is warmth without neediness.
facets.cognitive: The mind navigates social situations with a healthy awareness of social responsibility and etiquette. You respect community standards because they foster harmony, not because you are desperate for praise.
facets.psychic: The witness sees the social self as a useful interface, not a core identity. You can play social roles without becoming the mask.

balanced.text:
Somatic: The body is at ease in social settings. Posture is open but not performative. Eye contact is natural rather than strategic. There is a physical relaxation that comes from not needing the room to approve of you.

Emotional: Social engagement carries a quality of genuine interest. You participate in community not because you need validation but because connection is inherently nourishing. The emotional tone is warmth without neediness.

Cognitive: The mind navigates social situations with a healthy awareness of social responsibility and etiquette. You respect community standards because they foster harmony, not because you are desperate for praise.

Psychic: The witness sees the social self as a useful interface, not a core identity. You can play social roles without becoming the mask.

excess.label: The Vignette: Equilibrium

facets.overview: Meera loves company and also loves her own quiet; she can be warmed by a crowd without needing it to feel real. The social pull in her is a source of connection, not a leash. She belongs among people and remains herself among them.

excess.text: Meera loves company and also loves her own quiet; she can be warmed by a crowd without needing it to feel real. The social pull in her is a source of connection, not a leash. She belongs among people and remains herself among them.

deficient.label: Excess: The Performer

facets.somatic: The body is perpetually “on stage.” Posture is managed. Facial expressions are curated. After social events, there is a particular exhaustion that comes not from activity but from performance—the maintenance of a persona.
facets.emotional: The dominant transient states are Trāsa (anxiety about judgment), Asūyā (envy of those with higher status), and Mada (pride when recognition arrives). Self-worth oscillates violently between inflation and deflation based entirely on external feedback.
facets.cognitive: Every sentence in every conversation is pre-edited for impression management. The mind runs a constant background process: “How did that land? What do they think of me now? Did I say the right thing?” This consumes enormous cognitive bandwidth.
facets.psychic: The soul is being taught that the social self is a projection—a useful tool that has been mistaken for the master. The spiritual task is to discover who remains when the audience leaves.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Physical–Vital (identification with and care of the body) · Ontic mode: Faculty / latent tendency (a vāsanā — the bodily drive) · Valence: Faculty tending to affliction · Guṇa: Rajas–Tamas · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Latent cause · Key relation: One of the vāsanās; feeds Jīveśana (self-preservation).
coordinate.seat: Physical–Vital (identification with and care of the body)
coordinate.ontic_mode: Faculty / latent tendency (a vāsanā — the bodily drive)
coordinate.key_relation: One of the vāsanās; feeds Jīveśana (self-preservation).

balanced.label: Equilibrium

facets.somatic: The body is maintained as a fit vehicle for engagement—fed well, exercised, rested—without obsession. You notice physical sensations without being enslaved by them.
facets.emotional: There is a neutral affection for the body, like the feeling of a craftsman toward a well-maintained tool. Neither vanity nor neglect.
facets.cognitive: Thoughts about the body are practical and proportionate. You do not spend the morning in front of the mirror, nor do you ignore the body’s signals.
facets.psychic: The witness recognises the body as a temporary dwelling—precious but impermanent. There is care without clinging.

balanced.text:

Psychic: The witness recognises the body as a temporary dwelling—precious but impermanent. There is care without clinging.

excess.label: The Vignette: Equilibrium

facets.overview: Kira inhabits her body gladly—feeds it, moves it, rests it—treating it as a good home rather than either an idol or an enemy. She takes real pleasure in the physical without being ruled by it. The body is a partner, and she treats it like one.

excess.text: Kira inhabits her body gladly—feeds it, moves it, rests it—treating it as a good home rather than either an idol or an enemy. She takes real pleasure in the physical without being ruled by it. The body is a partner, and she treats it like one.

deficient.label: Excess: The Imprisoned Self

facets.somatic: Every physical sensation is amplified. A minor blemish, a new wrinkle, a slight pain becomes an emotional crisis. The body is no longer an instrument but a cage whose walls are inspected obsessively.
facets.emotional: The dominant transient states are Mada (vanity) and Glāni (a heavy, draining exhaustion tied to bodily anxiety). Every ageing sign is met with terror; every physical compliment is hoarded as proof of worth.
facets.cognitive: Thought is dominated by the body’s appearance and condition. Hours are spent on grooming routines, health forums, or hypochondriac research. The mind cannot think about anything else because the body has absorbed the entire identity.
facets.psychic: The soul is being confronted with a fundamental question: “Am I this body, or am I the awareness that inhabits it?” The excess is the teacher, pushing the identification to its breaking point.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental (the pull toward knowledge and study) · Ontic mode: Faculty / latent tendency (a vāsanā — the intellectual drive) · Valence: Faculty — wholesome in measure, binding as intellectual accumulation · Guṇa: Sattva–Rajas · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Latent cause · Key relation: One of the vāsanās; can bind even the seeker through over-study.
coordinate.seat: Mental (the pull toward knowledge and study)
coordinate.ontic_mode: Faculty / latent tendency (a vāsanā — the intellectual drive)
coordinate.valence: Faculty — wholesome in measure, binding as intellectual accumulation
coordinate.key_relation: One of the vāsanās; can bind even the seeker through over-study.

balanced.label: Equilibrium

facets.somatic: Study is balanced with practice. The body is used as a laboratory for the theories the mind has absorbed. You read about Prāṇāyāma and then practice it; you study the Guṇas and then observe them in your own behaviour.
facets.emotional: Knowledge brings a quiet joy—the satisfaction of understanding aligned with the humility of knowing how much remains unknown.
facets.cognitive: The mind uses scriptures as a map, not a destination. Knowledge refines conduct and deepens perception. You do not study to win arguments; you study to see more clearly.
facets.psychic: The witness holds knowledge lightly, knowing that the finger pointing at the moon is not the moon.

excess.label: The Vignette: Equilibrium

facets.overview: Ravi reads widely and lets what he learns change how he lives; knowledge, for him, is a lamp, not a trophy. He can hold a hard idea and also put the book down and be fully present at dinner. His learning feeds his living.

excess.text: Ravi reads widely and lets what he learns change how he lives; knowledge, for him, is a lamp, not a trophy. He can hold a hard idea and also put the book down and be fully present at dinner. His learning feeds his living.

deficient.label: Excess: The Intellectual Hoarder

facets.somatic: The body atrophies. Hours are spent in sedentary study. The physical world recedes as the mind builds elaborate conceptual structures that have no foundation in embodied experience.
facets.emotional: The dominant transient states are Mada (intellectual pride), Garva (haughtiness), and Amarsha (impatience with those who know less). Debate becomes blood sport.
facets.cognitive: The mind is a fortress of definitions, citations, and arguments. Every conversation is an opportunity to display knowledge. The irony is devastating: the person has read every text about ego-dissolution but their ego has never been larger.
facets.psychic: The soul is learning that information is not realisation, that knowing about fire is not the same as being warm.

attributes.attribute: Lokeśana: The Craving for Fame and Status
attributes.title: Lokeśana: The Craving for Fame and Status

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital–Mental (the hunger for recognition) · Ontic mode: Root craving (an eśana — a foundational drive) · Valence: Affliction (a root craving to see through) · Guṇa: Rajas-dominant · Scale: Interpersonal (Ādhibhautika) · Causal status: Root cause (a foundational craving that sparks the loop) · Key relation: One of the four eśanas; springs from Loka-vāsanā.

balanced.label: Equilibrium

facets.somatic: The body is relaxed in public spaces. There is no compulsive self-monitoring—no checking of reflection in shop windows, no adjusting of posture when a perceived authority enters the room.
facets.emotional: A quiet sense of purpose that does not require external affirmation. You contribute your best work because the work itself demands it, not because the audience is watching.
facets.cognitive: The mind evaluates feedback on its merits. Criticism does not destabilise; praise does not inflate. There is a steady internal compass that social weather cannot permanently alter.
facets.psychic: The witness understands that reputation is a shadow cast by action—useful but ultimately insubstantial. Identity rests in the witness, not in the reflection.

Psychic: The witness understands that reputation is a shadow cast by action—useful but ultimately insubstantial. Identity rests in the witness, not in the reflection.

excess.label: The Vignette: Equilibrium

facets.overview: Sana likes to be seen for good work and does not organize her life around it. Recognition is welcome when it comes and not chased when it doesn’t; her worth rests on something the crowd can neither grant nor take away. She wants to be respected, and she can also let it go.

excess.text: Sana likes to be seen for good work and does not organize her life around it. Recognition is welcome when it comes and not chased when it doesn’t; her worth rests on something the crowd can neither grant nor take away. She wants to be respected, and she can also let it go.

deficient.label: Excess: The Prisoner of the Gaze

facets.somatic: Chronic tension in the face and throat—the muscles of “presentation.” The body is perpetually braced for evaluation. Heart rate spikes at the ping of a notification.
facets.emotional: A volatile emotional economy where self-worth is denominated in likes, titles, and invitations. The transient states are Trāsa (fear of being “cancelled”), Asūyā (envy of those with higher visibility), and the particular agony of Autsukya (impatient longing for the next validation hit).
facets.cognitive: Every action is pre-filtered through the question: “How will this look?” Authenticity is sacrificed at the altar of optics. The person tailors every sentence to sound impressive rather than true.
facets.psychic: The soul is trapped behind a mask it has mistaken for its own face. The spiritual lesson is that the social self is a projection; the real self persists even when the audience leaves.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital–Mental (the hunger for wealth and security) · Ontic mode: Root craving (an eśana) · Valence: Affliction (a root craving) · Guṇa: Rajas-dominant · Scale: Individual, with material field · Causal status: Root cause · Key relation: One of the four eśanas; drives acquisitive action.
coordinate.seat: Vital–Mental (the hunger for wealth and security)
coordinate.key_relation: One of the four eśanas; drives acquisitive action.

balanced.label: Equilibrium

facets.somatic: The body is comfortable with enough. There is no hoarding impulse in the muscles, no clenched fist around possessions. Material resources are managed with the steady hand of a custodian rather than the tight grip of an owner.
facets.emotional: Financial matters are handled with equanimity. A downturn in the market does not produce existential terror; a windfall does not produce euphoria. Wealth is a tool, and tools do not define their wielder.
facets.cognitive: The mind understands the difference between sufficiency and excess. Financial planning is pragmatic rather than obsessive. Generosity flows naturally because there is no core belief in scarcity.
facets.psychic: The witness sees wealth as a trust to be managed for the benefit of the Whole, not a fortress to be defended.

balanced.text:
Somatic: The body is comfortable with enough. There is no hoarding impulse in the muscles, no clenched fist around possessions. Material resources are managed with the steady hand of a custodian rather than the tight grip of an owner.

Emotional: Financial matters are handled with equanimity. A downturn in the market does not produce existential terror; a windfall does not produce euphoria. Wealth is a tool, and tools do not define their wielder.

Cognitive: The mind understands the difference between sufficiency and excess. Financial planning is pragmatic rather than obsessive. Generosity flows naturally because there is no core belief in scarcity.

Psychic: The witness sees wealth as a trust to be managed for the benefit of the Whole, not a fortress to be defended.

excess.label: The Vignette: Equilibrium

facets.overview: Kira earns, saves, and spends with a clear head—money a useful tool she neither worships nor despises. ‘Enough’ is a number she can actually name, and having reached it, she is not driven to keep piling. Wealth serves her life rather than consuming it.

excess.text: Kira earns, saves, and spends with a clear head—money a useful tool she neither worships nor despises. ‘Enough’ is a number she can actually name, and having reached it, she is not driven to keep piling. Wealth serves her life rather than consuming it.

deficient.label: Excess: The Bottomless Pit

facets.somatic: A physical tightness in the chest and stomach—the somatic signature of “not enough.” The body is in a permanent state of low-grade acquisition mode. Shopping, checking investment portfolios, counting—these activities produce a brief somatic release followed by a deeper contraction.
facets.emotional: The dominant transient states are Lobha (greed—a tightening hunger that no amount of accumulation satisfies), Trāsa (anxiety about losing what has been gained), and the relentless Autsukya (impatient longing for the next acquisition). Financial loss is experienced not as a practical setback but as an assault on identity.
facets.cognitive: The mind reduces all relationships and opportunities to their financial dimension. People are evaluated for their economic utility. The internal monologue is dominated by calculations, projections, and scenarios of loss.
facets.psychic: The soul is learning that material security, pursued to its limit, reveals itself as a mirage—each destination recedes as you approach it.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital (the hunger for progeny and legacy) · Ontic mode: Root craving (an eśana) · Valence: Affliction (a root craving) · Guṇa: Rajas-dominant · Scale: Interpersonal / generational · Causal status: Root cause · Key relation: One of the four eśanas; the craving to extend the self through legacy.
coordinate.seat: Vital (the hunger for progeny and legacy)
coordinate.key_relation: One of the four eśanas; the craving to extend the self through legacy.

balanced.label: Equilibrium

facets.somatic: The body is at peace with its temporality. You hold a child, mentor a student, or tend a creative project with full presence, knowing that the act of nurturing is complete in itself, regardless of what endures.
facets.emotional: There is joy in contributing to the next generation or the next iteration without needing it to bear your name. The emotional quality is one of open-handed giving.
facets.cognitive: The mind sees the difference between contributing to the river and damming the river. You understand that true legacy is not in what bears your signature but in the quality of consciousness you transmit.
facets.psychic: The witness knows that continuity at the deepest level is not of the body or the name but of the Dharma—the universal order that persists beyond any individual expression.

balanced.text:
Somatic: The body is at peace with its temporality. You hold a child, mentor a student, or tend a creative project with full presence, knowing that the act of nurturing is complete in itself, regardless of what endures.

Emotional: There is joy in contributing to the next generation or the next iteration without needing it to bear your name. The emotional quality is one of open-handed giving.

Cognitive: The mind sees the difference between contributing to the river and damming the river. You understand that true legacy is not in what bears your signature but in the quality of consciousness you transmit.

Psychic: The witness knows that continuity at the deepest level is not of the body or the name but of the Dharma—the universal order that persists beyond any individual expression.

excess.label: The Vignette: Equilibrium

facets.overview: Meera pours real love into what will outlast her—her children, her work, the young people she mentors—and then lets them be free to become themselves. She plants trees whose shade she may never sit in, gladly, and does not need to control how they grow. Her legacy is a gift, not a leash.

deficient.label: Excess: The Puppet Master

facets.somatic: A clutching energy in the hands and arms—the physical signature of someone who cannot let go. Shoulders are raised as though bracing against the current of time.
facets.emotional: Asūyā (envy of other families’ or successors’ achievements), Mohā (delusion regarding the importance of one’s family name), and Amarsha (impatience when a student or child challenges your “wisdom”). The successor becomes a canvas for the ego’s unfinished self-portrait.
facets.cognitive: A narrative of ownership: “They are my legacy. They must do things my way.” The mind confuses guidance with control, love with possession.
facets.psychic: The soul is being asked: “Are you loving this person, or are you loving your own reflection in them?”
