attributes.title: Avidyā — The Root: Mistaking the Self
attributes.aspect: Avidyā is the root affliction — the structural error of mistaking the impermanent for permanent, the impure for pure, the painful for pleasant, and the not-Self for the Self. Patañjali names it the field in which all the other afflictions grow. It is not stupidity and not lack of education; it is a misperception so basic that it usually never becomes an object of attention at all.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Psychic–Mental (the root misperception of self) · Ontic mode: Affliction — the root kleśa (primal ignorance) · Valence: Affliction (the root to see through) · Guṇa: Tamas-dominant (obscuration) · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: Root cause (the soil from which the other four grow) · Key relation: Whole / root — the ground of Asmitā, Rāga, Dveṣa, and Abhiniveśa; mistakes the eternal Self for the transient.
coordinate.causal_status: Root cause (the soil from which the other four grow)
coordinate.key_relation: Whole / root — the ground of Asmitā, Rāga, Dveṣa, and Abhiniveśa; mistakes the eternal Self for the transient.

balanced.label: In excess — ignorance disguised as attainment

facets.overview: Cannot have excessive ignorance in a yogic sense; however, intellectual claims of removed ignorance without realization create spiritual arrogance.
facets.somatic: Body neglect from false transcendence claims, ungrounded physical state. Identification with body as Self, fear of death, body anxiety, illness from misperception of nature.
facets.emotional: Emotional bypassing claiming Avidya is removed, false transcendence, spiritual arrogance. All emotional suffering arises here—from misidentification with the impermanent, emotional turmoil.
facets.cognitive: Intellectual conviction without realization, spiritual pride, false claims of awakening, dangerous self-deception. Fundamental confusion about reality, mistaking impermanent for permanent, all mental suffering, foundation of all kleshas.
facets.psychic: The soul is subtly betrayed by the mind’s claim to have arrived; the lesson at play is humility — that realization cannot be performed or asserted, only lived. Avidya in its fullest form creates the entire structure of suffering—all other kleshas arise from it. The person mistakes everything fundamental. The soul is wholly veiled from itself, taking the perishable for its own being; the lesson waiting beneath all others is that one is simply not what one has mistaken oneself to be. For Dan, the passing show is simply reality, full stop. He is his thoughts, his roles, his possessions; the impermanent is treated as permanent, the borrowed as owned, and every loss therefore lands as a catastrophe. Living entirely inside the basic misperception, he suffers exactly as much as the misperception guarantees—which is to say, constantly. In conduct, a person living from refined viveka holds roles and possessions lightly: they can lose a title or a relationship and grieve cleanly without an identity collapse, because they never confused the role with the self. Where avidyā is full-grown, every threat to the body, the reputation, or the self-image registers as a threat to existence itself — so small slights become unbearable, and ordinary change feels like a small death. Maya, 41, is passed over for a promotion she expected. In the full-grown grip of avidyā, the thought is not ‘I did not get the role’ but ‘I am a failure’ — the event and the self fuse, and a sleepless, churning night follows in which her whole worth feels revoked. On a clearer morning weeks later, the same memory returns, but now there is a hairline gap between the fact and the self: the role was wanted, the role was not given, and the one who wanted it remains, untouched, watching. Nothing about the event changed. The coloring did.

balanced.vignette: Ravi has read the books and had the experiences and is now quite certain he ‘gets it’—which is precisely the problem. His ignorance has put on the robes of attainment; the subtle self-deception is now armored in spiritual language, harder to see through than ordinary confusion. He is asleep and dreaming that he is awake.

attributes.title: Asmitā — The Separate ‘I’
attributes.aspect: Asmitā is the confusion of the seer with the instrument of seeing — when pure awareness and the mind through which it looks appear as one and the same thing. Patañjali defines it as the apparent identity of the power of consciousness and the power of the mental faculty. Practically, it is the felt sense of being a separate, bounded someone: the ‘I’ that must be defended, enlarged, and confirmed.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Psychic–Mental (the ego-sense) · Ontic mode: Affliction — a kleśa (‘I-am-ness’) · Valence: Affliction (to attenuate; a working ‘I’ held lightly is its refined form) · Guṇa: Rajas–Tamas · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: First branch (the first growth from Avidyā) · Key relation: Branch of Avidyā; kin to Ahaṁkāra (Dim 3); parent to Rāga and Dveṣa.
coordinate.valence: Affliction (to attenuate; a working ‘I’ held lightly is its refined form)
coordinate.causal_status: First branch (the first growth from Avidyā)
coordinate.key_relation: Branch of Avidyā; kin to Ahaṁkāra (Dim 3); parent to Rāga and Dveṣa.

balanced.label: In excess — inflation, self-reference, the swollen self

facets.overview: Excessive Asmita is dominant ego-identification—the person is completely identified with personality, body, status, achievements. All other kleshas reinforce it.
facets.somatic: Body completely identified with as self, physical attachment to appearance and abilities, anxiety about body changes. Inability to maintain healthy boundaries, physical vulnerability, inability to act in world.
facets.emotional: Emotional defensive of ego, constant emotional reactivity to ego threats, suffering from ego-bruising. Identity confusion, inability to maintain healthy emotional boundaries, vulnerability to manipulation.
facets.cognitive: Complete mental identification with personality, no witness awareness, all suffering reinforced by ego. Confusion about self-identity, inability to function in world, premature dissolution without realization.
facets.psychic: The soul is eclipsed by the swollen self; the lesson at play is the quiet deflation of the separate ‘I,’ the recognition that what it defends so fiercely is not finally real. Healthy ego-function is necessary for practical living. Complete dissolution before realization is unstable; healthy ego serves discrimination. The soul’s lesson is mis-applied — the working self undone before its time; the invitation is to a maturity that can hold a functioning ‘I’ even while knowing it is not ultimate. Sam has read that the ego is the enemy and set about dissolving his—before he ever built a stable one—and now he cannot make a decision, hold a boundary, or say what he wants. The premature erasure of a healthy ‘I’ has left him not enlightened but ineffectual, blown about by everyone, mistaking his own dysfunction for selflessness. A refined asmitā still uses the word ‘I’ — it signs emails, keeps appointments, takes responsibility — but the sense of self is functional rather than absolute, a costume worn knowingly. In its inflated form it shows as the constant subtle scorekeeping of comparison, the need to be right, the hunger for recognition. In its over-corrected form — the working self prematurely talked away before it is genuinely transcended — it shows as an inability to hold healthy boundaries, make decisions, or function practically, the ordinary ego undermined before anything stable has taken its place. In a team meeting, Maya’s idea is improved upon by a colleague. Through inflated asmitā, the improvement lands as a wound — a flush of heat, a rehearsed rebuttal, the silent insistence that her version was better. Through refined asmitā, the same improvement lands as simply better, and there is an odd lightness in saying so out loud: the idea was never her, so its improvement costs her nothing.

balanced.vignette: Everything refers back to Ben. Every story becomes his story, every conversation an occasion for his opinion, every slight an assault on a self that has swollen to fill the room. The functional ‘I’ has inflated into a tyrant that must be fed and defended constantly, and the feeding is exhausting—for him and for everyone near him.

attributes.title: Rāga — The Pull Toward Pleasure
attributes.aspect: Rāga is attachment that follows pleasure — the dwelling on past enjoyments and the leaning-forward to repeat them. It is not the enjoyment itself, which is innocent, but the residue enjoyment leaves: a track laid down in memory that pulls the mind back toward the source and quietly organizes future behavior around getting more.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital (attraction, craving) · Ontic mode: Affliction — a kleśa (attachment) · Valence: Affliction (craving to attenuate) · Guṇa: Rajas-dominant · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: Branch (grows from Asmitā / Avidyā) · Key relation: Branch of Avidyā; antagonist-pair with Dveṣa; drives the pleasant pole of Valence (Dim 7).
coordinate.key_relation: Branch of Avidyā; antagonist-pair with Dveṣa; drives the pleasant pole of Valence (Dim 7).

balanced.label: In excess — craving, grasping, the never-enough

facets.overview: Excessive Raga creates addiction, compulsive seeking of pleasure, inability to stop pursuing what brought pleasure once, slavery to enjoyment.
facets.somatic: Addiction to pleasurable substances or activities, physical bondage to pleasure-objects, health damaged by pursuit. Physical suppression of natural responses, body in conflict with itself, illness from forced denial.
facets.emotional: Emotional slavery to pleasurable feelings, addiction to emotional highs, emotional suffering from unrealized desire. Emotional suppression and pretense of non-attachment, eventual emotional eruption, false coldness.
facets.cognitive: Obsessive mental focus on objects of pleasure, inability to think of anything else, mental bondage. Mental suppression of natural attraction, eventual breakdown, distorted thinking from forced denial.
facets.psychic: The soul forgets its own completeness and seeks itself in objects; the lesson at play is the discovery that no outer thing can fill an inner fullness that was never actually missing. Forced suppression of all attraction without wisdom creates harsh asceticism, denial of natural responses, eventual explosive release. The soul’s lesson is forced rather than understood; the invitation is to a dispassion that flowers from clear seeing, not from the violent denial of a still-active wanting. Determined to be ‘above’ desire, Priya has forcibly denied herself every pleasure—the good meal, the soft comfort, the ordinary joy—until her life has gone grey and brittle with renunciation. This is not freedom from craving but a war against it, and the war has hardened her into a joyless asceticism that is its own kind of bondage. With refined rāga, a person enjoys the good meal, the warm praise, the comfortable evening — fully — and then lets it pass without needing to bank it against future lack. In excess, the same pleasures become demands: the second drink, the next acquisition, the compulsive return to the thing that once delivered relief, each repetition delivering a little less while the reaching grows. Daily life narrows around the maintenance of supply. After the disappointing week, Maya orders the dress she has been eyeing. Through full-grown rāga, the click of ‘buy’ brings a real lift — followed within an hour by a flat, hungry feeling and the browser already open to the next thing; the pleasure was real and the relief did not last, and the gap is filled by reaching again. On a steadier day she buys the same dress, enjoys it plainly, and notices — without drama — that the wanting and the having were two different states, and that she is, underneath both, already alright.

balanced.text:
Excessive Raga creates addiction, compulsive seeking of pleasure, inability to stop pursuing what brought pleasure once, slavery to enjoyment.

Somatic: Addiction to pleasurable substances or activities, physical bondage to pleasure-objects, health damaged by pursuit. Physical suppression of natural responses, body in conflict with itself, illness from forced denial.

Emotional: Emotional slavery to pleasurable feelings, addiction to emotional highs, emotional suffering from unrealized desire. Emotional suppression and pretense of non-attachment, eventual emotional eruption, false coldness.

Cognitive: Obsessive mental focus on objects of pleasure, inability to think of anything else, mental bondage. Mental suppression of natural attraction, eventual breakdown, distorted thinking from forced denial.

Psychic: The soul forgets its own completeness and seeks itself in objects; the lesson at play is the discovery that no outer thing can fill an inner fullness that was never actually missing. Forced suppression of all attraction without wisdom creates harsh asceticism, denial of natural responses, eventual explosive release. The soul’s lesson is forced rather than understood; the invitation is to a dispassion that flowers from clear seeing, not from the violent denial of a still-active wanting. Determined to be ‘above’ desire, Priya has forcibly denied herself every pleasure—the good meal, the soft comfort, the ordinary joy—until her life has gone grey and brittle with renunciation. This is not freedom from craving but a war against it, and the war has hardened her into a joyless asceticism that is its own kind of bondage. With refined rāga, a person enjoys the good meal, the warm praise, the comfortable evening — fully — and then lets it pass without needing to bank it against future lack. In excess, the same pleasures become demands: the second drink, the next acquisition, the compulsive return to the thing that once delivered relief, each repetition delivering a little less while the reaching grows. Daily life narrows around the maintenance of supply. After the disappointing week, Maya orders the dress she has been eyeing. Through full-grown rāga, the click of ‘buy’ brings a real lift — followed within an hour by a flat, hungry feeling and the browser already open to the next thing; the pleasure was real and the relief did not last, and the gap is filled by reaching again. On a steadier day she buys the same dress, enjoys it plainly, and notices — without drama — that the wanting and the having were two different states, and that she is, underneath both, already alright.
balanced.vignette: One is never enough for Tom—one drink, one purchase, one hit of approval—each pleasure immediately generating the craving for the next, so that satisfaction evaporates on contact. He is not enjoying; he is grasping, chasing a fullness that recedes the faster he reaches. The wanting has eaten the having.

attributes.title: Dveṣa — The Recoil from Pain
attributes.aspect: Dveṣa is aversion that follows pain — the mirror image of rāga. It is the residue of past painful experience hardening into recoil: the dwelling on what hurt and the active avoidance of anything that resembles it. Where rāga reaches toward, dveṣa pushes away; together they form the two arms of the same grasping reflex.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital (aversion, recoil) · Ontic mode: Affliction — a kleśa (aversion) · Valence: Affliction (aversion to attenuate) · Guṇa: Rajas–Tamas · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: Branch (grows from Asmitā / Avidyā) · Key relation: Branch of Avidyā; antagonist-pair with Rāga; drives the unpleasant pole of Valence (Dim 7).
coordinate.key_relation: Branch of Avidyā; antagonist-pair with Rāga; drives the unpleasant pole of Valence (Dim 7).

balanced.label: In excess — hatred, blame, the hardened ‘no’

facets.overview: Excessive Dvesha creates chronic aversion, hatred, avoidance of growth-producing challenges, obsessive flight from unpleasantness.
facets.somatic: Avoidance of necessary physical challenges, stress from constant aversion, illness from chronic resistance. Accepting physical harm without protective response, inability to recognize physical danger.
facets.emotional: Chronic emotional aversion, hatred patterns, emotional suffering from inability to face difficulties. Emotional inability to feel appropriate concern, accepting toxic relationships, no protective response.
facets.cognitive: Obsessive avoidance thinking, hatred and rejection patterns, mental contraction from aversion. Inability to discriminate against harmful conditions, mental acceptance of what should be avoided.
facets.psychic: The soul is bound to what it hates as tightly as to what it craves; the lesson at play is forgiveness — the release of the self from the prison of its own recoil. Complete absence of aversion can lead to inability to recognize harm, accepting toxic conditions, no protective discrimination. The soul’s discernment is lost in a false, flattened equanimity; the invitation is to a clarity that can still say a clean ‘no’ to what genuinely harms. So committed to being accepting and non-judgmental, Dana has lost the ability to refuse anything—tolerating the exploitative friend, the abusive boss, the genuinely harmful situation, because saying ‘no’ feels like a failure of love. The healthy aversion that should protect her has been over-corrected into a dangerous inability to recognize and refuse real harm. Refined dveṣa allows a clean ‘no, thank you’ — a clear declining of what does not serve, without the heat of grievance. In excess it becomes resentment, fault-finding, the rehearsing of injuries, the conviction that certain people or experiences are simply wrong, and an avoidance that quietly shrinks the world. Over-corrected in the other direction — aversion suppressed entirely — a person loses the healthy ‘no’ altogether: unable to recognize what genuinely harms them, they tolerate toxic situations and people they would be wise to move away from. Maya runs into the colleague who got the promotion. Through full-grown dveṣa, her body tightens before a word is spoken — jaw, shoulders, a cool clipped voice — and the rest of the afternoon is spent silently re-litigating the unfairness. Through refined dveṣa, she registers the flare of dislike as a passing weather, greets him plainly, and finds the charge has already drained by the time she sits back down; the aversion arose and was simply not fed.

balanced.vignette: Ravi’s dislikes have hardened into hatreds. The person who wronged him, the group he opposes, the food he won’t eat—each is met not with clean refusal but with a clenched, blaming animosity that colors his whole day. The healthy ‘no’ has become a fist, and the constant contraction of hating is quietly corroding him from within.

attributes.title: Abhiniveśa — The Clinging to Life
attributes.aspect: Abhiniveśa is the deep, instinctive clinging to continued existence — the will to live and the flinch from annihilation. Patañjali notes that it is firmly rooted even in the wise, and arises by its own nature. It is the most subtle and persistent of the afflictions because it underlies every other fear: every smaller dread is, at bottom, a rehearsal of this one.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Physical–Vital (the survival instinct, fear of death) · Ontic mode: Affliction — a kleśa (clinging to life) · Valence: Affliction (the death-grip to attenuate) · Guṇa: Tamas-dominant · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: Deepest branch (the most tenacious, rooted even in the wise) · Key relation: Branch of Avidyā; kin to Jīveśana (Dim 12); the last kleśa to dissolve.
coordinate.seat: Physical–Vital (the survival instinct, fear of death)
coordinate.causal_status: Deepest branch (the most tenacious, rooted even in the wise)
coordinate.key_relation: Branch of Avidyā; kin to Jīveśana (Dim 12); the last kleśa to dissolve.

balanced.label: In excess — frantic self-preservation, control, the armored life

facets.overview: Extreme Abhinivesha creates phobic fear of death, anxiety disorders, hypochondria, obsessive concern with safety and life extension.
facets.somatic: Health anxiety, hypochondria, body in chronic fear-state, stress-related illness from death-fear. Reckless physical behavior, inability to protect physical body, neglect of survival needs.
facets.emotional: Chronic anxiety about death, phobic fear, emotional suffering from clinging, anxious gripping at life. Death-wish from depression, suicidal feelings (pathological not transcendent), emotional emptiness.
facets.cognitive: Obsessive thoughts about death and survival, mental contraction from fear, anxious planning to avoid death. Reckless thinking, suicidal ideation, mental disregard for life, inappropriate detachment from survival.
facets.psychic: The soul clings to the form it must one day release; the lesson at play is the deepest surrender, the loosening of the very root from which every smaller fear grows. False or premature absence of life-instinct can be suicidal ideation, reckless behavior, depression with death-wish—not genuine transcendence but pathology. The soul’s lesson is counterfeited — the flight from fear mistaken for freedom from it; the invitation is to meet the fear of death consciously rather than to override the body’s own wisdom. Dan courts danger with a strange flat calm—the reckless driving, the ignored symptom, the needless risk—as if the healthy instinct to preserve his own life had simply switched off. It is not courage; it is the absence of a basic, appropriate self-protection, and those who love him watch, helpless, as he treats his one irreplaceable life as though it were disposable. Where abhiniveśa is loosened, a person can take real risks, speak uncomfortable truths, age and change and let go of phases of life with grace, because survival is not silently at stake in every choice. In excess it shows as over-control — of health, of money, of the future, of other people — a life slowly armored against a threat that can never be fully secured against, often with a low background hum of dread driving the compulsive checking. Over-corrected in the other direction — the healthy life-instinct itself suppressed or lost — it can show as recklessness, a careless disregard for one’s own safety, even the dark pull toward self-destruction. A routine health scare — an ambiguous test result, a week’s wait. Through full-grown abhiniveśa, Maya cannot sleep; the mind runs every catastrophe, the body floods with adrenaline at 3 a.m., and the waiting feels like standing at an edge. Through a more refined relationship to it, the fear still comes — it is, after all, rooted even in the wise — but she can feel it as a wave that rises and passes, can hold the genuine uncertainty without being swept off her feet, and can notice, in the quiet after the wave, an awareness that was never in danger at all.

balanced.vignette: Ben has built his whole life into a fortress against death—the obsessive health regimens, the avoidance of every risk, the low constant hum of mortal dread beneath everything. So busy armoring himself against the end, he has forgotten to live in the middle; the frantic clinging to life has, ironically, drained the life right out of it.

attributes.title: Pramāṇa — Valid Cognition
attributes.aspect: Pramana is right cognition—correct knowledge through direct perception, inference, or authoritative testimony. It is one of the five types of mental modifications (vrittis) that yoga seeks to still.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental (the mind’s valid knowing) · Ontic mode: Vṛtti — a mental modification (valid cognition) · Valence: Neutral faculty (useful; to be stilled, not scorned) · Guṇa: Sattva-dominant (clarity) · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: Manifestation (perception, inference, and testimony) · Key relation: One of the five vṛttis — the sattvic mode; stilled in Citta-Vṛtti-Nirodha (Dim 11).
coordinate.valence: Neutral faculty (useful; to be stilled, not scorned)
coordinate.key_relation: One of the five vṛttis — the sattvic mode; stilled in Citta-Vṛtti-Nirodha (Dim 11).

balanced.label: Balanced — clear, accurate knowing

facets.overview: When Pramana operates clearly, the mind accurately knows what it perceives, infers, or learns from valid sources. Knowledge corresponds to reality.
facets.somatic: Accurate sensory perception, healthy sensory function, body that responds to actual reality, clear seeing.
facets.emotional: Emotional clarity from accurate perception, feelings based on reality not distortion, emotional truth.
facets.cognitive: Clear correct knowledge, accurate perception, sound reasoning, ability to learn from valid sources, mental clarity.
facets.psychic: The soul is well served by a mind that sees truly; valid knowing becomes a clear window onto the real, and discernment ripens toward viveka.

balanced.text:
When Pramana operates clearly, the mind accurately knows what it perceives, infers, or learns from valid sources. Knowledge corresponds to reality.

Somatic: Accurate sensory perception, healthy sensory function, body that responds to actual reality, clear seeing.

Emotional: Emotional clarity from accurate perception, feelings based on reality not distortion, emotional truth.

Cognitive: Clear correct knowledge, accurate perception, sound reasoning, ability to learn from valid sources, mental clarity.

Psychic: The soul is well served by a mind that sees truly; valid knowing becomes a clear window onto the real, and discernment ripens toward viveka.
balanced.vignette: When Priya needs to know something, she checks—weighs the evidence, tests the claim, distinguishes what she knows from what she merely assumes—and arrives at a judgment she can trust. Her knowing is clean and accurate, neither credulous nor paranoid. She believes true things for good reasons, which turns out to be rarer than it sounds.

excess.label: In excess — over-analysis, the tyranny of proof

facets.overview: Over-reliance on Pramana without spiritual practice can become intellectual pride, scientism, dismissal of subtler realities.
facets.somatic: Over-reliance on physical evidence, dismissal of subtle perception, materialist limitation.
facets.emotional: Cold intellectual approach, dismissal of emotional knowing, emotional coldness from rationalism.
facets.cognitive: Intellectual arrogance, dismissal of intuitive knowledge, scientism, mental rigidity about what’s real.
facets.psychic: The faculty meant to serve the soul crowds it out — knowing hardened into the compulsion to be certain, the mind mistaking accumulated proof for wisdom.

excess.text:
Over-reliance on Pramana without spiritual practice can become intellectual pride, scientism, dismissal of subtler realities.

Somatic: Over-reliance on physical evidence, dismissal of subtle perception, materialist limitation.

Emotional: Cold intellectual approach, dismissal of emotional knowing, emotional coldness from rationalism.

Cognitive: Intellectual arrogance, dismissal of intuitive knowledge, scientism, mental rigidity about what’s real.

Psychic: The faculty meant to serve the soul crowds it out — knowing hardened into the compulsion to be certain, the mind mistaking accumulated proof for wisdom.
excess.vignette: Ravi cannot accept anything without exhaustive proof. He second-guesses settled facts, demands certainty where none is available, and researches a restaurant for an hour before dinner—the healthy demand for evidence hypertrophied into a tyranny that paralyzes. He knows a great deal and can act on almost none of it, because nothing is ever proven quite enough.

deficient.label: Deficient — poor reality-testing, credulity

facets.overview: Lack of valid knowledge means living based on misperception, fantasy, or unreliable sources. The other vrittis (especially Viparyaya - wrong knowledge) dominate.
facets.somatic: Acting on inaccurate perception, physical consequences of misperception, accidents.
facets.emotional: Feelings based on distortion, emotional suffering from incorrect knowing.
facets.cognitive: Living in wrong knowledge, mental confusion, susceptibility to deception, no foundation for wisdom.
facets.psychic: The soul is left without reliable footing; when valid cognition fails, trust in one’s own seeing erodes and the inner life drifts on fantasy and hearsay. In balance, pramāṇa shows as good judgment: a person who checks before believing, distinguishes what they actually know from what they assume, and updates when the evidence changes. In excess, it hardens into a need to verify everything, an inability to trust intuition or another person’s word, a life spent demanding proof until spontaneity and warmth are squeezed out. In deficiency, it collapses into credulity — believing too quickly, unable to tell a sound source from a flattering one, swept by whatever was said most confidently. A friend forwards Maya a startling health claim. Through over-active pramāṇa she cannot let it rest until she has read six studies, and the evening dissolves into tabs and counter-tabs. Through deficient pramāṇa she would simply forward it on, alarmed and convinced. In balance she notes the claim, registers that she doesn’t yet know, makes one quick check of a reliable source, and moves on — holding the question lightly instead of being held by it.

deficient.text:
Lack of valid knowledge means living based on misperception, fantasy, or unreliable sources. The other vrittis (especially Viparyaya - wrong knowledge) dominate.

Somatic: Acting on inaccurate perception, physical consequences of misperception, accidents.

Emotional: Feelings based on distortion, emotional suffering from incorrect knowing.

Cognitive: Living in wrong knowledge, mental confusion, susceptibility to deception, no foundation for wisdom.

Psychic: The soul is left without reliable footing; when valid cognition fails, trust in one’s own seeing erodes and the inner life drifts on fantasy and hearsay. In balance, pramāṇa shows as good judgment: a person who checks before believing, distinguishes what they actually know from what they assume, and updates when the evidence changes. In excess, it hardens into a need to verify everything, an inability to trust intuition or another person’s word, a life spent demanding proof until spontaneity and warmth are squeezed out. In deficiency, it collapses into credulity — believing too quickly, unable to tell a sound source from a flattering one, swept by whatever was said most confidently. A friend forwards Maya a startling health claim. Through over-active pramāṇa she cannot let it rest until she has read six studies, and the evening dissolves into tabs and counter-tabs. Through deficient pramāṇa she would simply forward it on, alarmed and convinced. In balance she notes the claim, registers that she doesn’t yet know, makes one quick check of a reliable source, and moves on — holding the question lightly instead of being held by it.
deficient.vignette: Dan believes whatever he last heard. The confident stranger, the viral post, the too-good deal—each is accepted whole, without a flicker of reality-testing, and he is fleeced and fooled again and again with the same wide-eyed surprise. The faculty that would check a claim against reality has simply never come online.

attributes.title: Viparyaya — Erroneous Cognition
attributes.aspect: Viparyaya is wrong cognition—false knowledge not based on actual reality. Patanjali defines it as knowledge that is not founded on the true nature of its object.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental (the mind’s false knowing) · Ontic mode: Vṛtti — a mental modification (erroneous cognition) · Valence: Neutral faculty tending to affliction (misperception) · Guṇa: Tamas–Rajas (distortion) · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: Manifestation (knowing that does not match the real) · Key relation: One of the five vṛttis; antagonist to Pramāṇa; the ground on which the kleśas (Dim 19) build.
coordinate.causal_status: Manifestation (knowing that does not match the real)
coordinate.key_relation: One of the five vṛttis; antagonist to Pramāṇa; the ground on which the kleśas (Dim 19) build.

balanced.label: Balanced — error caught and corrected

facets.overview: Viparyaya is never positively desired. The balanced state is its absence—replacement by Pramana (correct knowledge) and discrimination.
facets.somatic: Accurate perception of physical reality, body acting on truth, healthy seeing.
facets.emotional: Emotional response to actual reality, freedom from misperception-based emotions.
facets.cognitive: Mind freed from false knowledge, replaced by correct cognition, clarity.
facets.psychic: The soul matures through the humility of catching its own errors; misperception seen and corrected becomes the very mechanism by which discrimination is born.

balanced.text:
Viparyaya is never positively desired. The balanced state is its absence—replacement by Pramana (correct knowledge) and discrimination.

Somatic: Accurate perception of physical reality, body acting on truth, healthy seeing.

Emotional: Emotional response to actual reality, freedom from misperception-based emotions.

Cognitive: Mind freed from false knowledge, replaced by correct cognition, clarity.

Psychic: The soul matures through the humility of catching its own errors; misperception seen and corrected becomes the very mechanism by which discrimination is born.
balanced.vignette: Meera misreads a situation—everyone does—but she catches it. A second look, a checked assumption, a ‘wait, maybe I’ve got this wrong,’ and the error is corrected before it hardens into a grudge or a bad decision. She holds her perceptions lightly enough to revise them, and so her mistakes stay small.

excess.label: In excess — confident, pervasive misperception

facets.overview: Excessive Viparyaya creates a life lived in fundamental error—conclusions drawn from misperceptions, mistakes compounding mistakes.
facets.somatic: Physical actions based on misperception, accidents, harm from wrong knowledge, body in confusion.
facets.emotional: Emotional suffering from wrong knowledge, feelings based on falsehoods, painful misunderstandings.
facets.cognitive: Mind entirely dominated by error, false beliefs, mental confusion, life lived in fundamental delusion.
facets.psychic: The soul lives inside a self-built distortion — here is the whole spiritual predicament in miniature: bondage is error mistaken for truth, the rope seen as a snake.

excess.text:
Excessive Viparyaya creates a life lived in fundamental error—conclusions drawn from misperceptions, mistakes compounding mistakes.

Somatic: Physical actions based on misperception, accidents, harm from wrong knowledge, body in confusion.

Emotional: Emotional suffering from wrong knowledge, feelings based on falsehoods, painful misunderstandings.

Cognitive: Mind entirely dominated by error, false beliefs, mental confusion, life lived in fundamental delusion.

Psychic: The soul lives inside a self-built distortion — here is the whole spiritual predicament in miniature: bondage is error mistaken for truth, the rope seen as a snake.
excess.vignette: Ravi is confidently, pervasively wrong. He has misread his colleague’s intentions, his own health, the state of his finances—and each misperception is held with total certainty, immune to correction, generating decisions built on sand. The errors don’t get caught because he cannot imagine he has made any; the misreading has become his reality.

deficient.label: Deficient — (the rare clarity in which error seldom takes hold)

facets.overview: Absence of Viparyaya is desirable. The deficient state would be hypothetical—occurs when wisdom replaces error.
facets.somatic: Body responds to actual reality, no actions based on error.
facets.emotional: Emotions correspond to real conditions, no suffering from misperception.
facets.cognitive: Mind operating on truth, no false knowledge.
facets.psychic: The soul is paralyzed by the fear of being wrong, unable to trust any perception — discrimination collapsing into endless second-guessing. Viparyaya is the only vṛtti that is, by definition, a distortion; its ‘balanced’ state is therefore the swift recognition and correction of error rather than its absence. A person near balance notices the mistaken read — ‘I assumed she was angry; she was just tired’ — and revises. In excess, viparyaya runs the show: the rope is a snake, the neutral email is an attack, the silence is contempt, and the person acts on the misreading as if it were fact, often setting in motion exactly the conflict they imagined. Its rare deficiency is the seasoned clarity in which the mind so reliably checks itself that gross error finds little purchase. Maya’s manager passes her in the hall without a word. Through full-grown viparyaya the mind instantly knows: ‘He’s decided against me; this is the cold shoulder.’ The whole afternoon is lived inside that conclusion — the curt replies, the bracing — until she learns he was simply absorbed in a family emergency. The snake was always a rope. The cost was real anyway, paid in an afternoon of suffering built on nothing.

deficient.text:
Absence of Viparyaya is desirable. The deficient state would be hypothetical—occurs when wisdom replaces error.

Somatic: Body responds to actual reality, no actions based on error.

Emotional: Emotions correspond to real conditions, no suffering from misperception.

Cognitive: Mind operating on truth, no false knowledge.

Psychic: The soul is paralyzed by the fear of being wrong, unable to trust any perception — discrimination collapsing into endless second-guessing. Viparyaya is the only vṛtti that is, by definition, a distortion; its ‘balanced’ state is therefore the swift recognition and correction of error rather than its absence. A person near balance notices the mistaken read — ‘I assumed she was angry; she was just tired’ — and revises. In excess, viparyaya runs the show: the rope is a snake, the neutral email is an attack, the silence is contempt, and the person acts on the misreading as if it were fact, often setting in motion exactly the conflict they imagined. Its rare deficiency is the seasoned clarity in which the mind so reliably checks itself that gross error finds little purchase. Maya’s manager passes her in the hall without a word. Through full-grown viparyaya the mind instantly knows: ‘He’s decided against me; this is the cold shoulder.’ The whole afternoon is lived inside that conclusion — the curt replies, the bracing — until she learns he was simply absorbed in a family emergency. The snake was always a rope. The cost was real anyway, paid in an afternoon of suffering built on nothing.
deficient.vignette: Sara sees clearly enough that error rarely gets a foothold in her—she reads situations accurately, checks her impressions, and is seldom fooled. It is less a dramatic virtue than a quiet absence of the usual distortions: where others project and misread, she simply, mostly, sees what is there.

attributes.title: Vikalpa — Conceptual Imagination
attributes.aspect: Vikalpa is verbal construction/imagination—mental activity with no corresponding reality, based purely on words and concepts. It is fantasy and conceptual imagining.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental (verbal and conceptual construction) · Ontic mode: Vṛtti — a mental modification (imagination on words without an object) · Valence: Neutral faculty (language and vision; empty abstraction in excess) · Guṇa: Rajas–Sattva · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: Manifestation (the mind building on words alone) · Key relation: One of the five vṛttis — the faculty of language, planning, and fantasy.
coordinate.ontic_mode: Vṛtti — a mental modification (imagination on words without an object)
coordinate.valence: Neutral faculty (language and vision; empty abstraction in excess)
coordinate.causal_status: Manifestation (the mind building on words alone)
coordinate.key_relation: One of the five vṛttis — the faculty of language, planning, and fantasy.

balanced.label: Balanced — language, planning, creativity in service

facets.overview: When Vikalpa is recognized, the yogi can use imagination creatively without being deceived by it. There is clear awareness of what is real vs. imagined.
facets.somatic: Body free from acting on imagined fears or desires, present-reality response.
facets.emotional: Freedom from emotions based on imagination, emotions based on reality, healthy use of imagination for creativity.
facets.cognitive: Conscious use of imagination, clear distinction between concepts and reality, creative thinking when appropriate.
facets.psychic: The soul is served by imagination held lightly — language, planning, and creativity used as instruments without being mistaken for the real.

balanced.text:
When Vikalpa is recognized, the yogi can use imagination creatively without being deceived by it. There is clear awareness of what is real vs. imagined.

Somatic: Body free from acting on imagined fears or desires, present-reality response.

Emotional: Freedom from emotions based on imagination, emotions based on reality, healthy use of imagination for creativity.

Cognitive: Conscious use of imagination, clear distinction between concepts and reality, creative thinking when appropriate.

Psychic: The soul is served by imagination held lightly — language, planning, and creativity used as instruments without being mistaken for the real.
balanced.vignette: Kavya’s imagination is a workshop. She plans, envisions, plays with ideas and possibilities, uses language and concept to build things that don’t yet exist—and then returns, cleanly, to the actual. The power to conceive the not-yet-real serves her real life; she can dream a project and then, unlike many dreamers, go and make it.

excess.label: In excess — rumination, fantasy, living in words

facets.overview: Excessive Vikalpa creates a life lived in fantasy—imagining experiences that haven’t occurred, suffering or rejoicing in pure mental constructs, disconnection from reality.
facets.somatic: Physical responses to imagined situations (anxiety from imagined fears, etc.), body in fantasy-driven states.
facets.emotional: Emotional suffering from imagined scenarios, emotional life dominated by fantasy, anxiety about imagined futures.
facets.cognitive: Mind lost in imagination, inability to distinguish real from imagined, mental fantasy substituting for engagement.
facets.psychic: The soul is lost among words and concepts about reality rather than reality itself; the lesson is to notice when the map has quietly replaced the territory.

excess.text:
Excessive Vikalpa creates a life lived in fantasy—imagining experiences that haven’t occurred, suffering or rejoicing in pure mental constructs, disconnection from reality.

Somatic: Physical responses to imagined situations (anxiety from imagined fears, etc.), body in fantasy-driven states.

Emotional: Emotional suffering from imagined scenarios, emotional life dominated by fantasy, anxiety about imagined futures.

Cognitive: Mind lost in imagination, inability to distinguish real from imagined, mental fantasy substituting for engagement.

Psychic: The soul is lost among words and concepts about reality rather than reality itself; the lesson is to notice when the map has quietly replaced the territory.
excess.vignette: Anil lives in his head. The imagined conversation, the rehearsed catastrophe, the elaborate fantasy of a life he isn’t living—his mind spins concepts and stories endlessly, and the words have become more real to him than the world. He ruminates and fantasizes and plans, and the planning has quietly replaced the doing.

deficient.label: Deficient — concreteness without abstraction or vision

facets.overview: Complete absence of imagination would eliminate creative capacity, mental flexibility, planning ability. Some Vikalpa serves practical and creative life.
facets.somatic: Inability to envision physical possibilities, lack of physical creativity.
facets.emotional: Lack of emotional imagination, inability to envision possibilities, emotional flatness.
facets.cognitive: Lack of creative thinking, inability to plan or envision, rigid concrete thinking, no imagination.
facets.psychic: The soul’s capacity for vision and meaning is starved; without the play of conception, hope, planning, and creativity wither. Vikalpa is the faculty of word-built mind — and it is not inherently a fault, for all planning, abstraction, art, and language run on it. In balance it is creativity and foresight: the ability to model a future, draft a plan, imagine a possibility. In excess it becomes the runaway commentary of rumination and fantasy — rehearsing conversations that will never happen, building elaborate catastrophes or daydreams with no object, mistaking the vividness of a thought for the reality of a thing. In deficiency, the capacity for abstraction thins: a person can be doggedly concrete, literal, and present, but without vision, metaphor, or the ability to plan beyond the visible. Lying awake, Maya conducts a flawless imaginary argument with her colleague — every line of his answered, every point won. The body tenses and the heart rate climbs as if the fight were happening; it is not, and it never will. This is vikalpa in excess: a whirl of pure words generating real adrenaline. In balance, she would catch the spin — ‘this is a conversation with no one’ — and let the imagined courtroom dissolve, freeing the same faculty to picture, instead, how she might simply ask him a real question tomorrow.

deficient.vignette: Dan is relentlessly literal. He handles the concrete and immediate competently and cannot, for the life of him, imagine an alternative, envision a future, or grasp an abstraction. Metaphor baffles him, vision eludes him, and possibilities he can’t already see simply don’t exist for him. The world is exactly what’s in front of him, and no more.

attributes.title: Nidrā — Sleep
attributes.aspect: Nidra is sleep—a mental modification of the absence of consciousness of objects. Patanjali defines it specifically as a state where the mind is supported by the cause of non-being.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Physical–Mental (the mode of dreamless rest) · Ontic mode: Vṛtti — a mental modification (sleep, a content of absence) · Valence: Neutral faculty (restorative; escape in excess) · Guṇa: Tamas-dominant · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: Manifestation (the mind’s mode of dreamless rest) · Key relation: One of the five vṛttis — as a vṛtti (a mode of mind), distinct from Nidrā the transient wave (Dim 9) and the sleep state (Dim 4).
coordinate.ontic_mode: Vṛtti — a mental modification (sleep, a content of absence)
coordinate.causal_status: Manifestation (the mind’s mode of dreamless rest)
coordinate.key_relation: One of the five vṛttis — as a vṛtti (a mode of mind), distinct from Nidrā the transient wave (Dim 9) and the sleep state (Dim 4).

balanced.label: Balanced — deep, restorative rest

facets.overview: Balanced Nidra provides healthy restorative sleep. The yogi may also enter conscious sleep states (yogic sleep/yoga nidra) maintaining awareness through deep rest.
facets.somatic: Restorative sleep, healthy sleep cycles, body restored through sleep, balanced energy.
facets.emotional: Emotional restoration through sleep, freedom from sleep disturbance, emotional balance from rest.
facets.cognitive: Healthy sleep that restores mind, freedom from insomnia, mental refreshment, potentially conscious sleep states.
facets.psychic: In deep sleep the soul touches its own causal ground, returning to the formless rest from which renewal is drawn; being continues even in oblivion.

balanced.text:
Balanced Nidra provides healthy restorative sleep. The yogi may also enter conscious sleep states (yogic sleep/yoga nidra) maintaining awareness through deep rest.

Somatic: Restorative sleep, healthy sleep cycles, body restored through sleep, balanced energy.

Emotional: Emotional restoration through sleep, freedom from sleep disturbance, emotional balance from rest.

Cognitive: Healthy sleep that restores mind, freedom from insomnia, mental refreshment, potentially conscious sleep states.

Psychic: In deep sleep the soul touches its own causal ground, returning to the formless rest from which renewal is drawn; being continues even in oblivion.
balanced.vignette: Grace sleeps the deep, dreamless sleep of the genuinely tired and wakes restored, the night having done its quiet repair-work on body and mind. Rest, for her, is not an escape but a return; she surrenders to it easily and rises replenished, carried by a healthy rhythm she doesn’t have to think about.

excess.label: In excess — oversleep, dullness, escape into unconsciousness

facets.overview: Excessive Nidra (tamasic) creates oversleeping, lethargy, dullness, mental fog, sluggishness, escape into unconsciousness.
facets.somatic: Excessive sleep damaging health, lethargy, body weighed down, sluggish metabolism, inability to be active.
facets.emotional: Emotional dullness, depression manifesting as oversleep, emotional escape into unconsciousness.
facets.cognitive: Mental dullness, fog, escape from thinking, mind dominated by tamas, no clarity.
facets.psychic: The soul hides in unconsciousness — sleep as escape, the tamasic retreat from the labor of waking awareness.

excess.text:
Excessive Nidra (tamasic) creates oversleeping, lethargy, dullness, mental fog, sluggishness, escape into unconsciousness.

Somatic: Excessive sleep damaging health, lethargy, body weighed down, sluggish metabolism, inability to be active.

Emotional: Emotional dullness, depression manifesting as oversleep, emotional escape into unconsciousness.

Cognitive: Mental dullness, fog, escape from thinking, mind dominated by tamas, no clarity.

Psychic: The soul hides in unconsciousness — sleep as escape, the tamasic retreat from the labor of waking awareness.
excess.vignette: Ben sleeps to disappear. Ten, eleven hours, naps on top, the duvet a refuge he retreats into whenever the day asks too much—the heaviness pulling him under not for rest but for oblivion. Sleep has become an escape hatch, and the more he uses it the duller and further-away his waking life becomes.

deficient.label: Deficient — insomnia, depletion, the mind that cannot let go

facets.overview: Insufficient Nidra (often from excessive rajas) creates insomnia, exhaustion, mental and physical breakdown, inability to function.
facets.somatic: Insomnia, exhaustion, body unable to repair, stress-related illness, accidents from fatigue, breakdown.
facets.emotional: Emotional reactivity from sleep deprivation, irritability, inability to regulate emotions.
facets.cognitive: Mental exhaustion, inability to think clearly, cognitive impairment, dangerous mental states from no rest.
facets.psychic: The soul is denied its nightly return to source; starved of rest, the whole system frays and the inner light dims. Patañjali’s subtle point is that sleep is itself a vṛtti — a modification whose object is the absence of objects — which is why we can remember having slept well. In balance, nidrā is healthy rest that restores body and mind and even, for the yogi, becomes a doorway to studying consciousness itself. In excess, it slides into oversleeping, daytime heaviness, and the use of unconsciousness as escape — the nap that is really an avoidance, the tamasic fog. In deficiency, it fails: insomnia, the exhausted body that cannot power down, the mind too gripped by the day’s other vṛttis to release into rest. After the imaginary argument, Maya cannot sleep — deficient nidrā, the mind’s other ripples too active to let the lake go still. On a different week, low and discouraged, she sleeps ten hours and still wakes heavy, using the dark as a place to not-be — nidrā in excess. In balance, sleep simply comes when the body is tired and the mind has set its commentary down, and she wakes restored, with the faint clean sense of having been, for a while, gone.

Somatic: Insomnia, exhaustion, body unable to repair, stress-related illness, accidents from fatigue, breakdown.

deficient.vignette: Night after night, Nadia’s mind will not let go. She lies in the dark rehearsing the day, unable to sink into the deep rest that would repair her, surfacing every hour, wired and exhausted at once. The natural surrender that sleep requires has jammed, and she pays for it in a daytime fog no amount of coffee can quite lift.

attributes.title: Smṛti — Memory
attributes.aspect: Smriti is memory—the retention of experienced objects. As a vritti, it is the mental modification that brings forth past experiences. In yoga, it has both ordinary and yogic dimensions.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental (the return of past experience) · Ontic mode: Vṛtti — a mental modification (memory) · Valence: Neutral faculty (recall; rumination in excess) · Guṇa: Sattva (clear recall), Rajas (rumination) · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: Manifestation (the retention and return of experience) · Key relation: One of the five vṛttis — the same faculty as Smṛti the wave (Dim 9); the vehicle of saṁskāra-recall.
coordinate.causal_status: Manifestation (the retention and return of experience)
coordinate.key_relation: One of the five vṛttis — the same faculty as Smṛti the wave (Dim 9); the vehicle of saṁskāra-recall.

balanced.label: Balanced — recall in service of learning and continuity

facets.overview: Healthy Smriti supports learning, wisdom, and practice. Patanjali also uses Smriti as a key practice—holding the object steadily in awareness, which is foundational to meditation.
facets.somatic: Healthy memory function, body memory supporting skills, sensory memory aiding life.
facets.emotional: Useful emotional memory aiding wisdom, healthy retention of experiences, learning from past.
facets.cognitive: Excellent memory function, learning capacity, accumulated wisdom, mental ability to hold objects in attention.
facets.psychic: The soul is served by memory that carries learning forward without binding; remembrance becomes the thread of a coherent life and, refined, the recollection of one’s own deeper nature.

balanced.text:
Healthy Smriti supports learning, wisdom, and practice. Patanjali also uses Smriti as a key practice—holding the object steadily in awareness, which is foundational to meditation.

Somatic: Healthy memory function, body memory supporting skills, sensory memory aiding life.

Emotional: Useful emotional memory aiding wisdom, healthy retention of experiences, learning from past.

Cognitive: Excellent memory function, learning capacity, accumulated wisdom, mental ability to hold objects in attention.

Psychic: The soul is served by memory that carries learning forward without binding; remembrance becomes the thread of a coherent life and, refined, the recollection of one’s own deeper nature.
balanced.vignette: Meera’s memory serves her—the lesson recalled at the right moment, the promise kept, the thread of her own history available and coherent. She remembers what matters and lets the rest fade, drawing on the past to inform the present without being trapped in it. Her recall is a resource, not a prison.

excess.label: In excess — the past replaying, rumination over old wounds

facets.overview: Excessive Smriti becomes dwelling in past, inability to be present, traumatic memory loops, obsessive recall of negative experiences.
facets.somatic: Body holding trauma memories, somatic re-experiencing, physical responses to memory.
facets.emotional: Emotional dwelling in past pain or pleasure, trauma loops, inability to release past, emotional bondage to memory.
facets.cognitive: Obsessive mental rumination, mind trapped in past, inability to be present, memory-based suffering.
facets.psychic: The soul is held captive by the past — memory as rumination, the replaying of what was, samskaras grooving ever deeper.

excess.text:
Excessive Smriti becomes dwelling in past, inability to be present, traumatic memory loops, obsessive recall of negative experiences.

Somatic: Body holding trauma memories, somatic re-experiencing, physical responses to memory.

Emotional: Emotional dwelling in past pain or pleasure, trauma loops, inability to release past, emotional bondage to memory.

Cognitive: Obsessive mental rumination, mind trapped in past, inability to be present, memory-based suffering.

Psychic: The soul is held captive by the past — memory as rumination, the replaying of what was, samskaras grooving ever deeper.
excess.vignette: The past will not stay past for Ravi. The old humiliation, the decade-old argument, the wound he cannot stop probing—they replay nightly, vivid as the day they happened, keeping griefs and grudges perpetually fresh. His memory has become a loop he cannot exit, re-suffering what it should merely have recorded.

deficient.label: Deficient — forgetfulness, lost continuity, lost lessons

facets.overview: Deficient Smriti creates inability to learn from experience, forgetfulness, no accumulated wisdom, repeated mistakes.
facets.somatic: Memory dysfunction, inability to remember physical skills or routines, dementia.
facets.emotional: Inability to learn from emotional experience, repeating emotional mistakes, no emotional wisdom.
facets.cognitive: Forgetfulness, inability to retain learning, no wisdom accumulation, cognitive impairment.
facets.psychic: The soul loses its thread of continuity; without memory’s service, learning cannot accumulate and identity itself frays. Smṛti is the re-presentation of past impressions — the faculty that gives life continuity and makes learning possible. In balance, it serves: a lesson recalled at the right moment, a face remembered, a skill retained, and — crucially for the yogi — the steady remembering of one’s own deepest intention. In excess, memory becomes a loop: old slights, regrets, and griefs replayed until the present is lived through the filter of the past, the wound rehearsed until it never heals. In deficiency, continuity frays — forgetfulness, the lost thread, lessons that do not stick, the same mistake made again because its cost was not retained. The colleague’s promotion stirs an older memory — a time years ago when Maya was overlooked — and through excess smṛti the two fuse, the present disappointment swelling with the full charge of the past, so that she is no longer responding to today but to a decade of accumulated slight. In balance, the old memory arises, is recognized as old, and is even useful — ‘I have survived this before’ — informing the present without flooding it. Same memory; in one case a chain, in the other a teacher.

deficient.vignette: Sara cannot hold onto her own life. Names, lessons, whole experiences slip away; the mistake made last year is made again this year with no warning from the past; the thread of continuity keeps breaking. It is a lost access to her own accumulated wisdom, so that she meets each situation stripped of what she has already learned.

attributes.title: Vyādhi — Disease
attributes.aspect: Vyadhi is disease or illness—the first of nine obstacles Patanjali names that distract the mind from yoga. Physical illness disturbs mental focus and impedes practice.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Physical (bodily distress) · Ontic mode: Obstacle — an antarāya (disease) · Valence: Affliction / obstacle (to remedy) · Guṇa: Tamas-tinged · Scale: Individual, with environmental cause · Causal status: Obstruction (bodily distress that stalls practice) · Key relation: One of the nine antarāyas; same as Vyādhi the wave (Dim 9); undermines the Annamaya ground.
coordinate.key_relation: One of the nine antarāyas; same as Vyādhi the wave (Dim 9); undermines the Annamaya ground.

balanced.label: Balanced — health as the ground of practice

facets.overview: When the body is healthy, it serves as a fit vehicle for yoga practice. Vyadhi is overcome through proper care, lifestyle, and yogic practices.
facets.somatic: Healthy disease-free body, strong immune function, vitality, balanced physiology, body fit for practice.
facets.emotional: Emotional stability supported by physical health, freedom from disease-related distress.
facets.cognitive: Mental clarity from healthy body, focus undisturbed by physical complaints, capacity for practice.
facets.psychic: The soul has a fit vehicle; bodily health, neither clung to nor neglected, becomes a quiet support for the inner work rather than a distraction from it.

balanced.text:
When the body is healthy, it serves as a fit vehicle for yoga practice. Vyadhi is overcome through proper care, lifestyle, and yogic practices.

Somatic: Healthy disease-free body, strong immune function, vitality, balanced physiology, body fit for practice.

Emotional: Emotional stability supported by physical health, freedom from disease-related distress.

Cognitive: Mental clarity from healthy body, focus undisturbed by physical complaints, capacity for practice.

Psychic: The soul has a fit vehicle; bodily health, neither clung to nor neglected, becomes a quiet support for the inner work rather than a distraction from it.
balanced.vignette: Priya keeps her body reasonably well—sleep, movement, food—not as an obsession but as the foundation everything else rests on. Healthy and unpreoccupied with health, she has the physical steadiness that lets her show up, day after day, for the work and the practice that matter.

excess.label: In excess — health-anxiety and hypochondria; identification with symptoms

facets.overview: Obsession with illness (excessive focus) becomes hypochondria, health anxiety, identification with sickness, life dominated by illness narrative.
facets.somatic: Hypochondria, exaggerated physical symptoms, somatization, illness identity becoming dominant. Active disease, body unable to practice, physical suffering, illness disrupting daily life.
facets.emotional: Emotional identification with illness, victim mentality around disease, anxiety about health. Emotional distress from illness, depression from sickness, frustration with body.
facets.cognitive: Obsessive mental focus on health, illness anxiety, mental dominance by disease concerns. Mental distraction by physical suffering, inability to focus on practice, mind dominated by symptoms.
facets.psychic: The soul is eclipsed by preoccupation with the body; health-anxiety turns the instrument into the obsession, the fixation itself a subtle bondage. Vyadhi as obstacle means disease is interfering with practice. When fully present, it creates inability to do yoga, distraction, suffering. The soul is asked to find its ground beyond the body precisely when the body fails; illness becomes a wall or a teacher depending on whether one identifies with the ailing form or witnesses it. The chronic pain makes everything harder—the focus won’t hold, the practice keeps getting interrupted, the simplest plans bend around the body’s demands. Sam is not imagining it; the illness is real, and it disrupts the steady ground that concentration and effort require. He is learning, slowly, to work with a body that will not cooperate. A balanced relationship to vyādhi treats the body as the vehicle of all practice — tended, rested, fed, and moved well enough to be forgotten. When illness genuinely strikes, it disturbs focus directly; and a mind already caught in obstacle can also become preoccupied with the body, magnifying small symptoms into a full-time concern. Its neglect-pole is the practitioner who pushes the body past its limits in the name of discipline until it breaks down and stops practice altogether. A bad cold arrives the week Maya finally established a morning sit. The congestion, the broken sleep, the low-grade ache make the cushion feel impossible, and within days the habit is gone — not abandoned by choice but dissolved by the body’s disturbance. Seeing it as vyādhi rather than as personal failure, she lets the practice shrink to two honest minutes a day until the body returns.

Emotional: Emotional identification with illness, victim mentality around disease, anxiety about health. Emotional distress from illness, depression from sickness, frustration with body.

Cognitive: Obsessive mental focus on health, illness anxiety, mental dominance by disease concerns. Mental distraction by physical suffering, inability to focus on practice, mind dominated by symptoms.

excess.vignette: Every twinge sends Ravi to the search bar and the doctor. He monitors his body obsessively, identifies with each symptom, and lives in a low dread of illness that has itself become the illness. The healthy care for the body has metastasized into anxiety, and the preoccupation with staying well is quietly ruining his days.

attributes.title: Styāna — Mental Dullness
attributes.aspect: Styana is mental dullness or apathy—a state of mental sluggishness where there is no enthusiasm for practice. It is a tamasic obstruction.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental (heaviness of mind) · Ontic mode: Obstacle — an antarāya (mental dullness) · Valence: Affliction / obstacle · Guṇa: Tamas-dominant · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: Obstruction (dullness that saps the will to practice) · Key relation: One of the nine antarāyas; kin to Ālasya and the tamasic vṛttis.
coordinate.causal_status: Obstruction (dullness that saps the will to practice)
coordinate.key_relation: One of the nine antarāyas; kin to Ālasya and the tamasic vṛttis.

balanced.label: Balanced — a clear, willing, alert mind

facets.overview: When Styana is overcome, the mind is alert and enthusiastic for practice. There is mental vitality and engagement.
facets.somatic: Alert physical presence, vital energy, body that supports active engagement, healthy nervous system.
facets.emotional: Enthusiasm and emotional engagement, joy in practice, emotional vitality.
facets.cognitive: Mental alertness, enthusiasm for yoga, sharp clarity, engaged thinking.
facets.psychic: The soul’s light reaches the surface through a clear, willing mind; enthusiasm for practice is itself a sign that the inner fire still burns.

balanced.text:
When Styana is overcome, the mind is alert and enthusiastic for practice. There is mental vitality and engagement.

Somatic: Alert physical presence, vital energy, body that supports active engagement, healthy nervous system.

Emotional: Enthusiasm and emotional engagement, joy in practice, emotional vitality.

Cognitive: Mental alertness, enthusiasm for yoga, sharp clarity, engaged thinking.

Psychic: The soul’s light reaches the surface through a clear, willing mind; enthusiasm for practice is itself a sign that the inner fire still burns.
balanced.vignette: Meera comes to her work and her practice with a clear, willing, alert mind—present, ready, the mental engine running clean. There is no fog to push through, no apathy to overcome; she simply shows up sharp, and the sharpness makes everything else possible.

excess.label: In excess — fog, apathy, no enthusiasm for practice

facets.overview: Excessive (and prolonged) Styana becomes chronic apathy, depression-like state, complete loss of motivation for practice or life.
facets.somatic: Severe lethargy, body weighed down by inertia, lack of physical energy, depression-like physical state.
facets.emotional: Emotional flatness, depression, loss of all enthusiasm, emotional dullness, no joy.
facets.cognitive: Severe mental fog, complete apathy, inability to think clearly, dulled cognition.
facets.psychic: The soul is smothered under tamas; chronic dullness is the heaviness that makes even the longing for freedom feel impossibly distant.

excess.text:
Excessive (and prolonged) Styana becomes chronic apathy, depression-like state, complete loss of motivation for practice or life.

Somatic: Severe lethargy, body weighed down by inertia, lack of physical energy, depression-like physical state.

Emotional: Emotional flatness, depression, loss of all enthusiasm, emotional dullness, no joy.

Cognitive: Severe mental fog, complete apathy, inability to think clearly, dulled cognition.

Psychic: The soul is smothered under tamas; chronic dullness is the heaviness that makes even the longing for freedom feel impossibly distant.
excess.vignette: A grey fog sits over Dan’s mind. He knows what he should do and cannot summon the interest to do it; the enthusiasm won’t come, the mental engine won’t turn over, and even things he once loved meet a flat ‘why bother.’ The dullness is not laziness exactly—it is the light behind the eyes gone dim.

deficient.label: Deficient — (the keen alertness in which dullness finds no foothold)

facets.overview: Styana as obstacle means dullness present. When absent, the mind is alert—this is the desired state.
facets.somatic: Alert body, vital energy, healthy responsiveness.
facets.emotional: Engaged emotions, enthusiasm, joy.
facets.cognitive: Sharp clear mind, alert thinking, enthusiastic engagement.
facets.psychic: The dullness lifts entirely — the keen, awake mind in which apathy finds no foothold, which is precisely the clarity practice exists to restore. Styāna is a dullness specifically of the mind — the mental enthusiasm gone flat, the sense that one simply cannot be bothered to engage, even where the body is willing. It is a tamasic fog over the intellect. In its grip, practice feels pointless not because of any argument against it but because the very appetite for it has drained away. The remedy is rarely more force; it is usually a change of energy — light, breath, movement — to lift the fog enough for willingness to return. Maya sits down to meditate and finds nothing there — no resistance exactly, just a grey flatness, a mind like wet wool. Five minutes feel like a chore with no flavor. This is styāna: the dullness that makes the most worthwhile thing feel like the most tedious. She opens a window, takes ten brisker breaths, and the fog thins enough that one real minute of attention becomes possible.

deficient.text:

Somatic: Alert body, vital energy, healthy responsiveness.

Emotional: Engaged emotions, enthusiasm, joy.

Cognitive: Sharp clear mind, alert thinking, enthusiastic engagement.

Psychic: The dullness lifts entirely — the keen, awake mind in which apathy finds no foothold, which is precisely the clarity practice exists to restore. Styāna is a dullness specifically of the mind — the mental enthusiasm gone flat, the sense that one simply cannot be bothered to engage, even where the body is willing. It is a tamasic fog over the intellect. In its grip, practice feels pointless not because of any argument against it but because the very appetite for it has drained away. The remedy is rarely more force; it is usually a change of energy — light, breath, movement — to lift the fog enough for willingness to return. Maya sits down to meditate and finds nothing there — no resistance exactly, just a grey flatness, a mind like wet wool. Five minutes feel like a chore with no flavor. This is styāna: the dullness that makes the most worthwhile thing feel like the most tedious. She opens a window, takes ten brisker breaths, and the fog thins enough that one real minute of attention becomes possible.
deficient.vignette: Sara’s mind is bright and quick; dullness simply finds no purchase in her. Where others push through fog, she meets each task with a natural keenness, alert and engaged almost by default. It is less a triumph over sloth than an absence of it—the mental weather is clear, and stays clear.

attributes.title: Saṁśaya — Corrosive Doubt
attributes.aspect: Samshaya is doubt that undermines practice. Not healthy questioning, but corrosive doubt that prevents commitment and progress on the path.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental (wavering conviction) · Ontic mode: Obstacle — an antarāya (corrosive doubt) · Valence: Affliction / obstacle · Guṇa: Rajas–Tamas · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: Obstruction (doubt that undermines commitment) · Key relation: One of the nine antarāyas; the corrosive form of Vitarka’s deliberation (Dim 9); dissolved by resolve and faith.
coordinate.key_relation: One of the nine antarāyas; the corrosive form of Vitarka’s deliberation (Dim 9); dissolved by resolve and faith.

balanced.label: Balanced — honest questioning that seeks truth

facets.overview: When Samshaya is overcome through faith (shraddha) and experience, the yogi practices with conviction. Healthy questioning remains, but corrosive doubt is gone.
facets.somatic: Body practiced with conviction, healthy commitment to practice, physical confidence.
facets.emotional: Emotional confidence in path, faith from experience, freedom from corrosive doubt.
facets.cognitive: Mental conviction supporting practice, clarity about path, healthy critical thinking without paralysis.
facets.psychic: The soul questions honestly and then trusts; doubt resolved into conviction frees the energy that endless skepticism would consume.

balanced.text:
When Samshaya is overcome through faith (shraddha) and experience, the yogi practices with conviction. Healthy questioning remains, but corrosive doubt is gone.

Somatic: Body practiced with conviction, healthy commitment to practice, physical confidence.

Emotional: Emotional confidence in path, faith from experience, freedom from corrosive doubt.

Cognitive: Mental conviction supporting practice, clarity about path, healthy critical thinking without paralysis.

Psychic: The soul questions honestly and then trusts; doubt resolved into conviction frees the energy that endless skepticism would consume.
balanced.vignette: Kavya questions things—the teaching, the plan, her own assumptions—not to avoid commitment but to arrive at something she can genuinely stand on. Her doubt is honest and productive; it tests, and having tested, it commits. She believes, in the end, but she has earned her belief.

excess.label: In excess — doubt that paralyzes commitment

facets.overview: Excessive Samshaya becomes chronic doubt that undermines all action, paralysis through skepticism, inability to commit to anything.
facets.somatic: Inability to sustain practice, body in inconsistent action, no commitment to physical routines.
facets.emotional: Emotional instability from doubt, inability to trust feelings, anxiety from uncertainty.
facets.cognitive: Paralyzing skepticism, mental inability to commit, constant questioning that prevents action.
facets.psychic: The soul is paralyzed at the threshold; chronic doubt undermines every step, the faculty meant to discern truth refusing to let it land.

excess.text:
Excessive Samshaya becomes chronic doubt that undermines all action, paralysis through skepticism, inability to commit to anything.

Somatic: Inability to sustain practice, body in inconsistent action, no commitment to physical routines.

Emotional: Emotional instability from doubt, inability to trust feelings, anxiety from uncertainty.

Cognitive: Paralyzing skepticism, mental inability to commit, constant questioning that prevents action.

Psychic: The soul is paralyzed at the threshold; chronic doubt undermines every step, the faculty meant to discern truth refusing to let it land.
excess.vignette: Ravi cannot commit to anything. Every path invites its counter-doubt, every teacher might be a fraud, every decision might be wrong—and so he circles endlessly, never choosing, never starting, protected from error by never risking anything. His doubt has stopped seeking truth and started preventing action.

deficient.label: Deficient — credulous certainty that never examines

facets.overview: Complete absence of all doubt becomes blind faith, gullibility, inability to discriminate, vulnerability to false teachers and ideas.
facets.somatic: Blind following of harmful practices, physical danger from no discrimination.
facets.emotional: Emotional gullibility, vulnerability to manipulation, blind devotion to wrong figures.
facets.cognitive: Blind faith, gullibility, absence of healthy critical thinking, vulnerability to deception.
facets.psychic: Doubt collapses into blind credulity; without the healthy check of discernment, the soul becomes prey to whatever certainty is offered it. Patañjali’s saṁśaya is not the healthy inquiry that strengthens understanding — that is welcome — but the corrosive doubt that undermines commitment: ‘Is this even working? Is this path real? Am I wasting my time?’ asked not to find an answer but in a way that quietly dissolves the will to continue. In balance, doubt is a question one actually pursues to resolution. In excess, it is a permanent hover that never lets practice take root. Its opposite pole, deficient doubt, is the credulity that swallows any teaching whole and never tests it. Three weeks into the new habit, with no dramatic results, the thought arrives: ‘This probably doesn’t do anything anyway.’ It is reasonable-sounding and quietly fatal — and the next morning’s sit is skipped on its authority. Recognizing it as saṁśaya, Maya neither believes it nor argues with it; she simply notes ‘doubt is present’ and keeps the appointment with herself anyway, letting results, if any, accumulate before she judges.

deficient.text:
