Somatic: Blind following of harmful practices, physical danger from no discrimination.

Emotional: Emotional gullibility, vulnerability to manipulation, blind devotion to wrong figures.

Cognitive: Blind faith, gullibility, absence of healthy critical thinking, vulnerability to deception.

Psychic: Doubt collapses into blind credulity; without the healthy check of discernment, the soul becomes prey to whatever certainty is offered it. Patañjali’s saṁśaya is not the healthy inquiry that strengthens understanding — that is welcome — but the corrosive doubt that undermines commitment: ‘Is this even working? Is this path real? Am I wasting my time?’ asked not to find an answer but in a way that quietly dissolves the will to continue. In balance, doubt is a question one actually pursues to resolution. In excess, it is a permanent hover that never lets practice take root. Its opposite pole, deficient doubt, is the credulity that swallows any teaching whole and never tests it. Three weeks into the new habit, with no dramatic results, the thought arrives: ‘This probably doesn’t do anything anyway.’ It is reasonable-sounding and quietly fatal — and the next morning’s sit is skipped on its authority. Recognizing it as saṁśaya, Maya neither believes it nor argues with it; she simply notes ‘doubt is present’ and keeps the appointment with herself anyway, letting results, if any, accumulate before she judges.
deficient.vignette: Dan never doubts. Whatever he has decided—the guru, the diet, the theory—is held with a total, unexamined certainty, immune to evidence and allergic to the question ‘but what if I’m wrong?’ The healthy questioning that would protect him from folly simply never fires, and he commits, wholeheartedly, to one mistaken certainty after another.

attributes.title: Pramāda — Carelessness
attributes.aspect: Pramada is carelessness or negligence—lack of awareness and attention in practice. It is a moment-to-moment forgetting of yogic awareness.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental–Vital (heedlessness) · Ontic mode: Obstacle — an antarāya (carelessness) · Valence: Affliction / obstacle · Guṇa: Tamas–Rajas · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: Obstruction (inattention that lets practice lapse) · Key relation: One of the nine antarāyas; antagonist to the vigilance of mindful attention.
coordinate.key_relation: One of the nine antarāyas; antagonist to the vigilance of mindful attention.

balanced.label: Balanced — sustained, vigilant attention

facets.overview: When Pramada is overcome, the yogi maintains constant mindful awareness. There is steady attention without forgetting one’s purpose and practice.
facets.somatic: Mindful physical presence, careful attention to body, healthy alertness, careful actions.
facets.emotional: Emotional mindfulness, careful attention to feelings, freedom from emotional carelessness.
facets.cognitive: Constant mental awareness, careful attention, mindfulness maintained, no negligence.
facets.psychic: The soul keeps its thread; steady mindful attention is the continuity through which all practice deepens.

balanced.text:
When Pramada is overcome, the yogi maintains constant mindful awareness. There is steady attention without forgetting one’s purpose and practice.

Somatic: Mindful physical presence, careful attention to body, healthy alertness, careful actions.

Emotional: Emotional mindfulness, careful attention to feelings, freedom from emotional carelessness.

Cognitive: Constant mental awareness, careful attention, mindfulness maintained, no negligence.

Psychic: The soul keeps its thread; steady mindful attention is the continuity through which all practice deepens.
balanced.vignette: Priya keeps the thread. In her work, her practice, her promises, she maintains a steady, vigilant attention—not tense, but present—so that things don’t slip through the cracks. She notices, she follows through, she catches the small error before it grows. The care is quiet and constant.

excess.label: In excess — chronic inattention, the dropped thread

facets.overview: Excessive Pramada is habitual carelessness—forgetting practice constantly, never coming back to awareness, mindlessness as a way of life.
facets.somatic: Careless physical action, accidents, neglect of body, mindless living. Hypervigilance creating tension, inability to relax, stress-related illness.
facets.emotional: Emotional carelessness, hurting others through negligence, emotional mindlessness. Anxious over-attention, inability to be at ease, emotional rigidity from constant vigilance.
facets.cognitive: Chronic mindlessness, no attention, mind always wandering, no return to awareness. Hypervigilant mind, anxiety, inability to rest mental attention, exhaustion from constant focus.
facets.psychic: The soul keeps forgetting itself; carelessness lets hard-won awareness slip again and again, the thread dropped almost as soon as it is picked up. Excessive vigilance without rest becomes hypervigilance, anxiety, inability to ever relax. Some natural attention/inattention rhythm is healthy. Vigilance hardens into hypervigilance; terrified of slipping, the soul monitors itself so relentlessly that it can never rest in the ease that is the point. Sara can never relax her guard. Terrified of dropping the thread, she monitors everything relentlessly—every task double-checked, every possible error pre-worried, no moment of unwatchful ease—until the vigilance itself has become an exhausting anxiety. The care that should be light and steady has hardened into a clenched, sleepless watchfulness. Pramāda is the moment-to-moment lapse of awareness — not a grand failure but the small, repeated forgetting of one’s own intention. It is autopilot: the meal eaten without tasting, the resolution forgotten by mid-morning, the practice that drifts because attention simply wandered off and was not brought back. In balance, attention is sustained and self-recovering; the mind wanders and returns. In excess, the thread is dropped and not picked up, so that whole stretches of life and practice pass unwitnessed. Maya resolves, sincerely, to pause and breathe before reacting to her colleague. By the time the colleague speaks, the resolution has vanished entirely — she is three sentences into a sharp reply before she even remembers she meant to pause. This is pramāda: not malice, just the dropped thread. Naming it, she treats each forgetting not as defeat but as the very moment to begin again — which is, in fact, the whole of the practice.

excess.text:

Somatic: Careless physical action, accidents, neglect of body, mindless living. Hypervigilance creating tension, inability to relax, stress-related illness.

Emotional: Emotional carelessness, hurting others through negligence, emotional mindlessness. Anxious over-attention, inability to be at ease, emotional rigidity from constant vigilance.

Cognitive: Chronic mindlessness, no attention, mind always wandering, no return to awareness. Hypervigilant mind, anxiety, inability to rest mental attention, exhaustion from constant focus.

Psychic: The soul keeps forgetting itself; carelessness lets hard-won awareness slip again and again, the thread dropped almost as soon as it is picked up. Terrified of dropping the thread, she monitors everything relentlessly—every task double-checked, every possible error pre-worried, no moment of unwatchful ease—until the vigilance itself has become an exhausting anxiety. The care that should be light and steady has hardened into a clenched, sleepless watchfulness. Pramāda is the moment-to-moment lapse of awareness — not a grand failure but the small, repeated forgetting of one’s own intention. It is autopilot: the meal eaten without tasting, the resolution forgotten by mid-morning, the practice that drifts because attention simply wandered off and was not brought back. In excess, the thread is dropped and not picked up, so that whole stretches of life and practice pass unwitnessed. Maya resolves, sincerely, to pause and breathe before reacting to her colleague. This is pramāda: not malice, just the dropped thread. Naming it, she treats each forgetting not as defeat but as the very moment to begin again — which is, in fact, the whole of the practice.
excess.vignette: Dan is always half-somewhere-else. The thread of attention keeps dropping—the forgotten commitment, the missed detail, the practice abandoned mid-stream—not from bad intent but from a chronic carelessness that never quite lands anywhere. Things fall through him like water through a sieve, and he is perpetually, mildly surprised by the consequences.

attributes.title: Ālasya — Sloth
attributes.aspect: Alasya is physical and mental laziness—lack of effort, sloth, postponing practice. It is a tamasic obstacle related to but distinct from Styana (mental dullness specifically).

coordinate.raw: Seat: Physical–Vital (inertia, disinclination to effort) · Ontic mode: Obstacle — an antarāya (sloth) · Valence: Affliction / obstacle · Guṇa: Tamas-dominant · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: Obstruction (inertia that resists effort) · Key relation: One of the nine antarāyas; same as Ālasya the wave (Dim 9); kin to Styāna.
coordinate.key_relation: One of the nine antarāyas; same as Ālasya the wave (Dim 9); kin to Styāna.

balanced.label: Balanced — willing, ready energy for what matters

facets.overview: When Alasya is overcome, the yogi practices with appropriate effort and consistency. Energy is available for sustained engagement.
facets.somatic: Available energy for practice, body that responds to effort, vitality, sustained physical engagement.
facets.emotional: Emotional motivation, willingness to make effort, enthusiasm.
facets.cognitive: Mental discipline, ability to exert effort, sustained focus through challenges.
facets.psychic: The soul’s energy is available for what matters; willing, ready effort is itself a form of inner aliveness.

balanced.text:
When Alasya is overcome, the yogi practices with appropriate effort and consistency. Energy is available for sustained engagement.

Somatic: Available energy for practice, body that responds to effort, vitality, sustained physical engagement.

Emotional: Emotional motivation, willingness to make effort, enthusiasm.

Cognitive: Mental discipline, ability to exert effort, sustained focus through challenges.

Psychic: The soul’s energy is available for what matters; willing, ready effort is itself a form of inner aliveness.
balanced.vignette: When something matters, Ravi moves. The energy is available, the will turns over easily, the task gets started without a battle. He rests when rest is due and acts when action is due; there is no heavy inertia to overcome, just a ready willingness that meets the day.

excess.label: In excess — heaviness, postponement, the will that won’t move

facets.overview: Excessive Alasya is chronic laziness, inability to act, postponement of everything, life of inactivity, sloth.
facets.somatic: Severe physical laziness, body weighed down, refusing all effort, deteriorating from inactivity.
facets.emotional: Emotional unwillingness to do anything, motivational paralysis, apathy.
facets.cognitive: Mental laziness, refusing to think, postponing all mental effort, chronic procrastination.
facets.psychic: The soul sinks into heaviness; sloth is the will that knows the good and cannot move toward it, the postponement that quietly becomes a life.

excess.text:
Excessive Alasya is chronic laziness, inability to act, postponement of everything, life of inactivity, sloth.

Somatic: Severe physical laziness, body weighed down, refusing all effort, deteriorating from inactivity.

Emotional: Emotional unwillingness to do anything, motivational paralysis, apathy.

Cognitive: Mental laziness, refusing to think, postponing all mental effort, chronic procrastination.

Psychic: The soul sinks into heaviness; sloth is the will that knows the good and cannot move toward it, the postponement that quietly becomes a life.
excess.vignette: The will won’t move in Tom. He knows exactly what to do and cannot make himself begin—the task postponed to tomorrow, then the tomorrow after, a leaden heaviness sitting between intention and action. It is not that he is resting; it is that a great inertia has settled on him, and every worthwhile thing waits behind it, undone.

deficient.label: Deficient — restless over-activity that cannot rest

facets.overview: Total absence of any rest (false absence of laziness) becomes burnout, overwork, inability to honor body’s need for rest.
facets.somatic: Burnout from no rest, exhaustion, overwork damaging body.
facets.emotional: Emotional exhaustion from no break, anxiety about resting, emotional overload.
facets.cognitive: Mental burnout, inability to rest mind, exhaustion from constant effort.
facets.psychic: The pendulum swings to driven restlessness; refusing all rest, the soul burns itself out and mistakes exhaustion for diligence. Ālasya is the heaviness of body and will — the sloth that postpones, that finds the cushion too far and the morning too early, that always intends to begin tomorrow. Where styāna is a dullness of mind, ālasya is a leadenness of will and limb. In balance, energy is available for what matters and rest for what doesn’t. In excess, everything worthwhile is deferred. Its opposite pole, a restless inability to rest at all, drives a person into constant motion that is its own kind of avoidance. The alarm is set for the morning sit, and when it sounds the bed is warm and the practice can, surely, happen later. ‘Later’ becomes evening becomes never. This is ālasya — not a decision against practice but a body-and-will too heavy to move toward it. Maya makes the obstacle’s job harder: she lays out the cushion the night before and shrinks the commitment to something almost too small to resist, so that the heaviness has nothing large to refuse.

deficient.text:
Total absence of any rest (false absence of laziness) becomes burnout, overwork, inability to honor body’s need for rest.

Somatic: Burnout from no rest, exhaustion, overwork damaging body.

Emotional: Emotional exhaustion from no break, anxiety about resting, emotional overload.

Cognitive: Mental burnout, inability to rest mind, exhaustion from constant effort.

Psychic: The pendulum swings to driven restlessness; refusing all rest, the soul burns itself out and mistakes exhaustion for diligence. Ālasya is the heaviness of body and will — the sloth that postpones, that finds the cushion too far and the morning too early, that always intends to begin tomorrow. Where styāna is a dullness of mind, ālasya is a leadenness of will and limb. In balance, energy is available for what matters and rest for what doesn’t. In excess, everything worthwhile is deferred. Its opposite pole, a restless inability to rest at all, drives a person into constant motion that is its own kind of avoidance. The alarm is set for the morning sit, and when it sounds the bed is warm and the practice can, surely, happen later. ‘Later’ becomes evening becomes never. This is ālasya — not a decision against practice but a body-and-will too heavy to move toward it. Maya makes the obstacle’s job harder: she lays out the cushion the night before and shrinks the commitment to something almost too small to resist, so that the heaviness has nothing large to refuse.
deficient.vignette: Nadia cannot stop. Rest feels like failure; stillness makes her itch; every hour must be filled with activity, productivity, motion. The healthy capacity to be at ease has been replaced by a restless over-doing, and she drives herself from task to task, exhausted, unable to grant herself the pause her system is begging for.

attributes.title: Avirati — Sensual Craving
attributes.aspect: Avirati is craving for sense objects—the inability to turn away from sensory pleasures. It pulls the mind away from yoga practice toward sensory engagement.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital (sensual pull) · Ontic mode: Obstacle — an antarāya (sensual craving / non-restraint) · Valence: Affliction / obstacle · Guṇa: Rajas-dominant · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: Obstruction (craving that pulls attention outward) · Key relation: One of the nine antarāyas; kin to Rāga (Dim 19) and Kāma-vāsanā (Dim 12); antagonist to Brahmacharya.
coordinate.key_relation: One of the nine antarāyas; kin to Rāga (Dim 19) and Kāma-vāsanā (Dim 12); antagonist to Brahmacharya.

balanced.label: Balanced — senses enjoyed without being ruled

facets.overview: When Avirati is overcome through Vairagya (dispassion), the senses no longer dominate. The yogi can engage with senses consciously without being dragged.
facets.somatic: Healthy relationship with sensory pleasure, body free from sensory addiction, balanced engagement.
facets.emotional: Emotional freedom from sensory craving, contentment, peace with sensory experience.
facets.cognitive: Mental freedom from sensory obsession, focus available for practice, no slavery to senses.
facets.psychic: The soul enjoys the senses without being ruled by them; dispassion here is not coldness but freedom, the senses placed at the service of a larger aim.

balanced.text:
When Avirati is overcome through Vairagya (dispassion), the senses no longer dominate. The yogi can engage with senses consciously without being dragged.

Somatic: Healthy relationship with sensory pleasure, body free from sensory addiction, balanced engagement.

Emotional: Emotional freedom from sensory craving, contentment, peace with sensory experience.

Cognitive: Mental freedom from sensory obsession, focus available for practice, no slavery to senses.

Psychic: The soul enjoys the senses without being ruled by them; dispassion here is not coldness but freedom, the senses placed at the service of a larger aim.
balanced.vignette: Kira enjoys the pleasures of the senses—food, music, touch, beauty—fully and freely, and is not ruled by them. She can have the dessert or leave it, log off the feed, walk away from the sale. The senses are a source of delight she visits, not a master she serves.

excess.label: In excess — the constant outward pull toward stimulation

facets.overview: Excessive Avirati is total slavery to senses—addiction, hedonism, inability to focus on anything but sensory gratification.
facets.somatic: Sensory addiction, body controlled by sense-cravings, health damaged by excess indulgence, depleted from sensory pursuit.
facets.emotional: Emotional slavery to sensory pleasures, compulsive seeking, emotional volatility from sense-driven life.
facets.cognitive: Mind enslaved by senses, no mental focus possible, scattered attention chasing sensory objects.
facets.psychic: The soul is pulled ceaselessly outward; enslaved to stimulation, it cannot turn inward long enough for anything subtle to register.

excess.text:
Excessive Avirati is total slavery to senses—addiction, hedonism, inability to focus on anything but sensory gratification.

Somatic: Sensory addiction, body controlled by sense-cravings, health damaged by excess indulgence, depleted from sensory pursuit.

Emotional: Emotional slavery to sensory pleasures, compulsive seeking, emotional volatility from sense-driven life.

Cognitive: Mind enslaved by senses, no mental focus possible, scattered attention chasing sensory objects.

Psychic: The soul is pulled ceaselessly outward; enslaved to stimulation, it cannot turn inward long enough for anything subtle to register.
excess.vignette: Ben is pulled outward all day, every day—the next snack, the next scroll, the next hit of stimulation—his attention captured helplessly by whatever pleasure is nearest. The senses have become a constant tug he cannot resist, and the inner quiet that anything deeper requires keeps getting drowned in the noise of wanting.

deficient.label: Deficient — joyless aversion to the senses, forced asceticism

facets.overview: Forced suppression of all sensory engagement creates harsh asceticism, denial of natural function, eventual breakdown of suppression.
facets.somatic: Sensory deprivation, atrophied function, harm from extreme denial.
facets.emotional: Emotional suppression of natural pleasure, harsh denial, eventual eruption.
facets.cognitive: Mental suppression with eventual breakdown, distorted thinking from forced denial.
facets.psychic: Renunciation forced rather than ripened becomes its own affliction; joyless aversion to the senses splits the soul against its own embodiment. Avirati is the mind’s outward pull toward sense-pleasure — the inability to turn the attention inward because the next pleasant thing is always more inviting than the quiet. It is the scroll, the snack, the notification, the small hit of stimulation that the mind reaches for in any pause. In balance, the senses are enjoyed without governing the day. In excess, every gap is immediately filled, and stillness becomes intolerable. Forced against its nature, it can swing to the deficient pole — a joyless, white-knuckled rejection of all pleasure that is its own distortion. Maya sits to meditate and within thirty seconds the hand reaches, almost on its own, for the phone — just to check, just for a second. The pull is not toward anything in particular; it is the sheer discomfort of an unfilled moment. This is avirati. She does not fight it with contempt; she simply puts the phone in another room before sitting, removing the easy object, and lets the craving rise and pass in the empty space it was reaching to fill.

deficient.text:
Forced suppression of all sensory engagement creates harsh asceticism, denial of natural function, eventual breakdown of suppression.

Somatic: Sensory deprivation, atrophied function, harm from extreme denial.

Emotional: Emotional suppression of natural pleasure, harsh denial, eventual eruption.

Cognitive: Mental suppression with eventual breakdown, distorted thinking from forced denial.

Psychic: Renunciation forced rather than ripened becomes its own affliction; joyless aversion to the senses splits the soul against its own embodiment. Avirati is the mind’s outward pull toward sense-pleasure — the inability to turn the attention inward because the next pleasant thing is always more inviting than the quiet. It is the scroll, the snack, the notification, the small hit of stimulation that the mind reaches for in any pause. In balance, the senses are enjoyed without governing the day. In excess, every gap is immediately filled, and stillness becomes intolerable. Forced against its nature, it can swing to the deficient pole — a joyless, white-knuckled rejection of all pleasure that is its own distortion. Maya sits to meditate and within thirty seconds the hand reaches, almost on its own, for the phone — just to check, just for a second. The pull is not toward anything in particular; it is the sheer discomfort of an unfilled moment. This is avirati. She does not fight it with contempt; she simply puts the phone in another room before sitting, removing the easy object, and lets the craving rise and pass in the empty space it was reaching to fill.
deficient.vignette: Priya has declared war on pleasure. The good meal, the soft comfort, the ordinary sensory joys are all refused as temptations, and her life has gone grey and grim with a forced asceticism. This is not freedom from the senses but a joyless aversion to them, and the rigidity of the denial is its own kind of bondage.

attributes.title: Bhrāntidarśana — False Perception
attributes.aspect: Bhrantidarshana is false perception or erroneous view—wrong understanding of practice, philosophy, or experience. Mistaking progress, misidentifying experiences.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental (mistaken understanding) · Ontic mode: Obstacle — an antarāya (false perception) · Valence: Affliction / obstacle · Guṇa: Tamas–Rajas · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: Obstruction (wrong view that misdirects practice) · Key relation: One of the nine antarāyas; kin to Viparyaya (Dim 20) and Avidyā (Dim 19).
coordinate.key_relation: One of the nine antarāyas; kin to Viparyaya (Dim 20) and Avidyā (Dim 19).

balanced.label: Balanced — accurate understanding of the path and one’s place on it

facets.overview: When Bhrantidarshana is overcome, the yogi has correct understanding of practice, experiences, and reality. There is clear discrimination about progress.
facets.somatic: Accurate perception of body’s signals, healthy embodied understanding.
facets.emotional: Accurate emotional understanding, no false self-assessment of feelings.
facets.cognitive: Correct understanding of practice and experiences, clear discrimination, accurate progress assessment.
facets.psychic: The soul sees the path and its own place on it accurately; right understanding keeps the whole journey honest.

balanced.text:
When Bhrantidarshana is overcome, the yogi has correct understanding of practice, experiences, and reality. There is clear discrimination about progress.

Somatic: Accurate perception of body’s signals, healthy embodied understanding.

Emotional: Accurate emotional understanding, no false self-assessment of feelings.

Cognitive: Correct understanding of practice and experiences, clear discrimination, accurate progress assessment.

Psychic: The soul sees the path and its own place on it accurately; right understanding keeps the whole journey honest.
balanced.vignette: Meera has a clear-eyed sense of the path and of where she actually stands on it—no inflated ideas of her own attainment, no confusion about what the practice is for. She sees the map and the territory accurately, and so she doesn’t waste years walking confidently in the wrong direction.

excess.label: In excess — spiritual self-deception, mistaking the map

facets.overview: Severe Bhrantidarshana creates deeply false views, mistaking progress, spiritual delusions, fixed wrong understandings that prevent correction.
facets.somatic: Misreading body’s signals consistently, harmful practices believed beneficial. Accurate physical perception.
facets.emotional: Mistaking emotional states (calling agitation peace), emotional self-deception. Accurate emotional awareness.
facets.cognitive: Deep false views about reality, spiritual delusions, mistaking experiences, fundamental misunderstanding of path. Clear understanding without delusion.
facets.psychic: The soul wanders a self-built map; spiritual delusion — mistaking imagination for attainment — is among the most dangerous obstacles precisely because it feels like progress. Absence of Bhrantidarshana is the desired state. The deficient version (false beliefs about having no false beliefs) is itself a form of Bhrantidarshana. A subtler trap: the soul grows certain it is now beyond all misperception, and that very confidence becomes the last and most invisible distortion. Karl has reached the subtlest version of the trap: he is now certain that his perception is finally, wholly free of distortion—that he sees things exactly as they are. This last, refined self-deception is the hardest of all, because it disguises itself as clarity, and a man convinced he can no longer be fooled is the easiest of all to fool. Bhrāntidarśana is erroneous understanding applied to the path itself — mistaking a pleasant experience for awakening, misjudging one’s own progress, following a wrong map with full conviction, or interpreting genuine experiences through a distorting frame. It is the most dangerous obstacle for the sincere because it disguises itself as attainment. In balance, a practitioner holds an accurate, humble picture of where they actually are. In excess, they are sincerely convinced of a position they have not reached, and the conviction itself bars further movement. After a few unusually calm sessions, the thought forms: ‘I’ve really gotten somewhere now — I’m past the basics.’ Subtly, practice loosens; the calm was pleasant and has been mistaken for arrival. This is bhrāntidarśana, and its symptom is precisely the slackening it produces. Recognizing it, Maya treats every good session as weather rather than as a summit reached, and keeps beginning as if she knows nothing — which, the tradition would say, is itself the more accurate view.

Cognitive: Deep false views about reality, spiritual delusions, mistaking experiences, fundamental misunderstanding of path. Clear understanding without delusion.

It is the most dangerous obstacle for the sincere because it disguises itself as attainment. In balance, a practitioner holds an accurate, humble picture of where they actually are. In excess, they are sincerely convinced of a position they have not reached, and the conviction itself bars further movement. Recognizing it, Maya treats every good session as weather rather than as a summit reached, and keeps beginning as if she knows nothing — which, the tradition would say, is itself the more accurate view.
excess.vignette: Ravi has mistaken the map for the territory. He believes he has attained states he has only read about, interprets ordinary experiences as profound breakthroughs, and has built an elaborate spiritual self-image on a foundation of misperception. The false view feels exactly like insight from the inside, which is precisely why it is so hard to correct.

attributes.title: Alabdha-bhūmikatva — Failure to Gain Ground
attributes.aspect: Alabdha-bhumikatva is the inability to reach the higher states of yoga despite practice. There is effort but no fruit—failure to gain ground.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental–Psychic (the plateau) · Ontic mode: Obstacle — an antarāya (failure to gain ground) · Valence: Affliction / obstacle · Guṇa: Rajas–Tamas · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: Obstruction (stalled progress) · Key relation: One of the nine antarāyas; the felt failure to advance through the stages (Dim 11).
coordinate.ontic_mode: Obstacle — an antarāya (failure to gain ground)
coordinate.key_relation: One of the nine antarāyas; the felt failure to advance through the stages (Dim 11).

balanced.label: Balanced — steady progress in due season

facets.overview: When Alabdha-bhumikatva is overcome, the yogi makes progress and attains higher states. Practice bears fruit, advancement occurs.
facets.somatic: Body shows progress in practice, gaining capacity, fruit of effort visible in body.
facets.emotional: Emotional advancement, transformation visible, fruits of practice felt.
facets.cognitive: Mental progress, higher states attained, advancement on path, gaining new ground.
facets.psychic: The soul ripens in its own season; steady progress, trusted without demand, is its own quiet reassurance.

balanced.text:
When Alabdha-bhumikatva is overcome, the yogi makes progress and attains higher states. Practice bears fruit, advancement occurs.

Somatic: Body shows progress in practice, gaining capacity, fruit of effort visible in body.

Emotional: Emotional advancement, transformation visible, fruits of practice felt.

Cognitive: Mental progress, higher states attained, advancement on path, gaining new ground.

Psychic: The soul ripens in its own season; steady progress, trusted without demand, is its own quiet reassurance.
balanced.vignette: Sunita progresses at the pace real growth actually takes—some plateaus, some leaps, a general steady deepening over years. She does not force it or fret about it; the ground gets gained in its own due season, and she trusts the slow work enough to keep showing up for it.

excess.label: In excess — over-attachment to attainment; spiritual materialism, grasping for higher states

facets.overview: Excessive focus on reaching higher states becomes attachment to attainment, spiritual materialism, frustrated grasping for experiences.
facets.somatic: Forcing physical attainment, harm from over-effort, body strained by grasping for states. Body not responding to practice, no physical advancement, plateau or regression.
facets.emotional: Emotional frustration with progress, spiritual materialism, attachment to experiences. Frustration from no emotional progress, despair about path, emotional stagnation.
facets.cognitive: Mental obsession with attainment, grasping for higher states, anxiety about progress. Mental discouragement, no breakthrough states, no insight despite effort, stuck.
facets.psychic: The soul grasps at the very states it seeks; attachment to attainment turns practice into spiritual materialism, the wanting itself blocking the door. When Alabdha-bhumikatva is the dominant state—much practice but no progress, ground not gained, advancement blocked. The plateau holds; sincere effort yields no ground, and the gap between labor and fruit tests whether the soul can continue without the reward it expected. Dan has practiced faithfully for years and feels utterly stuck. The effort is real and sustained, and the ground simply will not come—the same plateau, month after month, no discernible deepening, the door that won’t open however he pushes. He is doing the work and not, for now, receiving its fruit, and the not-receiving tests everything in him. Alabdha-bhūmikatva is the obstacle of the plateau — sincere, sustained effort that nonetheless seems to yield no higher ground. The practice is done; the fruit does not come; and the gap between effort and result breeds discouragement, which is itself the obstacle deepening. In balance, a practitioner trusts the slow ripening and continues without demanding a timeline. When the obstacle dominates, the failure to advance becomes proof, to the doubting mind, that advance is impossible — and the practice is abandoned just short of where it might have turned. Months in, Maya feels she is exactly where she started — same restlessness, same wandering mind, no breakthrough. The lack of visible progress feels like a verdict. This is alabdha-bhūmikatva, and its danger is that the plateau is often the last stretch before the ground gives way to something new. She holds the long view, lowers her demand for results, and keeps practicing on the plateau itself, which is where the real work quietly happens.

excess.text:
Excessive focus on reaching higher states becomes attachment to attainment, spiritual materialism, frustrated grasping for experiences.

Somatic: Forcing physical attainment, harm from over-effort, body strained by grasping for states. Body not responding to practice, no physical advancement, plateau or regression.

Emotional: Emotional frustration with progress, spiritual materialism, attachment to experiences. Frustration from no emotional progress, despair about path, emotional stagnation.

Cognitive: Mental obsession with attainment, grasping for higher states, anxiety about progress. Mental discouragement, no breakthrough states, no insight despite effort, stuck.

Psychic: The soul grasps at the very states it seeks; attachment to attainment turns practice into spiritual materialism, the wanting itself blocking the door. She holds the long view, lowers her demand for results, and keeps practicing on the plateau itself, which is where the real work quietly happens.
excess.vignette: Ben treats spiritual attainment like a collection to acquire—chasing states, comparing his ‘progress,’ grasping for the next higher experience, impatient with anything that feels like a plateau. The grasping itself has become the obstacle; so busy reaching for the next rung, he never actually stands on the one he is on.

attributes.title: Anavasthitatva — Instability
attributes.aspect: Anavasthitatva is the inability to maintain a state once attained—instability where ground is gained then lost. The mind cannot stabilize in higher states.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental–Psychic (loss of a gained level) · Ontic mode: Obstacle — an antarāya (instability) · Valence: Affliction / obstacle · Guṇa: Rajas-dominant · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: Obstruction (the loss of an attained ground) · Key relation: One of the nine antarāyas; counterpart to Alabdha-bhūmikatva — losing rather than failing to reach.
coordinate.causal_status: Obstruction (the loss of an attained ground)
coordinate.key_relation: One of the nine antarāyas; counterpart to Alabdha-bhūmikatva — losing rather than failing to reach.

balanced.label: Balanced — ground gained and held

facets.overview: When Anavasthitatva is overcome, the yogi stabilizes attained states. Progress is maintained, ground is held, advancement consolidates.
facets.somatic: Maintained physical practice, stabilized practice routines, body’s gains preserved.
facets.emotional: Emotional stability in attained states, maintained transformation, lasting changes.
facets.cognitive: Mental stabilization in higher states, sustained meditation, consolidated wisdom.
facets.psychic: The soul consolidates what it gains; a state once reached becomes stable ground for the next, and progress stops leaking away.

balanced.text:
When Anavasthitatva is overcome, the yogi stabilizes attained states. Progress is maintained, ground is held, advancement consolidates.

Somatic: Maintained physical practice, stabilized practice routines, body’s gains preserved.

Emotional: Emotional stability in attained states, maintained transformation, lasting changes.

Cognitive: Mental stabilization in higher states, sustained meditation, consolidated wisdom.

Psychic: The soul consolidates what it gains; a state once reached becomes stable ground for the next, and progress stops leaking away.
balanced.vignette: When Meera reaches a new level—of calm, of clarity, of skill—she can hold it; the gain stabilizes and becomes the new floor she stands on, rather than a peak she slides back down from. She integrates what she attains, and so her progress accumulates instead of endlessly resetting.

excess.label: In excess — rigid clinging to attained states; attachment to specific experiences

facets.overview: Excessive rigidity in trying to maintain states becomes attachment to specific experiences, refusing natural fluctuations, gripping at attainment.
facets.somatic: Rigid attempt to maintain physical states, inability to flow with body’s natural changes. Inability to maintain physical practice, body gains lost quickly, inconsistent body.
facets.emotional: Anxious gripping at emotional states, fear of losing peace, rigid emotional control. Emotional gains lost rapidly, instability, peace coming and going without consolidation.
facets.cognitive: Rigid attempt to maintain meditation states, frustration with natural mental fluctuations. Meditation states lost immediately, mental gains slipping away, inability to stabilize.
facets.psychic: The soul clings rigidly to particular experiences; attachment to a remembered state prevents the fluid movement that real growth requires. When Anavasthitatva is dominant—states are reached but immediately lost, fluctuation prevents consolidation, ground constantly slipping away. The hardest obstacle of the path: ground is gained and instantly lost, the calm of Tuesday gone by Thursday — until the soul learns to treat this fluctuation as the ordinary texture of consolidation rather than as failure. Sam keeps arriving and slipping back. He reaches a real steadiness—for a week, a day, an hour—and then it is gone, the gain dissolving as quickly as it came, so that he is forever starting over from the same place. The progress won’t stabilize; whatever he attains runs through his fingers, and the chronic instability wears him down. Anavasthitatva is the inability to hold a state once it has been attained — the calm reached on Tuesday gone by Thursday, the insight that fades, the ground gained and then lost again and again. It is the final obstacle of the list precisely because it troubles those who have begun to succeed. In balance, a state once reached becomes a stable platform for the next. When the obstacle dominates, the mind cannot settle into any gain, and the repeated loss of hard-won ground is exhausting and demoralizing — unless it is understood as the ordinary texture of consolidation rather than as failure. For one beautiful week the morning sits are deep and the days are calmer; then, with no clear cause, it all evaporates, and Maya is back to a scattered, resistant mind, mourning the steadiness she briefly had. This is anavasthitatva. Rather than read the loss as proof that the gain was illusory, she treats stabilization as its own long phase of practice — expecting the tide to go in and out many times before the ground is truly held.

excess.text:
Excessive rigidity in trying to maintain states becomes attachment to specific experiences, refusing natural fluctuations, gripping at attainment.

Somatic: Rigid attempt to maintain physical states, inability to flow with body’s natural changes. Inability to maintain physical practice, body gains lost quickly, inconsistent body.

Emotional: Anxious gripping at emotional states, fear of losing peace, rigid emotional control. Emotional gains lost rapidly, instability, peace coming and going without consolidation.

Cognitive: Rigid attempt to maintain meditation states, frustration with natural mental fluctuations. Meditation states lost immediately, mental gains slipping away, inability to stabilize.

excess.vignette: Karl reached a beautiful state once and has been clutching at it ever since—rigidly trying to reproduce the exact experience, attached to a specific attainment, unable to let the practice move on. The clinging has frozen him; so busy gripping the ground he gained, he cannot take the next step off it.

attributes.title: Maitrī — Friendliness Toward the Happy
attributes.aspect: Maitri is friendliness or loving-kindness toward those who are happy and successful. It dissolves envy and creates serene mental clarity. One of the four locks/keys to mental peace.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital (the heart’s warmth) · Ontic mode: Virtue — a brahmavihāra (loving-kindness) · Valence: Virtue to cultivate (near enemy: attachment; far enemy: ill-will) · Guṇa: Sattva-dominant · Scale: Interpersonal (Ādhibhautika) · Causal status: Condition (warms the heart toward all beings) · Key relation: One of the four brahmavihāras — the key for those who are happy; antidote to Dveṣa (Dim 19).
coordinate.valence: Virtue to cultivate (near enemy: attachment; far enemy: ill-will)
coordinate.causal_status: Condition (warms the heart toward all beings)
coordinate.key_relation: One of the four brahmavihāras — the key for those who are happy; antidote to Dveṣa (Dim 19).

balanced.label: Balanced — genuine gladness at another’s good fortune

facets.overview: When Maitri is established, a person feels genuine warmth and friendliness toward happy and successful people. Envy dissolves, mental serenity arises.
facets.somatic: Open warm physical presence with happy others, healthy nervous system, body that doesn’t tense around success.
facets.emotional: Genuine joy at others’ happiness, warmth in relationships, freedom from envy, emotional abundance.
facets.cognitive: Mental serenity, freedom from comparison thinking, clarity from non-envious mind, peaceful thinking.
facets.psychic: The soul recognizes itself in another’s happiness; gladness at others’ good fortune dissolves the boundary between self and other, which is the secret the brahmavihāras exist to teach.

balanced.text:
When Maitri is established, a person feels genuine warmth and friendliness toward happy and successful people. Envy dissolves, mental serenity arises.

Somatic: Open warm physical presence with happy others, healthy nervous system, body that doesn’t tense around success.

Emotional: Genuine joy at others’ happiness, warmth in relationships, freedom from envy, emotional abundance.

Cognitive: Mental serenity, freedom from comparison thinking, clarity from non-envious mind, peaceful thinking.

Psychic: The soul recognizes itself in another’s happiness; gladness at others’ good fortune dissolves the boundary between self and other, which is the secret the brahmavihāras exist to teach.
balanced.vignette: When her friend lands the dream job, Priya’s first feeling is uncomplicated gladness—warm, genuine, no undertow of comparison. She celebrates without keeping score, delighting in others’ good fortune as if it were partly her own. Friendliness, in her, is a reflex, and it makes her a joy to succeed around.

excess.label: In excess — ingratiating over-attachment, people-pleasing

facets.overview: Excessive Maitri without discernment becomes inability to address others’ faults, false agreeableness, sycophancy.
facets.somatic: Physical inability to take stands, weakness in face of harm to happy others.
facets.emotional: Emotional sycophancy, false agreeableness, inability to give honest feedback.
facets.cognitive: Mental inability to discriminate, avoiding necessary critical assessment, false harmony.
facets.psychic: The soul loses itself in the need to be liked; friendliness curdles into people-pleasing, and the self disappears into approval rather than into love.

excess.text:
Excessive Maitri without discernment becomes inability to address others’ faults, false agreeableness, sycophancy.

Somatic: Physical inability to take stands, weakness in face of harm to happy others.

Emotional: Emotional sycophancy, false agreeableness, inability to give honest feedback.

Cognitive: Mental inability to discriminate, avoiding necessary critical assessment, false harmony.

Psychic: The soul loses itself in the need to be liked; friendliness curdles into people-pleasing, and the self disappears into approval rather than into love.
excess.vignette: Ravi’s warmth toward everyone has a needy edge. He must be liked by all, agrees with whoever he is talking to, showers approval to secure approval in return, and cannot bear anyone’s displeasure. The genuine friendliness has slid into people-pleasing; he has so merged his goodwill with his need to be loved that he no longer quite knows what he actually thinks.

deficient.label: Deficient — envy, comparison, the soured response to others’ joy

facets.overview: Absence of Maitri creates envy, jealousy of successful people, bitter comparison, suffering at others’ joy.
facets.somatic: Tension in presence of successful others, stress from envy, physical signs of jealousy.
facets.emotional: Envy and jealousy, bitterness, emotional suffering at others’ success, inability to feel for happy others.
facets.cognitive: Comparison thinking, mental bitterness, distorted perception of success, agitation from envy.
facets.psychic: The soul contracts into the prison of comparison; envy at another’s joy is the exact measure of how separate one still feels. Maitrī is the warm, open friendliness that meets another’s happiness with gladness rather than comparison. In balance, it lets you celebrate a friend’s success cleanly, without the small inner subtraction that asks ‘what about me.’ In excess, friendliness can curdle into ingratiation — an over-attachment to being liked, a people-pleasing that loses its own ground. In deficiency, its absence is envy: the happiness of others lands as a wound, the social feed becomes a daily measuring of oneself against everyone’s highlight reel, and other people’s good news quietly diminishes one’s own day. A close friend announces a wonderful new job. Through deficient maitrī, Maya’s congratulations are warm on the surface and cold underneath — a flush of ‘why is it always them,’ a comparison that sours the rest of her evening. Through balanced maitrī, the friend’s joy is simply contagious; there is room in her for it, and the gladness she feels for them turns out to leave her own mind lighter, not heavier — which is exactly the clarifying effect Patañjali promised.

deficient.text:
Absence of Maitri creates envy, jealousy of successful people, bitter comparison, suffering at others’ joy.

Somatic: Tension in presence of successful others, stress from envy, physical signs of jealousy.

Emotional: Envy and jealousy, bitterness, emotional suffering at others’ success, inability to feel for happy others.

Cognitive: Comparison thinking, mental bitterness, distorted perception of success, agitation from envy.

Psychic: The soul contracts into the prison of comparison; envy at another’s joy is the exact measure of how separate one still feels. Maitrī is the warm, open friendliness that meets another’s happiness with gladness rather than comparison. In balance, it lets you celebrate a friend’s success cleanly, without the small inner subtraction that asks ‘what about me.’ In excess, friendliness can curdle into ingratiation — an over-attachment to being liked, a people-pleasing that loses its own ground. deficient.vignette: When a colleague succeeds, something in Dan curdles. He smiles and congratulates and, underneath, measures the win against his own life and finds himself diminished by it. Another’s good fortune registers not as shared gladness but as a personal loss, and the constant comparison poisons friendships he genuinely wants to keep.

attributes.title: Karuṇā — Compassion Toward the Suffering
attributes.aspect: Karuna is compassion toward those who are suffering. It dissolves cruelty and indifference, creating mental purity and serenity.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital (tenderness toward pain) · Ontic mode: Virtue — a brahmavihāra (compassion) · Valence: Virtue to cultivate (near enemy: pity / grief; far enemy: cruelty) · Guṇa: Sattva-dominant · Scale: Interpersonal (Ādhibhautika) · Causal status: Condition (opens the heart to another’s suffering) · Key relation: One of the four brahmavihāras — the key for those who suffer; the ripened form of Śoka’s Karuṇā Rasa (Dim 8).
coordinate.valence: Virtue to cultivate (near enemy: pity / grief; far enemy: cruelty)
coordinate.causal_status: Condition (opens the heart to another’s suffering)
coordinate.key_relation: One of the four brahmavihāras — the key for those who suffer; the ripened form of Śoka’s Karuṇā Rasa (Dim 8).

balanced.label: Balanced — warm, steady compassion that can act

facets.overview: When Karuna is established, genuine compassion arises in face of suffering. The yogi feels for others without absorption, helps wisely.
facets.somatic: Open heart center, warm physical presence with suffering others, healthy compassionate engagement.
facets.emotional: Genuine compassion and warmth toward suffering, ability to help from balance, emotional generosity.
facets.cognitive: Wisdom about how to alleviate suffering, clear thinking compassion, mental purity from caring.
facets.psychic: The soul meets suffering with a compassion that can act — the heart open and the ground steady, mirroring the boundless mercy it is learning to embody.

balanced.text:
When Karuna is established, genuine compassion arises in face of suffering. The yogi feels for others without absorption, helps wisely.

Somatic: Open heart center, warm physical presence with suffering others, healthy compassionate engagement.

Emotional: Genuine compassion and warmth toward suffering, ability to help from balance, emotional generosity.

Cognitive: Wisdom about how to alleviate suffering, clear thinking compassion, mental purity from caring.

Psychic: The soul meets suffering with a compassion that can act — the heart open and the ground steady, mirroring the boundless mercy it is learning to embody.
balanced.vignette: When someone is suffering, Meera can stay—present, warm, moved—and also steady enough to actually help. She feels the other’s pain without being swept under by it, and from that grounded compassion she does the useful thing: the meal delivered, the call made, the hard truth gently told. Her heart is open and her hands still work.

excess.label: In excess — over-identification, burnout, drowning in another’s pain

facets.overview: Excessive Karuna becomes emotional absorption in others’ pain, codependent rescuing, overwhelm from compassion, burnout.
facets.somatic: Compassion fatigue, physical exhaustion from over-helping, body absorbing others’ tension.
facets.emotional: Emotional overwhelm by others’ suffering, codependent rescuing, emotional drowning.
facets.cognitive: Mental confusion about helping vs enabling, inability to maintain perspective.
facets.psychic: The soul drowns in another’s pain, losing the steadiness from which real help flows; compassion without equanimity exhausts itself.

excess.text:
Excessive Karuna becomes emotional absorption in others’ pain, codependent rescuing, overwhelm from compassion, burnout.

Somatic: Compassion fatigue, physical exhaustion from over-helping, body absorbing others’ tension.

Emotional: Emotional overwhelm by others’ suffering, codependent rescuing, emotional drowning.

Cognitive: Mental confusion about helping vs enabling, inability to maintain perspective.

Psychic: The soul drowns in another’s pain, losing the steadiness from which real help flows; compassion without equanimity exhausts itself.
excess.vignette: Nadia feels everyone’s pain as her own, and it is drowning her. She cannot hear of a suffering without taking it fully into her body, cannot set a boundary, cannot stop giving until she is depleted—and in the depletion she becomes useless to the very people she is trying to save. Her compassion has no shore, and it is pulling her under.

deficient.label: Deficient — cruelty, cold indifference, the turned-away face

facets.overview: Absence of Karuna creates cruelty, indifference to suffering, hardness of heart, inability to care.
facets.somatic: Closed body in presence of suffering, physical coldness, hard presence.
facets.emotional: Emotional coldness, cruelty, inability to feel for sufferers, hardness of heart.
facets.cognitive: Mental dismissal of others’ suffering, callous thinking, rationalization for not caring.
facets.psychic: The soul hardens and turns away; the closed heart is the surest sign of a self still defended against the world’s pain. Karuṇā is compassion toward those who suffer — the open-hearted wish to relieve pain, steady enough to remain present to it. In balance, it lets you stay with someone’s grief without either fleeing or collapsing, and to help where help is possible. In excess, compassion loses its footing and becomes over-identification: taking on another’s pain as one’s own until one is flooded, exhausted, and finally burned out — a common fate of caregivers who feel everything and cannot put it down. In deficiency, it hardens into indifference or even cruelty: the suffering of others met with avoidance, contempt, or a wall. A colleague breaks down in tears over a personal loss. Through deficient karuṇā, Maya feels only awkwardness and the urge to escape the room. Through excess karuṇā, she absorbs the grief so completely that she carries it home and cannot sleep, no use to anyone. Through balanced karuṇā, she stays — present, warm, unhurried — feeling the pain without becoming it, able to offer the simple steadiness that actually helps, and able, afterward, to set it down.

deficient.vignette: Suffering leaves Dan cold. The homeless man, the grieving friend, the colleague in crisis—each meets a turned-away face, an impatience, sometimes a flicker of contempt for weakness. Where compassion should arise there is only indifference, and the hardness that protects him from others’ pain has quietly walled him off from their humanity, and his own.

attributes.title: Muditā — Joy at the Virtuous
attributes.aspect: Mudita is joy at others’ virtue or happiness, particularly the gladness at seeing meritorious actions. It dissolves competitive envy regarding spiritual or moral accomplishment.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital (gladness at another’s good) · Ontic mode: Virtue — a brahmavihāra (sympathetic joy) · Valence: Virtue to cultivate (near enemy: comparison / flattery; far enemy: envy) · Guṇa: Sattva-dominant · Scale: Interpersonal (Ādhibhautika) · Causal status: Condition (rejoices in another’s good fortune) · Key relation: One of the four brahmavihāras — the key for the fortunate and virtuous; antidote to Asūyā (envy, Dim 9).
coordinate.valence: Virtue to cultivate (near enemy: comparison / flattery; far enemy: envy)
coordinate.key_relation: One of the four brahmavihāras — the key for the fortunate and virtuous; antidote to Asūyā (envy, Dim 9).

balanced.label: Balanced — sympathetic joy at goodness and merit

facets.overview: When Mudita is established, the yogi feels genuine joy at others’ virtue and spiritual progress. This dissolves competitive spiritual envy.
facets.somatic: Body that lights up at others’ goodness, healthy joyful response, warm presence.
facets.emotional: Genuine emotional joy at virtue, freedom from spiritual envy, warm celebration of goodness.
facets.cognitive: Mental celebration of virtue, freedom from comparison about progress, joyful thinking.
facets.psychic: The soul delights in goodness wherever it appears, taking joy in another’s virtue as its own; this gladness quietly erodes the comparing, competing ego.

balanced.text:
When Mudita is established, the yogi feels genuine joy at others’ virtue and spiritual progress. This dissolves competitive spiritual envy.

Somatic: Body that lights up at others’ goodness, healthy joyful response, warm presence.

Emotional: Genuine emotional joy at virtue, freedom from spiritual envy, warm celebration of goodness.

Cognitive: Mental celebration of virtue, freedom from comparison about progress, joyful thinking.

Psychic: The soul delights in goodness wherever it appears, taking joy in another’s virtue as its own; this gladness quietly erodes the comparing, competing ego.
balanced.vignette: When Kavya sees genuine goodness—a mentor’s integrity, a stranger’s courage, a friend’s hard-won growth—she feels a clean, sympathetic joy, an uplift at the sight of merit that inspires rather than diminishes her. She can admire without envy and celebrate excellence wherever she finds it. Others’ virtue gladdens her and quietly raises her own sights.

excess.label: In excess — idealization, hero-worship that loses discernment

facets.overview: Excessive Mudita can become overlooking faults, refusing to recognize problems, false positivity about everything.
facets.somatic: Inappropriate positive response to harmful situations.
facets.emotional: False positivity, inability to feel appropriate concern, emotional bypassing of legitimate issues.
facets.cognitive: Inability to recognize problems, distorted positive thinking, denial of legitimate concerns.
facets.psychic: The soul loses discernment in idealization; admiration becomes hero-worship, projecting onto another the light it has not yet claimed in itself.

excess.text:
Excessive Mudita can become overlooking faults, refusing to recognize problems, false positivity about everything.

Somatic: Inappropriate positive response to harmful situations.

Emotional: False positivity, inability to feel appropriate concern, emotional bypassing of legitimate issues.

Cognitive: Inability to recognize problems, distorted positive thinking, denial of legitimate concerns.

Psychic: The soul loses discernment in idealization; admiration becomes hero-worship, projecting onto another the light it has not yet claimed in itself.
excess.vignette: Ben’s admiration has no brakes. He idolizes his teachers, his heroes, his gurus—projecting perfection onto them, excusing their flaws, following them past the point where discernment should kick in. The healthy joy at goodness has become hero-worship, and in surrendering his judgment to those he admires, he has made himself easy to mislead.

deficient.label: Deficient — competitive resentment, the begrudging of others’ goodness

facets.overview: Absence of Mudita creates jealousy of virtue, resentment of others’ spiritual progress, competitive comparison on the path.
facets.somatic: Physical tension around virtuous others, stress from spiritual comparison.
facets.emotional: Jealousy of virtue, resentment of others’ progress, spiritual envy, bitter comparison.
facets.cognitive: Mental competition in spirituality, distorted thinking about progress, agitation from comparison.
facets.psychic: The soul begrudges what it cannot yet celebrate; resentment at another’s goodness reveals the ego measuring itself against everyone. Muditā is gladness at another’s virtue, merit, and good fortune — particularly the appreciative joy at seeing someone do genuine good. It is the specific antidote to the subtle, corrosive envy that can arise even on the spiritual path, where another’s evident progress or kindness provokes not admiration but a competitive ache. In balance, the goodness of others is a delight and even an inspiration. In excess, appreciation tips into idealization or hero-worship that loses discernment. In deficiency, it sours into the begrudging response that cannot let another’s goodness simply be good — that must diminish it, doubt its motives, or measure itself against it. Maya watches a peer give freely of their time to a struggling newcomer, patient and generous in a way she knows she rarely manages. Through deficient muditā, the sight stings — a competitive ‘they’re just doing it to look good’ that protects her from feeling smaller. Through balanced muditā, the same scene simply gladdens her, and the gladness carries a clean inspiration: not ‘I should compete’ but ‘how good that this exists,’ and a quiet wish to be more like that herself.

Cognitive: Mental competition in spirituality, distorted thinking about progress, agitation from comparison.

Psychic: The soul begrudges what it cannot yet celebrate; resentment at another’s goodness reveals the ego measuring itself against everyone. deficient.vignette: When someone is praised for their virtue, Ravi bristles. Another’s goodness feels like an accusation; their integrity, a rebuke to his own; and so he minimizes it, finds the hidden angle, begrudges the credit. The joy that others’ merit should evoke curdles in him into competitive resentment, and he is left, again, a little smaller for it.

attributes.title: Upekṣā — Equanimity Toward the Wicked
attributes.aspect: Upeksha is equanimity (sometimes translated as ‘indifference’) toward those who are unvirtuous or wicked. Not approval, but freedom from being disturbed by their vice. It preserves mental peace.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Psychic–Mental (the steady, even heart) · Ontic mode: Virtue — a brahmavihāra (equanimity) · Valence: Virtue to cultivate (near enemy: indifference; far enemy: agitation / partiality) · Guṇa: Sattva, tending beyond the guṇas · Scale: Interpersonal (Ādhibhautika), grounded in the individual · Causal status: Condition (holds the heart even amid what cannot be changed) · Key relation: One of the four brahmavihāras — the key for the difficult and beyond-help; kin to Śama (Dim 8) and Udāsīnatā (Dim 10).
coordinate.valence: Virtue to cultivate (near enemy: indifference; far enemy: agitation / partiality)
coordinate.causal_status: Condition (holds the heart even amid what cannot be changed)
coordinate.key_relation: One of the four brahmavihāras — the key for the difficult and beyond-help; kin to Śama (Dim 8) and Udāsīnatā (Dim 10).

balanced.label: Balanced — non-disturbance in the face of wrongdoing

facets.overview: When Upeksha is established, the yogi maintains equanimity in presence of unvirtuous behavior. There is wise discernment without reactive disturbance.
facets.somatic: Calm body in presence of harmful others, healthy nervous system, no reactive tension.
facets.emotional: Emotional equanimity facing wickedness, freedom from being thrown by others’ vice, peace.
facets.cognitive: Mental peace despite others’ wrong actions, clear discrimination without reactive judgment, serenity.
facets.psychic: The soul rests undisturbed before wrongdoing, neither condoning nor inflamed; equanimity is the highest of the four, the steadiness of one no longer ruled by reaction.

balanced.text:
When Upeksha is established, the yogi maintains equanimity in presence of unvirtuous behavior. There is wise discernment without reactive disturbance.

Somatic: Calm body in presence of harmful others, healthy nervous system, no reactive tension.

Emotional: Emotional equanimity facing wickedness, freedom from being thrown by others’ vice, peace.

Cognitive: Mental peace despite others’ wrong actions, clear discrimination without reactive judgment, serenity.

Psychic: The soul rests undisturbed before wrongdoing, neither condoning nor inflamed; equanimity is the highest of the four, the steadiness of one no longer ruled by reaction.
balanced.vignette: Confronted with someone behaving badly, Sunita does not lose her center. She sees the wrongdoing clearly, responds to it wisely—firmly where firmness is needed—and does not let it disturb her inner equilibrium or curdle into hatred. Her equanimity is not indifference; it is the steadiness from which she can act justly rather than merely react.

excess.label: In excess — detachment that slides into apathy or permissiveness

facets.overview: Excessive Upeksha becomes cold indifference, inability to respond to genuine harm, false detachment that enables evil.
facets.somatic: Physical indifference to harm being done, inability to take protective action.
facets.emotional: Emotional coldness, inability to feel appropriate concern about harm, false detachment.
facets.cognitive: Mental indifference to wrongdoing, inability to discriminate when action needed, dispassion masking avoidance.
facets.psychic: The soul’s stillness slides into cold indifference; detachment hardens into a refusal to care, mistaking apathy for peace.

excess.text:
Excessive Upeksha becomes cold indifference, inability to respond to genuine harm, false detachment that enables evil.

Somatic: Physical indifference to harm being done, inability to take protective action.

Emotional: Emotional coldness, inability to feel appropriate concern about harm, false detachment.

Cognitive: Mental indifference to wrongdoing, inability to discriminate when action needed, dispassion masking avoidance.

Psychic: The soul’s stillness slides into cold indifference; detachment hardens into a refusal to care, mistaking apathy for peace.
excess.vignette: Dan’s ‘equanimity’ has become a shrug at everything. Cruelty, injustice, harm done in front of him—all met with a detached ‘not my business,’ ‘who’s to judge,’ a serenity that has quietly become permissiveness. The non-disturbance that should free him to respond wisely has slid into an apathy that lets real wrong go unchallenged.

deficient.label: Deficient — reactive outrage, agitation, the urge to punish

facets.overview: Absence of Upeksha creates being constantly disturbed by others’ vice, reactive judgment, mental agitation about others’ wrongs.
facets.somatic: Stress and tension from reacting to others’ wrongs, body in chronic agitation.
facets.emotional: Emotional disturbance by others’ vice, reactive judgment, agitation about wrongdoing.
facets.cognitive: Mental agitation about others’ faults, obsessive judgment, mind unable to find peace because of others.
facets.psychic: The soul is seized by reactive outrage; the urge to punish reveals how much the world’s agitation still governs the inner state. Upekṣā is equanimity toward those who act badly — not approval, not condoning, but freedom from being inwardly disturbed by them. It is the most easily misread of the four: it does not mean indifference to wrongdoing or the abandonment of justice, but the refusal to let another’s wickedness churn one’s own mind into agitation, outrage, and the compulsive urge to fix or punish. In balance, one can oppose wrong action clearly and effectively precisely because one is not internally captured by it. In excess, equanimity can decay into a detached apathy or a passivity that fails to act where action is needed. In deficiency, it is absent: the wrongdoing of others seizes the mind, and one is dragged, again and again, into reactive disturbance. Online, Maya reads about someone behaving with real cruelty. Through deficient upekṣā, the outrage takes her over — an afternoon lost to angry scrolling, the mind churning, her own peace handed entirely to a stranger’s bad behavior. Through balanced upekṣā, she sees the wrong clearly, lets it be wrong, takes any genuine action available to her, and declines to let it colonize her mind — keeping the inner clarity from which effective, rather than merely reactive, response becomes possible.

deficient.text:

Somatic: Stress and tension from reacting to others’ wrongs, body in chronic agitation.

Emotional: Emotional disturbance by others’ vice, reactive judgment, agitation about wrongdoing.

Cognitive: Mental agitation about others’ faults, obsessive judgment, mind unable to find peace because of others.

Psychic: The soul is seized by reactive outrage; the urge to punish reveals how much the world’s agitation still governs the inner state. Through balanced upekṣā, she sees the wrong clearly, lets it be wrong, takes any genuine action available to her, and declines to let it colonize her mind — keeping the inner clarity from which effective, rather than merely reactive, response becomes possible.
deficient.vignette: Every wrongdoing detonates in Ravi. The rude driver, the unjust policy, the person behaving badly online—each seizes him with a reactive outrage, an agitation, a burning urge to punish that ruins his afternoon and changes nothing. He cannot witness a wrong without being personally inflamed by it, and the inflammation exhausts him far more than it corrects the world.

attributes.title: Viveka — Discrimination
attributes.aspect: Viveka is the discriminating intelligence that distinguishes the eternal Self from the temporary not-Self — the recognition, as Śaṅkara puts it, that Brahman alone is permanent and the world is mithyā, apparent rather than ultimately real. It is the first qualification because it is the eye of the whole path; without it, one cannot even tell what is worth seeking. It is not contempt for the world but accuracy about its nature.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental–Psychic (discernment of the real from the unreal) · Ontic mode: Virtue / qualification (the first of the fourfold discipline) · Valence: Virtue to cultivate · Guṇa: Sattva-dominant · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: Foundation / prerequisite (the discernment that begins the path) · Key relation: First of the fourfold qualification; kin to Viveka-Khyāti (Dim 11); the ground of Vairāgya.
coordinate.seat: Mental–Psychic (discernment of the real from the unreal)
coordinate.ontic_mode: Virtue / qualification (the first of the fourfold discipline)
coordinate.causal_status: Foundation / prerequisite (the discernment that begins the path)
coordinate.key_relation: First of the fourfold qualification; kin to Viveka-Khyāti (Dim 11); the ground of Vairāgya.

balanced.label: Balanced — clear discernment of the lasting from the fleeting

facets.overview: Active discrimination in every encounter—seeing what is eternal vs. temporary, real vs. apparent. Wisdom-in-action.
facets.somatic: Body’s relationship to phenomena guided by discrimination.
facets.emotional: Emotional discrimination between real love and attachment, real joy and excitement.
facets.cognitive: Mind continuously discriminating eternal from temporal in every situation.
facets.psychic: The soul’s defining instrument is awake — the steady discernment of the eternal from the fleeting that is the doorway to liberation.

balanced.text:
Active discrimination in every encounter—seeing what is eternal vs. temporary, real vs. apparent. Wisdom-in-action.

Somatic: Body’s relationship to phenomena guided by discrimination.

Emotional: Emotional discrimination between real love and attachment, real joy and excitement.

Cognitive: Mind continuously discriminating eternal from temporal in every situation.

Psychic: The soul’s defining instrument is awake — the steady discernment of the eternal from the fleeting that is the doorway to liberation.
balanced.vignette: In a world of shiny distractions, Priya keeps her eye on what lasts. She can tell the fleeting from the enduring—the passing mood from the abiding value, the borrowed opinion from the real conviction—and she orders her life by the difference. Her discernment is not cold; it is a kind of wisdom about what actually deserves her one life.

excess.label: In excess — dry intellectualism, discrimination as mere analysis

facets.overview: Endless analytical discrimination preventing engagement—using viveka to avoid life.
facets.somatic: Body paralyzed by over-discrimination, unable to act.
facets.emotional: Emotional disengagement disguised as discrimination.
facets.cognitive: Analysis paralysis from constant categorizing.
facets.psychic: The soul hides from life behind analysis; discrimination turned into endless mental sorting becomes a way of avoiding the surrender it was meant to enable.

excess.text:
Endless analytical discrimination preventing engagement—using viveka to avoid life.

Somatic: Body paralyzed by over-discrimination, unable to act.

Emotional: Emotional disengagement disguised as discrimination.

Cognitive: Analysis paralysis from constant categorizing.

Psychic: The soul hides from life behind analysis; discrimination turned into endless mental sorting becomes a way of avoiding the surrender it was meant to enable.
excess.vignette: Ravi can discriminate the real from the unreal beautifully—on paper. He dissects every philosophy, parses every distinction, and lives no differently for any of it; the discrimination has stayed in his head, a dry intellectual game that never once reorganized how he actually spends a Tuesday. He knows what matters and is moved by none of it.

deficient.label: Deficient — confusion of the temporary with the permanent

facets.overview: No discrimination—every appearance taken as ultimate, every passing emotion as truth.
facets.somatic: Body driven by every stimulus indiscriminately.
facets.emotional: Emotional life without discrimination.
facets.cognitive: Mind without discriminating power, takes appearances as final.
facets.psychic: The soul sleeps in undifferentiated identification, taking every passing appearance as ultimate; without viveka no path can even begin. In balance, viveka shows as a person who can be fully engaged with the temporary — work, relationships, projects — while never quite forgetting that none of it is the lasting thing; they invest without ultimately depending. In excess, discrimination can dry into mere intellectual analysis, a clever sorting of categories that never touches the heart and can even breed a subtle disdain. In deficiency, the temporary is taken for the permanent: the job, the body, the relationship are treated as though they will last forever and as though they are the very self, so that every change registers as catastrophe. Maya pours herself into a project that then gets cancelled. Without viveka, the loss feels total — a piece of her self, gone. With viveka in balance, she grieves the real loss cleanly while some steady part of her notes: this was always among the passing things; the one who built it remains. The discrimination does not numb the grief; it keeps the grief from becoming an identity-collapse.

deficient.text:
No discrimination—every appearance taken as ultimate, every passing emotion as truth.

Somatic: Body driven by every stimulus indiscriminately.

Emotional: Emotional life without discrimination.

Cognitive: Mind without discriminating power, takes appearances as final.

Psychic: The soul sleeps in undifferentiated identification, taking every passing appearance as ultimate; without viveka no path can even begin. The discrimination does not numb the grief; it keeps the grief from becoming an identity-collapse.
deficient.vignette: Dan builds his life on sand and calls it rock. He pours himself into the temporary—the status, the possessions, the passing pleasures—as though they were permanent, and is genuinely blindsided each time one crumbles. The faculty that would tell the lasting from the fleeting has never engaged, and so he keeps mistaking the one for the other, at great and repeated cost.

attributes.title: Vairāgya — Dispassion
attributes.aspect: Vairāgya is dispassion toward enjoyments — and Śaṅkara extends its reach remarkably far: not only toward gross sensual pleasures but toward subtle ones, even, he says, toward the pleasures of heaven itself. All pleasures, however refined, are seen as fleeting and as binding. Vairāgya is the natural consequence of viveka: once you genuinely see what is temporary, you stop staking your peace on it. It is not the killing of enjoyment but the end of dependence on it.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital–Psychic (dispassion toward the transient) · Ontic mode: Virtue / qualification (the second of the fourfold discipline) · Valence: Virtue to cultivate (near enemy: aversion; deficiency: attachment) · Guṇa: Sattva-dominant · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: Prerequisite (the turning-away that frees the mind) · Key relation: Second of the fourfold qualification; the fruit of Viveka; antagonist to Rāga (Dim 19).
coordinate.ontic_mode: Virtue / qualification (the second of the fourfold discipline)
coordinate.valence: Virtue to cultivate (near enemy: aversion; deficiency: attachment)
coordinate.causal_status: Prerequisite (the turning-away that frees the mind)
coordinate.key_relation: Second of the fourfold qualification; the fruit of Viveka; antagonist to Rāga (Dim 19).

balanced.label: Balanced — freedom from craving without suppression

facets.overview: Natural dispassion arising from understanding the limitation of all pleasures. Free from grasping without bitterness.
facets.somatic: Body engages with what’s necessary without compulsive seeking.
facets.emotional: Emotional contentment without dependence on stimulation.
facets.cognitive: Mind free from compulsive pleasure-pursuit.
facets.psychic: The soul rests in its own fullness and so holds the world’s pleasures lightly; dispassion flowers naturally from having seen their limit.

balanced.text:
Natural dispassion arising from understanding the limitation of all pleasures. Free from grasping without bitterness.

Somatic: Body engages with what’s necessary without compulsive seeking.

Emotional: Emotional contentment without dependence on stimulation.

Cognitive: Mind free from compulsive pleasure-pursuit.

Psychic: The soul rests in its own fullness and so holds the world’s pleasures lightly; dispassion flowers naturally from having seen their limit.
balanced.vignette: Kira wants things—she is human—and she is not ruled by the wanting. She can enjoy what comes and release what goes, holding her desires loosely enough that their absence does not devastate her. Her dispassion is not coldness but freedom: she has stopped needing the world to be a certain way in order to be all right.

excess.label: In excess — cold detachment, life-denial, forced renunciation

facets.overview: Forced dispassion as suppression—aversion to legitimate enjoyments, harsh asceticism.
facets.somatic: Body forced into denial of natural functions.
facets.emotional: Emotional rejection masquerading as dispassion.
facets.cognitive: Mental disgust toward natural enjoyments.
facets.psychic: The soul forces what should ripen; renunciation driven by aversion rather than understanding hardens into life-denial.

excess.text:
Forced dispassion as suppression—aversion to legitimate enjoyments, harsh asceticism.

Somatic: Body forced into denial of natural functions.

Emotional: Emotional rejection masquerading as dispassion.

Cognitive: Mental disgust toward natural enjoyments.

Psychic: The soul forces what should ripen; renunciation driven by aversion rather than understanding hardens into life-denial.
excess.vignette: Karl has renounced desire so thoroughly that he has renounced life.
