Pleasure is suspect, attachment a weakness, warmth a leak—and behind the disciplined detachment is a man gone cold and grey, mistaking the suppression of his heart for its liberation. His ‘dispassion’ is really a quiet war on being alive.

deficient.label: Deficient — helpless attachment, the mind ruled by craving

facets.overview: Total pursuit of pleasures—no recognition of their insufficiency. Maximum samsaric entanglement.
facets.somatic: Body in constant pleasure-pursuit.
facets.emotional: Emotional dependence on continuous stimulation.
facets.cognitive: Mind always planning next pleasure.
facets.psychic: The soul is ruled by craving, mistaking the next pleasure for the home it longs for; here samsara turns its tightest. In balance, vairāgya lets a person enjoy good things fully and let them go cleanly — present to pleasure without being owned by it. In excess, it hardens into cold, life-denying detachment: a forced renunciation that suppresses rather than understands, treating the world with a grim severity that is its own subtle bondage. In deficiency, there is no dispassion at all — the mind is ruled by craving, lurching from one desired object to the next, its peace permanently outsourced to whatever it currently wants. On a hard evening, the old reflex to soothe herself by buying something rises in Maya. With deficient vairāgya she obeys it, and the relief fades within the hour into the next wanting. With excess vairāgya she would shame herself for even feeling the pull, gripping a brittle abstinence. With balanced vairāgya she feels the craving arise, recognizes it as the reaching of a mind that has briefly forgotten it is already alright, and lets it pass — not with force, but with the simple seeing that the thing would not have delivered what she actually wanted anyway.

deficient.text:
Total pursuit of pleasures—no recognition of their insufficiency. Maximum samsaric entanglement.

Somatic: Body in constant pleasure-pursuit.

Emotional: Emotional dependence on continuous stimulation.

Cognitive: Mind always planning next pleasure.

Psychic: The soul is ruled by craving, mistaking the next pleasure for the home it longs for; here samsara turns its tightest. In balance, vairāgya lets a person enjoy good things fully and let them go cleanly — present to pleasure without being owned by it. In excess, it hardens into cold, life-denying detachment: a forced renunciation that suppresses rather than understands, treating the world with a grim severity that is its own subtle bondage. In deficiency, there is no dispassion at all — the mind is ruled by craving, lurching from one desired object to the next, its peace permanently outsourced to whatever it currently wants. On a hard evening, the old reflex to soothe herself by buying something rises in Maya. With deficient vairāgya she obeys it, and the relief fades within the hour into the next wanting. With excess vairāgya she would shame herself for even feeling the pull, gripping a brittle abstinence. With balanced vairāgya she feels the craving arise, recognizes it as the reaching of a mind that has briefly forgotten it is already alright, and lets it pass — not with force, but with the simple seeing that the thing would not have delivered what she actually wanted anyway.
deficient.vignette: Dan is at the mercy of every craving. The desire arises and he obeys—the purchase, the fling, the second helping, the impulse followed the instant it appears—with no space between wanting and grabbing. The mind is simply ruled by its appetites, and he mistakes the obedience for freedom, wondering vaguely why satisfaction never lasts.

attributes.title: Śama — Inner Quietude (Treasure 1)
attributes.aspect: Śama is the inward quietude of the mind — the first of the six treasures. Śaṅkara is careful: it is not the forceful suppression of thought but the natural settling that comes from seeing the limited fulfillment the sense-objects actually offer. When the mind no longer believes the next object will complete it, it stops chasing, and a genuine quiet becomes available for the work of inquiry.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental–Psychic (the quiet mind) · Ontic mode: Virtue / treasure (first of the ṣaṭ-sampat) · Valence: Virtue to cultivate · Guṇa: Sattva-dominant · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: Condition (a settled mind) · Key relation: First of the six treasures; Śama the sthāyibhāva (Dim 8) cultivated as a qualification.
coordinate.key_relation: First of the six treasures; Śama the sthāyibhāva (Dim 8) cultivated as a qualification.

balanced.label: Balanced — a naturally settled, available mind

facets.overview: Natural mental quietude. Mind available for higher engagement because it no longer chases endlessly.
facets.somatic: Body’s nervous system at ease through quiet mind.
facets.emotional: Emotional restfulness from inner stillness.
facets.cognitive: Mind quiet, available, focused at will.
facets.psychic: The soul is reflected in a naturally quiet mind — the lake stilled enough to mirror what is real.

balanced.text:
Natural mental quietude. Mind available for higher engagement because it no longer chases endlessly.

Somatic: Body’s nervous system at ease through quiet mind.

Emotional: Emotional restfulness from inner stillness.

Cognitive: Mind quiet, available, focused at will.

Psychic: The soul is reflected in a naturally quiet mind — the lake stilled enough to mirror what is real.
balanced.vignette: Meera’s mind is like a pond that has settled—naturally calm, available, quiet enough that she can think clearly and rest deeply. It is not that she never has a thought; it is that the mind comes to stillness easily when she asks it to, a settled inner water she can see straight to the bottom of.

excess.label: In excess — forced suppression, a clamped-down stillness

facets.overview: Forced mental suppression creating internal pressure. Quietude masking suppression.
facets.somatic: Body tense from suppression of natural mental movements.
facets.emotional: Emotional flatness as imposed control.
facets.cognitive: Mental violence against thought activity.
facets.psychic: The soul presses the mind into a false stillness; suppression masquerading as peace builds pressure beneath the calm.

excess.text:
Forced mental suppression creating internal pressure. Quietude masking suppression.

Somatic: Body tense from suppression of natural mental movements.

Emotional: Emotional flatness as imposed control.

Cognitive: Mental violence against thought activity.

Psychic: The soul presses the mind into a false stillness; suppression masquerading as peace builds pressure beneath the calm.
excess.vignette: Ravi ‘controls his mind’ by sitting on it. He clamps down on every stray thought, forces a rigid stillness, wrestles the restlessness into a tense silence that is really just suppression wearing calm’s clothing. Beneath the clamped surface the agitation still churns, pressurized and unacknowledged, and the forcing exhausts him more than the restlessness ever did.

deficient.label: Deficient — a restless, scattered, agitated mind

facets.overview: Restless mind constantly chasing sense-objects. No availability for higher engagement.
facets.somatic: Body driven by restless mental activity.
facets.emotional: Emotional life always reaching outward.
facets.cognitive: Mind in chronic agitation.
facets.psychic: The soul cannot find itself in a mind that will not settle; restless chasing leaves no surface still enough for reflection. In balance, śama is a mind that can rest in itself, available and unhurried. In excess, a person mistakes suppression for peace — a clamped-down, white-knuckled stillness that costs constant effort and erupts when the lid slips. In deficiency, the mind is simply restless: scattered, agitated, unable to settle long enough for anything deep to occur.

deficient.text:
Restless mind constantly chasing sense-objects. No availability for higher engagement.

Somatic: Body driven by restless mental activity.

Emotional: Emotional life always reaching outward.

Cognitive: Mind in chronic agitation.

Psychic: The soul cannot find itself in a mind that will not settle; restless chasing leaves no surface still enough for reflection. In balance, śama is a mind that can rest in itself, available and unhurried. In excess, a person mistakes suppression for peace — a clamped-down, white-knuckled stillness that costs constant effort and erupts when the lid slips. In deficiency, the mind is simply restless: scattered, agitated, unable to settle long enough for anything deep to occur.
deficient.vignette: Sara’s mind never stops churning. Thoughts pile on thoughts, worries breed worries, and stillness of any kind is a country she has never visited; even lying down to rest, the inner noise runs on. The scattered, agitated mind gives her no peace and no clarity, and she has half-concluded that this is simply what having a mind is like.

attributes.title: Dama — Outer Restraint (Treasure 2)
attributes.aspect: Dama complements śama: where śama quiets the mind from within, dama restrains the sense organs from without. Śaṅkara describes it not as the destruction of the senses but as withdrawing them from their objects and placing them, properly, at rest. The eye need not chase every sight, the hand need not reach for every pleasant thing; the senses are returned to their proper place rather than left to drag the mind around.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Physical–Vital (mastery of the sense-organs) · Ontic mode: Virtue / treasure (second of the ṣaṭ-sampat) · Valence: Virtue to cultivate · Guṇa: Sattva-dominant · Scale: Individual, with a sensory field · Causal status: Condition (restraint of the senses) · Key relation: Second of the six treasures; the outer counterpart to Śama; kin to Pratyāhāra (Dim 11).
coordinate.key_relation: Second of the six treasures; the outer counterpart to Śama; kin to Pratyāhāra (Dim 11).

balanced.label: Balanced — senses used well and placed at rest

facets.overview: Senses naturally restrained—each in its proper place, none dominating. Voluntary sensory direction.
facets.somatic: Senses functioning healthily under voluntary control.
facets.emotional: Emotional life not whipsawed by sense-stimulation.
facets.cognitive: Mind not constantly pulled by sense-objects.
facets.psychic: The soul governs the senses as a wise ruler governs a kingdom — each in its place, none usurping the throne.

balanced.text:
Senses naturally restrained—each in its proper place, none dominating. Voluntary sensory direction.

Somatic: Senses functioning healthily under voluntary control.

Emotional: Emotional life not whipsawed by sense-stimulation.

Cognitive: Mind not constantly pulled by sense-objects.

Psychic: The soul governs the senses as a wise ruler governs a kingdom — each in its place, none usurping the throne.
balanced.vignette: Kavya uses her senses well and can also set them down. She savors the meal and stops when full, enjoys the screen and closes it, takes in the world’s inputs and grants herself quiet from them when quiet is needed. The senses serve her rather than commanding her; she is the one holding the remote.

excess.label: In excess — harsh sense-denial, mortification

facets.overview: Forced sense-suppression creating sensory deprivation pathology.
facets.somatic: Sense organs atrophied through forced denial.
facets.emotional: Emotional life cut off from sensory richness.
facets.cognitive: Mental rejection of sensory experience entirely.
facets.psychic: The soul wages war on its own instrument; harsh sense-denial becomes its own affliction, mortification mistaken for mastery.

excess.text:
Forced sense-suppression creating sensory deprivation pathology.

Somatic: Sense organs atrophied through forced denial.

Emotional: Emotional life cut off from sensory richness.

Cognitive: Mental rejection of sensory experience entirely.

Psychic: The soul wages war on its own instrument; harsh sense-denial becomes its own affliction, mortification mistaken for mastery.
excess.vignette: Michael has declared war on his own senses. Pleasure of any kind is mortified, comfort denied, the body’s ordinary enjoyments treated as enemies to be starved—and the harsh sense-denial has left him not free but brittle, at war with the very instrument through which he must live. He mistakes the mortification for mastery.

deficient.label: Deficient — senses running the person, no restraint

facets.overview: Senses completely uncontrolled—each runs its own way. Total sensory bondage.
facets.somatic: Body’s senses each chasing their own objects.
facets.emotional: Emotional life as sense-driven chaos.
facets.cognitive: Mind dragged in all directions by senses.
facets.psychic: The soul is dethroned by its own senses; pulled in every direction, it cannot gather itself toward anything higher. In balance, dama is the easy ability to not look, not reach, not consume — to let an impulse pass without acting on it. In excess, it becomes harsh mortification, a punishing war on the senses. In deficiency, the senses run the person: every notification answered, every craving obeyed, the attention permanently captured by whatever is brightest.

deficient.text:
Senses completely uncontrolled—each runs its own way. Total sensory bondage.

Somatic: Body’s senses each chasing their own objects.

Emotional: Emotional life as sense-driven chaos.

Cognitive: Mind dragged in all directions by senses.

Psychic: The soul is dethroned by its own senses; pulled in every direction, it cannot gather itself toward anything higher. In balance, dama is the easy ability to not look, not reach, not consume — to let an impulse pass without acting on it. In excess, it becomes harsh mortification, a punishing war on the senses. In deficiency, the senses run the person: every notification answered, every craving obeyed, the attention permanently captured by whatever is brightest.
deficient.vignette: Dan’s senses run the show. Whatever they crave, he provides—the next taste, the next sight, the next stimulation—helplessly, all day, with no capacity to say ‘enough’ or ‘not now.’ There is no restraint anywhere in him, and so he is dragged from one sensory pull to the next, exhausted and never quite satisfied.

attributes.title: Uparati — Inner Independence (Treasure 3)
attributes.aspect: Uparati is cessation — the inner withdrawal from leaning on external objects, even religious practices, for one’s support. Śaṅkara calls it the spirit of sannyāsa, renunciation, carried inwardly: the mind no longer props itself on outer supports because it has begun to find its ground within. It is a maturing of the path, where forms are used but no longer depended upon.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital–Psychic (independence from external supports) · Ontic mode: Virtue / treasure (third of the ṣaṭ-sampat) · Valence: Virtue to cultivate · Guṇa: Sattva-dominant · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: Condition (self-withdrawal from distraction) · Key relation: Third of the six treasures; the settled cessation that follows Śama and Dama.
coordinate.key_relation: Third of the six treasures; the settled cessation that follows Śama and Dama.

balanced.label: Balanced — not leaning on externals for inner support

facets.overview: Inner non-dependence on external supports. Practices done from fullness, not need.
facets.somatic: Body engages externally without dependence.
facets.emotional: Emotional self-sufficiency without isolation.
facets.cognitive: Mind not propped up by external structures.
facets.psychic: The soul stands on its own ground, drawing support from within rather than leaning on outer props; practice flows from fullness, not need.

balanced.text:
Inner non-dependence on external supports. Practices done from fullness, not need.

Somatic: Body engages externally without dependence.

Emotional: Emotional self-sufficiency without isolation.

Cognitive: Mind not propped up by external structures.

Psychic: The soul stands on its own ground, drawing support from within rather than leaning on outer props; practice flows from fullness, not need.
balanced.vignette: Sunita does not need the props. She has her routines and structures and can also stand without them—her inner steadiness does not collapse when the schedule breaks, the retreat ends, or the familiar supports fall away. She leans on what helps without being unable to stand when it is gone; the ground she rests on is, increasingly, inside her.

excess.label: In excess — spiritual bypass, abandoning needed structure too soon

facets.overview: Premature renunciation of all external supports including needed ones. Spiritual isolation.
facets.somatic: Body rejecting necessary external supports.
facets.emotional: Emotional withdrawal from healthy connections.
facets.cognitive: Mental rejection of all structure.
facets.psychic: The soul kicks away the ladder before it has climbed; abandoning needed structure too soon is spiritual bypass dressed as freedom.

excess.text:
Premature renunciation of all external supports including needed ones. Spiritual isolation.

Somatic: Body rejecting necessary external supports.

Emotional: Emotional withdrawal from healthy connections.

Cognitive: Mental rejection of all structure.

Psychic: The soul kicks away the ladder before it has climbed; abandoning needed structure too soon is spiritual bypass dressed as freedom.
excess.vignette: Ben read that a real practitioner needs no external structure, and so he threw his out—the schedule, the community, the disciplined routine—long before he had the inner steadiness to replace them. The ‘spiritual bypass’ has left him unsupported and drifting, mistaking the abandonment of needed scaffolding for the freedom that only comes after it has done its work.

deficient.label: Deficient — total dependence on external props and routines

facets.overview: Total dependence on external supports for inner state. No inner foundation.
facets.somatic: Body constantly needing external props.
facets.emotional: Emotional life entirely dependent on circumstances.
facets.cognitive: Mind has no inner standing without external supports.
facets.psychic: The soul has no inner foundation, borrowing its every state from external supports and collapsing when they are removed. In balance, uparati lets a person use structures — routines, communities, practices — without being psychologically dependent on them; if they fall away, the inner ground holds. In excess, it can become a premature ‘I don’t need any of that,’ a spiritual bypass that abandons supportive structure before the inner ground is actually established. In deficiency, the person is wholly propped on externals — unable to feel settled without the right setting, the right routine, the right group — and is destabilized whenever any of it shifts.

deficient.text:
Total dependence on external supports for inner state. No inner foundation.

Somatic: Body constantly needing external props.

Emotional: Emotional life entirely dependent on circumstances.

Cognitive: Mind has no inner standing without external supports.

Psychic: The soul has no inner foundation, borrowing its every state from external supports and collapsing when they are removed. In balance, uparati lets a person use structures — routines, communities, practices — without being psychologically dependent on them; if they fall away, the inner ground holds. In excess, it can become a premature ‘I don’t need any of that,’ a spiritual bypass that abandons supportive structure before the inner ground is actually established. In deficiency, the person is wholly propped on externals — unable to feel settled without the right setting, the right routine, the right group — and is destabilized whenever any of it shifts.
deficient.vignette: Dan cannot function without his props. The exact routine, the specific setting, the familiar supports—remove any of them and he falls apart, his steadiness entirely borrowed from external structure. Nothing holds him up from within; he is a building with no frame, standing only as long as the scaffolding stays exactly in place.

attributes.title: Titikṣā — Endurance (Treasure 4)
attributes.aspect: Titikṣā is the patient endurance of the dvandvas, the pairs of opposites — heat and cold, pleasure and pain, success and failure, honor and dishonor — borne, Śaṅkara says, without complaint and without being inwardly disturbed. It is not masochism; it is the steadiness that comes from no longer needing conditions to be a certain way in order to be at peace. The opposites still come; one simply stops being thrown by them.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital–Physical (endurance of the pairs of opposites) · Ontic mode: Virtue / treasure (fourth of the ṣaṭ-sampat) · Valence: Virtue to cultivate · Guṇa: Sattva–Tamas (steady bearing) · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: Condition (bears heat and cold, pleasure and pain, undisturbed) · Key relation: Fourth of the six treasures; kin to Kṣamā (Dim 10).
coordinate.seat: Vital–Physical (endurance of the pairs of opposites)
coordinate.causal_status: Condition (bears heat and cold, pleasure and pain, undisturbed)
coordinate.key_relation: Fourth of the six treasures; kin to Kṣamā (Dim 10).

balanced.label: Balanced — calm endurance of life’s opposites

facets.overview: Natural endurance of life’s dualities without complaint. Energy conserved for the goal.
facets.somatic: Body resilient to extremes without seeking constant remediation.
facets.emotional: Emotional steadiness through life’s ups and downs.
facets.cognitive: Mind unbothered by passing dualities.
facets.psychic: The soul endures the play of opposites without complaint, conserving its energy for what matters; the dualities lose their power to disturb.

balanced.text:
Natural endurance of life’s dualities without complaint. Energy conserved for the goal.

Somatic: Body resilient to extremes without seeking constant remediation.

Emotional: Emotional steadiness through life’s ups and downs.

Cognitive: Mind unbothered by passing dualities.

Psychic: The soul endures the play of opposites without complaint, conserving its energy for what matters; the dualities lose their power to disturb.
balanced.vignette: When life brings its opposites—heat and cold, praise and blame, comfort and hardship—Meera endures them with a calm that neither denies the difficulty nor is undone by it. She can sit with discomfort, wait out a hard season, bear what must be borne, without drama and without collapse. Her forbearance is quiet strength, not grim endurance.

excess.label: In excess — grim, masochistic over-endurance

facets.overview: Forced endurance becoming masochism. Refusing necessary care, glorifying suffering.
facets.somatic: Body harmed by refusing legitimate care.
facets.emotional: Emotional suppression as spiritual identity.
facets.cognitive: Mental pride in endurance becoming attachment.
facets.psychic: The soul glorifies its own suffering; endurance turned masochistic refuses the care it legitimately needs.

excess.text:
Forced endurance becoming masochism. Refusing necessary care, glorifying suffering.

Somatic: Body harmed by refusing legitimate care.

Emotional: Emotional suppression as spiritual identity.

Cognitive: Mental pride in endurance becoming attachment.

Psychic: The soul glorifies its own suffering; endurance turned masochistic refuses the care it legitimately needs.
excess.vignette: Ravi turns endurance into a competition with himself. He seeks out hardship to prove he can take it, refuses comfort he actually needs, wears his suffering as a badge—a grim, masochistic over-endurance that mistakes self-punishment for strength. He can bear anything, and in the bearing has forgotten that not everything needs to be borne.

deficient.label: Deficient — reactivity to every discomfort

facets.overview: Complaint at every discomfort. Constant remedy-seeking. No inner stability through dualities.
facets.somatic: Body’s every sensation triggers reaction.
facets.emotional: Emotional turbulence with every change.
facets.cognitive: Mind constantly preoccupied with discomforts.
facets.psychic: The soul is tossed by every discomfort, seeking remedy at each small pain, never finding the steadiness that lies just past reactivity. In balance, titikṣā is the quiet capacity to sit with discomfort — cold, hunger, criticism, delay — without it becoming an emergency, and without making a show of the bearing. In excess, it tips into grim over-endurance, a masochistic pride in suffering that refuses help and punishes the body. In deficiency, every discomfort is an outrage — the slightly-too-warm room, the minor criticism, the small delay each capable of derailing the whole mood.

deficient.text:
Complaint at every discomfort. Constant remedy-seeking. No inner stability through dualities.

Somatic: Body’s every sensation triggers reaction.

Emotional: Emotional turbulence with every change.

Cognitive: Mind constantly preoccupied with discomforts.

Psychic: The soul is tossed by every discomfort, seeking remedy at each small pain, never finding the steadiness that lies just past reactivity. In balance, titikṣā is the quiet capacity to sit with discomfort — cold, hunger, criticism, delay — without it becoming an emergency, and without making a show of the bearing. In excess, it tips into grim over-endurance, a masochistic pride in suffering that refuses help and punishes the body. In deficiency, every discomfort is an outrage — the slightly-too-warm room, the minor criticism, the small delay each capable of derailing the whole mood.
deficient.vignette: The smallest discomfort undoes Dan. The room too warm, the wait too long, the mild criticism, the minor inconvenience—each provokes an outsized reaction, a complaint, a need to escape it immediately. He has no capacity to simply sit with the unpleasant, and so life’s ordinary frictions become, for him, a series of small emergencies.

attributes.title: Śraddhā — Functional Faith (Treasure 6)
attributes.aspect: Śraddhā is faith in the scripture and the teacher as means of knowing what cannot be known otherwise. Śaṅkara is precise: it is not blind belief but a functional trust — the working confidence that lets a seeker act on a teaching before they have personally verified it, since without that provisional trust no one would ever begin. It is the ascertainment that the teaching is a valid means of knowledge, held firmly enough to practice on.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital–Psychic (trust in teaching and Self) · Ontic mode: Virtue / treasure (treasure 6 of the ṣaṭ-sampat) · Valence: Virtue to cultivate · Guṇa: Sattva-dominant · Scale: Individual, toward teacher and scripture · Causal status: Condition (the faith that sustains practice) · Key relation: One of the six treasures; antidote to Saṁśaya (doubt, Dim 21).
coordinate.key_relation: One of the six treasures; antidote to Saṁśaya (doubt, Dim 21).

balanced.label: Balanced — trust that lets one act before proof

facets.overview: Faith as functional trust enabling reception. Receptive without naivete; trusting after discernment.
facets.somatic: Body in receptive posture before teaching.
facets.emotional: Emotional warmth combined with discrimination.
facets.cognitive: Mind trusting after appropriate discrimination.
facets.psychic: The soul trusts enough to act before proof; faith becomes the receptive opening through which grace and teaching can enter.

balanced.text:
Faith as functional trust enabling reception. Receptive without naivete; trusting after discernment.

Somatic: Body in receptive posture before teaching.

Emotional: Emotional warmth combined with discrimination.

Cognitive: Mind trusting after appropriate discrimination.

Psychic: The soul trusts enough to act before proof; faith becomes the receptive opening through which grace and teaching can enter.
balanced.vignette: Kira can act before she has proof. When the path ahead is right but unverifiable—the leap of a new venture, the trust of a new relationship, the commitment to a practice whose fruits lie years off—she moves anyway, on a faith that is not credulity but a reasoned trust. The willingness to begin before certainty is, she has found, the only way anything real ever starts.

excess.label: In excess — blind credulity, surrender of discernment

facets.overview: Blind faith without discernment. Cult-like submission to false teachers.
facets.somatic: Body in slavish submission.
facets.emotional: Emotional dependency on guru.
facets.cognitive: Mental abdication to authority.
facets.psychic: The soul surrenders its discernment; faith without viveka becomes credulity, the open door through which false teachers walk.

excess.text:
Blind faith without discernment. Cult-like submission to false teachers.

Somatic: Body in slavish submission.

Emotional: Emotional dependency on guru.

Cognitive: Mental abdication to authority.

Psychic: The soul surrenders its discernment; faith without viveka becomes credulity, the open door through which false teachers walk.
excess.vignette: Sam believes whatever he is told. The teacher, the doctrine, the confident claim—each is swallowed whole, discernment surrendered at the door, faith curdled into blind credulity. He calls it trust; it is really the abdication of his own judgment, and it has made him a follower of whoever speaks with enough conviction, however wrong.

deficient.label: Deficient — corrosive skepticism that cannot begin

facets.overview: Skeptical doubt of all teachers and scriptures. No reception possible.
facets.somatic: Body closed to teaching.
facets.emotional: Emotional defensiveness.
facets.cognitive: Mind always finding fault, no learning.
facets.psychic: The soul cannot receive, holding everything at the arm’s length of doubt; corrosive skepticism leaves the path forever untried. In balance, śraddhā lets a person commit to a practice and a teaching long enough to test it in their own experience — the trust that makes the experiment possible. In excess, it degrades into blind credulity: the surrender of all discernment, the swallowing whole of whatever a teacher says. In deficiency, corrosive skepticism prevents any beginning at all — every teaching held at arm’s length, every practice abandoned before it could bear fruit, the mind too busy doubting to ever actually try.

deficient.text:
Skeptical doubt of all teachers and scriptures. No reception possible.

Somatic: Body closed to teaching.

Emotional: Emotional defensiveness.

Cognitive: Mind always finding fault, no learning.

Psychic: The soul cannot receive, holding everything at the arm’s length of doubt; corrosive skepticism leaves the path forever untried. In balance, śraddhā lets a person commit to a practice and a teaching long enough to test it in their own experience — the trust that makes the experiment possible. In excess, it degrades into blind credulity: the surrender of all discernment, the swallowing whole of whatever a teacher says. In deficiency, corrosive skepticism prevents any beginning at all — every teaching held at arm’s length, every practice abandoned before it could bear fruit, the mind too busy doubting to ever actually try.
deficient.vignette: Dan cannot begin anything he cannot first prove will work. Every path is met with a corrosive skepticism, every commitment deferred until certainty arrives—which it never does—and so he stands forever at the trailhead, arms crossed, having verified nothing because he would risk nothing. The doubt that should protect him has become a wall against every beginning.

attributes.title: Samādhāna — Sustained Steadiness (Treasure 5)
attributes.aspect: Samādhāna is mental equipoise — the developed capacity to sustain attention, without scattering, on the object of inquiry. Śaṅkara describes it as the non-wavering of the mind and its ability to stay focused on Brahman. It is the fruit of the earlier treasures: a mind that has been quieted (śama), whose senses are restrained (dama), and that no longer leans on externals (uparati) becomes capable of the deep, sustained, one-pointed attention that real inquiry demands.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental–Psychic (sustained one-pointed steadiness) · Ontic mode: Virtue / treasure (treasure 5 of the ṣaṭ-sampat) · Valence: Virtue to cultivate · Guṇa: Sattva-dominant · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: Condition (holds the mind steady on its aim) · Key relation: One of the six treasures; kin to Dhāraṇā and Samādhi (Dim 11); the culmination of the treasures.
coordinate.causal_status: Condition (holds the mind steady on its aim)
coordinate.key_relation: One of the six treasures; kin to Dhāraṇā and Samādhi (Dim 11); the culmination of the treasures.

balanced.label: Balanced — one-pointed attention available at will

facets.overview: Sustained one-pointed concentration on the goal. Mind doesn’t scatter despite stimulations.
facets.somatic: Body steady to support sustained concentration.
facets.emotional: Emotional steadiness allowing sustained focus.
facets.cognitive: Mind focused, undistracted, equipoised.
facets.psychic: The soul gathers itself to a single point; one-pointed steadiness becomes the lens that focuses all the other qualifications toward their goal.

balanced.text:
Sustained one-pointed concentration on the goal. Mind doesn’t scatter despite stimulations.

Somatic: Body steady to support sustained concentration.

Emotional: Emotional steadiness allowing sustained focus.

Cognitive: Mind focused, undistracted, equipoised.

Psychic: The soul gathers itself to a single point; one-pointed steadiness becomes the lens that focuses all the other qualifications toward their goal.
balanced.vignette: When Priya needs to concentrate, she can—gathering her attention onto one thing and holding it there, at will, for as long as the task requires. The one-pointedness is available and flexible; she can focus deeply and then release, the mind a well-trained instrument that comes when called and rests when dismissed.

excess.label: In excess — rigid fixation, brittle concentration

facets.overview: Forced concentration creating headaches. Tension confused with focus.
facets.somatic: Body strained from forced concentration.
facets.emotional: Emotional anxiety about maintaining focus.
facets.cognitive: Mental tension that itself prevents focus.
facets.psychic: The soul cramps around its own effort; forced fixation mistakes tension for focus and exhausts the attention it strains to hold.

excess.text:
Forced concentration creating headaches. Tension confused with focus.

Somatic: Body strained from forced concentration.

Emotional: Emotional anxiety about maintaining focus.

Cognitive: Mental tension that itself prevents focus.

Psychic: The soul cramps around its own effort; forced fixation mistakes tension for focus and exhausts the attention it strains to hold.
excess.vignette: Ravi concentrates like a clenched fist. His focus is real but brittle—rigidly fixed, effortful, shattering at the first interruption and leaving him rattled. He has mistaken tension for concentration, and the white-knuckle grip he keeps on his attention makes it fragile precisely where it should be supple.

deficient.label: Deficient — a scattered mind that cannot hold focus

facets.overview: Scattered mind—unable to focus on anything for sustained time.
facets.somatic: Body restless, unable to support attention.
facets.emotional: Emotional dispersal preventing focus.
facets.cognitive: Mind constantly jumping, no equipoise.
facets.psychic: The soul is scattered across a hundred objects, unable to rest its attention long enough for anything deep to form. In balance, samādhāna is the ability to gather and hold attention at will — to stay with a question, a practice, or a presence long enough for it to open. In excess, concentration can become a rigid, effortful fixation that strains and snaps. In deficiency, the mind cannot hold anything — it scatters at the first pull, so that even sincere inquiry never goes below the surface before the attention is gone.

deficient.text:
Scattered mind—unable to focus on anything for sustained time.

Somatic: Body restless, unable to support attention.

Emotional: Emotional dispersal preventing focus.

Cognitive: Mind constantly jumping, no equipoise.

Psychic: The soul is scattered across a hundred objects, unable to rest its attention long enough for anything deep to form. In balance, samādhāna is the ability to gather and hold attention at will — to stay with a question, a practice, or a presence long enough for it to open. In excess, concentration can become a rigid, effortful fixation that strains and snaps. In deficiency, the mind cannot hold anything — it scatters at the first pull, so that even sincere inquiry never goes below the surface before the attention is gone.
deficient.vignette: Sara’s attention is confetti. She cannot hold a single focus for more than a moment before it scatters—the mind leaping to the next thing, and the next, so that deep or sustained work of any kind slips through her fingers. The gathered, one-pointed attention that real accomplishment requires is, for now, entirely beyond her reach.

attributes.title: Mumukṣutva — The Longing for Liberation
attributes.aspect: Mumukṣutva is the fourth and crowning qualification — the burning longing for liberation. Śaṅkara makes its importance unmistakable: without it, even all the other qualifications together produce only a very accomplished scholar; with it, they become a living path. It arises, he notes, from a sense of one’s own inadequacy and insecurity in the face of one’s true nature — a longing born not of greed but of a deep recognition that nothing temporary will ever suffice.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Psychic (the burning desire for freedom) · Ontic mode: Virtue / qualification (the fourth of the fourfold discipline) · Valence: Virtue to cultivate — the one desire to intensify · Guṇa: Sattva-dominant (aspiration) · Scale: Individual, toward the transcendent · Causal status: Prerequisite / driving fire (the longing that powers the whole path) · Key relation: Fourth of the fourfold qualification — the ‘one fire’; Autsukya (longing, Dim 9) refined toward liberation.
coordinate.ontic_mode: Virtue / qualification (the fourth of the fourfold discipline)
coordinate.valence: Virtue to cultivate — the one desire to intensify
coordinate.causal_status: Prerequisite / driving fire (the longing that powers the whole path)
coordinate.key_relation: Fourth of the fourfold qualification — the ‘one fire’; Autsukya (longing, Dim 9) refined toward liberation.

balanced.label: Balanced — a steady, fueling fire for freedom

facets.overview: Sustained burning longing for liberation. Intense but not anxious; passionate but not grasping.
facets.somatic: Body energized by spiritual longing without strain.
facets.emotional: Emotional intensity directed toward freedom.
facets.cognitive: Mind focused by sustained aspiration.
facets.psychic: The soul’s deepest longing has awakened — the burning desire for freedom that gives all the other qualifications their fire and direction.

balanced.text:
Sustained burning longing for liberation. Intense but not anxious; passionate but not grasping.

Somatic: Body energized by spiritual longing without strain.

Emotional: Emotional intensity directed toward freedom.

Cognitive: Mind focused by sustained aspiration.

Psychic: The soul’s deepest longing has awakened — the burning desire for freedom that gives all the other qualifications their fire and direction.
balanced.vignette: Beneath everything Hari does runs a steady fire—a genuine, fueling longing for freedom, for truth, for the real thing beneath the surface of his life. It is not frantic; it is a deep, warm intensity that gets him to the cushion each morning and keeps him honest through the plateaus. The desire for liberation is the engine, and it burns clean.

excess.label: In excess — spiritual greed, grasping for enlightenment

facets.overview: Anxious craving for realization creating obstacle. Spiritual grasping.
facets.somatic: Body in strain from forced aspiration.
facets.emotional: Emotional anxiety about progress.
facets.cognitive: Mental impatience preventing deep engagement.
facets.psychic: The soul’s longing curdles into anxious grasping; the very intensity that should carry it becomes a spiritual impatience that blocks the door.

excess.text:
Anxious craving for realization creating obstacle. Spiritual grasping.

Somatic: Body in strain from forced aspiration.

Emotional: Emotional anxiety about progress.

Cognitive: Mental impatience preventing deep engagement.

Psychic: The soul’s longing curdles into anxious grasping; the very intensity that should carry it becomes a spiritual impatience that blocks the door.
excess.vignette: Ben wants enlightenment the way others want money—grasping, comparing, impatient, treating liberation as one more prize to acquire and display. The very intensity of the wanting has become the obstacle; his spiritual greed grabs at the fruit and tramples the tree, and the freedom he chases so hard recedes exactly because he is chasing it.

deficient.label: Deficient — lukewarm interest that never moves

facets.overview: Casual interest without burning longing. Hobby-spirituality producing no transformation.
facets.somatic: Body without spiritual energy.
facets.emotional: Emotional dabbling without commitment.
facets.cognitive: Mind treats spirituality as one interest among many.
facets.psychic: The soul merely dabbles, curious but not yet aflame; without real longing, no amount of technique produces transformation. In balance, mumukṣutva is the quiet, steady fire that keeps a person returning to the path through every plateau and discouragement — a longing strong enough to organize a life around. In excess, it can curdle into spiritual greed: a grasping, impatient demand for enlightenment that treats liberation as one more acquisition and strains for it. In deficiency, it is merely lukewarm — a pleasant interest in spiritual ideas that never costs anything, never moves the feet, and so never arrives. Maya reads the same teaching she has read a hundred times — ‘you are not the body or the mind’ — and on most days it is just words. But on a particular evening, after a particular disappointment, something in her actually wants it to be true with her whole being, wants to know it rather than merely think it. That wanting is mumukṣutva, and it changes everything about how she sits down the next morning. The teaching has not changed; the fire that meets it has.

deficient.text:
Casual interest without burning longing. Hobby-spirituality producing no transformation.

Somatic: Body without spiritual energy.

Emotional: Emotional dabbling without commitment.

Cognitive: Mind treats spirituality as one interest among many.

Psychic: The soul merely dabbles, curious but not yet aflame; without real longing, no amount of technique produces transformation. In balance, mumukṣutva is the quiet, steady fire that keeps a person returning to the path through every plateau and discouragement — a longing strong enough to organize a life around. In excess, it can curdle into spiritual greed: a grasping, impatient demand for enlightenment that treats liberation as one more acquisition and strains for it. In deficiency, it is merely lukewarm — a pleasant interest in spiritual ideas that never costs anything, never moves the feet, and so never arrives. Maya reads the same teaching she has read a hundred times — ‘you are not the body or the mind’ — and on most days it is just words. But on a particular evening, after a particular disappointment, something in her actually wants it to be true with her whole being, wants to know it rather than merely think it. That wanting is mumukṣutva, and it changes everything about how she sits down the next morning. The teaching has not changed; the fire that meets it has.
deficient.vignette: Dan is mildly interested in truth, in the way one is mildly interested in a documentary. There is no fire, no urgency, no real longing to pull him past the comfortable surface of his life—just a lukewarm curiosity that never once moves him to actually practice, or change, or seek. Without the fuel of genuine desire, he stays exactly where he is, indefinitely.

attributes.title: Abhayam — Fearlessness
attributes.aspect: Abhayam is the absence of fear—not the absence of awareness of danger, but the absence of psychological fear that paralyzes action. It is the first divine quality listed in Chapter 16 and considered foundational for spiritual growth.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital–Psychic · Ontic mode: Virtue — a daivī quality (fearlessness) · Valence: Virtue to cultivate · Guṇa: Sattva-dominant · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: Foundation (the first divine quality, ground of the rest) · Key relation: First of the daivī sampat; antagonist to Bhaya (Dim 8); rooted in Self-knowledge.
coordinate.causal_status: Foundation (the first divine quality, ground of the rest)
coordinate.key_relation: First of the daivī sampat; antagonist to Bhaya (Dim 8); rooted in Self-knowledge.

balanced.label: Balanced — courage rooted in awareness

facets.overview: When Abhayam is present, a person acts wisely with awareness of risks but without paralyzing fear. There is courage rooted in awareness of true Self that nothing can ultimately harm.
facets.somatic: Steady physical presence in challenging situations, calm nervous system under threat, healthy adrenal function, body that responds appropriately to actual danger without chronic fear response, courageous physical bearing.
facets.emotional: Emotional courage and security, freedom from chronic anxiety, ability to feel fear momentarily without being overwhelmed, emotional grounding in deeper Self, courageous engagement with life.
facets.cognitive: Mental clarity in face of challenges, ability to think clearly under pressure, freedom from anxious thinking, courageous decision-making, wisdom about what truly threatens versus apparent threats.
facets.psychic: The soul rests in what cannot be harmed; fearlessness is the natural fragrance of a self that has begun to know its own indestructibility.

balanced.text:
When Abhayam is present, a person acts wisely with awareness of risks but without paralyzing fear. There is courage rooted in awareness of true Self that nothing can ultimately harm.

Somatic: Steady physical presence in challenging situations, calm nervous system under threat, healthy adrenal function, body that responds appropriately to actual danger without chronic fear response, courageous physical bearing.

Emotional: Emotional courage and security, freedom from chronic anxiety, ability to feel fear momentarily without being overwhelmed, emotional grounding in deeper Self, courageous engagement with life.

Cognitive: Mental clarity in face of challenges, ability to think clearly under pressure, freedom from anxious thinking, courageous decision-making, wisdom about what truly threatens versus apparent threats.

Psychic: The soul rests in what cannot be harmed; fearlessness is the natural fragrance of a self that has begun to know its own indestructibility.
balanced.vignette: Standing to give the hardest presentation of her career, Priya feels the fear and speaks anyway—clear-eyed about the real risks, and unparalyzed by them. Her courage is not the absence of fear but the willingness to act in its presence, grounded in a steady awareness of what actually matters. She is afraid, and she is free.

excess.label: In excess — reckless bravado, denial of real risk

facets.overview: Excessive ‘fearlessness’ without wisdom becomes reckless bravado, denial of legitimate threats, and inability to protect oneself or others appropriately.
facets.somatic: Reckless physical behavior, ignoring legitimate physical dangers, unnecessary physical harm, dismissing physical signals of threat.
facets.emotional: False bravado masking actual fear, emotional dismissal of legitimate concerns, inability to acknowledge vulnerability, emotional denial.
facets.cognitive: Mental dismissal of real risks, poor judgment about danger, inability to think strategically about threats, recklessness disguised as courage.
facets.psychic: Fearlessness hardens into recklessness; the soul mistakes the suppression of healthy caution for true courage.

excess.text:
Excessive ‘fearlessness’ without wisdom becomes reckless bravado, denial of legitimate threats, and inability to protect oneself or others appropriately.

Somatic: Reckless physical behavior, ignoring legitimate physical dangers, unnecessary physical harm, dismissing physical signals of threat.

Emotional: False bravado masking actual fear, emotional dismissal of legitimate concerns, inability to acknowledge vulnerability, emotional denial.

Cognitive: Mental dismissal of real risks, poor judgment about danger, inability to think strategically about threats, recklessness disguised as courage.

Psychic: Fearlessness hardens into recklessness; the soul mistakes the suppression of healthy caution for true courage.
excess.vignette: Ravi calls it fearlessness; his family calls it a death wish. He denies real dangers, dismisses sensible caution as cowardice, and takes needless risks to prove something to himself—the reckless bravado of a man who has confused ignoring fear with mastering it. The heedlessness will, one day, collect its bill.

deficient.label: Deficient — chronic fear, anxiety, paralysis

facets.overview: Absence of Abhayam manifests as chronic fear, anxiety, paralysis in face of challenges, and inability to live fully due to constant apprehension.
facets.somatic: Chronic stress response and adrenal exhaustion, tension and tightness in body, freeze responses to ordinary situations, anxiety-related physical symptoms (panic, palpitations).
facets.emotional: Chronic anxiety and fearfulness, emotional paralysis, inability to face challenges, constant apprehension, emotional cowardice, avoidance of life.
facets.cognitive: Anxious and fearful thinking patterns, mental paralysis when facing decisions, catastrophizing, inability to think clearly under pressure, mental contraction from fear.
facets.psychic: The soul lives walled in by dread; chronic fear is the precise sign of identification with the perishable. Fearlessness in balance is not the absence of fear but the refusal to be ruled by it — the capacity to act rightly while fully aware of the risk, grounded in a sense of what cannot finally be lost. In excess it becomes bravado that denies legitimate danger and endangers self and others. In deficiency it collapses into the chronic apprehension that shrinks a life, where the fear of loss governs every choice. Maya must say something unpopular but true in a meeting. Deficient abhayam keeps her silent, the fear of disapproval winning. Excess would have her say it carelessly, bulldozing the room. In balance, she feels the fear fully — the racing heart, the dry mouth — and speaks anyway, plainly, because something steadier in her knows that her worth does not actually hang on the room’s approval.

deficient.text:
Absence of Abhayam manifests as chronic fear, anxiety, paralysis in face of challenges, and inability to live fully due to constant apprehension.

Somatic: Chronic stress response and adrenal exhaustion, tension and tightness in body, freeze responses to ordinary situations, anxiety-related physical symptoms (panic, palpitations).

Emotional: Chronic anxiety and fearfulness, emotional paralysis, inability to face challenges, constant apprehension, emotional cowardice, avoidance of life.

Cognitive: Anxious and fearful thinking patterns, mental paralysis when facing decisions, catastrophizing, inability to think clearly under pressure, mental contraction from fear.

Psychic: The soul lives walled in by dread; chronic fear is the precise sign of identification with the perishable. Fearlessness in balance is not the absence of fear but the refusal to be ruled by it — the capacity to act rightly while fully aware of the risk, grounded in a sense of what cannot finally be lost. In excess it becomes bravado that denies legitimate danger and endangers self and others. In deficiency it collapses into the chronic apprehension that shrinks a life, where the fear of loss governs every choice. Maya must say something unpopular but true in a meeting. Deficient abhayam keeps her silent, the fear of disapproval winning. Excess would have her say it carelessly, bulldozing the room. In balance, she feels the fear fully — the racing heart, the dry mouth — and speaks anyway, plainly, because something steadier in her knows that her worth does not actually hang on the room’s approval.
deficient.vignette: A low dread runs under everything Dan does. He avoids the risk, declines the opportunity, stays small and safe, paralyzed by a chronic anxiety that treats every uncertainty as a threat. The fear is not about anything in particular; it is the water he swims in, and it has quietly shrunk his life to the size of his comfort zone.

attributes.title: Akrodha — Freedom from Anger
attributes.aspect: Akrodha is the absence of destructive anger. It does not mean the absence of all righteous indignation, but freedom from the kind of anger that distorts perception, harms others, and degrades consciousness. It is one of the divine qualities.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital · Ontic mode: Virtue — a daivī quality (freedom from anger) · Valence: Virtue to cultivate · Guṇa: Sattva-dominant · Scale: Interpersonal (Ādhibhautika) · Causal status: Condition (non-reactivity to provocation) · Key relation: A daivī quality; the direct antagonist to Krodha (its āsurī counterpart, 4.2).
coordinate.ontic_mode: Virtue — a daivī quality (freedom from anger)
coordinate.key_relation: A daivī quality; the direct antagonist to Krodha (its āsurī counterpart, 4.2).

balanced.label: Balanced — calm that can still register injustice

facets.overview: When Akrodha is present, a person can recognize injustice and respond firmly without being consumed by destructive anger. There is the capacity for righteous response without the bondage of rage.
facets.somatic: Relaxed physical state, healthy nervous system, absence of tension from suppressed rage, good cardiovascular health, steady physical presence even when challenged, calm body language.
facets.emotional: Emotional equanimity that doesn’t suppress feelings, ability to feel disappointment or frustration without being consumed, emotional clarity, capacity to address wrongs from peace rather than rage.
facets.cognitive: Clear thinking unclouded by rage, ability to discriminate appropriate response from reactive aggression, mental peace, wisdom in addressing injustice, freedom from revenge thinking.
facets.psychic: The soul meets provocation without being seized by it; freedom from anger is the spaciousness in which a clear response can arise.

balanced.text:
When Akrodha is present, a person can recognize injustice and respond firmly without being consumed by destructive anger. There is the capacity for righteous response without the bondage of rage.

Somatic: Relaxed physical state, healthy nervous system, absence of tension from suppressed rage, good cardiovascular health, steady physical presence even when challenged, calm body language.

Emotional: Emotional equanimity that doesn’t suppress feelings, ability to feel disappointment or frustration without being consumed, emotional clarity, capacity to address wrongs from peace rather than rage.

Cognitive: Clear thinking unclouded by rage, ability to discriminate appropriate response from reactive aggression, mental peace, wisdom in addressing injustice, freedom from revenge thinking.

Psychic: The soul meets provocation without being seized by it; freedom from anger is the spaciousness in which a clear response can arise.
balanced.vignette: When treated unjustly, Meera stays calm—not because she does not notice the wrong, but because she can register it clearly without being hijacked by rage. Her equanimity leaves her free to respond wisely, to name the injustice and address it, rather than to explode or to seethe. She is unruffled and not, for a moment, blind.

excess.label: In excess — suppressed anger, false serenity

facets.overview: Excessive Akrodha as suppression of all anger leads to denied feelings, inability to assert oneself, enabling of harm, and eventual explosive release.
facets.somatic: Tension from suppressed anger, stress-related illness, physical symptoms of held emotion, eventual physical breakdown, somatic symptoms.
facets.emotional: Suppressed anger that doesn’t process, inability to feel or express legitimate anger, emotional dishonesty, allowing harm without response.
facets.cognitive: Mental suppression of legitimate grievances, denial of valid responses, false peace masking inner turmoil, inability to think clearly about wrongs.
facets.psychic: Equanimity curdles into passivity; the soul suppresses even the clean fire that should meet genuine injustice.

excess.text:
Excessive Akrodha as suppression of all anger leads to denied feelings, inability to assert oneself, enabling of harm, and eventual explosive release.

Somatic: Tension from suppressed anger, stress-related illness, physical symptoms of held emotion, eventual physical breakdown, somatic symptoms.

Emotional: Suppressed anger that doesn’t process, inability to feel or express legitimate anger, emotional dishonesty, allowing harm without response.

Cognitive: Mental suppression of legitimate grievances, denial of valid responses, false peace masking inner turmoil, inability to think clearly about wrongs.

Psychic: Equanimity curdles into passivity; the soul suppresses even the clean fire that should meet genuine injustice.
excess.vignette: Ravi never gets angry, he will tell you proudly—and beneath the serene surface, a lifetime of unexpressed anger has pooled into a cold resentment. The ‘freedom from anger’ is really a suppression; he has confused not-feeling with peace, and the buried heat leaks out sideways in passive-aggression and a chronic, low-grade bitterness he cannot name.

deficient.label: Deficient — reactivity, the short fuse, the flooded mind

facets.overview: Absence of Akrodha manifests as chronic anger, rage reactions, hostility, and constant agitation that destroys relationships and well-being.
facets.somatic: Chronic stress response, high blood pressure, cardiovascular disease, muscular tension, stress-related illness, body in constant fight mode.
facets.emotional: Chronic anger and rage, emotional volatility, hostility toward others, emotional reactivity to small triggers, inability to feel peace, bitter resentment.
facets.cognitive: Mental obsession with grievances, revenge fantasies, distorted thinking from anger, inability to see clearly, cognitive impairment from rage, vengeful planning.
facets.psychic: The soul is repeatedly hijacked; chronic reactivity hands its peace to whatever happens to provoke it.

deficient.text:
Absence of Akrodha manifests as chronic anger, rage reactions, hostility, and constant agitation that destroys relationships and well-being.

Somatic: Chronic stress response, high blood pressure, cardiovascular disease, muscular tension, stress-related illness, body in constant fight mode.

Emotional: Chronic anger and rage, emotional volatility, hostility toward others, emotional reactivity to small triggers, inability to feel peace, bitter resentment.

Cognitive: Mental obsession with grievances, revenge fantasies, distorted thinking from anger, inability to see clearly, cognitive impairment from rage, vengeful planning.

Psychic: The soul is repeatedly hijacked; chronic reactivity hands its peace to whatever happens to provoke it.
deficient.vignette: Dan’s fuse is a millimeter long. The slow driver, the wrong order, the mild disagreement—each detonates an immediate, flooding anger that overwhelms his judgment and scorches whoever is nearest. He is ruled by his temper, swept from one flare to the next, and the constant reactivity leaves him exhausted and, increasingly, alone.

attributes.title: Dayā — Compassion
attributes.aspect: Daya is the active quality of compassion—feeling for others’ suffering and being moved to alleviate it. It is one of the divine qualities and reflects recognition of the same Self in all beings.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital · Ontic mode: Virtue — a daivī quality (compassion) · Valence: Virtue to cultivate · Guṇa: Sattva-dominant · Scale: Interpersonal (Ādhibhautika) · Causal status: Condition (tenderness toward all beings) · Key relation: A daivī quality; kin to Karuṇā (Dim 22).
coordinate.key_relation: A daivī quality; kin to Karuṇā (Dim 22).

balanced.label: Balanced — warm care that can also act

facets.overview: When Daya is present, a person feels genuine empathy and acts to help others without being overwhelmed or codependent. There is heartful care combined with wisdom about how to truly help.
facets.somatic: Open heart center, warm physical presence, healthy heart and circulation, gentle physical contact with others, body that expresses care, healthy nervous system.
facets.emotional: Genuine empathy and emotional warmth, ability to feel others’ suffering without absorption, emotional generosity, healing emotional presence, balanced emotional engagement.
facets.cognitive: Wisdom about how to help versus enable, clear thinking about others’ true needs, mental discernment in compassion, ability to maintain perspective while caring.
facets.psychic: The soul feels the suffering of others as not finally other; compassion is the felt recognition of a shared being.

balanced.text:
When Daya is present, a person feels genuine empathy and acts to help others without being overwhelmed or codependent. There is heartful care combined with wisdom about how to truly help.

Somatic: Open heart center, warm physical presence, healthy heart and circulation, gentle physical contact with others, body that expresses care, healthy nervous system.

Emotional: Genuine empathy and emotional warmth, ability to feel others’ suffering without absorption, emotional generosity, healing emotional presence, balanced emotional engagement.

Cognitive: Wisdom about how to help versus enable, clear thinking about others’ true needs, mental discernment in compassion, ability to maintain perspective while caring.

Psychic: The soul feels the suffering of others as not finally other; compassion is the felt recognition of a shared being.
balanced.vignette: When her neighbor falls ill, Kavya shows up—warm, present, moved, and also practical enough to organize the meals and make the calls. Her compassion feels the suffering fully and does not drown in it; the caring flows into competent help. She has a soft heart and steady hands, and the combination is a quiet blessing to everyone around her.

excess.label: In excess — over-identification, enabling, burnout

facets.overview: Excessive Daya without wisdom becomes codependent rescuing, emotional overwhelm from others’ pain, enabling, and loss of personal boundaries.
facets.somatic: Physical exhaustion from over-helping, neglect of own physical needs, body weighed down by others’ burdens, compassion fatigue.
facets.emotional: Emotional overwhelm from absorbing others’ pain, codependent rescuing, inability to set boundaries, emotional martyrdom, suffering for others to one’s own detriment.
facets.cognitive: Inability to see when help becomes enabling, mental confusion about appropriate response, loss of perspective, identification with others’ suffering.
facets.psychic: Compassion without boundaries drowns the helper; the soul loses its footing in another’s pain and can no longer act.

excess.text:
Excessive Daya without wisdom becomes codependent rescuing, emotional overwhelm from others’ pain, enabling, and loss of personal boundaries.

Somatic: Physical exhaustion from over-helping, neglect of own physical needs, body weighed down by others’ burdens, compassion fatigue.

Emotional: Emotional overwhelm from absorbing others’ pain, codependent rescuing, inability to set boundaries, emotional martyrdom, suffering for others to one’s own detriment.

Cognitive: Inability to see when help becomes enabling, mental confusion about appropriate response, loss of perspective, identification with others’ suffering.

Psychic: Compassion without boundaries drowns the helper; the soul loses its footing in another’s pain and can no longer act.
excess.vignette: Nadia cannot say no to anyone’s pain. She takes on every burden, rescues every struggler, gives past exhaustion, and enables the very dysfunction she is trying to soothe—her compassion so boundless it has no shore. In drowning in others’ suffering she has become useless to them and to herself, and calls the depletion love.

deficient.label: Deficient — hardness, indifference to suffering

facets.overview: Absence of Daya creates emotional coldness, indifference to suffering, inability to feel for others, and selfish disregard for those in need.
facets.somatic: Closed and tight chest area, restricted heart and breathing, physical coldness in presence of suffering, hard physical presence toward others.
facets.emotional: Emotional coldness and indifference, inability to feel others’ pain, hardness of heart, selfish emotional preoccupation, lack of care.
facets.cognitive: Mental dismissal of others’ suffering, rationalizations for not helping, selfish thinking, inability to consider others’ experiences, callous reasoning.
facets.psychic: The soul contracts into cold indifference; the closed heart marks a self still defended against the world.

deficient.text:
Absence of Daya creates emotional coldness, indifference to suffering, inability to feel for others, and selfish disregard for those in need.

Somatic: Closed and tight chest area, restricted heart and breathing, physical coldness in presence of suffering, hard physical presence toward others.

Emotional: Emotional coldness and indifference, inability to feel others’ pain, hardness of heart, selfish emotional preoccupation, lack of care.

Cognitive: Mental dismissal of others’ suffering, rationalizations for not helping, selfish thinking, inability to consider others’ experiences, callous reasoning.

Psychic: The soul contracts into cold indifference; the closed heart marks a self still defended against the world.
deficient.vignette: Suffering slides off Dan. The beggar, the grieving friend, the colleague in crisis all meet a flat indifference, sometimes a flicker of contempt for weakness. Where compassion should stir, there is only a cool detachment, and the hardness that spares him others’ pain has quietly cost him his connection to their humanity.

attributes.title: Hrī — Modesty
attributes.aspect: Hrih is the natural sense of modesty and shame regarding inappropriate or unworthy actions. It is the inner moral compass that signals when conduct violates dharma, creating spontaneous restraint.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital–Mental · Ontic mode: Virtue — a daivī quality (modesty / moral conscience) · Valence: Virtue to cultivate · Guṇa: Sattva · Scale: Interpersonal (Ādhibhautika) · Causal status: Condition (the healthy conscience that recoils from wrong) · Key relation: A daivī quality; the wholesome form of Vrīḍā (Dim 9); antagonist to Dambha (4.1).
coordinate.ontic_mode: Virtue — a daivī quality (modesty / moral conscience)
coordinate.causal_status: Condition (the healthy conscience that recoils from wrong)
coordinate.key_relation: A daivī quality; the wholesome form of Vrīḍā (Dim 9); antagonist to Dambha (4.1).

balanced.label: Balanced — a wholesome conscience and humility

facets.overview: When Hrih is present, a person naturally avoids actions that would compromise dignity or violate ethical principles. There is innate sense of propriety without false prudishness.
facets.somatic: Naturally modest physical presence, appropriate physical boundaries, dignified body language, healthy sense of physical privacy.
facets.emotional: Innate sense of emotional propriety, ability to feel appropriate shame when warranted, healthy moral compass, emotional dignity.
facets.cognitive: Clear inner sense of right and wrong action, mental restraint based on values, wisdom about appropriate conduct, conscience that guides without crippling.
facets.psychic: The soul carries a healthy reverence — the capacity for proper shame that keeps action aligned with conscience.

balanced.text:
When Hrih is present, a person naturally avoids actions that would compromise dignity or violate ethical principles. There is innate sense of propriety without false prudishness.

Somatic: Naturally modest physical presence, appropriate physical boundaries, dignified body language, healthy sense of physical privacy.

Emotional: Innate sense of emotional propriety, ability to feel appropriate shame when warranted, healthy moral compass, emotional dignity.

Cognitive: Clear inner sense of right and wrong action, mental restraint based on values, wisdom about appropriate conduct, conscience that guides without crippling.

Psychic: The soul carries a healthy reverence — the capacity for proper shame that keeps action aligned with conscience.
balanced.vignette: Deepa has a working conscience—a healthy capacity for the wholesome shame that says ‘that wasn’t right, make it good’—paired with a genuine humility about her own place. She can feel appropriately embarrassed, apologize cleanly, and hold herself to a standard without self-flagellation. The inner sense of ‘ought’ is alive in her, and it keeps her honest.

excess.label: In excess — shame, self-erasure, chronic embarrassment

facets.overview: Excessive Hrih becomes false modesty, shame-based existence, inability to act due to fear of criticism, and chronic self-judgment.
facets.somatic: Physical shrinking and hiding, inability to express oneself physically, shame-based body language, physical paralysis from shame.
facets.emotional: Chronic shame and self-judgment, paralyzing modesty, emotional contraction, inability to celebrate or express joy, false humility.
facets.cognitive: Constant self-criticism, mental shame, inability to act for fear of being seen, paralysis from concern about judgment, distorted self-image.
facets.psychic: Modesty collapses into crippling self-effacement; the soul shrinks from its own legitimate light, mistaking smallness for virtue.

excess.text:
Excessive Hrih becomes false modesty, shame-based existence, inability to act due to fear of criticism, and chronic self-judgment.

Somatic: Physical shrinking and hiding, inability to express oneself physically, shame-based body language, physical paralysis from shame.

Emotional: Chronic shame and self-judgment, paralyzing modesty, emotional contraction, inability to celebrate or express joy, false humility.

Cognitive: Constant self-criticism, mental shame, inability to act for fear of being seen, paralysis from concern about judgment, distorted self-image.

Psychic: Modesty collapses into crippling self-effacement; the soul shrinks from its own legitimate light, mistaking smallness for virtue.
excess.vignette: Sara is drowning in shame. Every mistake becomes proof of her worthlessness, every social moment a chance to be mortified, every flaw a catastrophe—the healthy conscience hypertrophied into a relentless self-erasure. She apologizes for existing, shrinks from every room, and the constant shame has become a prison she cannot see the bars of.

deficient.label: Deficient — shamelessness, the loss of conscience

facets.overview: Absence of Hrih manifests as shamelessness, inability to feel proper restraint, crude behavior, and lack of inner moral compass.
facets.somatic: Crude physical behavior, lack of physical boundaries, inappropriate physical conduct, disrespect for own and others’ physical dignity.
facets.emotional: Shamelessness and crudeness, inability to feel appropriate shame, lack of moral feeling, emotional coarseness.
facets.cognitive: No internal sense of right action, mental rationalizations for any behavior, inability to discriminate dignified from undignified, missing conscience.
facets.psychic: The soul loses its inner compass; shamelessness is the inability to feel the quiet ‘no’ of conscience before wrong.

deficient.text:
Absence of Hrih manifests as shamelessness, inability to feel proper restraint, crude behavior, and lack of inner moral compass.

Somatic: Crude physical behavior, lack of physical boundaries, inappropriate physical conduct, disrespect for own and others’ physical dignity.

Emotional: Shamelessness and crudeness, inability to feel appropriate shame, lack of moral feeling, emotional coarseness.

Cognitive: No internal sense of right action, mental rationalizations for any behavior, inability to discriminate dignified from undignified, missing conscience.

Psychic: The soul loses its inner compass; shamelessness is the inability to feel the quiet ‘no’ of conscience before wrong.
deficient.vignette: Dan feels no shame, and it shows. He lies without a flicker, takes credit that is not his, behaves badly in public with a bland unconcern—the inner sense of ‘that’s not right’ simply absent. Where conscience should live there is a smooth blankness, and without it, nothing checks him; he does what serves him and sleeps fine.

attributes.title: Mārdavam — Gentleness
attributes.aspect: Märdavam is gentleness—softness in conduct, manner, and approach. It is the quality of being kind in interaction with others, not harsh or rough. It reflects refinement and compassion in expression.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital · Ontic mode: Virtue — a daivī quality (gentleness / softness) · Valence: Virtue to cultivate · Guṇa: Sattva-dominant · Scale: Interpersonal (Ādhibhautika) · Causal status: Condition (a soft, non-harsh disposition) · Key relation: A daivī quality; direct antagonist to Pāruṣyam (harshness, 4.3).
coordinate.key_relation: A daivī quality; direct antagonist to Pāruṣyam (harshness, 4.3).

balanced.label: Balanced — strength expressed as gentleness

facets.overview: When Märdavam is present, a person interacts with others gently and kindly while maintaining strength. There is softness without weakness, gentleness without timidity.
facets.somatic: Gentle physical touch and presence, soft body language, peaceful physical demeanor, healthy nervous system from peaceful engagement.
facets.emotional: Emotional softness and warmth, kind emotional expression, gentle approach to others, emotional tenderness combined with strength.
facets.cognitive: Gentle thinking patterns, mental refinement, sensitive consideration of others, thoughtful and considerate mental engagement.
facets.psychic: The soul meets the world with a softness that is not weakness; gentleness is strength that has no need to harden.

balanced.text:
When Märdavam is present, a person interacts with others gently and kindly while maintaining strength. There is softness without weakness, gentleness without timidity.

Somatic: Gentle physical touch and presence, soft body language, peaceful physical demeanor, healthy nervous system from peaceful engagement.

Emotional: Emotional softness and warmth, kind emotional expression, gentle approach to others, emotional tenderness combined with strength.

Cognitive: Gentle thinking patterns, mental refinement, sensitive consideration of others, thoughtful and considerate mental engagement.

Psychic: The soul meets the world with a softness that is not weakness; gentleness is strength that has no need to harden.
balanced.vignette: Ravi is a big, capable man whose strength comes out as gentleness—patient with the frightened, tender with the small, firm without ever being harsh. There is nothing weak in his softness; it is the gentleness of someone strong enough not to need to dominate. People feel safe around him, and they are right to.

excess.label: In excess — softness without spine, conflict-avoidance

facets.overview: Excessive Märdavam can become weak softness, inability to take firm action when needed, over-sensitivity, and ineffective gentleness in face of harshness.
facets.somatic: Physical weakness and inability to assert, lack of physical strength when needed, over-yielding to others’ physical assertions.
facets.emotional: Emotional weakness disguised as gentleness, inability to be firm, over-sensitivity, lack of healthy assertiveness.
facets.cognitive: Mental weakness and inability to take strong positions, over-accommodating thinking, lack of mental firmness when needed.
facets.psychic: Gentleness slides into spinelessness; the soul cannot hold a needed edge and is shaped by whoever pushes hardest.

excess.text:
Excessive Märdavam can become weak softness, inability to take firm action when needed, over-sensitivity, and ineffective gentleness in face of harshness.

Somatic: Physical weakness and inability to assert, lack of physical strength when needed, over-yielding to others’ physical assertions.

Emotional: Emotional weakness disguised as gentleness, inability to be firm, over-sensitivity, lack of healthy assertiveness.

Cognitive: Mental weakness and inability to take strong positions, over-accommodating thinking, lack of mental firmness when needed.

Psychic: Gentleness slides into spinelessness; the soul cannot hold a needed edge and is shaped by whoever pushes hardest.
excess.vignette: Ben is so gentle he is useless in a crisis. Conflict of any kind sends him fleeing; he cannot deliver hard news, hold a boundary, or oppose anyone, and calls the collapse ‘being kind.’ The softness has no spine in it, and his inability to ever be firm quietly permits a great deal of harm he is too gentle to prevent.

deficient.label: Deficient — hardness, abrasiveness, the bruising manner

facets.overview: Absence of Märdavam creates harshness, roughness in conduct, hurtful manner of interaction, and inability to soften when appropriate.
facets.somatic: Harsh physical contact, rough body language, abrasive physical presence, ungentle touch.
facets.emotional: Emotional harshness and roughness, inability to soften emotionally, emotional brusqueness, lack of warmth.
facets.cognitive: Harsh thinking patterns, rough mental engagement, inability to consider sensitivities, blunt and unrefined mental approach.
facets.psychic: The soul roughens; harshness in manner and speech is the surface sign of an inner brittleness.

deficient.text:
Absence of Märdavam creates harshness, roughness in conduct, hurtful manner of interaction, and inability to soften when appropriate.

Somatic: Harsh physical contact, rough body language, abrasive physical presence, ungentle touch.

Emotional: Emotional harshness and roughness, inability to soften emotionally, emotional brusqueness, lack of warmth.

Cognitive: Harsh thinking patterns, rough mental engagement, inability to consider sensitivities, blunt and unrefined mental approach.

Psychic: The soul roughens; harshness in manner and speech is the surface sign of an inner brittleness.
deficient.vignette: Dan’s manner bruises. Blunt to the point of cruelty, impatient with weakness, abrasive without noticing, he leaves small wounds wherever he goes and is puzzled that people find him hard. The gentleness that would soften his considerable strength is simply missing, and his competence arrives, always, with an edge that cuts.

attributes.title: Satya — Truthfulness
attributes.aspect: Satya is truthfulness in thought, speech, and action. It is alignment with what is real and right, expressed with love (Satyam priyahitamchayat — truth spoken with love or be silent). It includes both factual truth and the deeper truth of Self.
