Somatic: Steady and relaxed physical presence in all conditions, consistent energy levels, stable health despite circumstances, calm breathing and nervous system, poised physical bearing.

Emotional: Profound emotional peace in the face of pleasure or pain, emotional stability despite life changes, absence of emotional reactivity, equanimous response to success and failure, deep inner contentment.

Cognitive: Mental steadiness and peace, clarity of thought regardless of circumstances, wisdom in response, freedom from mental turbulence, mental peace as a stable baseline.

Psychic: The soul rests in equanimity, even-minded in gain and loss, the steadiness the Gītā calls yoga itself.
balanced.vignette: In gain and loss alike, Ravi holds a genuine even-mindedness—neither elated by the win nor crushed by the setback, meeting both from the same steady center. The equanimity isn’t indifference; it’s a depth that lets him act clearly precisely because he isn’t swept away.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Excessive equanimity can become apathy, indifference, and disconnection from life. It becomes a dispassionate withdrawal rather than engaged peace.
facets.somatic: Physical indifference and lack of engagement, poor physical responsiveness, disconnected physical presence, inability to protect or care for body.
facets.emotional: Emotional coldness and indifference, lack of appropriate emotional response, emotional numbness, inability to care about what matters, emotional disconnection.
facets.cognitive: Mental indifference and apathy, inability to be moved by suffering, mental disconnection from values, inability to take action when needed.
facets.psychic: The soul forces a flat composure, mistaking numbness for the living evenness of the sage.

excess.text:
Excessive equanimity can become apathy, indifference, and disconnection from life. It becomes a dispassionate withdrawal rather than engaged peace.

Somatic: Physical indifference and lack of engagement, poor physical responsiveness, disconnected physical presence, inability to protect or care for body.

Emotional: Emotional coldness and indifference, lack of appropriate emotional response, emotional numbness, inability to care about what matters, emotional disconnection.

Cognitive: Mental indifference and apathy, inability to be moved by suffering, mental disconnection from values, inability to take action when needed.

Psychic: The soul forces a flat composure, mistaking numbness for the living evenness of the sage.
excess.vignette: Sam forces equanimity into numbness—suppressing every high and low, refusing to feel the natural swing of things, mistaking a flat deadness for even-mindedness. The living balance has become a clamped-down denial, and the suppression is its own quiet turbulence underneath.

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.overview: Absence of equanimity results in being tossed about by life’s circumstances, emotional reactivity, suffering in the face of ups and downs, and mental instability.
facets.somatic: Physical agitation in changing circumstances, inconsistent energy levels, nervous system dysregulation, poor physical health from stress reactivity.
facets.emotional: Emotional reactivity to every circumstance, emotional suffering with life’s ups and downs, emotional instability, inability to maintain inner peace, emotional exhaustion.
facets.cognitive: Mental turbulence and suffering, inability to think clearly in challenging circumstances, mental reactivity, mental suffering from attachment to outcomes.
facets.psychic: The soul is tossed by every pair of opposites, its inner weather wholly ruled by events.

deficient.text:
Absence of equanimity results in being tossed about by life’s circumstances, emotional reactivity, suffering in the face of ups and downs, and mental instability.

Somatic: Physical agitation in changing circumstances, inconsistent energy levels, nervous system dysregulation, poor physical health from stress reactivity.

Emotional: Emotional reactivity to every circumstance, emotional suffering with life’s ups and downs, emotional instability, inability to maintain inner peace, emotional exhaustion.

Cognitive: Mental turbulence and suffering, inability to think clearly in challenging circumstances, mental reactivity, mental suffering from attachment to outcomes.

Psychic: The soul is tossed by every pair of opposites, its inner weather wholly ruled by events.
deficient.vignette: Dan is swept fully by every swing—soaring with the gain, plummeting with the loss, his inner state a helpless echo of his outer fortune. The even-mindedness that meets both the same way is a steadiness he’s never known; he rides the seesaw, all the way up and all the way down.

attributes.title: Dvandva (The Dualities of Existence)
attributes.aspect: Dvandva refers to the pairs of opposites that dictate physical life: heat and cold, pleasure and pain, honor and dishonor, success and failure, birth and death. Humans must learn to remain unmoved by these pairs.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital–Mental · Ontic mode: Framework — the pairs of opposites (dvandva) · Valence: Affliction-field (to rise above) · Guṇa: Rajas · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Condition of bondage · Key relation: The pleasure / pain pairs; transcended by Titikṣā (Dim 23) and Samatvam.
coordinate.key_relation: The pleasure / pain pairs; transcended by Titikṣā (Dim 23) and Samatvam.

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.overview: When relating to dualities wisely, a person accepts their alternating nature as inevitable, learns from both, and does not cling to one or reject the other.
facets.somatic: Physical adaptation to changing conditions without suffering, resilience in the face of physical challenges, acceptance of the body’s natural cycles, healthy response to comfort and discomfort.
facets.emotional: Emotional wisdom about the transitory nature of feelings, emotional non-attachment to pleasant or unpleasant experiences, emotional flexibility, emotional maturity in handling life’s alternations.
facets.cognitive: Mental understanding of the nature of opposites, mental freedom from compulsive preference for one side, wisdom about temporary nature of all conditions, mental balance.
facets.psychic: The soul stands between the pairs of opposites — heat and cold, praise and blame — without being claimed by either.

balanced.text:
When relating to dualities wisely, a person accepts their alternating nature as inevitable, learns from both, and does not cling to one or reject the other.

Somatic: Physical adaptation to changing conditions without suffering, resilience in the face of physical challenges, acceptance of the body’s natural cycles, healthy response to comfort and discomfort.

Emotional: Emotional wisdom about the transitory nature of feelings, emotional non-attachment to pleasant or unpleasant experiences, emotional flexibility, emotional maturity in handling life’s alternations.

Cognitive: Mental understanding of the nature of opposites, mental freedom from compulsive preference for one side, wisdom about temporary nature of all conditions, mental balance.

Psychic: The soul stands between the pairs of opposites — heat and cold, praise and blame — without being claimed by either.
balanced.vignette: When the pairs come—heat and cold, praise and blame, comfort and hardship—Ravi bears them with a settled patience, neither denying the difference nor being tossed by it. He’s learned to sit in the discomfort and let the pleasant pass, at home amid the opposites that rule most people.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Over-focus on the constancy of dualities can lead to fatalism, nihilism, and giving up on meaningful effort or change. There is resignation without wisdom.
facets.somatic: Resignation to physical suffering without attempting improvement, poor health from lack of effort, physical passivity, neglect of physical well-being.
facets.emotional: Emotional resignation and despair, inability to hope for improvement, emotional nihilism, lack of emotional engagement with life.
facets.cognitive: Mental fatalism, belief that nothing can change, mental despair, inability to see possibility beyond dualities.
facets.psychic: The soul over-detaches, refusing to feel the natural pull of the opposites at all.

excess.text:
Over-focus on the constancy of dualities can lead to fatalism, nihilism, and giving up on meaningful effort or change. There is resignation without wisdom.

Somatic: Resignation to physical suffering without attempting improvement, poor health from lack of effort, physical passivity, neglect of physical well-being.

Emotional: Emotional resignation and despair, inability to hope for improvement, emotional nihilism, lack of emotional engagement with life.

Cognitive: Mental fatalism, belief that nothing can change, mental despair, inability to see possibility beyond dualities.

Psychic: The soul over-detaches, refusing to feel the natural pull of the opposites at all.
excess.vignette: Sam turns bearing the opposites into a masochistic contest—seeking out cold, courting hardship, refusing comfort to prove he’s above the pairs. The equanimity toward the dualities has become a grim over-endurance, and the proving is its own agitation.

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.overview: Inability to accept the dualities leads to constant suffering, perpetual desire to cling to one side and reject the other, and fundamental misunderstanding of existence.
facets.somatic: Physical suffering and resistance to change, poor adaptation to circumstances, physical tension from trying to maintain one state, health problems from resistance.
facets.emotional: Emotional suffering from resistance to the inevitable, emotional craving for permanence, inability to accept natural changes, emotional disturbance.
facets.cognitive: Mental resistance to reality, inability to accept inevitable changes, mental suffering from denial, constant mental struggle against conditions.
facets.psychic: The soul is helplessly swung between the dualities, elated and crushed by turns.

deficient.text:
Inability to accept the dualities leads to constant suffering, perpetual desire to cling to one side and reject the other, and fundamental misunderstanding of existence.

Somatic: Physical suffering and resistance to change, poor adaptation to circumstances, physical tension from trying to maintain one state, health problems from resistance.

Emotional: Emotional suffering from resistance to the inevitable, emotional craving for permanence, inability to accept natural changes, emotional disturbance.

Cognitive: Mental resistance to reality, inability to accept inevitable changes, mental suffering from denial, constant mental struggle against conditions.

Psychic: The soul is helplessly swung between the dualities, elated and crushed by turns.
deficient.vignette: Dan is at the mercy of every swing between the opposites—wretched in the heat, miserable in the cold, lifted by praise and shattered by blame. The capacity to bear the pairs of opposites with an even mind is one he’s never developed; each swing owns him completely while it lasts.

attributes.title: Kama-Krodha-Lobha (Lust-Anger-Greed Triad)
attributes.aspect: Kama (lust), Krodha (anger), and Lobha (greed) are identified as the triple gates to hell—the specific emotional and psychological forces that degrade human consciousness and bind the mind to suffering.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital · Ontic mode: Affliction-triad — the three gates of hell · Valence: Affliction (to transmute) · Guṇa: Rajas–Tamas · Scale: Individual / interpersonal · Causal status: Root distortions · Key relation: Lust-anger-greed — the triad the Gītā (16.21) names the gates of hell; Kāma / Krodha kin to Dim 24.
coordinate.ontic_mode: Affliction-triad — the three gates of hell
coordinate.key_relation: Lust-anger-greed — the triad the Gītā (16.21) names the gates of hell; Kāma / Krodha kin to Dim 24.

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.overview: When these forces are balanced and understood, a person can acknowledge desires and frustrations without being consumed by them. There is healthy engagement without degradation.
facets.somatic: Physical health from moderation, appropriate physical engagement without excess, healthy body function, absence of diseases from indulgence.
facets.emotional: Emotional clarity about desires and frustrations, emotional capacity to feel without being swept away, emotional strength and stability, emotional satisfaction without compulsion.
facets.cognitive: Mental clarity about true vs. false needs, mental strength to direct desires wisely, mental peace without compulsive pursuit, mental wisdom in engagement.
facets.psychic: The soul recognizes the threefold gate to ruin — lust, anger, greed — and keeps watch at the gate.

balanced.text:
When these forces are balanced and understood, a person can acknowledge desires and frustrations without being consumed by them. There is healthy engagement without degradation.

Somatic: Physical health from moderation, appropriate physical engagement without excess, healthy body function, absence of diseases from indulgence.

Emotional: Emotional clarity about desires and frustrations, emotional capacity to feel without being swept away, emotional strength and stability, emotional satisfaction without compulsion.

Cognitive: Mental clarity about true vs. false needs, mental strength to direct desires wisely, mental peace without compulsive pursuit, mental wisdom in engagement.

Psychic: The soul recognizes the threefold gate to ruin — lust, anger, greed — and keeps watch at the gate.
balanced.vignette: Ravi knows the three gates—lust, anger, greed—and keeps a clear eye on them, feeling their pull without being dragged through. Aware of the triad as the triad, he’s largely free of their grip, and the freedom shows in a life not run by craving, rage, or grasping.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: When these three forces are excessive, they dominate consciousness: insatiable lust drives compulsive behavior, anger erupts at frustration, and greed creates endless craving. The gates to degradation open.
facets.somatic: Physical deterioration from excess and indulgence, addictive behaviors, poor health from lust and gluttony, physical aggression from anger, depletion from compulsive pursuit.
facets.emotional: Emotional slavery to desires, emotional volatility and rage, emotional corruption from greed, emotional suffering from insatiability, emotional degradation.
facets.cognitive: Mental obsession with desired objects, mental rage and cruelty, mental obsession with acquisition, mental degradation and darkness, inability to think of anything else.
facets.psychic: The soul flings the gate wide, ruled openly by craving, rage, and grasping.

excess.text:
When these three forces are excessive, they dominate consciousness: insatiable lust drives compulsive behavior, anger erupts at frustration, and greed creates endless craving. The gates to degradation open.

Somatic: Physical deterioration from excess and indulgence, addictive behaviors, poor health from lust and gluttony, physical aggression from anger, depletion from compulsive pursuit.

Emotional: Emotional slavery to desires, emotional volatility and rage, emotional corruption from greed, emotional suffering from insatiability, emotional degradation.

Cognitive: Mental obsession with desired objects, mental rage and cruelty, mental obsession with acquisition, mental degradation and darkness, inability to think of anything else.

Psychic: The soul flings the gate wide, ruled openly by craving, rage, and grasping.
excess.vignette: Sam is run by the three—the lust, the flaring anger, the bottomless greed—each seizing him in turn, the triad of gates flung wide and his life pouring through them. Consumed by all three, he mistakes the compulsions for desires and the bondage for living.

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.overview: Complete suppression of these forces without understanding creates psychological pressure, eventual explosive release, and disconnection from life energy.
facets.somatic: Physical suppression and tension, lack of physical vitality, eventual physical breakdown when suppressed forces erupt.
facets.emotional: Emotional suppression and coldness, emotional pressure building beneath surface, inability to feel alive, eventual emotional explosion.
facets.cognitive: Mental suppression and tension, inability to engage meaningfully, mental heaviness from repression, eventual mental breakdown.
facets.psychic: The soul denies these forces in itself, and the unwatched gate stands open unawares.

deficient.text:
Complete suppression of these forces without understanding creates psychological pressure, eventual explosive release, and disconnection from life energy.

Somatic: Physical suppression and tension, lack of physical vitality, eventual physical breakdown when suppressed forces erupt.

Emotional: Emotional suppression and coldness, emotional pressure building beneath surface, inability to feel alive, eventual emotional explosion.

Cognitive: Mental suppression and tension, inability to engage meaningfully, mental heaviness from repression, eventual mental breakdown.

Psychic: The soul denies these forces in itself, and the unwatched gate stands open unawares.
deficient.vignette: So terrified of lust, anger, and greed, Dan has crushed even their healthy roots—no desire, no assertion, no ambition left, a grey lifelessness he mistakes for virtue. In slamming the three gates he’s walled off the very drives a full life needs, and calls the deadness freedom.

attributes.title: Karpanya-Dosa (Flaw of Self-Pity)
attributes.aspect: Karpanya-Dosa is a specific existential crisis characterized by paralyzing anxiety, faint-heartedness, self-pity, and total loss of clarity regarding one’s life direction and purpose. It manifests as victim consciousness.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital · Ontic mode: Affliction — a vice that binds · Valence: Affliction (to transmute) · Guṇa: Tamas · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Distortion · Key relation: the flaw of faint-hearted self-pity (kārpaṇya, Gītā 2.7); kin to Dainya (Dim 9).
coordinate.key_relation: the flaw of faint-hearted self-pity (kārpaṇya, Gītā 2.7); kin to Dainya (Dim 9).

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.overview: When self-pity is transcended, a person takes responsibility for circumstances and their response to them. There is clarity about direction and commitment to action.
facets.somatic: Strong and confident physical bearing, physical courage and readiness, decisive physical action, good health from engagement.
facets.emotional: Emotional maturity and responsibility, emotional courage, absence of victim mentality, emotional strength and resilience, emotional clarity about direction.
facets.cognitive: Clear mental understanding of one’s role and responsibility, mental clarity about direction, mental courage and determination, mental freedom from self-pity.
facets.psychic: The soul, like Arjuna corrected, sheds the self-pity that weakens resolve, and stands up to its task.

balanced.text:
When self-pity is transcended, a person takes responsibility for circumstances and their response to them. There is clarity about direction and commitment to action.

Somatic: Strong and confident physical bearing, physical courage and readiness, decisive physical action, good health from engagement.

Emotional: Emotional maturity and responsibility, emotional courage, absence of victim mentality, emotional strength and resilience, emotional clarity about direction.

Cognitive: Clear mental understanding of one’s role and responsibility, mental clarity about direction, mental courage and determination, mental freedom from self-pity.

Psychic: The soul, like Arjuna corrected, sheds the self-pity that weakens resolve, and stands up to its task.
balanced.vignette: When real difficulty strikes, Ravi meets it with strength rather than self-pity—feeling the pain honestly without collapsing into ‘why me.’ Free of the flaw of karpanya, he doesn’t waste his force on victimhood; he grieves cleanly and then does what the situation asks.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Excessive self-responsibility without compassion can lead to harsh self-judgment, inability to accept help, and emotional isolation.
facets.somatic: Over-exertion and physical burnout, inability to ask for help, physical exhaustion from carrying everything alone.
facets.emotional: Harsh self-judgment, inability to accept support, emotional isolation, inability to be vulnerable.
facets.cognitive: Over-responsibility and harsh mental judgment, inability to see legitimate limitations, mental pressure and stress.
facets.psychic: The soul indulges its helplessness, wearing weakness as identity.

excess.text:
Excessive self-responsibility without compassion can lead to harsh self-judgment, inability to accept help, and emotional isolation.

Somatic: Over-exertion and physical burnout, inability to ask for help, physical exhaustion from carrying everything alone.

Emotional: Harsh self-judgment, inability to accept support, emotional isolation, inability to be vulnerable.

Cognitive: Over-responsibility and harsh mental judgment, inability to see legitimate limitations, mental pressure and stress.

Psychic: The soul indulges its helplessness, wearing weakness as identity.
excess.vignette: Sam has made a home in self-pity—every setback an occasion for ‘why does this always happen to me,’ the victimhood rehearsed, nursed, and worn. The flaw of karpanya runs deep in him; the self-pity has become an identity, and it drains the strength he’d need to actually change anything.

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.overview: When Karpanya-Dosa is strong, there is profound victim consciousness, paralysis, self-pity, and loss of direction. The person is unable to act.
facets.somatic: Physical paralysis and inaction, poor health from immobility and stress, physical withdrawal from life, loss of physical vitality.
facets.emotional: Profound self-pity and victimhood, emotional paralysis, emotional despair and helplessness, emotional heaviness and burden, emotional withdrawal.
facets.cognitive: Complete loss of mental clarity and direction, mental paralysis, victim consciousness, mental despair, inability to see solutions or take action.
facets.psychic: The soul over-corrects into a hard refusal of all vulnerability, mistaking brittleness for strength.

deficient.text:
When Karpanya-Dosa is strong, there is profound victim consciousness, paralysis, self-pity, and loss of direction. The person is unable to act.

Somatic: Physical paralysis and inaction, poor health from immobility and stress, physical withdrawal from life, loss of physical vitality.

Emotional: Profound self-pity and victimhood, emotional paralysis, emotional despair and helplessness, emotional heaviness and burden, emotional withdrawal.

Cognitive: Complete loss of mental clarity and direction, mental paralysis, victim consciousness, mental despair, inability to see solutions or take action.

Psychic: The soul over-corrects into a hard refusal of all vulnerability, mistaking brittleness for strength.
deficient.vignette: Dan is so hard on himself that he permits no acknowledgment of genuine difficulty—every struggle dismissed as weakness, every legitimate pain denied. In fleeing self-pity so completely, he’s lost the honest self-compassion a person needs, and the unacknowledged struggles harden, unfelt, beneath the harshness.

attributes.title: Santosa (Contentment)
attributes.aspect: Santosa is the human experience of joy derived entirely from within, independent of external acquisitions, achievements, milestones, or material changes. It is inner fulfillment without dependence on external conditions.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital–Mental · Ontic mode: Virtue — a quality of freedom · Valence: Virtue to cultivate · Guṇa: Sattva-dominant · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Condition · Key relation: contentment (santoṣa); same as the niyama (Dim 11).
coordinate.key_relation: contentment (santoṣa); same as the niyama (Dim 11).

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.overview: When contentment is present, a person experiences deep satisfaction and joy from within, regardless of external circumstances. Life circumstances are appreciated, but happiness is not dependent on them.
facets.somatic: Physical ease and relaxation from lack of striving, good health from reduced stress, natural vitality and energy, appreciation of physical well-being.
facets.emotional: Deep inner joy and satisfaction, emotional peace and fulfillment, emotional appreciation of life as it is, emotional freedom from endless wanting.
facets.cognitive: Mental peace and clarity, freedom from mental restlessness, wisdom about true sources of happiness, mental appreciation and gratitude.
facets.psychic: The soul rests in contentment, full in itself, no longer leaking its peace into the next acquisition.

balanced.text:
When contentment is present, a person experiences deep satisfaction and joy from within, regardless of external circumstances. Life circumstances are appreciated, but happiness is not dependent on them.

Somatic: Physical ease and relaxation from lack of striving, good health from reduced stress, natural vitality and energy, appreciation of physical well-being.

Emotional: Deep inner joy and satisfaction, emotional peace and fulfillment, emotional appreciation of life as it is, emotional freedom from endless wanting.

Cognitive: Mental peace and clarity, freedom from mental restlessness, wisdom about true sources of happiness, mental appreciation and gratitude.

Psychic: The soul rests in contentment, full in itself, no longer leaking its peace into the next acquisition.
balanced.vignette: Kira’s flat is small and her life ordinary, and most evenings she feels, without irony, that she has enough. Her contentment isn’t resignation but a quiet sufficiency; she wants things, works for them, and isn’t tormented by their absence.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Excessive contentment becomes apathy, laziness, and unwillingness to improve or grow. It manifests as passive acceptance of problematic situations.
facets.somatic: Physical laziness and lack of motivation, poor health from lack of engagement, stagnation and heaviness.
facets.emotional: Emotional indifference and apathy, lack of motivation to address problems, passive acceptance of suffering.
facets.cognitive: Mental complacency, lack of motivation for improvement or growth, acceptance of false limitations.
facets.psychic: Contentment curdles into complacency, the soul excusing stagnation as peace.

excess.text:
Excessive contentment becomes apathy, laziness, and unwillingness to improve or grow. It manifests as passive acceptance of problematic situations.

Somatic: Physical laziness and lack of motivation, poor health from lack of engagement, stagnation and heaviness.

Emotional: Emotional indifference and apathy, lack of motivation to address problems, passive acceptance of suffering.

Cognitive: Mental complacency, lack of motivation for improvement or growth, acceptance of false limitations.

Psychic: Contentment curdles into complacency, the soul excusing stagnation as peace.
excess.vignette: Ben is so ‘content’ that nothing ever changes—the stalled career, the neglected health, the tepid life all accepted with a shrug dressed as serenity. His contentment has curdled into complacency, a spiritual-sounding excuse never to rise from the chair.

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.overview: Lack of contentment creates chronic dissatisfaction, endless wanting, and inability to enjoy present circumstances. Happiness is always deferred to the future.
facets.somatic: Physical stress and exhaustion from endless striving, poor health from chronic stress, constant physical agitation.
facets.emotional: Chronic dissatisfaction and craving, inability to enjoy present moment, emotional exhaustion from endless wanting, emotional suffering.
facets.cognitive: Mental restlessness and constant wanting, inability to be satisfied, mental exhaustion from chasing, inability to appreciate what is.
facets.psychic: The soul is chronically dissatisfied, its peace always one acquisition away.

deficient.text:
Lack of contentment creates chronic dissatisfaction, endless wanting, and inability to enjoy present circumstances. Happiness is always deferred to the future.

Somatic: Physical stress and exhaustion from endless striving, poor health from chronic stress, constant physical agitation.

Emotional: Chronic dissatisfaction and craving, inability to enjoy present moment, emotional exhaustion from endless wanting, emotional suffering.

Cognitive: Mental restlessness and constant wanting, inability to be satisfied, mental exhaustion from chasing, inability to appreciate what is.

Psychic: The soul is chronically dissatisfied, its peace always one acquisition away.
deficient.vignette: Nothing is ever enough for Dana—the raise disappointing the day it lands, the new thing flawed by week’s end, the horizon of ‘then I’ll be happy’ forever receding. She’s chronically, exhaustingly dissatisfied, and the discontent poisons even her good fortune.

attributes.title: Asūya (Envy/Chronic Fault-Finding)
attributes.aspect: Asūya is a toxic psychological state where the mind is blinded by jealousy. It forces a person to actively seek out and fabricate flaws in others, rendering them incapable of absorbing wisdom or recognizing truth. It is the opposite of receptive learning.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital · Ontic mode: Affliction — a vice that binds · Valence: Affliction (to transmute) · Guṇa: Rajas–Tamas · Scale: Interpersonal (Ādhibhautika) · Causal status: Distortion · Key relation: envy / chronic fault-finding (asūyā); same as Asūyā (Dim 9); antagonist to Anasūya.
coordinate.key_relation: envy / chronic fault-finding (asūyā); same as Asūyā (Dim 9); antagonist to Anasūya.

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.overview: When Asūya is absent and the mind is appropriately balanced, a person can honestly assess others—seeing both strengths and weaknesses without distortion. There is fair perception and constructive feedback when warranted.
facets.somatic: Relaxed facial expression and open body language toward others, healthy physical engagement in social situations, calm nervous system in presence of others’ success, natural ease in interactions.
facets.emotional: Emotional generosity toward others’ achievements, ability to feel genuine appreciation, emotional security that doesn’t require others to be diminished, healthy emotional response to success around oneself.
facets.cognitive: Clear and balanced perception of others, ability to learn from anyone regardless of personal feelings, mental honesty in assessment, open receptivity to wisdom from all sources.
facets.psychic: The soul catches the impulse to envy and find fault, and turns it toward appreciation instead.

balanced.text:
When Asūya is absent and the mind is appropriately balanced, a person can honestly assess others—seeing both strengths and weaknesses without distortion. There is fair perception and constructive feedback when warranted.

Somatic: Relaxed facial expression and open body language toward others, healthy physical engagement in social situations, calm nervous system in presence of others’ success, natural ease in interactions.

Emotional: Emotional generosity toward others’ achievements, ability to feel genuine appreciation, emotional security that doesn’t require others to be diminished, healthy emotional response to success around oneself.

Cognitive: Clear and balanced perception of others, ability to learn from anyone regardless of personal feelings, mental honesty in assessment, open receptivity to wisdom from all sources.

Psychic: The soul catches the impulse to envy and find fault, and turns it toward appreciation instead.
balanced.vignette: Ravi meets others’ success and virtue with genuine appreciation—no reflexive fault-finding, no compulsion to locate the flaw that would cut the admirable down to size. Free of asuya, he can let others be good and gifted without feeling diminished by it.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Excessive Asūya creates a chronic pattern of fault-finding, where the mind becomes addicted to discovering or inventing flaws in others. This blocks all learning and creates social isolation.
facets.somatic: Tension in facial muscles (especially around eyes and mouth), tight chest and restricted breathing, physical signs of suppressed anger, stress-related health issues, hardness in body language.
facets.emotional: Chronic bitterness and resentment, inability to feel joy at others’ success, emotional poisoning from constant negativity, isolation due to repelled relationships, emotional exhaustion from perpetual criticism.
facets.cognitive: Mind addicted to finding flaws, inability to absorb teachings or wisdom, distorted perception of reality, mental fabrication of negative qualities in others, blocked learning, cognitive bias toward negative assessment.
facets.psychic: The soul lives in chronic fault-finding, its eye trained to others’ flaws and fortunes.

excess.text:
Excessive Asūya creates a chronic pattern of fault-finding, where the mind becomes addicted to discovering or inventing flaws in others. This blocks all learning and creates social isolation.

Somatic: Tension in facial muscles (especially around eyes and mouth), tight chest and restricted breathing, physical signs of suppressed anger, stress-related health issues, hardness in body language.

Emotional: Chronic bitterness and resentment, inability to feel joy at others’ success, emotional poisoning from constant negativity, isolation due to repelled relationships, emotional exhaustion from perpetual criticism.

Cognitive: Mind addicted to finding flaws, inability to absorb teachings or wisdom, distorted perception of reality, mental fabrication of negative qualities in others, blocked learning, cognitive bias toward negative assessment.

Psychic: The soul lives in chronic fault-finding, its eye trained to others’ flaws and fortunes.
excess.vignette: Sam can’t let anything be simply good—every success trailing his ‘yes, but,’ every virtue met with the hidden flaw he’s quick to name, a chronic fault-finding that sours every room. The envy dressed as discernment eats at him, and at everyone near him.

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.overview: Complete absence of any critical faculty leads to naive idealization, inability to recognize genuine flaws or dangers, and gullibility in relationships.
facets.somatic: Physical vulnerability from inability to recognize threats, poor judgment about who to trust physically, susceptibility to manipulation that affects body and health.
facets.emotional: Emotional naiveté and idealization, vulnerability to emotional manipulation, inability to set appropriate boundaries, blind devotion that can be exploited.
facets.cognitive: Inability to discriminate or assess critically, accepting everything as good, mental gullibility, lack of healthy skepticism, vulnerability to false teachings.
facets.psychic: The soul, over-correcting, refuses all discernment of real fault, a flattering blindness.

deficient.text:
Complete absence of any critical faculty leads to naive idealization, inability to recognize genuine flaws or dangers, and gullibility in relationships.

Somatic: Physical vulnerability from inability to recognize threats, poor judgment about who to trust physically, susceptibility to manipulation that affects body and health.

Emotional: Emotional naiveté and idealization, vulnerability to emotional manipulation, inability to set appropriate boundaries, blind devotion that can be exploited.

Cognitive: Inability to discriminate or assess critically, accepting everything as good, mental gullibility, lack of healthy skepticism, vulnerability to false teachings.

Psychic: The soul, over-correcting, refuses all discernment of real fault, a flattering blindness.
deficient.vignette: Dan finds no fault in anything—praising the shoddy, excusing the harmful, unable to name a real flaw when naming it matters. In fleeing all fault-finding, he’s lost the discernment that would let him tell the excellent from the poor, and his blanket approval helps no one.

attributes.title: Anasūya (Freedom from Malice/Non-Envious Mind)
attributes.aspect: Anasūya is the prerequisite state of consciousness for learning. It is an open, unbegrudging, and receptive attitude that allows a human to appreciate goodness and higher knowledge without cynicism. It enables wisdom absorption.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital · Ontic mode: Virtue — a quality of freedom · Valence: Virtue to cultivate · Guṇa: Sattva · Scale: Interpersonal (Ādhibhautika) · Causal status: Condition · Key relation: the non-envious mind (anasūyā); direct antagonist to Asūya.
coordinate.key_relation: the non-envious mind (anasūyā); direct antagonist to Asūya.

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.overview: When Anasūya is present, a person approaches life and others with openness and appreciation. There is genuine receptivity to wisdom, ability to recognize and celebrate goodness, and freedom from cynicism.
facets.somatic: Open and receptive body language, soft and warm facial expression, healthy nervous system from positive engagement, physical ease in receiving from others, natural posture of openness.
facets.emotional: Emotional warmth and appreciation, joy in others’ achievements, emotional security and abundance mindset, genuine warmth in relationships, emotional freedom from comparison.
facets.cognitive: Open and receptive mind, ability to absorb wisdom from all sources, mental celebration of others’ insights, freedom from cynical filtering, capacity for genuine learning.
facets.psychic: The soul looks upon others without malice, the non-envious mind that can receive teaching and rejoice in others’ good.

balanced.text:
When Anasūya is present, a person approaches life and others with openness and appreciation. There is genuine receptivity to wisdom, ability to recognize and celebrate goodness, and freedom from cynicism.

Somatic: Open and receptive body language, soft and warm facial expression, healthy nervous system from positive engagement, physical ease in receiving from others, natural posture of openness.

Emotional: Emotional warmth and appreciation, joy in others’ achievements, emotional security and abundance mindset, genuine warmth in relationships, emotional freedom from comparison.

Cognitive: Open and receptive mind, ability to absorb wisdom from all sources, mental celebration of others’ insights, freedom from cynical filtering, capacity for genuine learning.

Psychic: The soul looks upon others without malice, the non-envious mind that can receive teaching and rejoice in others’ good.
balanced.vignette: Meera’s mind is remarkably free of malice—she wishes others well without effort, finds no pleasure in their stumbles, and carries no store of envy toward anyone. The non-envious heart makes her easy to be around and easy in herself; there’s nothing corrosive running underneath.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Excessive Anasūya without discernment can become naive acceptance, idealization of all teachings, and inability to filter out harmful influences.
facets.somatic: Physical openness to harmful influences, lack of protective instincts, vulnerability to bad influences affecting health.
facets.emotional: Emotional naiveté and idealization, vulnerability to emotional manipulation, inability to recognize harmful relationships, indiscriminate emotional acceptance.
facets.cognitive: Inability to filter teachings critically, accepting all ideas without discernment, mental gullibility, vulnerability to false wisdom, lack of healthy skepticism.
facets.psychic: The soul forces a relentless positivity, denying the real faults that discernment should see.

excess.text:
Excessive Anasūya without discernment can become naive acceptance, idealization of all teachings, and inability to filter out harmful influences.

Somatic: Physical openness to harmful influences, lack of protective instincts, vulnerability to bad influences affecting health.

Emotional: Emotional naiveté and idealization, vulnerability to emotional manipulation, inability to recognize harmful relationships, indiscriminate emotional acceptance.

Cognitive: Inability to filter teachings critically, accepting all ideas without discernment, mental gullibility, vulnerability to false wisdom, lack of healthy skepticism.

Psychic: The soul forces a relentless positivity, denying the real faults that discernment should see.
excess.vignette: Sam performs a relentless positivity—refusing to acknowledge any genuine problem, suppressing all critical judgment, mistaking a forced niceness for a non-envious heart. The good-will has become a denial; in refusing all malice he’s also refused all honest discernment, and the suppression curdles underneath.

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.overview: Absence of Anasūya creates a closed, cynical, and begrudging mind that cannot absorb wisdom or appreciate goodness. It prevents all genuine learning.
facets.somatic: Closed and defensive body language, tension and rigidity in face and body, physical resistance to receiving, restrictive breathing patterns.
facets.emotional: Cynicism and chronic suspicion, inability to feel appreciation, emotional poverty mindset, begrudging relationships, blocked emotional flow.
facets.cognitive: Closed mind that rejects wisdom, cynical filtering of all teachings, mental scarcity mindset, inability to learn, mental isolation from wisdom traditions.
facets.psychic: The soul’s mind is clouded by malice, unable to learn from or rejoice in anyone.

deficient.text:
Absence of Anasūya creates a closed, cynical, and begrudging mind that cannot absorb wisdom or appreciate goodness. It prevents all genuine learning.

Somatic: Closed and defensive body language, tension and rigidity in face and body, physical resistance to receiving, restrictive breathing patterns.

Emotional: Cynicism and chronic suspicion, inability to feel appreciation, emotional poverty mindset, begrudging relationships, blocked emotional flow.

Cognitive: Closed mind that rejects wisdom, cynical filtering of all teachings, mental scarcity mindset, inability to learn, mental isolation from wisdom traditions.

Psychic: The soul’s mind is clouded by malice, unable to learn from or rejoice in anyone.
deficient.vignette: Dan’s mind is full of malice—the envy, the fault-finding, the quiet satisfaction at others’ failures running beneath his surface. The non-envious heart the teaching describes is foreign to him; he can’t quite wish anyone well, and the constant low-grade ill-will poisons him first.

attributes.title: Dvesha (Aversion/Repulsion)
attributes.aspect: Dvesha is the psychological twin to attachment (raga). It is the irrational emotional resistance or hatred toward people, objects, or situations that disrupt our comfort zones, driving constant mental agitation and suffering.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital · Ontic mode: Affliction — aversion (dveṣa) · Valence: Affliction (to transmute) · Guṇa: Rajas–Tamas · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Branch of Avidyā · Key relation: Same as Dveṣa the kleśa (Dim 19); antagonist-pair with Rāga.
coordinate.key_relation: Same as Dveṣa the kleśa (Dim 19); antagonist-pair with Rāga.

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.overview: When Dvesha is transcended, a person responds to unpleasant circumstances and people with wisdom rather than reactive resistance. There is acceptance without endorsement, and discrimination without hatred.
facets.somatic: Relaxed physical response to unpleasant stimuli, calm nervous system in challenging situations, healthy physical boundaries without violence, natural physical equanimity.
facets.emotional: Emotional equanimity toward what we dislike, freedom from reactive hatred, ability to set boundaries without aggression, emotional maturity in facing unpleasantness.
facets.cognitive: Mental clarity about what we don’t prefer without obsessive resistance, wisdom in handling difficulties, freedom from compulsive avoidance, balanced discrimination.
facets.psychic: The soul meets the unwanted with a clean refusal, declining without the heat of hatred.

balanced.text:
When Dvesha is transcended, a person responds to unpleasant circumstances and people with wisdom rather than reactive resistance. There is acceptance without endorsement, and discrimination without hatred.

Somatic: Relaxed physical response to unpleasant stimuli, calm nervous system in challenging situations, healthy physical boundaries without violence, natural physical equanimity.

Emotional: Emotional equanimity toward what we dislike, freedom from reactive hatred, ability to set boundaries without aggression, emotional maturity in facing unpleasantness.

Cognitive: Mental clarity about what we don’t prefer without obsessive resistance, wisdom in handling difficulties, freedom from compulsive avoidance, balanced discrimination.

Psychic: The soul meets the unwanted with a clean refusal, declining without the heat of hatred.
balanced.vignette: When something is genuinely not for her, Meera feels a clean ‘no’ and acts on it—without the heat of hatred or the drama of blame. Her aversion is discernment, not contraction; she can refuse and even oppose without her heart closing, the ‘no’ firm and unpoisoned.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Excessive Dvesha creates chronic hatred, irrational aversion, and obsessive avoidance. The mind is constantly agitated by what it rejects, creating as much bondage as attachment.
facets.somatic: Tension and tightness when encountering disliked stimuli, physical aggression or defensive postures, stress-related illness from chronic resistance, nervous system in constant fight-or-flight.
facets.emotional: Chronic hatred and resentment, emotional reactivity to triggers, inability to be at peace with what is, emotional poisoning from rejection, vengeful feelings.
facets.cognitive: Obsessive thinking about disliked objects/people, mental agitation from constant aversion, inability to be present due to mental avoidance, distorted perception colored by hatred.
facets.psychic: The soul hardens into chronic aversion, organizing its life around what it cannot bear.

excess.text:
Excessive Dvesha creates chronic hatred, irrational aversion, and obsessive avoidance. The mind is constantly agitated by what it rejects, creating as much bondage as attachment.

Somatic: Tension and tightness when encountering disliked stimuli, physical aggression or defensive postures, stress-related illness from chronic resistance, nervous system in constant fight-or-flight.

Emotional: Chronic hatred and resentment, emotional reactivity to triggers, inability to be at peace with what is, emotional poisoning from rejection, vengeful feelings.

Cognitive: Obsessive thinking about disliked objects/people, mental agitation from constant aversion, inability to be present due to mental avoidance, distorted perception colored by hatred.

Psychic: The soul hardens into chronic aversion, organizing its life around what it cannot bear.
excess.vignette: Ravi’s dislikes have hardened into hatreds—the person who wronged him, the group he opposes met not with clean refusal but with a clenched, blaming animosity. The healthy ‘no’ has become a fist, and the constant contraction of hating corrodes him from within.

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.overview: Complete absence of Dvesha without wisdom leads to inability to recognize harm or set appropriate boundaries. Accepting everything regardless of impact.
facets.somatic: Inability to physically protect oneself from harm, accepting physical harm without resistance, no instinct to move away from danger.
facets.emotional: Inability to feel appropriate concern about harmful situations, emotional acceptance of toxic relationships, no protective emotional response.
facets.cognitive: Inability to discriminate against harmful ideas or behaviors, accepting destructive influences, lack of mental boundaries, vulnerability to harm.
facets.psychic: The soul loses all healthy aversion, unable to refuse even what genuinely harms it.

deficient.text:
Complete absence of Dvesha without wisdom leads to inability to recognize harm or set appropriate boundaries. Accepting everything regardless of impact.

Somatic: Inability to physically protect oneself from harm, accepting physical harm without resistance, no instinct to move away from danger.

Emotional: Inability to feel appropriate concern about harmful situations, emotional acceptance of toxic relationships, no protective emotional response.

Cognitive: Inability to discriminate against harmful ideas or behaviors, accepting destructive influences, lack of mental boundaries, vulnerability to harm.

Psychic: The soul loses all healthy aversion, unable to refuse even what genuinely harms it.
deficient.vignette: So committed to acceptance, Dana can’t refuse anything—tolerating the exploitative, the abusive, the genuinely harmful because saying ‘no’ feels like a failure of love. The healthy aversion that should protect her has been over-corrected into a dangerous inability to recognize and refuse real harm.

attributes.title: Mada (Arrogance/Intoxication of Success)
attributes.aspect: Mada is the behavioral blindness born from pride—whether due to wealth, intelligence, beauty, lineage, or status—that causes an individual to treat others with condescension and disregard cosmic order. It is intoxication with one’s own importance.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital–Mental · Ontic mode: Affliction — a vice that binds · Valence: Affliction (to transmute) · Guṇa: Rajas–Tamas · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Distortion · Key relation: pride-intoxication of success (mada); same as Mada (Dim 9).
coordinate.key_relation: pride-intoxication of success (mada); same as Mada (Dim 9).

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.overview: When Mada is absent, a person can hold success, wealth, or status with humility. There is appreciation for achievements without inflating self-importance, and continued respect for all beings.
facets.somatic: Natural and relaxed physical bearing despite success, healthy self-care without vanity, body that serves rather than displays, grounded physical presence.
facets.emotional: Humility despite achievements, emotional respect for all people regardless of status, gratitude for success rather than entitlement, emotional security that doesn’t need external validation.
facets.cognitive: Clear awareness that success doesn’t make one fundamentally superior, mental humility about achievements, recognition of factors beyond personal control, wisdom about impermanence of status.
facets.psychic: The soul holds its successes lightly, neither intoxicated by them nor pretending they are nothing.

balanced.text:
When Mada is absent, a person can hold success, wealth, or status with humility. There is appreciation for achievements without inflating self-importance, and continued respect for all beings.

Somatic: Natural and relaxed physical bearing despite success, healthy self-care without vanity, body that serves rather than displays, grounded physical presence.

Emotional: Humility despite achievements, emotional respect for all people regardless of status, gratitude for success rather than entitlement, emotional security that doesn’t need external validation.

Cognitive: Clear awareness that success doesn’t make one fundamentally superior, mental humility about achievements, recognition of factors beyond personal control, wisdom about impermanence of status.

Psychic: The soul holds its successes lightly, neither intoxicated by them nor pretending they are nothing.
balanced.vignette: When success comes, Ravi holds it lightly—grateful, unintoxicated, aware that the win is partly luck and wholly temporary. He can achieve and be praised without swelling, and the humility keeps him teachable and human where success makes so many insufferable.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Excessive Mada creates intoxicating arrogance where the person treats themselves as inherently superior to others. This blindness leads to violation of natural order and harm to others.
facets.somatic: Inflated and showy physical bearing, contemptuous body language toward perceived inferiors, physical excess and indulgence, posture of superiority, neglect of basic human needs in pursuit of display.
facets.emotional: Contempt and condescension toward others, emotional disconnection from common humanity, inflated sense of self-worth, emotional cruelty to those of lower status, intoxicating pride.
facets.cognitive: Mental blindness to one’s own faults, inability to receive feedback, distorted self-perception, contempt for others’ intelligence or worth, mental violation of natural order, delusions of grandeur.
facets.psychic: The soul is drunk on its achievements, arrogance blinding it to its own faults.

excess.text:
Excessive Mada creates intoxicating arrogance where the person treats themselves as inherently superior to others. This blindness leads to violation of natural order and harm to others.

Somatic: Inflated and showy physical bearing, contemptuous body language toward perceived inferiors, physical excess and indulgence, posture of superiority, neglect of basic human needs in pursuit of display.

Emotional: Contempt and condescension toward others, emotional disconnection from common humanity, inflated sense of self-worth, emotional cruelty to those of lower status, intoxicating pride.

Cognitive: Mental blindness to one’s own faults, inability to receive feedback, distorted self-perception, contempt for others’ intelligence or worth, mental violation of natural order, delusions of grandeur.

Psychic: The soul is drunk on its achievements, arrogance blinding it to its own faults.
excess.vignette: Success has gone to Ben’s head like wine—swaggering, condescending, certain his winning proves his superiority, drunk on his own achievement. The intoxication of mada has made him unbearable and blind; he mistakes a lucky streak for a birthright, and looks down from a height that won’t last.

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.overview: Complete absence of any sense of accomplishment can manifest as excessive self-deprecation, inability to recognize one’s worth, and false humility that disrespects one’s gifts.
facets.somatic: Diminished and shrinking physical presence, poor self-care, body language of unworthiness, physical neglect.
facets.emotional: Excessive self-deprecation, inability to feel appropriate pride, emotional unworthiness, false humility that creates resentment.
facets.cognitive: Inability to recognize one’s own gifts and contributions, distorted view of own worth, mental dismissal of legitimate achievements, undermining one’s own legitimate confidence.
facets.psychic: The soul cannot own its real accomplishments at all, a false humility that is its own distortion.

deficient.text:
Complete absence of any sense of accomplishment can manifest as excessive self-deprecation, inability to recognize one’s worth, and false humility that disrespects one’s gifts.

Somatic: Diminished and shrinking physical presence, poor self-care, body language of unworthiness, physical neglect.

Emotional: Excessive self-deprecation, inability to feel appropriate pride, emotional unworthiness, false humility that creates resentment.

Cognitive: Inability to recognize one’s own gifts and contributions, distorted view of own worth, mental dismissal of legitimate achievements, undermining one’s own legitimate confidence.

Psychic: The soul cannot own its real accomplishments at all, a false humility that is its own distortion.
deficient.vignette: Dan can’t own even his genuine achievements—deflecting every success, minimizing every win, so allergic to arrogance that he’s erased his legitimate pride. In fleeing mada so hard, he’s lost the healthy self-regard that lets a person stand on what they’ve actually earned.

attributes.title: Moha (Delusion/Mental Obfuscation)
attributes.aspect: Moha is a state of cognitive paralysis or infatuation. It makes an individual mistake the temporary for the permanent, the harmful for the beneficial, and untruth for truth. It is the primary obscuration of clear perception.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental · Ontic mode: Affliction — delusion (moha) · Valence: Affliction (to see through) · Guṇa: Tamas-dominant · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Mechanism of concealment · Key relation: Same as Moha (Dim 9); the clouding of judgment.
coordinate.key_relation: Same as Moha (Dim 9); the clouding of judgment.

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.overview: When Moha is dispelled, a person perceives reality clearly without distortion. There is accurate understanding of what is temporary vs. permanent, helpful vs. harmful, and true vs. false.
facets.somatic: Clear-eyed and alert physical presence, accurate physical responses to reality, healthy physical choices based on truth, body that responds appropriately to actual circumstances.
facets.emotional: Emotional clarity and freedom from confusion, ability to feel appropriately based on actual situation, emotional honesty, freedom from emotional infatuation that distorts perception.
facets.cognitive: Crystal clear perception of reality, freedom from cognitive distortions, accurate assessment of permanence and impermanence, wise discrimination between true and false.
facets.psychic: The soul recognizes the fog of delusion when it descends, and waits for clarity rather than acting blind.

balanced.text:
When Moha is dispelled, a person perceives reality clearly without distortion. There is accurate understanding of what is temporary vs. permanent, helpful vs. harmful, and true vs. false.

Somatic: Clear-eyed and alert physical presence, accurate physical responses to reality, healthy physical choices based on truth, body that responds appropriately to actual circumstances.

Emotional: Emotional clarity and freedom from confusion, ability to feel appropriately based on actual situation, emotional honesty, freedom from emotional infatuation that distorts perception.

Cognitive: Crystal clear perception of reality, freedom from cognitive distortions, accurate assessment of permanence and impermanence, wise discrimination between true and false.

Psychic: The soul recognizes the fog of delusion when it descends, and waits for clarity rather than acting blind.
balanced.vignette: When confusion clouds a situation, Ravi can usually see through it—the delusions, the wishful stories, the self-serving distortions—to what’s actually there. The clarity isn’t cold; it’s a hard-won capacity to not be fooled, least of all by himself.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Excessive Moha creates profound delusion where the person is completely lost in cognitive paralysis. Major life decisions become impossible, and reality is fundamentally misperceived.
facets.somatic: Physical paralysis and confusion in actions, poor coordination from mental fog, physical mistakes from distorted perception, neglecting bodily needs due to confusion.
facets.emotional: Emotional infatuation and obsession, attachment to harmful relationships, inability to feel reality clearly, emotional confusion and turmoil, emotional paralysis.
facets.cognitive: Profound cognitive paralysis, complete inability to discriminate truth from falsity, infatuation that obscures all wisdom, mental fog and confusion, severely distorted perception.
facets.psychic: The soul lives wholly within the fog, its judgment obscured at every turn.

excess.text:
Excessive Moha creates profound delusion where the person is completely lost in cognitive paralysis. Major life decisions become impossible, and reality is fundamentally misperceived.

Somatic: Physical paralysis and confusion in actions, poor coordination from mental fog, physical mistakes from distorted perception, neglecting bodily needs due to confusion.

Emotional: Emotional infatuation and obsession, attachment to harmful relationships, inability to feel reality clearly, emotional confusion and turmoil, emotional paralysis.

Cognitive: Profound cognitive paralysis, complete inability to discriminate truth from falsity, infatuation that obscures all wisdom, mental fog and confusion, severely distorted perception.

Psychic: The soul lives wholly within the fog, its judgment obscured at every turn.
excess.vignette: Sam lives in a fog of self-serving delusion—misreading his relationships, his finances, his own motives, each confusion held with total certainty. The moha runs deep and invisible; he can’t see that he can’t see, and every decision built on the fog goes wrong in ways he never traces to their source.

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.overview: While complete absence of Moha is the ideal, in this context, deficiency could manifest as harsh, dispassionate analysis without warmth or human connection.
facets.somatic: Cold and detached physical presence, lack of warmth in interactions, mechanical physical responses.
facets.emotional: Emotional coldness that mistakes detachment for clarity, inability to engage with normal human emotions, harsh emotional analysis.
facets.cognitive: Hyper-analytical thinking without compassion, mental dismissiveness of emotional realities, harsh judgment masquerading as clarity.
facets.psychic: The soul presumes itself wholly clear-eyed, a confidence that is delusion’s subtlest form.

deficient.text:
While complete absence of Moha is the ideal, in this context, deficiency could manifest as harsh, dispassionate analysis without warmth or human connection.

Somatic: Cold and detached physical presence, lack of warmth in interactions, mechanical physical responses.

Emotional: Emotional coldness that mistakes detachment for clarity, inability to engage with normal human emotions, harsh emotional analysis.

Cognitive: Hyper-analytical thinking without compassion, mental dismissiveness of emotional realities, harsh judgment masquerading as clarity.

Psychic: The soul presumes itself wholly clear-eyed, a confidence that is delusion’s subtlest form.
deficient.vignette: Dan has grown so coldly disenchanted, so obsessed with seeing through everything, that he’s lost the capacity for ordinary wonder and warmth—clarity hardened into a joyless disillusionment. In fleeing all delusion he’s also negated the healthy enchantments that make a life livable, and the world has gone grey.

attributes.title: Matsarya (Jealousy/Competitiveness)
attributes.aspect: Matsarya is the painful human experience of misery caused by witnessing another person’s prosperity, leading to a perpetual, anxious state of comparison. Unlike envy (asuya), this is more about competitive anxiety and personal suffering.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital · Ontic mode: Affliction — a vice that binds · Valence: Affliction (to transmute) · Guṇa: Rajas–Tamas · Scale: Interpersonal (Ādhibhautika) · Causal status: Distortion · Key relation: jealousy / competitiveness (mātsarya); kin to Asūya.
coordinate.key_relation: jealousy / competitiveness (mātsarya); kin to Asūya.

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.overview: When Matsarya is absent, a person experiences abundance mindset and can celebrate others’ success genuinely. There is healthy comparison for learning but no compulsive measuring.
facets.somatic: Relaxed physical presence around successful others, healthy nervous system, no stress reaction to others’ achievements, physical energy free for one’s own pursuits.
facets.emotional: Genuine joy at others’ success, emotional security that doesn’t depend on relative standing, abundance mindset, freedom from comparison suffering, emotional generosity.
facets.cognitive: Mental clarity that allows celebration of others, ability to learn from successful people without resentment, freedom from compulsive comparison, mental focus on own path.
facets.psychic: The soul notices its competitive jealousy and lets it relax into a shared gladness at others’ good.

balanced.text:
When Matsarya is absent, a person experiences abundance mindset and can celebrate others’ success genuinely. There is healthy comparison for learning but no compulsive measuring.

Somatic: Relaxed physical presence around successful others, healthy nervous system, no stress reaction to others’ achievements, physical energy free for one’s own pursuits.

Emotional: Genuine joy at others’ success, emotional security that doesn’t depend on relative standing, abundance mindset, freedom from comparison suffering, emotional generosity.

Cognitive: Mental clarity that allows celebration of others, ability to learn from successful people without resentment, freedom from compulsive comparison, mental focus on own path.

Psychic: The soul notices its competitive jealousy and lets it relax into a shared gladness at others’ good.
balanced.vignette: When a peer outshines him, Ravi feels a clean admiration rather than a competitive sting—secure enough in himself that another’s excellence doesn’t diminish him. Free of matsarya, he can celebrate the people who surpass him, and the freedom makes him a generous colleague and a peaceful man.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Excessive Matsarya creates a constant painful state of comparison. Every other person’s success becomes a personal wound, creating chronic suffering and anxiety.
facets.somatic: Chronic stress response, tension and sleep disturbances, stress-related illness, physical exhaustion from competitive striving, restless physical state.
facets.emotional: Chronic emotional pain from others’ success, perpetual anxiety about relative standing, emotional bitterness, inability to feel content, emotional torment from comparison.
facets.cognitive: Compulsive mental comparison with others, distorted thinking dominated by competition, inability to focus on own life, mental obsession with rankings and status, anxiety-driven thinking.
facets.psychic: The soul measures itself ceaselessly against others, jealousy souring every comparison.

excess.text:
Excessive Matsarya creates a constant painful state of comparison. Every other person’s success becomes a personal wound, creating chronic suffering and anxiety.

Somatic: Chronic stress response, tension and sleep disturbances, stress-related illness, physical exhaustion from competitive striving, restless physical state.

Emotional: Chronic emotional pain from others’ success, perpetual anxiety about relative standing, emotional bitterness, inability to feel content, emotional torment from comparison.

Cognitive: Compulsive mental comparison with others, distorted thinking dominated by competition, inability to focus on own life, mental obsession with rankings and status, anxiety-driven thinking.

Psychic: The soul measures itself ceaselessly against others, jealousy souring every comparison.
excess.vignette: Every rival’s win is a wound to Ben—the promotion that went to someone else, the peer who’s further along, each success met with a gnawing competitive jealousy he can’t quite hide. Consumed by matsarya, he measures his life against others’ and finds it, always, a defeat.

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.overview: Complete absence of any competitive drive could manifest as lack of motivation to improve, no awareness of standards, and complacency.
facets.somatic: Lack of physical drive or vitality, complacent body language, lethargic physical state.
facets.emotional: Emotional flatness about achievement, lack of inspiration from others’ success, complacency and unmotivation.
facets.cognitive: No mental engagement with standards of excellence, complacency, lack of healthy motivation to grow, mental disengagement from achievement.
facets.psychic: The soul, over-correcting, abandons all healthy aspiration, mistaking ambition for sin.

deficient.text:
Complete absence of any competitive drive could manifest as lack of motivation to improve, no awareness of standards, and complacency.

Somatic: Lack of physical drive or vitality, complacent body language, lethargic physical state.

Emotional: Emotional flatness about achievement, lack of inspiration from others’ success, complacency and unmotivation.

Cognitive: No mental engagement with standards of excellence, complacency, lack of healthy motivation to grow, mental disengagement from achievement.

Psychic: The soul, over-correcting, abandons all healthy aspiration, mistaking ambition for sin.
deficient.vignette: Dan is so indifferent to excellence that he’s lost all healthy drive—unmoved by others’ achievements, unstirred to grow, mistaking his flatness for equanimity. In having no competitive spark at all, he’s also lost the aspiration that would push him to become anything; nothing anyone does moves him to reach.

attributes.title: Hridayadaurbalyam (Weakness of Heart/Misplaced Compassion)
attributes.aspect: Hridayadaurbalyam is weakness of the heart manifesting as misplaced compassion, wrong identification, emotional sympathy that paralyzes right action, and incapacity to remain firmly grounded in emotional turmoil. It is what Krishna diagnoses in Arjuna.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital · Ontic mode: Affliction — a vice that binds · Valence: Affliction (to transmute) · Guṇa: Tamas · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Distortion · Key relation: weakness of heart / misplaced compassion (Gītā); kin to Kārpaṇya and the heart-knot.
coordinate.key_relation: weakness of heart / misplaced compassion (Gītā); kin to Kārpaṇya and the heart-knot.

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.overview: When Hridayadaurbalyam is transcended, a person has strong heart wisdom—able to feel deeply while remaining grounded in dharma. Compassion is wisely directed, not misplaced.
facets.somatic: Strong heart center and chest, healthy cardiovascular function, grounded physical presence even in emotional situations, body anchored despite feelings.
facets.emotional: Strong emotional foundation, ability to feel deeply without losing direction, healthy compassion that doesn’t disable, emotional strength combined with sensitivity.
facets.cognitive: Clear mental discrimination even in emotional situations, wisdom about appropriate response to suffering, mental grounding despite heart feelings, ability to act rightly.
facets.psychic: The soul distinguishes true compassion from the weakness of heart that shrinks from necessary action.

balanced.text:
When Hridayadaurbalyam is transcended, a person has strong heart wisdom—able to feel deeply while remaining grounded in dharma. Compassion is wisely directed, not misplaced.

Somatic: Strong heart center and chest, healthy cardiovascular function, grounded physical presence even in emotional situations, body anchored despite feelings.

Emotional: Strong emotional foundation, ability to feel deeply without losing direction, healthy compassion that doesn’t disable, emotional strength combined with sensitivity.

Cognitive: Clear mental discrimination even in emotional situations, wisdom about appropriate response to suffering, mental grounding despite heart feelings, ability to act rightly.

Psychic: The soul distinguishes true compassion from the weakness of heart that shrinks from necessary action.
balanced.vignette: When his compassion tempts him toward a weakness that would do real harm—coddling the addict, sparing the confrontation someone needs—Ravi finds the strength to do the harder, truer, kinder thing. Like Arjuna urged past his collapse, his heart is strong enough to act rightly even when it aches.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Severe Hridayadaurbalyam (the excessive state of weakness) creates total emotional paralysis—the person cannot act rightly because feelings overwhelm wisdom. This was Arjuna’s crisis.
facets.somatic: Physical collapse and paralysis, weakness in body, inability to take action, physical signs of emotional overwhelm, breakdown of physical capacity.
facets.emotional: Emotional overwhelm and paralysis, misplaced compassion that prevents right action, drowning in feelings, emotional confusion about right path.
facets.cognitive: Complete mental paralysis, inability to think clearly through emotional fog, loss of discrimination, mental collapse, inability to remember duty.
facets.psychic: The soul indulges a sentimental softness that cannot do the hard, kind thing.

excess.text:
Severe Hridayadaurbalyam (the excessive state of weakness) creates total emotional paralysis—the person cannot act rightly because feelings overwhelm wisdom. This was Arjuna’s crisis.

Somatic: Physical collapse and paralysis, weakness in body, inability to take action, physical signs of emotional overwhelm, breakdown of physical capacity.

Emotional: Emotional overwhelm and paralysis, misplaced compassion that prevents right action, drowning in feelings, emotional confusion about right path.

Cognitive: Complete mental paralysis, inability to think clearly through emotional fog, loss of discrimination, mental collapse, inability to remember duty.

Psychic: The soul indulges a sentimental softness that cannot do the hard, kind thing.
excess.vignette: Sam’s ‘compassion’ is really a weakness of heart—enabling the addict, permitting the harm, avoiding every hard truth because facing it feels unkind. The misplaced softness lets real damage proceed, and he mistakes his inability to be firm for a tenderness, when it’s closer to a failure of nerve.

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.overview: Complete absence of any heart feeling becomes cold disregard for others, inability to feel appropriate emotional response, and cruelty.
facets.somatic: Closed and hard physical presence, lack of physical warmth, cold body language.
facets.emotional: Emotional coldness and cruelty, inability to feel for others, hardness of heart, missing empathy.
facets.cognitive: Calculating thinking without heart, mental cruelty, inability to consider emotional dimensions, harsh logic without compassion.
facets.psychic: The soul hardens entirely against feeling, mistaking callousness for strength.

deficient.text:
Complete absence of any heart feeling becomes cold disregard for others, inability to feel appropriate emotional response, and cruelty.

Somatic: Closed and hard physical presence, lack of physical warmth, cold body language.

Emotional: Emotional coldness and cruelty, inability to feel for others, hardness of heart, missing empathy.

Cognitive: Calculating thinking without heart, mental cruelty, inability to consider emotional dimensions, harsh logic without compassion.

Psychic: The soul hardens entirely against feeling, mistaking callousness for strength.
deficient.vignette: Dan’s heart has gone hard—no compassion to be weakened, no tenderness to misplace, only a cool indifference to others’ suffering. The weakness of heart isn’t his problem; the absence of heart is, and where misplaced compassion would at least be a distortion of care, he offers no care to distort.

attributes.title: Atma-Nivedanam (Total Self-Offering)
attributes.aspect: Atma-nivedanam is total self-offering — surrendering body, mind, intelligence, and all results to the Divine. The culminating practice. In daily life this manifests as the spirit captured in Bhagavad Gita 9.27: ‘Whatever you do, whatever you eat, whatever you offer, whatever you give away, whatever austerity you perform—do it as an offering to me.’ Removes the personal burden of doership.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Psychic–Vital · Ontic mode: Practice — total self-surrender (ātma-nivedana) · Valence: Virtue / the highest bhakti · Guṇa: Sattva-dominant · Scale: Individual, toward the Divine · Causal status: Means (complete self-offering) · Key relation: The ninth and highest limb of Bhakti; kin to Īśvara-praṇidhāna (Dim 11).
coordinate.key_relation: The ninth and highest limb of Bhakti; kin to Īśvara-praṇidhāna (Dim 11).

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.overview: All of life genuinely offered to the Divine. The ‘I am the doer’ weight dissolves.
facets.somatic: Body’s actions all offered as worship.
facets.emotional: Emotional life surrendered without burden.
facets.cognitive: Mind genuinely offers all to the Divine.
facets.psychic: The soul offers its very selfhood to the Divine, the final surrender in which not only deeds but the doer is given.

balanced.text:
All of life genuinely offered to the Divine. The ‘I am the doer’ weight dissolves.

Somatic: Body’s actions all offered as worship.

Emotional: Emotional life surrendered without burden.

Cognitive: Mind genuinely offers all to the Divine.

Psychic: The soul offers its very selfhood to the Divine, the final surrender in which not only deeds but the doer is given.
balanced.vignette: Ravi has begun, genuinely, to offer the whole of himself to something larger—not a dramatic gesture but a daily surrender of his will, his outcomes, his very sense of being the doer. The self-offering has lightened him; carrying less of the weight of ‘me,’ he moves through his days more freely.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Passive surrender that abdicates responsibility — ‘God will do everything.’ Magical thinking.
facets.somatic: Body inactive expecting Divine intervention.
facets.emotional: Childish dependency masquerading as surrender.
facets.cognitive: Mental abdication of agency.
facets.psychic: The soul performs surrender while secretly withholding, an offering staged for itself.

excess.text:
Passive surrender that abdicates responsibility — ‘God will do everything.’ Magical thinking.

Somatic: Body inactive expecting Divine intervention.

Emotional: Childish dependency masquerading as surrender.

Cognitive: Mental abdication of agency.

Psychic: The soul performs surrender while secretly withholding, an offering staged for itself.
excess.vignette: Sam performs total surrender—the theatrical devotion, the announced self-offering—while keeping a tight grip on everything that matters to him. The self he ‘offers’ is a costume; the real one stays firmly in charge, and the performance of surrender is one more thing the ego is running.

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.overview: Total ego-doership. Every accomplishment claimed personally; every failure resented. Maximum burden.
facets.somatic: Body strained from constant self-effort.
facets.emotional: Emotional life of pride and disappointment cycles.
facets.cognitive: Mind carries the heavy weight of being ‘the doer.’
facets.psychic: The soul keeps its self wholly its own, unable to give the one gift that would free it.

deficient.text:
Total ego-doership. Every accomplishment claimed personally; every failure resented. Maximum burden.

Somatic: Body strained from constant self-effort.

Emotional: Emotional life of pride and disappointment cycles.

Cognitive: Mind carries the heavy weight of being ‘the doer.’

Psychic: The soul keeps its self wholly its own, unable to give the one gift that would free it.
deficient.vignette: Dan can surrender nothing—not his will, not his outcomes, not the smallest sense of control—gripping the whole of himself with both hands. The total self-offering the teaching describes is not something he’s failed at; it’s a release he’d sooner die than attempt, and so he carries every ounce of his own weight, alone.

attributes.title: The Leap Into the Unknown
attributes.aspect: All spiritual realization ultimately requires a leap into the unknown—surrendering known frameworks, self-conceptions, even spiritual concepts themselves.
